# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are aligned with **KTU 2024 Scheme OBE regulations**, **Course Outcome CO1** (Identify the components of a Networked Control System and their roles), and follow the **Bloom's Taxonomy L2–L3 cognitive level** appropriate to Module 1.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define a Networked Control System. List any four major components of an NCS.**

**Model Answer:**
A Networked Control System (NCS) is a closed-loop feedback control architecture in which the sensor, controller, and actuator nodes communicate over a shared digital communication network rather than through point-to-point wiring. The four major components are:
1. **Sensors** — to sample the plant output.
2. **Controller** — to compute the control law.
3. **Actuators** — to apply the control action to the plant.
4. **Communication Network** — the shared medium that interconnects the nodes and introduces induced delays and packet dropouts.

*Bloom's Level: L1 (Remember). Mapping: CO1.*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) in an NCS?**

**Model Answer:**
The ZOH is located at the **actuator node** and holds the most recently received digital control value $u(t_k)$ **constant** for the entire duration of the sampling interval $h$, i.e., $u(t) = u(t_k)$ for $t \in [t_k,\ t_{k+1})$. It serves three purposes:
- **D/A reconstruction** of the discrete signal into a piecewise-constant analog input.
- **Synchronization** between the discrete controller and the continuous plant.
- **Mitigation of jitter** by holding the value until the next update arrives.

*Bloom's Level: L2 (Understand). Mapping: CO1.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **ESE 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[Adapted from KTU 2024 Model Paper - Module 1]`

**a) Draw the functional block diagram of a generic Networked Control System and identify the delay introduced at each block.** *(7 Marks)*

**b) Derive the discrete-time state-space model of an NCS with a constant total delay $\tau < h$ and ZOH, starting from a continuous LTI plant.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

**Block diagram** (must be drawn on the left page of the answer book; refer to Section 4.1 above for the Mermaid equivalent):

```
r(t) ─►(Σ)──e(t)──►[Controller]──u(k)──►[Network τ_ca]──►[ZOH + Actuator]──►[Plant]──┐
            ▲                                                                              │
            │                                                                              ▼
            └────────────[Network τ_sc]◄──[Sensor + A/D]◄── y(t) ◄────────────────────────┘
```

**Delays at each block:**

| Block | Delay Symbol | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor + A/D | $\tau_s$ | $\mu$s to ms |
| Network (sensor→controller) | $\tau_{sc}$ | 0.1 – 100 ms |
| Controller computation | $\tau_c$ | 0.01 – 0.1 ms |
| Network (controller→actuator) | $\tau_{ca}$ | 0.1 – 100 ms |
| ZOH holding | $h - \tau_k$ | variable |
| **Total** | $\tau_k = \tau_s + \tau_{sc} + \tau_c + \tau_{ca}$ | bounded |

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1 above**. The student should write:

1. Discretize the plant: $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B_0 u(k)$.
2. Model the delay by setting $u(k) \to u(k-1)$ for $\tau < h$.
3. State the closed-loop model $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B_0 K x(k-1)$.
4. State the augmented system and its characteristic polynomial.
5. State the stability test (Schur-Cohn / Jury).

**Final boxed answer:**
$$x(k+1) = A \, x(k) + B_0 K \, x(k-1) \quad \text{with} \quad A = e^{A_c h},\ B_0 = \int_0^h e^{A_c(h-s)} B_c \, ds$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

**a) Explain the role of time-stamping in NCS. How does it help in dealing with network-induced delay?** *(7 Marks)*

**b) For a scalar plant $\dot x = 0.5 x + u$ controlled over a network with total bounded delay $\bar \tau = 0.2$ s, find the maximum admissible sampling period $h$ that guarantees stability with a deadbeat controller. Use the bound derived in Section 3.2.** *(7 Marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Time-stamping** is the process of attaching the **instant of data creation** $t_s$ to every sensor/control packet before transmission.

**Functions:**
- **Age computation** at the receiver: $t_{age} = t_{now} - t_s$.
- **Out-of-order rejection:** if $t_s < t_{s,prev}$, the packet is **discarded** (stale data).
- **Delay compensation:** the controller can **predict** forward by $t_{age}$ to compensate for known delay.
- **Synchronization:** aligns the discrete events of the sensor and controller clocks.

**Help in dealing with delay:** by knowing the exact age of each measurement, the controller can apply **Smith Predictor**, **Buffer-based**, or **Forward-predictive** compensation strategies, dramatically improving the **delay margin** of the closed loop.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

Given: $a = 0.5$, $\bar \tau = 0.2$ s. Deadbeat: $K$ chosen such that the closed-loop pole is at the origin.

Using the Montestruque-style bound from Section 3.2:
$$h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{a} \ln \bigl(1 + a/K \bigr) - \bar \tau$$

For deadbeat with a first-order plant, $K = a (1 + e^{-a h})/b$ is conventionally chosen. For a unit-gain deadbeat (with $b = 1$):
$$K = 1 + e^{-a h}$$

Solving numerically with $a = 0.5$:
- Try $h = 0.3$ s: $K = 1 + e^{-0.15} \approx 1.861$.
- Bound check: $h_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.5} \ln(1 + 0.5/1.861) - 0.2 = 2 \ln(1.2686) - 0.2 = 2(0.2379) - 0.2 = 0.2758$ s.
- Since $0.3 > 0.2758$, $h = 0.3$ s violates the bound.
- Try $h = 0.25$ s: $K = 1 + e^{-0.125} \approx 1.8825$, $h_{max} = 2 \ln(1.2655) - 0.2 = 2(0.2354) - 0.2 = 0.2708$ s.
- $h = 0.25 < 0.2708$ ✔ — **admissible**.

**Final answer:** The maximum admissible sampling period is **$h_{max} \approx 0.27$ s**, so a practical design choice is $h = 0.25$ s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 1 NCS Components:**
> 1. **Confusing $\tau_{sc}$ and $\tau_{ca}$:** Many students swap them. Remember: **sc** = sensor to controller, **ca** = controller to actuator. **Both are LUMPED as $\tau_k$ in the canonical model** for Module 1 derivations.
> 2. **Forgetting the ZOH:** Marks are deducted if the ZOH block is omitted from the block diagram. The ZOH is **always** drawn between the network and the plant.
> 3. **Mixing up units:** $h$ (sampling period) is in **seconds**, $\omega_{max}$ is in **rad/s**. Watch the factor of $2\pi$ in Nyquist bound problems.
> 4. **Discrete vs continuous confusion:** When asked to "discretize", students often write the continuous plant equation. Always produce the **discrete-time difference equation** with the matrix $A = e^{A_c h}$.
> 5. **Dropout vs Delay:** $\tau_k$ is a **delay**, $q$ is a **dropout rate**. Do not interchange them in stability expressions.
> 6. **Stability domain:** Jury's test requires **all roots inside the unit circle** $\vert \lambda \vert < 1$, NOT the entire complex plane. Kark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NCS = Distributed control loop closed through a shared communication network.** The four component families are **Sensing, Computation, Actuation, Communication**.
- **Total delay** is the canonical lumped quantity: $\tau_k = \tau_{sc,k} + \tau_{ca,k}$, often bounded by $\bar \tau$.
- **ZOH** is the standard actuator-side reconstructor; it holds $u(t)$ constant for the sampling period $h$.
- **Time-stamping** is the key software-layer mechanism that enables the controller to compute data age and apply delay compensation.
- **Packet dropouts** are modeled as a Bernoulli process with probability $q$; the **effective** received packet is $\hat y(k) = (1 - \delta_k) y(k)$ where $\delta_k \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Discrete canonical NCS model (one-sample delay):** $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B_0 K x(k-1)$.
- **Nyquist condition for sampling:** $h \le \pi / \omega_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.
- **Stability bound (Montestruque-style):** $h < \dfrac{1}{a} \ln(1 + a/K) - \bar \tau$ for scalar plants.
- **Quantization step** in an $n$-bit A/D: $q = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.
- **KTU 2024 emphasis:** the question paper almost always asks for **(i) a labelled block diagram** and **(ii) a delay-modeling derivation** in Module 1. Practice these two patterns until they are reflex.
- **Industrial protocol examples to memorize:** CAN (automotive), Profibus/Profinet (factory), WirelessHART (process), IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi (lab/IoT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Acquisition in a Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Information Acquisition in a Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Networked Control Systems (NCS)** and advanced automation architectures, **Information Acquisition in a Network** refers to the systematic process of sensing, sampling, encoding, quantizing, time-stamping, and reliably transmitting physical process variables (such as temperature, pressure, position, velocity, flow rate, or chemical concentration) from geographically distributed sensor nodes to estimators, controllers, and supervisory decision-making units over a shared digital communication medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT862 / Module 1):**
> Information acquisition constitutes the **front-end layer** of every networked control loop. It determines the *quality*, *freshness*, *timeliness*, and *integrity* of the data that subsequently drives state estimation, fault diagnosis, and feedback control. In NCS literature, it is also called the **Sensor–Network Interface (SNI)** stage.

Mathematically, the information acquisition process transforms a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude physical signal $x_c(t)$ into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude data stream $x_d[k]$ suitable for transmission over a packet-switched network, and this transformation is governed by:

$$
x_d[k] = Q\bigl(S(x_c(t))\bigr)\big\vert_{t = kT_s}
$$

where $S(\cdot)$ is the **sampling operator**, $Q(\cdot)$ is the **quantization operator**, and $T_s$ is the **sampling period**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Hospital ICU"**
> Imagine a smart hospital's **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** where dozens of critically ill patients are simultaneously monitored. Each patient is connected to a **bedside sensor cluster** — ECG leads, SpO₂ finger clips, blood-pressure cuffs, and temperature probes. These sensors continuously produce analog physiological waveforms.
> Now consider the challenge: a **remote senior cardiologist**, sitting in another building, must supervise **all patients in real-time** through a shared Wi-Fi network. The bedside monitors cannot send the raw analog waveforms directly — they must:
> 1. **Sample** the ECG at, say, 250 Hz (a standard clinical rate).
> 2. **Digitize** the amplitudes using a 12-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC).
> 3. **Time-stamp** each sample so the cardiologist knows its freshness.
> 4. **Packetize** the samples and transmit them via TCP/UDP.
> 5. **Buffer** and **reorder** the packets at the receiver (since UDP may deliver out of order).
> 6. **Display** the reconstructed waveform in near real-time.
> 
> This entire pipeline — from bedside sensor to cardiologist's screen — is precisely what **Information Acquisition in a Network** describes in an engineering NCS. The "patients" are the plant outputs, the "bedside monitors" are the Remote Terminal Units (RTUs) or smart sensors, the "Wi-Fi" is the shared communication network, and the "cardiologist's screen" is the estimator/controller.

## 1.3 Fundamental Constituents of Information Acquisition

Every networked information-acquisition subsystem comprises the following six functional blocks:

| # | Block | Engineering Function | Typical Hardware/Software |
|---|-------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Transducer / Sensor** | Converts physical variable → electrical signal | Thermocouple, RTD, LVDT, MEMS accelerometer |
| 2 | **Signal Conditioning** | Amplification, filtering, linearization | Op-amp, anti-aliasing filter |
| 3 | **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** | Sampling + quantization | 12-bit/16-bit/24-bit ADC ICs |
| 4 | **Microcontroller / DSP** | Pre-processing, time-stamping, encoding | ARM Cortex, TI C2000 |
| 5 | **Network Interface Controller (NIC)** | Packetization, MAC, PHY | Ethernet, CAN, Wireless transceiver |
| 6 | **Receiver Buffer & Synchronizer** | Reordering, jitter compensation | OS-level network stack, PTP clock |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants and Standards You MUST Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, where $f_{max}$ is the highest frequency component of the signal.
> - **Standard ADC Resolutions in Industrial NCS:** 8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit.
> - **Standard Communication Baud Rates:** CAN — 125 kbps, 250 kbps, 500 kbps, 1 Mbps; Ethernet — 10/100/1000 Mbps.
> - **Quantization Step (LSB):** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$, where $V_{FS}$ is full-scale voltage and $n$ is the bit-depth.
> - **Time-Stamp Drift Bound (IEEE 1588 PTP):** sub-microsecond to nanosecond accuracy.
> - **Standard Reference:** Shannon's original 1948 paper *"A Mathematical Theory of Communication"* (Bell System Technical Journal).

## 1.5 Why Information Acquisition is the *Cornerstone* of NCS

> [!NOTE]
> **Garbage-In, Garbage-Out (GIGO) Principle:**
> No controller, no matter how sophisticated (MPC, $H_\infty$, sliding-mode, fuzzy), can compensate for **stale, lossy, or coarsely quantized measurements**. The **quality of feedback control is fundamentally upper-bounded by the quality of information acquisition.** This is why Module 1 of PECPT862 begins here — it is the *root* of every subsequent design decision.

In Module 1's KTU valuation scheme, examiners frequently test whether a student understands that:
1. **Sampling period $T_s$** directly determines the achievable closed-loop bandwidth.
2. **Quantization resolution $n$ bits** directly limits the steady-state accuracy.
3. **Network-induced delay $\tau$** from acquisition to controller degrades the phase margin.
4. **Packet dropout probability $p$** reduces the effective disturbance-rejection capability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Information Acquisition

The discipline is anchored on three orthogonal technical pillars. KTU examiners routinely test these as the structural framework of any 14-mark question.

### Pillar I — Sampling (Time Discretization)

- **Definition:** The conversion of a continuous-time signal $x_c(t)$ into a sequence of instantaneous values $x[k] = x_c(kT_s)$.
- **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ (Hz) can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s > 2 f_{max}$.
- **Aliasing:** If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, high-frequency content "folds back" into the low-frequency band, producing a false lower-frequency signal. Prevention requires an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cutoff at $f_c = f_s / 2$.

### Pillar II — Quantization (Amplitude Discretization)

- **Definition:** The mapping of each continuous-amplitude sample into one of $2^n$ discrete levels, where $n$ is the ADC bit-depth.
- **Quantization Step (LSB):** The smallest distinguishable voltage increment.
- **Quantization Error:** The difference between the true analog value and its quantized representation. For a uniform mid-tread quantizer, this error is bounded and treated as additive white noise for analysis.

### Pillar III — Networked Transmission (Spatial-Temporal Distribution)

- **Definition:** The conveyance of the digitized, time-stamped samples to a remote estimator/controller over a shared packet-switched network.
- **Network-Induced Imperfections:** Delay ($\tau$), jitter ($\sigma_\tau$), packet dropout ($p$), out-of-order delivery, and bandwidth limitation.
- **Medium Access Control (MAC) Protocols:** Deterministic (TDMA, Token Ring) vs. stochastic (CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every formula here is a potential 1–3 mark question in the KTU board exam.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbols & Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|---------|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ in Hz, $f_{max}$ in Hz | Strict inequality for reconstruction |
| 2 | Sampling Period | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | $T_s$ in seconds | Inverse relationship |
| 3 | Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$ | $V_{FS}$ in Volts, $n$ in bits | $V_{FS} = V_{ref}^+ - V_{ref}^-$ |
| 4 | Maximum Quantization Error | $e_q^{max} = \pm \Delta/2$ | Volts | Mid-tread quantizer |
| 5 | RMS Quantization Noise | $\sigma_q = \Delta/\sqrt{12} = \Delta / (2\sqrt{3})$ | Volts | Assumes uniform distribution |
| 6 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | Each extra bit adds ~6 dB |
| 7 | Resolution (percent) | $\text{Res}\% = 100/(2^n - 1)$ | % | Useful for instrumentation |
| 8 | Effective Number of Bits (ENOB) | $\text{ENOB} = (\text{SINAD} - 1.76)/6.02$ | bits | Real-world ADC metric |
| 9 | Network Delay (sensor-to-controller) | $\tau_{sc} = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx}$ | seconds | Sum of sub-delays |
| 10 | Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bits/second | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = SNR |
| 11 | Effective Data Rate (sampled) | $R = n \cdot f_s \cdot N_{ch}$ | bits/second | $N_{ch}$ = number of channels |
| 12 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N = f_s/2$ | Hz | Anti-alias filter cutoff |
| 13 | Multi-rate Sampling (Lifting) | $T_{slow} = N \cdot T_{fast}$ | seconds | Integer ratio $N$ |
| 14 | Kalman Filter Innovation | $\tilde{y}[k] = y[k] - C\hat{x}[k\vert k-1]$ | vector | Drives state update |
| 15 | Covariance Update (Kalman) | $P[k\vert k] = (I - KC)P[k\vert k-1]$ | matrix | Posteriori error covariance |
| 16 | Event-Triggered Threshold | $\vert x[k] - x[k-1] \vert > \sigma$ | same units as $x$ | $\sigma$ = trigger threshold |
| 17 | Packet Dropout Compensation (ZOH) | $\hat{x}[k] = A\hat{x}[k-1]$ if packet lost | state vector | Zero-Order Hold at receiver |
| 18 | Time-Stamp Age of Information | $\Delta_{AoI}[k] = k - u(k)$ | time steps | $u(k)$ = generation time of last received sample |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Industry Cares About Every Formula Above:**
> 1. **Smart Grids:** SCADA systems acquire voltage, current, and phasor measurements from thousands of PMUs (Phasor Measurement Units) at 30–120 samples/second. SQNR dictates the precision of state estimation.
> 2. **Automotive CAN Bus:** Engine ECU samples crankshaft position, MAP, MAF at 1 kHz, quantizes to 12 bits, and broadcasts over CAN at 500 kbps. Bandwidth constraints force careful channel allocation.
> 3. **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** WirelessHART sensor networks use TDMA + channel hopping to deliver 2.4 GHz ISM-band data deterministically; time-synchronization (IEEE 1588) is critical.
> 4. **Aerospace Flight Control:** Fly-by-wire systems use **triplex-redundant** sensors with 14-bit ADCs and cross-channel validation to detect single-point failures.
> 5. **Process Control (Oil & Gas):** Remote wellhead RTUs use 24-bit ADCs and cellular LTE-M to transmit slowly-varying pressures and temperatures, where SQNR matters more than $f_s$.

## 2.4 Architectural Layers of Information Acquisition

The NCS acquisition layer can be visualized as a **three-tier hierarchy**:

- **Tier 1 — Field Level:** Sensors, actuators, local I/O, signal conditioning.
- **Tier 2 — Control Level:** PLCs, RTUs, local controllers, HMI stations.
- **Tier 3 — Supervisory Level:** SCADA servers, historians, cloud analytics, MPC optimizers.

Information flows **upward** (telemetry) and **downward** (commands), and at each tier crossing it must be **re-acquired, re-timestamped, and re-validated**.

## 2.5 Sampling Strategies — A Comparative Study

| Strategy | Trigger Condition | Network Load | Use Case |
|----------|-------------------|--------------|----------|
| **Periodic (Time-Triggered)** | Every $T_s$ seconds | Constant & predictable | Deterministic control loops |
| **Event-Triggered** | $\vert x[k] - x[k_{last}]\vert > \sigma$ | Adaptive, often lower | Battery-powered wireless sensors |
| **Send-on-Delta** | $\vert x - x_{last\_sent}\vert > \delta$ | Reduces redundant data | Slow-varying process variables |
| **Stochastic Sampling** | Random Poisson process | Probabilistic load | Security / covert NCS |
| **Self-Triggered** | Controller pre-computes next sampling instant | Energy-efficient | Embedded NCS |
| **Multi-Rate** | Different $T_s$ per channel | Mismatched but bounded | Heterogeneous sensor fusion |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Style:** All derivations below are written in the same structured, multi-line, step-explained style expected of a 7–8 mark sub-question in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Step Size from Full-Scale Voltage

**Problem Setup:** A 12-bit ADC is used to digitize a signal with full-scale range $V_{FS} = 10\text{ V}$. Determine (a) the quantization step (LSB), (b) the maximum quantization error, (c) the RMS quantization noise, and (d) the SQNR in dB.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation from the formula sheet.**

The LSB (Least Significant Bit) voltage is given by:

$$
\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

Here, $V_{FS} = 10\text{ V}$ and $n = 12$ bits. Compute the denominator first:

$$
2^{12} - 1 = 4096 - 1 = 4095
$$

Now substitute into the LSB equation:

$$
\Delta = \frac{10\text{ V}}{4095} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V} = 2.442\text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum quantization error.**

For a mid-tread quantizer, the error is bounded by half the LSB:

$$
e_q^{max} = \pm \frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm \frac{2.442 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \pm 1.221 \times 10^{-3}\text{ V} = \pm 1.221\text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the RMS quantization noise voltage.**

Assuming the quantization error is uniformly distributed over $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$, the variance is:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e^2\, de = \frac{1}{\Delta} \left[\frac{e^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}
$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$
\sigma_q^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 (\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta^3 / 8}{3} = \frac{1}{\Delta} \cdot \frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_q = \frac{\Delta}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{\Delta}{2\sqrt{3}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the value of $\Delta$.**

$$
\sigma_q = \frac{2.442 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \cdot 1.7321} = \frac{2.442 \times 10^{-3}}{3.4641} = 7.051 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V} = 0.7051\text{ mV}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the SQNR in dB.**

The standard formula for an ideal N-bit ADC, assuming a full-scale sinusoidal input, is:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76
$$

Substitute $n = 12$:

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\text{ dB}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the result.**

A 12-bit ADC provides approximately 74 dB of signal-to-quantization-noise ratio. This means the noise floor is about $10^{-74/20} = 1/5012 \approx 0.02\%$ of the full-scale signal — excellent for industrial control applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nyquist Rate and Anti-Aliasing Filter Design

**Problem Setup:** A temperature sensor in a chemical reactor produces a signal whose significant spectral content lies between DC and 5 Hz. However, the sensor output also contains 50 Hz mains-frequency noise. The ADC must sample this signal. Determine (a) the minimum sampling frequency, (b) the Nyquist frequency, and (c) the cutoff frequency of the anti-aliasing filter.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum signal frequency.**

The *information-bearing* signal extends up to $f_{sig} = 5\text{ Hz}$. The 50 Hz noise is unwanted interference.

**Step 2 — Apply the Nyquist criterion to the *desired* signal.**

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{sig} = 2 \times 5 = 10\text{ Hz}
$$

So the minimum sampling rate is 10 Hz. In practice, engineers oversample by a factor of 5–10× to allow for filter roll-off and to improve SQNR, so a practical choice would be $f_s = 50\text{ Hz}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Nyquist frequency for the chosen $f_s$.**

$$
f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the anti-aliasing filter cutoff.**

The anti-aliasing filter must pass all signal content below $f_{sig} = 5$ Hz with negligible attenuation, while sufficiently attenuating the 50 Hz noise *before* it can alias back into the 0–25 Hz Nyquist band. The filter cutoff $f_c$ is typically placed just above the signal bandwidth:

$$
f_c \approx 1.5 \times f_{sig} = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\text{ Hz}
$$

A 2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter with $f_c = 7.5$ Hz would provide approximately 40 dB/decade roll-off, attenuating the 50 Hz component by:

$$
\text{Atten} \approx 40 \log_{10}\left(\frac{50}{7.5}\right) = 40 \log_{10}(6.667) = 40 \times 0.824 = 32.95\text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify that aliasing is suppressed.**

At the Nyquist frequency $f_N = 25$ Hz, the 50 Hz noise is now "beyond" the Nyquist band. Even if a small residual leaks through, it would alias to $f_{alias} = \vert f_{noise} - f_s \vert = \vert 50 - 50 \vert = 0$ Hz (DC), but with the 33 dB attenuation applied, its amplitude is negligible compared to the signal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Rate Sampling and the Lifting Operator

**Problem Setup:** A networked control system has two sensors: a fast position encoder sampled at $T_f = 1\text{ ms}$ and a slow temperature sensor sampled at $T_s = 10\text{ ms}$. Derive the multi-rate lifted representation that aligns both streams to a common period.

**Step 1 — Identify the integer ratio $N$ between the two periods.**

$$
N = \frac{T_s}{T_f} = \frac{10\text{ ms}}{1\text{ ms}} = 10
$$

So the slow sensor updates once for every 10 updates of the fast sensor.

**Step 2 — Define the lifted fast-rate measurement vector.**

The fast-rate measurements collected over one slow period form a stacked vector:

$$
\underline{y}_f[k] = \begin{bmatrix} y_f[(kN - N + 1)] \\ y_f[(kN - N + 2)] \\ \vdots \\ y_f[kN] \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the slow-rate measurement scalar (repeated to align).**

The slow measurement is held constant over the lifted interval by a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):

$$
\underline{y}_s[k] = \begin{bmatrix} y_s[k-1] \\ y_s[k-1] \\ \vdots \\ y_s[k-1] \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the combined lifted measurement vector.**

$$
\underline{Y}[k] = \begin{bmatrix} \underline{y}_f[k] \\ \underline{y}_s[k] \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2N \times 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the lifted system matrices.**

If the continuous-time plant is $\dot{x} = A_c x + B_c u$, $y_f = C_f x$, $y_s = C_s x$, then the lifted state transition from the beginning of one slow period to the next is:

$$
\Phi = e^{A_c T_s} = \left(e^{A_c T_f}\right)^{N} = A_f^N
$$

where $A_f = e^{A_c T_f}$ is the fast-rate discrete transition matrix.

**Step 6 — Form the lifted discrete-time state-space model.**

$$
x[k+1] = A_f^N x[k] + \Gamma\, U[k]
$$

$$
\underline{Y}[k] = \underline{C}\, x[k] + \underline{D}\, U[k]
$$

where $\Gamma$, $\underline{C}$, and $\underline{D}$ are constructed from the block matrices of the fast-rate model. This lifted model now has period $T_s = 10$ ms and can be analyzed with standard single-rate tools.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Discrete-Time Kalman Filter for Noisy Measurements

**Problem Setup:** A linear plant is modeled as $x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k] + w[k]$, with measurement $y[k] = C x[k] + v[k]$, where $w \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$ and $v \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$. Derive the discrete-time Kalman filter update equations.

**Step 1 — Write the a-priori state estimate (prediction step).**

$$
\hat{x}[k \vert k-1] = A \hat{x}[k-1 \vert k-1] + B u[k-1]
$$

**Step 2 — Write the a-priori error covariance (prediction step).**

$$
P[k \vert k-1] = A\, P[k-1 \vert k-1]\, A^T + Q
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Kalman gain.**

The Kalman gain minimizes the a-posteriori covariance $P[k\vert k]$:

$$
K[k] = P[k \vert k-1] C^T \left(C\, P[k \vert k-1]\, C^T + R\right)^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Measurement update of the state estimate.**

The innovation (residual) $\tilde{y}[k] = y[k] - C\hat{x}[k\vert k-1]$ is the new information brought by the measurement:

$$
\hat{x}[k \vert k] = \hat{x}[k \vert k-1] + K[k]\, \tilde{y}[k]
$$

**Step 5 — Measurement update of the error covariance.**

$$
P[k \vert k] = (I - K[k]\, C)\, P[k \vert k-1]
$$

**Step 6 — Iterative loop.**

The recursion repeats from Step 1 for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ Under observability and detectability conditions, the filter converges and the steady-state covariance $P_\infty$ satisfies the **Discrete Algebraic Riccati Equation (DARE):**

$$
P_\infty = A P_\infty A^T + Q - A P_\infty C^T (C P_\infty C^T + R)^{-1} C P_\infty A^T
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

The Kalman gain $K[k]$ is the *optimal* blending factor between the model-based prediction and the noisy measurement. When the measurement noise $R$ is large, $K \to 0$ and the filter trusts the model. When the process noise $Q$ is large, $K$ grows and the filter trusts the measurements more.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Age of Information (AoI) for a Periodic Sampler

**Problem Setup:** A sensor generates a status update at intervals of $T_s = 100$ ms, and the update is delivered to the estimator with a constant delay $\tau = 20$ ms. Derive the time-averaged Age of Information (AoI).

**Step 1 — Define the AoI function.**

AoI $\Delta(t)$ is the elapsed time since the *last successfully received* update was generated. For periodic generation with constant delay, the AoI evolves as a sawtooth waveform.

**Step 2 — Write the AoI evolution between updates.**

Between time $t_k$ (reception of $k$-th update) and $t_{k+1} = t_k + T_s + \tau$ (reception of $(k+1)$-th update), the AoI grows linearly:

$$
\Delta(t) = (t - t_k) + T_s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the area under one sawtooth period.**

The area under the triangle from $t = t_k$ to $t = t_{k+1}$ is:

$$
A_{tooth} = \frac{1}{2} \times (T_s + \tau) \times (T_s + \tau) = \frac{(T_s + \tau)^2}{2}
$$

Wait — the peak AoI just before the new update arrives is $T_s + \tau$ (it has been $T_s + \tau$ since the *previous* update was generated). The AoI drops to $\tau$ immediately after the new update is received. So the area under one sawtooth is a trapezoid:

$$
A_{tooth} = \int_{0}^{T_s} (s + \tau)\, ds = \left[\frac{s^2}{2} + \tau s\right]_0^{T_s} = \frac{T_s^2}{2} + \tau T_s
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the time-averaged AoI.**

$$
\overline{\Delta} = \frac{A_{tooth}}{T_s} = \frac{\frac{T_s^2}{2} + \tau T_s}{T_s} = \frac{T_s}{2} + \tau
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\overline{\Delta} = \frac{0.100}{2} + 0.020 = 0.050 + 0.020 = 0.070\text{ s} = 70\text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Optimize the sampling period to minimize AoI.**

If the generation cost per update is $C_g$ and we wish to minimize $\overline{\Delta}$ subject to an average rate constraint, the optimal $T_s$ emerges. For a single-hop M/M/1 queue with service rate $\mu$, the optimal generation rate $\lambda^*$ is:

$$
\lambda^* = \frac{\mu}{1 + \sqrt{1 + \mu^2 C_g^2}} + \text{correction terms}
$$

This result (Kaul et al., 2012) shows that **minimizing AoI requires a non-zero inter-arrival time** — generating updates too frequently *increases* AoI due to queueing.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Event-Triggered Sampling Threshold

**Problem Setup:** A first-order plant $x[k+1] = a x[k] + b u[k]$ is monitored by a sensor that transmits only when $\vert x[k] - x[k_{last}]\vert > \sigma$. Find the maximum inter-event time $T_{max}$ such that stability is preserved.

**Step 1 — Closed-loop dynamics with ZOH between events.**

Between transmission events, the controller applies the last received state. The closed-loop becomes:

$$
x[k+1] = (a + b K) x[k] + b K (x[k_{last}] - x[k])
$$

Define the "drift" $e[k] = x[k] - x[k_{last}]$.

**Step 2 — Lyapunov function candidate.**

$$
V[k] = x[k]^T P x[k], \quad P > 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta V$.**

$$
\Delta V = x[k+1]^T P x[k+1] - x[k]^T P x[k] \leq -\alpha V[k]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the event-trigger condition.**

The trigger fires when $\vert e[k] \vert > \sigma \vert x[k] \vert$. The maximum inter-event time is bounded by the time it takes for the drift to grow from 0 to $\sigma \vert x \vert$:

$$
T_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{\log(\sigma)}{\log(\vert a + bK \vert)} \right\rfloor
$$

for $0 < \vert a + bK \vert < 1$.

**Step 5 — Compute average transmission rate.**

The expected inter-event time is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{event}] = \frac{1}{1 - (1 - \sigma)^{T_{max}}}
$$

giving an average transmission rate reduction of typically 60–90% compared to periodic sampling for slowly-varying signals.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — End-to-End Information Acquisition Pipeline

The following Mermaid block renders a **block-level functional flow** of an entire networked information-acquisition system, from the physical plant to the remote estimator. This is the architecture most frequently drawn in KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Plant] --> B[Sensor Cluster]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning Amplify and Filter]
    C --> D[Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Microcontroller Time Stamp and Encode]
    F --> G[Network Interface Controller Packetize]
    G --> H[Shared Digital Network Switched Ethernet or CAN or Wireless]
    H --> I[Receiver Buffer Reorder and Dejitter]
    I --> J[Estimator or Controller Kalman Filter MPC or PID]
    J --> K[Actuator Command Generator]
    K --> L[Physical Plant Feedback Loop Closed]

    classDef hw fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000;
    classDef sw fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000;
    classDef net fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000;

    class A,B,C,D,L hw;
    class E,F,I,J,K sw;
    class G,H net;
```

**Reading Guide for KTU Answer Scripts:**
- The blocks above the **Shared Digital Network** belong to the **sensor/field side**.
- The blocks below the network belong to the **controller/estimator side**.
- The **delay** $\tau$ introduced by the network is the single most important parameter for stability analysis in later modules.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Three-Tier Hierarchical NCS Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1[Tier 1 Field Level]
        A1[Temperature Sensor]
        A2[Pressure Sensor]
        A3[Flow Sensor]
        A4[Smart Sensor MCU]
    end

    subgraph T2[Tier 2 Control Level]
        B1[PLC 1]
        B2[PLC 2]
        B3[RTU]
        B4[Local HMI]
    end

    subgraph T3[Tier 3 Supervisory Level]
        C1[SCADA Server]
        C2[Database Historian]
        C3[Cloud Analytics]
        C4[MPC Optimizer]
    end

    A1 --> A4
    A2 --> A4
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B1
    A4 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> B4
    B2 --> B4
    B3 --> C1
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C4 --> B1
    C4 --> B2

    classDef t1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000;
    classDef t2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000;
    classDef t3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000;

    class A1,A2,A3,A4 t1;
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 t2;
    class C1,C2,C3,C4 t3;
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Data Flow Timing in a Networked Sampling Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Smart Sensor
    participant N as Network
    participant C as Controller
    participant A as Actuator
    participant P as Plant

    Note over S,P: Sampling instant t = kT_s
    S->>S: Sample x_c kT_s
    S->>S: Quantize to n bits
    S->>S: Time-stamp and packetize
    S->>N: Transmit packet at t = kT_s + t_proc
    N->>C: Deliver packet at t = kT_s + t_proc + t_queue + t_tx
    C->>C: Compute u k = K x_hat k
    C->>A: Send command at t = kT_s + tau_sc + tau_ca
    A->>P: Apply u k to plant
    P-->>S: New measurement becomes available
```

**Notation used in the diagram (for KTU answer scripts):**
- $t_{proc}$: sensor-side processing delay
- $t_{queue}$: queuing delay in network switches/routers
- $t_{tx}$: physical-layer transmission delay
- $\tau_{sc}$: sensor-to-controller delay
- $\tau_{ca}$: controller-to-actuator delay
- Total loop delay: $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — State Diagram of a Networked Sensor Node

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sampling: Trigger fires periodic or event based
    Sampling --> Quantizing: ADC conversion complete
    Quantizing --> Encoding: Digital value ready
    Encoding --> Transmitting: Network channel free CSMA check
    Transmitting --> WaitingAck: Packet sent
    WaitingAck --> Idle: ACK received or timeout
    WaitingAck --> Retransmit: NACK or timeout
    Retransmit --> Transmitting: Retransmit counter less than max
    Retransmit --> DropPacket: Retransmit counter equals max
    DropPacket --> Idle: Log failure increment dropout counter
```

This state diagram is the **exact flow** implemented inside any IEEE 802.15.4 (WirelessHART, Zigbee) or CAN-based sensor node, and is a **very high-yield diagram** for KTU exam preparation.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Block Diagram of a Kalman Filter Inside the Estimator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State x at k] --> B[Plant Model A B]
    B --> C[Predicted State x_hat k given k minus 1]
    D[Control Input u k minus 1] --> B
    C --> E[Predicted Output C x_hat k given k minus 1]
    F[Measurement y k] --> G[Innovation Residual]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Kalman Gain K k]
    H --> I[Updated State x_hat k given k]
    C --> I
    I --> J[State Estimate Output to Controller]
    I --> K[Covariance Update P k given k]
    K --> L[Predict Covariance P k plus 1 given k]
    L --> M[Compute K k plus 1]
    M --> H

    classDef proc fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000;
    classDef sig fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000;

    class A,D,F,J sig;
    class B,C,E,G,H,I,K,L,M proc;
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Define "Information Acquisition" in a Networked Control System. List its three fundamental stages and explain the role of an anti-aliasing filter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Information acquisition in an NCS is the process of sensing physical process variables, converting them into digital form, and transmitting them over a shared communication network to estimators and controllers with the necessary timeliness and integrity.

**Three Fundamental Stages (1 mark):**
1. **Sampling** — discretizing the continuous-time signal in time.
2. **Quantization** — discretizing the continuous-amplitude signal in level.
3. **Networked Transmission** — packetization, addressing, and reliable delivery over the shared medium.

**Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter (1 mark):** An anti-aliasing low-pass filter placed before the ADC removes frequency components above the Nyquist frequency $f_N = f_s/2$. This prevents high-frequency noise from "folding back" into the lower frequency band during sampling, which would otherwise corrupt the digitized signal and degrade controller performance.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`
**Write the formula for Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) of an ideal $n$-bit ADC. If a 10-bit ADC replaces a 12-bit ADC in an NCS sensor, calculate the loss in SQNR in dB.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76
$$

**Calculation for 12-bit ADC (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{12} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00\text{ dB}
$$

**Calculation for 10-bit ADC and Difference (1 mark):**

$$
\text{SQNR}_{10} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76 = 60.20 + 1.76 = 61.96\text{ dB}
$$

$$
\Delta\text{SQNR} = 74.00 - 61.96 = 12.04\text{ dB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Reducing the bit-depth from 12 to 10 bits causes a loss of approximately **12 dB** in SQNR, equivalent to losing 2 effective bits of resolution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units.** Always write Hz, s, V, dB, or bits next to every numerical answer.
> 2. **Confusing $f_s$ and $f_N$.** $f_s$ is the sampling rate; $f_N = f_s/2$ is the Nyquist frequency. Examiners award ½ mark for using the wrong one.
> 3. **Skipping the anti-aliasing filter discussion.** Many students compute the Nyquist rate but forget to mention how aliasing is physically prevented. Loss of 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing vague block diagrams.** Use **labeled, rectangular blocks with arrows** in the direction of signal flow. Mermaid-style or hand-drawn both acceptable, but arrows must be present.
> 5. **Writing "Kalman filter reduces noise" without derivation.** The examiner expects the **innovation update equation** explicitly written.
> 6. **Failing to state assumptions.** Always state "assuming uniform quantization", "assuming signal is bandlimited to $f_{max}$", etc.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**A networked temperature monitoring system for a pharmaceutical reactor uses the following components:**
- A type-K thermocouple with sensitivity $S = 41\,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$.
- A signal conditioning amplifier with gain $G = 250$.
- A 14-bit ADC with full-scale range $V_{FS} = 5\text{ V}$.
- A CAN bus transmitting at 500 kbps.
- The reactor temperature varies slowly, with dominant dynamics below 0.5 Hz, but the sensor output also picks up 50 Hz mains pickup and broadband EMI.

**Answer the following:**

**(a) Compute the quantization step (LSB) of the ADC in both volts and in equivalent temperature units ($^\circ$C). Comment on whether this resolution is adequate for pharmaceutical process control. [7 marks]**

**(b) Design a complete information-acquisition scheme for this system, including (i) the anti-aliasing filter specification, (ii) the sampling rate selection with justification, and (iii) the required CAN bus utilization for 4 such channels. [7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Quantization Resolution [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the LSB voltage (1 mark).**

For a 14-bit ADC with full-scale $V_{FS} = 5$ V:

$$
\Delta_V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{14} - 1} = \frac{5}{16383} = 3.052 \times 10^{-4}\text{ V} = 0.3052\text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert LSB to equivalent temperature (2 marks).**

The full signal chain gain from temperature to ADC input is:

$$
G_{chain} = S \times G = (41 \times 10^{-6}) \times 250 = 1.025 \times 10^{-2}\text{ V}/^\circ\text{C} = 10.25\text{ mV}/^\circ\text{C}
$$

Therefore, the temperature equivalent of one LSB is:

$$
\Delta_T = \frac{\Delta_V}{G_{chain}} = \frac{0.3052 \times 10^{-3}}{1.025 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.02977\,^\circ\text{C} \approx 0.030\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum quantization error in temperature (1 mark).**

$$
e_{T}^{max} = \pm \frac{\Delta_T}{2} = \pm 0.0149\,^\circ\text{C} \approx \pm 0.015\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — SQNR calculation (1 mark).**

$$
\text{SQNR} = 6.02 \times 14 + 1.76 = 84.28 + 1.76 = 86.04\text{ dB}
$$

**Step 5 — Adequacy discussion (2 marks).**

Pharmaceutical reactors typically require temperature control accuracy of $\pm 0.1\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $\pm 0.5\,^\circ\text{C}$, depending on the product. Our quantization error of $\pm 0.015\,^\circ\text{C}$ is **about 7 to 33 times finer** than the typical control tolerance. Therefore, the 14-bit resolution is **more than adequate**. In fact, even a 12-bit ADC would suffice ($\Delta_T \approx 0.12\,^\circ\text{C}$). The extra bits are useful for headroom against EMI and noise.

#### Part (b) — Complete Acquisition Scheme Design [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Anti-Aliasing Filter Specification (2 marks).**

The desired signal band is 0 to 0.5 Hz. The interference components are 50 Hz mains and broadband EMI. We must choose a sampling rate first, then place the filter.

A practical oversampling factor of 20× gives $f_s = 10$ Hz (1 mark for the choice and justification). The Nyquist frequency is $f_N = 5$ Hz.

A **2nd-order Butterworth low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = 1$ Hz (twice the signal bandwidth) provides:
- Negligible attenuation (< 0.1 dB) at 0.5 Hz.
- Attenuation at 50 Hz: $40 \log_{10}(50/1) = 40 \times 1.699 = 67.96$ dB (1 mark for calculation).

This is more than sufficient to suppress 50 Hz pickup and prevent aliasing.

**Step 2 — Sampling Rate Selection (2 marks).**

Choosing $f_s = 10$ Hz satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$ Hz by a factor of 10. The 10× oversampling provides:
- Margin for anti-aliasing filter roll-off.
- Improved SQNR by $10\log_{10}(10) = 10$ dB due to oversampling noise shaping.
- Headroom for unforeseen high-frequency dynamics during reactor transients.

**Step 3 — CAN Bus Utilization (3 marks).**

Each CAN frame in standard format (11-bit ID) carries up to 8 bytes = 64 bits of payload. For 1 temperature channel at $f_s = 10$ Hz with 14-bit samples, we need:

- 2 bytes per sample (16 bits, with 2 padding bits) = 16 bits/sample.
- Data rate per channel: $16 \times 10 = 160$ bps.
- For 4 channels: $4 \times 160 = 640$ bps.
- With 50% protocol overhead (framing, arbitration, inter-frame spacing), actual line rate required: $\approx 1280$ bps.
- CAN bus utilization at 500 kbps: $1280 / 500000 = 0.256\%$.

**Conclusion:** The CAN bus is **grossly under-utilized** (< 1%), leaving ample bandwidth for hundreds of additional channels, actuator commands, and diagnostic messages. The system is well-designed.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]`

**A wireless sensor network (WSN) deployed across a large agricultural field uses 50 sensor nodes to monitor soil moisture and ambient temperature. Each node samples at 1 Hz with a 12-bit ADC and transmits over IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee) at 250 kbps.**

**(a) Explain the various network-induced imperfections that degrade information acquisition in this WSN. Derive an expression for the total sensor-to-sink delay $\tau_{sc}$ and discuss its effect on the freshness of acquired data. [7 marks]**

**(b) Propose and justify an **event-triggered acquisition strategy** for this WSN, showing mathematically how the average transmission rate can be reduced by 80% compared to periodic sampling. Discuss the trade-off between communication savings and estimation error. [7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Network-Induced Imperfections and Delay Analysis [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify the imperfections (3 marks).**

1. **Communication delay $\tau_{sc}$:** Time from sensor sampling instant to sink reception. Caused by processing, queuing, transmission, and propagation.
2. **Jitter $\sigma_\tau$:** Random variation in delay between successive packets. Caused by CSMA/CA backoff and varying queuing.
3. **Packet loss probability $p$:** Fraction of packets dropped due to collision, channel fading, or buffer overflow. Typical WSN values: 1–10%.
4. **Out-of-order delivery:** Packets may arrive at the sink in a different order than transmitted, requiring re-sequencing buffers.
5. **Bandwidth limitation:** The 250 kbps raw rate of IEEE 802.15.4 is shared across all 50 nodes, so per-node throughput is limited.
6. **Time synchronization drift:** Without proper synchronization (e.g., IEEE 1588 or FTSP), time-stamps drift apart, corrupting fusion.

**Step 2 — Derive the total sensor-to-sink delay (2 marks).**

$$
\tau_{sc} = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop}
$$

where:
- $\tau_{proc}$ = microcontroller processing time (typically 1–10 ms)
- $\tau_{queue}$ = MAC-layer queuing delay (depends on channel contention; can be 0–500 ms)
- $\tau_{tx}$ = physical-layer transmission time = $\frac{L_{packet}}{R_{bit}} = \frac{127 \times 8}{250 \times 10^3} = 4.06$ ms (for a max-size 127-byte IEEE 802.15.4 frame)
- $\tau_{prop}$ = propagation delay over the field (< 1 µs for ranges up to 300 m, negligible)

**Step 3 — Effect on data freshness (2 marks).**

The **Age of Information (AoI)** for a periodic sampler with constant delay is:

$$
\overline{\Delta} = \frac{T_s}{2} + \tau_{sc} = 0.5 + \tau_{sc}\text{ seconds}
$$

For typical WSN values $\tau_{sc} \approx 50$ ms, the AoI is 0.55 s. For a slow-varying agricultural variable (moisture changes over minutes), this is acceptable. For faster dynamics, it would not be.

#### Part (b) — Event-Triggered Strategy with 80% Reduction [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Define the trigger rule (1 mark).**

Each node transmits only when:

$$
\vert x[k] - x[k_{last\_sent}] \vert > \sigma
$$

where $\sigma$ is the trigger threshold.

**Step 2 — Model the soil-moisture signal (1 mark).**

Soil moisture is a **random walk** with small incremental changes. The probability that the change exceeds $\sigma$ in one step is:

$$
P(\text{trigger at step } k) = p_\sigma = P(\vert \Delta x \vert > \sigma) \approx 2\, Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_{\Delta x}}\right)
$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Q-function and $\sigma_{\Delta x}$ is the standard deviation of step-to-step change.

**Step 3 — Compute the average transmission rate (2 marks).**

The average inter-event time is $1/p_\sigma$. The average transmission rate is:

$$
\overline{f}_{tx} = f_s \cdot p_\sigma
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 80% reduction (2 marks).**

For an 80% reduction, we need $p_\sigma = 0.2$. So:

$$
Q\!\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sigma_{\Delta x}}\right) = 0.1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{\sigma}{\sigma_{\Delta x}} = Q^{-1}(0.1) \approx 1.2816
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma \approx 1.28\, \sigma_{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 5 — Trade-off with estimation error (1 mark).**

The maximum error between the true signal and the last-sent value, just before a new transmission, is $\sigma$. So the **maximum estimation error** equals the trigger threshold. Reducing $p_\sigma$ (more savings) requires larger $\sigma$ (more error). The designer must balance communication energy savings against the application's tolerance for error.

**Conclusion:** By setting $\sigma = 1.28 \sigma_{\Delta x}$, the WSN achieves an 80% reduction in transmissions while bounding the estimation error to at most $1.28 \sigma_{\Delta x}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Checklist — Master These 25 Points:**

1. **Information Acquisition** = Sampling + Quantization + Networked Transmission.
2. **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — strict inequality for reconstruction.
3. **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s/2$ — anti-aliasing filter cutoff should be placed near here.
4. **Quantization Step (LSB):** $\Delta = V_{FS}/(2^n - 1)$.
5. **Max Quantization Error:** $\pm \Delta/2$ for a mid-tread quantizer.
6. **RMS Quantization Noise:** $\Delta / (2\sqrt{3}) = \Delta/\sqrt{12}$.
7. **SQNR Formula:** $6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB — each extra bit gives ~6 dB improvement.
8. **Aliasing** is prevented by an **anti-aliasing filter** placed *before* the ADC, not after.
9. **Oversampling** by factor $K$ improves effective SQNR by $10\log_{10}(K)$ dB.
10. **Network-induced delay** $\tau = \tau_{proc} + \tau_{queue} + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop}$.
11. **Time-stamping** at the sensor is essential for accurate state estimation.
12. **Three MAC paradigms:** TDMA (deterministic), CSMA (stochastic), Token Ring (deterministic).
13. **Event-triggered sampling** reduces transmissions by transmitting only on significant change.
14. **Multi-rate systems** use the **lifting operator** to align fast and slow streams.
15. **Kalman filter** is the optimal linear estimator for Gaussian noise systems.
16. **Innovation residual** $\tilde{y}[k] = y[k] - C\hat{x}[k \vert k-1]$ drives the Kalman update.
17. **Age of Information (AoI)** for periodic sampling: $\overline{\Delta} = T_s/2 + \tau$.
18. **Packet dropout** is mitigated by **ZOH** or **prediction** at the receiver.
19. **GIGO Principle:** No controller can fix bad measurements.
20. **Standard industrial bit-depths:** 12, 14, 16, 24 bits.
21. **Standard CAN baud rates:** 125 k, 250 k, 500 k, 1 M bps.
22. **Shannon–Hartley capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps.
23. **Trade-off Triangle:** Sample rate ↑ → SQNR ↑ AND bandwidth ↑ AND delay ↓ — but hardware cost ↑.
24. **Time-triggered vs event-triggered:** Time-triggered is predictable; event-triggered is efficient.
25. **Three-Tier Hierarchy:** Field (sensors) → Control (PLCs) → Supervisory (SCADA/Cloud).

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 — Information Acquisition in a Network Notes.**
> You are now equipped to attempt any 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark question KTU may set on this topic. Cross-link this material with **Module 2 (State Estimation)** and **Module 3 (Networked Control Architecture)** for full-syllabus mastery.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Actuators over a Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control of Actuators over a Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control of Actuators over a Network** refers to the closed-loop control paradigm in which the controller and the physical actuator (and/or plant) are not collocated, but are interconnected through a **shared digital communication network** (e.g., Ethernet, CAN, Wi-Fi, 5G, or industrial fieldbus). Reference commands and feedback signals are transmitted as **data packets** subject to **network-induced delay (τ)**, **packet dropout (ρ)**, and **jitter**.

In formal terms, an *actuator* is the final control element (motor, valve, servo, hydraulic piston, etc.) that converts a control signal $u(t)$ into physical energy acting on the plant. When this actuator is driven across a packet-switched or shared bus, the control loop becomes a **Networked Control System (NCS)** of the form:

$$
\boxed{\,\dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t-\tau), \quad u(t) = K\,\hat{x}(t-\tau_{ca})\,}
$$

where $\hat{x}$ is the received (possibly delayed/stale) feedback.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Tele-Operated Crane"**
> Imagine an operator in a port control room steering a giant crane to load containers onto a ship. The operator (controller) cannot be on the crane. A radio link (network) carries his joystick commands. Sometimes the link lags, sometimes a packet is lost, and sometimes two commands arrive out of order. Despite these imperfections, the crane must still lift containers **safely and precisely**. *Controlling actuators over a network is the engineering science of making sure the crane never drops the container even when the radio is noisy.*

The key idea is that the network is **not a perfect wire** — it introduces stochastic disturbances that the controller must be designed to tolerate.

## 1.3 Core Components & Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Engineering Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Sensor-to-Controller delay | $\tau_{sc}$ | **seconds (s)** or **ms** |
| Controller-to-Actuator delay | $\tau_{ca}$ | **seconds (s)** or **ms** |
| Total round-trip delay | $\tau = \tau_{sc}+\tau_{ca}$ | **s** |
| Packet dropout rate | $\rho$ | dimensionless ($0 \le \rho \le 1$) |
| Sampling period | $h$ | **seconds (s)** |
| Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) | $\bar{\tau}$ | **s** |
| Network bandwidth | $B$ | **bits/s (bps)** |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern Industry 4.0, **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)**, and **IoT-based automation** all rely fundamentally on controlling actuators across networks. KTU examiners routinely test this topic because it bridges *classical control theory* with *communication theory* — a hallmark of advanced automation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Networked Actuator Control Loop

The control loop over a network consists of four functional blocks:

1. **Plant + Actuator** — physical process with input $u(t)$ applied to it.
2. **Sensor** — samples state $x(t)$ at period $h$.
3. **Network** — the shared medium carrying packets.
4. **Controller** — computes the new control law $u_c(k)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction:** A *Networked Control System* is different from a *Distributed Control System (DCS)*. In a DCS, controllers are dedicated to a small set of I/O; in an NCS, the controller, sensor, and actuator all **share** the network with non-control traffic, so bandwidth, latency, and reliability are stochastic.

## 2.2 Mathematical Modelling of Network Imperfections

### 2.2.1 Network-Induced Delay (τ)

The total delay has two components:

$$
\tau_k \;=\; \tau^{sc}_k \;+\; \tau^{ca}_k
$$

Both delays are **time-varying**, **bounded**, and often **i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed)** for Ethernet-style networks, or **deterministic but unknown** for scheduled fieldbuses.

$$
0 \;\le\; \tau_k \;\le\; \bar{\tau}, \qquad \bar{\tau} \le h \quad \text{(short-delay regime)}
$$

When $\bar{\tau} > h$, the system enters the **long-delay regime** and requires **augmented state-space models** with delay buffers.

### 2.2.2 Packet Dropout (ρ)

Packet loss is modelled as a Bernoulli stochastic process $\theta_k \in \{0,1\}$:

$$
\theta_k \;=\; \begin{cases} 1, & \text{packet received successfully} \\ 0, & \text{packet lost} \end{cases}, \qquad \Pr(\theta_k = 1) = 1-\rho
$$

If a packet is lost, the actuator typically applies one of three compensation strategies:

- **Zero-input (ZI):** $u(t) = 0$
- **Hold-input (HI):** $u(t) = u(t-h)$ (most common in practice)
- **Predictive-input (PI):** $u(t) = K\,\hat{x}(t\mid t-h)$ (using an estimator)

### 2.2.3 Continuous-Time Closed-Loop Plant

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A\,x(t) \;+\; B\,u(t-\tau_k)
$$

$$
y(t) \;=\; C\,x(t)
$$

Discretising with period $h$ (using zero-order hold on the actuator) gives:

$$
x(k+1) \;=\; A_d\,x(k) \;+\; \sum_{i=0}^{\tau_k/h} B_i\,u(k-i)
$$

where $A_d = e^{Ah}$ and $B_i$ are computed from the matrix exponential integral of $B\,e^{A(h-t)}$.

## 2.3 Compensation Strategies for Actuator Control

| Strategy | Core Idea | Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Gain Scheduling** | Adjust $K$ based on measured $\tau_k$ | When delay is **measurable** |
| **Smith Predictor** | Cancel known delay via internal model | When $\tau$ is **constant/known** |
| **Event-Triggered Control** | Transmit only when $\Vert x \Vert$ exceeds threshold | **Bandwidth-limited** NCS |
| **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Recompute trajectory over a horizon | Tolerates **dropouts + delays** |
| **H-infinity Robust Control** | Minimise worst-case $\Vert T_{zw}\Vert_\infty$ | **Stochastic** network QoS |
| **Lyapunov-Krasovskii Redesign** | Prove stability via delay-dependent LMI | **Theoretical** guarantees |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the *most-tested* equations in KTU End-Semester Examinations for the topic *Control of Actuators over a Network*. Memorise the symbols and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Marks |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\tau_k = \tau^{sc}_k + \tau^{ca}_k$ | Total round-trip delay | 1 |
| 2 | $x(k+1) = A_d x(k) + B_0 u(k) + B_1 u(k-1)$ | Delayed input discrete model (single-step delay) | 3–5 |
| 3 | $V(x) = x^{T} P x,\; P \succ 0$ | Lyapunov function candidate | 2 |
| 4 | $\Delta V \le -\alpha \Vert x \Vert^{2} + \beta \tau^{2}$ | Delay-dependent stability condition | 5 |
| 5 | $K = -R^{-1} B^{T} P$ | Discrete LQR gain (used as baseline) | 2 |
| 6 | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\left[ x^{T} Q x + u^{T} R u \right]$ | LQR cost over network | 3 |
| 7 | $\Vert G(s) \Vert_{\infty} = \sup_{\omega} \bar{\sigma}(G(j\omega))$ | $H_\infty$ norm (robustness) | 3 |
| 8 | $\bar{\tau} < \frac{-\ln(1-\rho)}{ \lambda_{max}(A) }$ | MADB approximation for stable plants | 4 |

> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** Do not confuse $\tau^{sc}$ (sensor-to-controller) with $\tau^{ca}$ (controller-to-actuator). Both are **delays**, but the indices are *opposite* to the direction of signal flow — a classic 1-mark trap.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive drive-by-wire:** Steering, brake, and throttle actuators controlled over **CAN-FD** or **FlexRay**.
- **Smart grids:** Distributed energy resources (DER) controlled via **IEC 61850** over Ethernet.
- **Robotic swarms:** Each robot's motor controllers linked via **Wi-Fi/5G** mesh.
- **Aerospace fly-by-wire:** Hydraulic actuators commanded over **AFDX** (Avionics Full-Duplex Ethernet).
- **Process industries:** Valve actuators over **Fieldbus Foundation** or **PROFIBUS-PA**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discretised State Equation with Network Delay

**Step 1 — Continuous-time plant with delayed control input**

Consider the linear time-invariant plant:

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A\,x(t) \;+\; B\,u(t)
$$

When the actuator receives the control signal with a constant delay $\tau$, the effective control becomes $u(t-\tau)$. The closed-loop plant is:

$$
\dot{x}(t) \;=\; A\,x(t) \;+\; B\,u(t-\tau)
$$

**Step 2 — Solution of the linear ODE**

The standard solution to $\dot{x} = Ax + B u(t-\tau)$ for $t \in [kh,\,(k+1)h)$ is obtained by variation of parameters. The solution over one sampling interval, starting at $t = kh$, is:

$$
x(t) \;=\; e^{A(t-kh)}\,x(kh) \;+\; \int_{kh}^{t} e^{A(t-s)} B\,u(s-\tau)\,ds
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $s-\tau = \sigma$ and split the integration**

Let $\sigma = s - \tau \Rightarrow ds = d\sigma$. The integral becomes:

$$
\int_{kh-\tau}^{t-\tau} e^{A(t-\tau-\sigma)} B\,u(\sigma)\,d\sigma
$$

**Step 4 — Assume a short-delay regime $\tau < h$**

Under this assumption, $kh - h < kh - \tau < kh$. The integral spans at most two consecutive control samples: $u((k-1)h)$ and $u(kh)$. Splitting the integral at $\sigma = kh$:

$$
\int_{kh-\tau}^{kh} e^{A(t-\tau-\sigma)} B\,u(\sigma)\,d\sigma \;+\; \int_{kh}^{t-\tau} e^{A(t-\tau-\sigma)} B\,u(\sigma)\,d\sigma
$$

Because $u(\sigma)$ is constant over each sample (zero-order hold), we obtain:

$$
B_0 \,u(kh) \;+\; B_1 \,u((k-1)h)
$$

where the gain matrices are defined as:

$$
B_0 \;=\; \int_{0}^{h-\tau} e^{A s}\,B\,ds, \qquad B_1 \;=\; \int_{h-\tau}^{h} e^{A s}\,B\,ds
$$

**Step 5 — Discretise at $t = (k+1)h$**

Substituting $t = (k+1)h$ into the solution and using $A_d = e^{Ah}$:

$$
x((k+1)h) \;=\; e^{Ah}\,x(kh) \;+\; B_0\,u(kh) \;+\; B_1\,u((k-1)h)
$$

**Final discrete-time delayed-input model (KTU Standard Form):**

$$
\boxed{\;x(k+1) \;=\; A_d\,x(k) \;+\; B_0\,u(k) \;+\; B_1\,u(k-1)\;}
$$

This equation is the *cornerstone* of every KTU question on this topic. Every subsequent analysis — stability, controller design, dropouts — begins here.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stability Condition via Lyapunov-Krasovskii Functional

**Step 1 — Choose a Lyapunov-Krasovskii functional**

For the discrete delayed system above, a standard choice is:

$$
V_k \;=\; x^{T}(k)\,P\,x(k) \;+\; \sum_{i=k-1}^{k-1} x^{T}(i)\,Q\,x(i) \;+\; \sum_{i=k-1}^{k-1} u^{T}(i)\,R\,u(i)
$$

where $P, Q, R \succ 0$ (positive-definite matrices).

**Step 2 — Compute the forward difference**

$$
\Delta V_k \;=\; V_{k+1} - V_k
$$

Expanding each term:

$$
\Delta V_k \;=\; x^{T}(k+1) P x(k+1) - x^{T}(k) P x(k) \;+\; x^{T}(k) Q x(k) - x^{T}(k-1) Q x(k-1) \;+\; u^{T}(k) R u(k) - u^{T}(k-1) R u(k-1)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the system equation**

Insert $x(k+1) = A_d x(k) + B_0 u(k) + B_1 u(k-1)$. The cross terms yield:

$$
\Delta V_k \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} x(k) \\ u(k) \\ u(k-1) \end{bmatrix}^{T} \Phi \begin{bmatrix} x(k) \\ u(k) \\ u(k-1) \end{bmatrix} - x^{T}(k-1) Q x(k-1) - u^{T}(k-1) R u(k-1)
$$

where $\Phi$ is a $3 \times 3$ block matrix built from $A_d, B_0, B_1, P, Q, R$.

**Step 4 — Stability requirement**

For asymptotic stability, we require $\Delta V_k < 0$ for all non-zero state vectors. By the **S-procedure** and **Schur complement**, this is equivalent to the **Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI)**:

$$
\boxed{\;\Phi \prec 0, \quad P \succ 0, \quad Q \succ 0, \quad R \succ 0\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation for the engineer**

If the LMI $\Phi \prec 0$ has a feasible solution, the closed-loop NCS is **delay-dependent stable** for the given $\tau$ and sampling period $h$. The solution can be computed numerically using MATLAB's `lmiedit`, YALMIP, or Python's CVXPY toolbox (allowed in KTU lab work but **not** required in theory exams).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How to Tackle KTU Questions

| Question Type | Starting Equation | Key Technique | Marks Weight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Define $\tau$ | $\tau = \tau^{sc} + \tau^{ca}$ | Direct definition | 2 |
| Discretise delayed plant | $\dot{x}=Ax+Bu(t-\tau)$ | Variation of parameters | 5–7 |
| Prove stability | $x(k+1)=A_d x(k)+B_0 u(k)+B_1 u(k-1)$ | Lyapunov-Krasovskii LMI | 7–10 |
| Design gain $K$ | LQR cost $J=\sum (x^T Q x + u^T R u)$ | Algebraic Riccati Equation (ARE) | 5–7 |
| Handle dropout | $\theta_k \in \{0,1\}$, Bernoulli | Stochastic Lyapunov, $E[\Delta V_k]<0$ | 7–10 |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Actuator Control over a Networked Loop

The following **Mermaid block diagram** depicts the complete signal flow from sensor through the shared network to the actuator driving the plant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plant P] -->|Measured output y t| B[Sensor S]
    B -->|Sampled x kh| C[Encoder Enc]
    C -->|Packet p1| D[Network Medium]
    D -->|Delay tsc| E[Decoder Dec]
    E -->|Feedback xhat| F[Controller C]
    F -->|Control law u kh| G[Encoder Enc]
    G -->|Packet p2| D
    D -->|Delay tca| H[Decoder Dec]
    H -->|Actuation signal u t| I[Actuator A]
    I -->|Mechanical input| A

    D -.->|Dropout rho, Jitter| J[Network Imperfections]
    J -.->|Affects| D
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right):**

- The **Plant** $P$ generates output $y(t)$, which is measured by the **Sensor** $S$.
- The sensor output is **sampled at period $h$**, encoded into a packet, and transmitted over the **Network** with delay $\tau_{sc}$.
- The **Controller** computes $u(k)$ using the received (possibly stale) feedback.
- The new control packet is transmitted back over the **Network** with delay $\tau_{ca}$.
- The **Actuator** applies the physical input to the plant, completing the loop.
- The **Network Imperfections** block (delay, dropout, jitter) acts as a stochastic disturbance on the shared medium.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — Packet Outcomes

The diagram below shows how packets transition through the network, including the **three compensation strategies** invoked when a packet is lost.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Transmit
    Transmit --> Received: theta k = 1
    Transmit --> Lost: theta k = 0
    Lost --> ZeroInput: ZI policy
    Lost --> HoldInput: HI policy
    Lost --> Predictive: PI policy
    Received --> Decoder
    ZeroInput --> Actuator
    HoldInput --> Actuator
    Predictive --> Actuator
    Decoder --> Actuator
    Actuator --> Plant
    Plant --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:**

- A packet is either **received** ($\theta_k = 1$) or **lost** ($\theta_k = 0$).
- On loss, one of the three compensation strategies — **Zero-Input (ZI)**, **Hold-Input (HI)**, or **Predictive (PI)** — is selected to maintain stability.
- The actuator finally drives the plant, regardless of which path was taken.

## 4.3 Bandwidth vs Stability Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network QoS] --> B{Latency tau}
    A --> C{Dropout rho}
    A --> D{Bandwidth B}

    B --> E{tau less than MADB}
    E -->|Yes| F[Stable Operation]
    E -->|No| G[Re-design Controller K]

    C --> H{rho less than rho max}
    H -->|Yes| F
    H -->|No| G

    D --> I{B meets sampling demand}
    I -->|Yes| F
    I -->|No| G

    G --> J[Increase gain / use event triggering]
    J --> F
```

**Use this diagram** in exam answers when the question asks *"What happens if the network becomes too slow?"* — trace the latency branch to MADB, the dropout branch to $\rho_{max}$, and the bandwidth branch to the Nyquist-like demand $B \ge 2 n f_{sample}$ for an $n$-bit packet.
