## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT862, Module 2]`**
*Define the term "agent" in the context of a Networked Multi-Agent Control System. List the four defining properties an entity must possess to be called an agent.*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

An **agent** in a Networked Multi-Agent Control System is an autonomous, software-hardware entity embedded in a physical node that perceives its local environment, communicates with neighboring agents over a shared network, and acts to achieve local and team-level objectives.

The four defining properties are:
1. **Autonomy** — local decision-making without external command.
2. **Reactivity** — timely response to sensed changes.
3. **Proactivity** — goal-directed, anticipatory behavior.
4. **Social Ability** — peer-to-peer communication for coordination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is listing only *autonomy* and *communication*. Examiners strictly enforce the four-property definition (autonomy, reactivity, proactivity, social ability) and will cap your mark at 1.5/3 if any one is missing.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECPT862, Module 2]`**
*Explain the concept of "algebraic connectivity" $\lambda_2(L)$ of a graph. Why is it the single most important scalar metric for assessing a networked control system?*

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~90 words):**

The **algebraic connectivity** $\lambda_2(L)$ is the second-smallest eigenvalue of the graph Laplacian $L = D - A$. It is non-zero if and only if the graph is connected.

Its importance for networked control systems is threefold:
1. **Topological health indicator** — $\lambda_2 > 0$ is a necessary condition for consensus reachability.
2. **Convergence-speed governor** — the consensus settling time scales as $1 / \lambda_2$.
3. **Robustness measure** — the larger $\lambda_2$, the more resilient the system is to link failures and delays.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write $\lambda_1$ instead of $\lambda_2$ or confuse it with spectral radius. Always state explicitly that $\lambda_1 = 0$ and $\lambda_2 > 0$ is the metric of interest.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECPT862, Module 2]`

**(a)** *Derive the consensus update law for $N$ agents connected via an undirected graph with Laplacian $L$. State and prove the necessary and sufficient condition for asymptotic consensus.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A team of 4 autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) is deployed in a square formation. Each AUV's surge-velocity dynamics are scalar $A = 1.05$, $B = 0.8$, and the consensus gain is $K = 0.25$. The communication graph is a complete graph $K_4$. Determine whether asymptotic velocity consensus is achieved. If not, suggest the smallest gain modification that would guarantee consensus, and compute the resulting settling time within $1\%$ error.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Define local dynamics $x_i(k+1) = A x_i(k) + B u_i(k)$. Write the consensus law $u_i(k) = -K \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} a_{ij}(x_i - x_j)$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Stack into $x(k+1) = (I_N \otimes A - L \otimes BK) x(k)$. Diagonalize $L = U \Lambda U^{\top}$ and apply the modal transform to obtain $N$ decoupled modes $\tilde{x}_i(k+1) = (A - \lambda_i BK) \tilde{x}_i(k)$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* The first mode $\lambda_1 = 0$ conserves the team average; all other modes must satisfy $\rho(A - \lambda_i BK) < 1$.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* State the final necessary and sufficient condition:
- Graph must be connected ($\lambda_2 > 0$),
- $A - \lambda_i BK$ Schur-stable $\forall i \ge 2$.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 (1.5 marks):* Laplacian of complete graph $K_4$ has eigenvalues $0, 4, 4, 4$.

*Step 2 (1.5 marks):* Modal pole: $A - \lambda_{\max} BK = 1.05 - 4 \times 0.8 \times 0.25 = 1.05 - 0.8 = 0.25$. Since $\vert 0.25 \vert < 1$, consensus **IS achieved**. (Note: examiner usually sets it borderline, so state clearly that the answer is "yes, achieved.")

*Step 3 (2 marks):* To guarantee a settling time within $1\%$, we use $0.25^k \le 0.01$. Compute $k \ge \ln(0.01) / \ln(0.25) = 3.32$ — so $k = 4$ time-steps.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Convert time-step to seconds given a sampling period $T_s$ (assume $T_s = 0.1\,\text{s}$ unless otherwise stated): $T_{ss} = 4 \times 0.1 = 0.4\,\text{s}$. State explicitly that this satisfies the $500\,\text{ms}$ microgrid benchmark from IEC 61850.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECPT862, Module 2]`

**(a)** *Explain the three network-induced imperfections that arise when an electrical control system is migrated to a connectivity-based architecture. For each imperfection, give the engineering origin and its mathematical representation.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A wireless sensor-actuator network of 5 nodes monitors a 415 V industrial bus. Each node has identical first-order dynamics $A = 1.1$, $B = 1$. The network introduces a constant delay $\tau = 2$ time-steps and a packet dropout rate $\bar{\gamma} = 0.15$. Using the delay-margin and dropout-bound formulas, determine the maximum allowable consensus gain $K$ that guarantees $\epsilon$-consensus with bound $\epsilon = 0.05$. Assume the graph Laplacian maximum eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}(L) = 4$ for the mesh topology used.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks (allocate 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for structure):**

*Imperfection 1 — Time Delay (2 marks):*
- **Origin:** finite propagation speed, queuing at routers, OS scheduling latency.
- **Mathematical representation:** $x_i(k - \tau)$ enters the consensus sum, or in the $z$-domain: $z^{-\tau}$.

*Imperfection 2 — Packet Dropout (2 marks):*
- **Origin:** congestion, wireless fading, EMI, link failure.
- **Mathematical representation:** Bernoulli process $\gamma_k \in \{0, 1\}$ with $\Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \bar{\gamma}$; the received signal is $\gamma_k y_j(k)$.

*Imperfection 3 — Quantization (2 marks):*
- **Origin:** finite ADC resolution, bandwidth-limited digital channel.
- **Mathematical representation:** $\mathcal{Q}(x) = \Delta \left\lfloor x/\Delta + 0.5 \right\rfloor$ with step $\Delta = \text{FSR} / 2^b$ for $b$-bit ADC.

*Closing line (1 mark):* State that all three must be analyzed jointly for any realistic NCS design.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Apply the delay-margin formula:
$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{\pi}{2 \lambda_{\max}(L) \sigma_{\max}(BK)} = \frac{\pi}{2 \times 4 \times (1 \cdot K)} = \frac{\pi}{8K}
$$
Require $\tau_{\max} > 2 \Rightarrow K < \pi / 16 \approx 0.1963$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Apply the dropout bound:
$$
\bar{\gamma} < 1 - \prod_i \vert \lambda_i(A - BK) \vert
$$
For a single scalar mode: $\vert A - BK \vert = \vert 1.1 - K \vert$. With $\bar{\gamma} = 0.15$, we need $\vert 1.1 - K \vert < 0.85$, so $0.25 < K < 1.95$.

*Step 3 (1.5 marks):* Intersect the two intervals:
- From delay: $K < 0.1963$
- From dropout: $0.25 < K < 1.95$

The intersection is **empty** — meaning the *nominal* parameter set cannot guarantee asymptotic consensus. The system must operate in $\epsilon$-consensus regime.

*Step 4 (1.5 marks):* For $\epsilon$-consensus with bound $\epsilon = 0.05$, accept $K = 0.2$ (slightly violating the strict delay margin by $1.9\%$). The steady-state disagreement is bounded by:
$$
\vert\vert \delta_{\infty} \vert\vert_2 \le \frac{\tau \lambda_{\max}(L) \sigma_{\max}(BK)}{\pi - \tau \lambda_{\max}(L) \sigma_{\max}(BK)} \vert\vert \delta_0 \vert\vert_2
$$
which can be tuned below $0.05$ by reducing $K$ to $0.18$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting $\lambda_{\max}$ vs $\lambda_2$:** The delay formula uses $\lambda_{\max}$, not $\lambda_2$. Mixing them is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Ignoring the empty intersection:** Always check both constraints together. The correct engineering answer is "redesign the graph to lower $\lambda_{\max}$, e.g., by adding a star-center node," not just "pick $K$."
> 3. **Skipping the unit check:** All formulas assume consistent time units; mixing seconds and time-steps in the same equation costs a full mark in the 14-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section the night before the exam. It is a one-page density-optimized summary of Module 2.**

- [ ] **The Three Eras:** Pure-Electrical → SCADA-Centralized → Distributed-Agent.
- [ ] **Four Agent Properties:** Autonomy, Reactivity, Proactivity, Social Ability.
- [ ] **Five-Layer CPS Stack:** Physical, Network, Data, Agent, Application.
- [ ] **Graph Laplacian:** $L = D - A$, symmetric PSD, $\lambda_1 = 0$.
- [ ] **Consensus Theorem:** Connected graph + $\rho(A - \lambda_i BK) < 1$ for all $i \ge 2$ = asymptotic consensus.
- [ ] **Convergence Rate:** $\beta \le \sqrt{1 - \lambda_2(L)\, \sigma_{\min}(Q_P)}$.
- [ ] **Delay Margin:** $\tau_{\max} = \pi / \big( 2 \lambda_{\max}(L) \sigma_{\max}(BK) \big)$.
- [ ] **Dropout Bound:** $\bar{\gamma} < 1 - \prod_i \vert \lambda_i(A - BK) \vert$.
- [ ] **Quantization Bound:** $\Delta \le \sqrt{\lambda_2(L)} \sigma_{\min}(P BK) / \big( 2 \sigma_{\max}(P) \big)$.
- [ ] **Lyapunov Function:** $V(k) = x^{\top}(k)(L \otimes P) x(k)$, $P \succ 0$.
- [ ] **Settling Time:** $T_{ss} \approx \ln(0.01) / \ln(\rho_{\max})$.
- [ ] **Standard Metrics:** Latency $\le 4\,$ms, PDR $\ge 99.999\%$, $T_{ss} \le 500\,$ms, Jitter $\le 100\,\mu$s.
- [ ] **Standards Toolkit:** IEEE 1547, IEEE 2030.5, IEC 61850, ISO/IEC 30141, IEEE 1588 (PTP), ISO 26262 (functional safety).
- [ ] **Five Justifications for Agent-Based:** Fault tolerance, plug-and-play, geographic dispersion, computational tractability, market-based dispatch.
- [ ] **Common Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In exam scripts, write `\vert x \vert` for absolute value, never `|x|`, when copying from a typeset table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra (Module 2):** *"If the graph is connected and the feedback gain kills the worst spectral mode, the agents agree — and how fast they agree is exactly what the algebraic connectivity tells you."* Memorize this sentence; it is the single most-likely opening line of a 7-mark KTU Part B question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hosting Mechanism and System Architecture for ABC
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hosting Mechanism and System Architecture for ABC

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Activity-Based Computing (ABC)

**Activity-Based Computing (ABC)** is a distributed computing paradigm in which the **"activity"** — a logical, user-centric collection of related computational tasks, services, and resources — is treated as a **first-class entity** that can be *captured, suspended, transported, and resumed* across heterogeneous networked devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Activity-Based Computing is a model in which a user can seamlessly migrate an in-progress task (activity) from one device to another, preserving its state, services, and context, by relying on a hosting mechanism supported by a distributed system architecture."

The **Hosting Mechanism** in ABC is the **run-time substrate** (middleware + container) that:
1. Encapsulates an active session (an *Activity Container*).
2. Listens for incoming activities on a device.
3. Manages **service discovery**, **resource binding**, and **context propagation**.
4. Supports **live migration** of activities between hosts without loss of state.

The **System Architecture for ABC** defines the layered structural blueprint comprising *Capture, Hosting, Migration, and Resume* sub-systems, typically realized through a **Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)** over IP-based networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Travelling Workspace":**
> 
> Imagine you are a doctor checking patient records on a **tablet in the ward**. While reviewing, you need to move to an **operation theatre desktop** for a procedure. In ABC, your **entire open workspace** — the open file, scroll position, connected monitor, running calculations, and the network printer you were using — is "lifted" *as a single package* from the tablet and **dropped onto the theatre desktop in mid-operation**, exactly where you left off, **without losing anything**.
> 
> That magical "lifting, carrying, and placing" is the **Hosting Mechanism**. The blueprint that decides *where* this can happen and *how* devices talk to each other is the **System Architecture**.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Standard Metrics in ABC

| Metric | Standard Value (KTU Reference) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Activity Latency | **< 500 ms** | Time to suspend + transport + resume an activity |
| State Encapsulation Size | **< 2 MB** (typical) | Snapshot of activity state for migration |
| Discovery Range | **LAN / WLAN** (≤ 100 m) | Local service discovery via UPnP / mDNS |
| Context Refresh | **1–5 seconds** | Polling interval for environmental changes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Whenever ABC is asked in exams, the examiner expects you to state that *"an Activity is the atomic unit of user work"*, and the **host** is the *"physical/virtual container that executes the activity"*. This is a guaranteed 2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of ABC Architecture

ABC is built upon three orthogonal pillars that together form the hosting ecosystem:

### Pillar 1 — Activity as a First-Class Entity
- An **Activity** aggregates: *(i)* user input state, *(ii)* output state, *(iii)* service bindings, *(iv)* device context, and *(v)* execution thread.
- It is **independent of the host device** — the host is merely a *run-time container*.
- A unique **Activity ID (AID)** is generated using a hash, e.g.:

$$AID = H(MAC_{host} \Vert timestamp \Vert user_{id})$$

where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash (SHA-1 / SHA-256) and $\Vert$ denotes concatenation.

### Pillar 2 — Hosting Mechanism
The hosting mechanism is a **layered middleware** that performs four critical functions:

1. **Activity Capture** — Snapshots the live state of an in-progress task.
2. **Serialization** — Converts the state into a transport-agnostic format (e.g., **XML / Protocol Buffers**).
3. **Transport** — Moves the serialized activity over the network using **TCP / UDP / SOAP / REST**.
4. **Re-hydration / Resume** — Restores the activity on the target host and re-binds services.

### Pillar 3 — Distributed System Architecture
- **Tiers**: *User Devices → Local Hosts → Network Middleware → Service Registry*
- **Topology**: Peer-to-Peer (P2P) for small groups; **Client–Broker–Server** for enterprise deployment.
- **Communication**: SOAP over HTTP (legacy) or **REST/JSON over HTTPS** (modern ABC).

## 2.2 Operational Workflow — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 — Initiation:** User starts an activity on Device A. The host binds required services from a *Service Registry*.
- **Step 2 — Context Capture:** The host continuously monitors user context (location, network, device capabilities).
- **Step 3 — Trigger Event:** A *migration trigger* occurs (e.g., user leaves a geofence, device battery drops below 20 %, or user manually selects "Move Activity").
- **Step 4 — Suspension:** Host A freezes the activity, serializes state, and packages it.
- **Step 5 — Negotiation:** Host A queries the network to identify a suitable Host B (via service discovery).
- **Step 6 — Migration:** Encapsulated activity is transmitted.
- **Step 7 — Resume:** Host B deserializes, restores, and re-binds services.
- **Step 8 — Garbage Collection:** Host A deletes the local snapshot after ACK.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Activity ID generation | $AID = H(HostMAC \Vert t_s \Vert UID)$ | $t_s$ = start time (s) |
| 2 | Total migration latency | $T_{mig} = T_{cap} + T_{ser} + T_{tx} + T_{res}$ | **seconds** |
| 3 | Capture time | $T_{cap} = \dfrac{S_{state}}{R_{IO}}$ | $S_{state}$ = state size (bytes) |
| 4 | Transmission time | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{P_{size}}{B_{net}}$ | $B_{net}$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| 5 | Resume time | $T_{res} = \alpha \cdot S_{state} + \beta$ | $\alpha, \beta$ empirical |
| 6 | Discovery delay | $T_{disc} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{req}} \cdot N_{hosts}$ | $\lambda$ = req rate (req/s) |
| 7 | Reliability constraint | $P_{success} = 1 - (1-p)^n \geq 0.99$ | $p$ = per-hop success, $n$ = hops |
| 8 | Battery trigger threshold | $E_{trigger} = 0.20 \cdot E_{full}$ | **20 %** — standard ABC rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** ABC is foundational to **smart hospitals, smart factories (Industry 4.0), IoT-driven control rooms, and digital twin ecosystems** where operators physically move between stations.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Networked Control Systems (NCS):** Operator handovers in SCADA control rooms.
- **Healthcare:** Seamless transfer of patient-monitoring sessions between ICU, ward, and theatre.
- **Industrial IoT:** Migrating a control loop dashboard between an engineer's tablet and a wall-mounted HMI.
- **Smart Classrooms:** Lecture sessions migrating between a teacher's laptop and a smartboard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Migration Time $T_{mig}$

We model ABC migration as a **pipeline of four serial stages**. Because the stages occur in sequence (the activity cannot be transmitted before capture, etc.), the total time is the **sum of individual stage times**.

**Step 1 — Define the four stages.**
1. Capture time: $T_{cap}$
2. Serialization time: $T_{ser}$
3. Transmission time: $T_{tx}$
4. Resume time: $T_{res}$

**Step 2 — Apply serial-stage composition rule.**

$$T_{mig} = T_{cap} + T_{ser} + T_{tx} + T_{res}$$

**Step 3 — Express each stage in measurable quantities.**

The capture time depends on the size of the state being captured and the host's I/O throughput:

$$T_{cap} = \frac{S_{state}}{R_{IO}}$$

Serialization time is linear in state size, with proportionality constant $k_{ser}$ (seconds/byte) determined by the encoding format (XML, JSON, Protobuf):

$$T_{ser} = k_{ser} \cdot S_{state}$$

Transmission time follows the classic link-capacity relation (Shannon-style):

$$T_{tx} = \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}}$$

where $P_{size}$ is the packet size in bits and $B_{net}$ is the effective bandwidth in bits per second.

Resume time is empirically modeled as a linear function of state size with offset $\beta$:

$$T_{res} = \alpha \cdot S_{state} + \beta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the master equation.**

$$T_{mig} = \frac{S_{state}}{R_{IO}} + k_{ser} \cdot S_{state} + \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \alpha \cdot S_{state} + \beta$$

**Step 5 — Group the state-size terms.**

Let $K = \dfrac{1}{R_{IO}} + k_{ser} + \alpha$ (a constant with units of seconds/byte):

$$T_{mig} = K \cdot S_{state} + \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \beta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This compact form shows that **migration time grows linearly with state size** and **inversely with bandwidth**. Doubling the bandwidth roughly halves the transmission component.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discovery Delay $T_{disc}$

When Host A wants to migrate an activity, it must first discover a suitable Host B. If the network contains $N_{hosts}$ candidate hosts and each discovery request is sent at rate $\lambda_{req}$ (requests per second), the average time to receive a response is governed by the **expected waiting time** of a Poisson process:

$$T_{disc} = \frac{N_{hosts}}{\lambda_{req}}$$

To meet KTU's Quality-of-Service target of $T_{disc} \le 100\,\text{ms}$, we solve for the minimum request rate:

$$\lambda_{req} \ge \frac{N_{hosts}}{0.1}$$

For $N_{hosts} = 5$ devices, we get $\lambda_{req} \ge 50\,\text{req/s}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reliability Constraint

For an ABC migration to traverse $n$ network hops, each with per-hop success probability $p$, the overall success probability is:

$$P_{success} = 1 - (1-p)^n$$

For KTU-grade reliability ($P_{success} \ge 0.99$) and $n = 3$ hops:

$$1 - (1-p)^3 \ge 0.99$$

$$(1-p)^3 \le 0.01$$

$$1-p \le 0.01^{1/3} = 0.2154$$

$$p \ge 0.7846$$

So each hop must have a success probability of at least **78.46 %** to guarantee 99 % end-to-end reliability.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Trigger Condition | Migration Decision | Target Host Type |
|---|---|---|
| Battery $E \le 0.20 E_{full}$ | **Migrate immediately** | Nearest powered host |
| User leaves geofence | **Migrate** | New local host |
| Network bandwidth $B \le 1\,\text{Mbps}$ | **Suspend + wait** | Re-evaluate after 30 s |
| Target host unavailable | **Buffer locally** | Resume on current host |
| Service registry unreachable | **Abort migration** | Notify user, log error |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ABC System Architecture — Layered Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph USER_LAYER["USER LAYER"]
        U1[End User]
        U2[Mobile Operator]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE_LAYER["DEVICE LAYER / ACTIVITY HOSTS"]
        D1[Smartphone]
        D2[Desktop Workstation]
        D3[Wall Mounted HMI]
        D4[Tablet]
    end

    subgraph MIDDLEWARE_LAYER["ABC MIDDLEWARE / HOSTING MECHANISM"]
        M1[Activity Capture Engine]
        M2[Serialization Module]
        M3[Migration Controller]
        M4[Resume Engine]
        M5[Service Discovery]
        M6[Context Manager]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK_LAYER["NETWORK LAYER"]
        N1[TCP UDP SOAP REST]
        N2[WiFi LAN 5G]
    end

    subgraph SERVICE_LAYER["SERVICE LAYER"]
        S1[Service Registry]
        S2[Authentication Server]
        S3[Context Database]
    end

    U1 --> D1
    U2 --> D2
    D1 --> M1
    D2 --> M3
    D3 --> M5
    D4 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> N1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> M4
    M4 --> D2
    M5 --> S1
    M6 --> S3
    S2 --> M3
```

## 4.2 Activity Migration Flow — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UserA as User
    participant HostA as Host A Source
    participant Net as Network Middleware
    participant HostB as Host B Target
    participant SReg as Service Registry

    UserA->>HostA: Start Activity
    HostA->>SReg: Discover Services
    SReg-->>HostA: Service Bindings
    HostA->>UserA: Activity Running
    Note over HostA: Trigger Event Detected
    HostA->>HostA: Capture and Serialize State
    HostA->>Net: Transmit Encapsulated Activity
    Net->>HostB: Deliver Activity Packet
    HostB->>HostB: Deserialize and Re hydrate
    HostB->>SReg: Re bind Services
    HostB->>UserA: Activity Resumed on Host B
    HostA->>HostA: Garbage Collect Snapshot
```

## 4.3 Functional Component Matrix

| Component | Function | Layer | Protocol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Activity Capture Engine | Snapshots live state | Middleware | Internal API |
| Serialization Module | Converts state to XML / Protobuf | Middleware | XML, JSON |
| Migration Controller | Routes activity to target host | Middleware | TCP / UDP |
| Service Discovery | Finds suitable host + services | Middleware | UPnP, mDNS |
| Context Manager | Monitors user / device context | Middleware | Sensors, GPS |
| Service Registry | Stores available services | Service | LDAP, REST |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Activity-Based Computing (ABC). List any two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**
Activity-Based Computing is a distributed computing paradigm in which a user’s in-progress task — known as an *activity* — is treated as a **first-class, host-independent entity** that can be **suspended, transported, and resumed** across heterogeneous networked devices.

**Two key characteristics:**
1. **Activity as a first-class entity** — independent of the device that hosts it.
2. **Seamless migration** — supports live movement of activities between hosts without loss of state, services, or context.

*(Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 = 3)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the Hosting Mechanism in ABC?**

**Model Answer:**
The Hosting Mechanism is the run-time middleware that acts as the *container* and *executor* of an activity on a device. Its roles are:
1. **Encapsulation** of activity state (capture and serialization).
2. **Execution and management** of the activity during its lifetime on that host.
3. **Service discovery and binding** to external resources required by the activity.
4. **Coordination of migration** — suspension, transport, and hand-off to a target host.

*(Marks: 3)*

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose ONE

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **system architecture of Activity-Based Computing (ABC)** with a neat block diagram. Clearly describe the functions of the **User, Device, Middleware, Network, and Service layers.** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step activity migration process** in ABC. What are the major triggers that initiate a migration? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) System Architecture of ABC (7 Marks)**

The ABC system architecture is a **five-layer hierarchical model**:

1. **User Layer (1 mark):** The end-user or operator who initiates and interacts with activities. Users may move physically between smart spaces.

2. **Device Layer / Activity Hosts (1 mark):** Heterogeneous devices (smartphones, desktops, tablets, HMIs) that act as *hosts* executing the activity. Each host runs the ABC middleware.

3. **Middleware / Hosting Mechanism Layer (2 marks):** The core of ABC. It contains:
   - *Activity Capture Engine* — snapshots live state.
   - *Serialization Module* — encodes state for transport.
   - *Migration Controller* — negotiates and routes the activity.
   - *Resume Engine* — restores the activity on the target host.
   - *Service Discovery* and *Context Manager* sub-modules.

4. **Network Layer (1 mark):** Provides transport via TCP/UDP, SOAP/REST, over Wi-Fi/LAN/5G.

5. **Service Layer (2 marks):** Hosts the *Service Registry*, *Authentication Server*, and *Context Database* that the middleware queries for binding services to activities.

*(A neat labelled block diagram earns the remaining credit. Refer to SECTION 4 diagram.)*

**(b) Step-by-Step Migration Process (7 Marks)**

1. **Initiation (1 mark):** Activity starts on Host A; services are bound from the registry.
2. **Context Monitoring (1 mark):** Context Manager continuously tracks user location, device battery, and network state.
3. **Trigger Detection (1 mark):** A migration trigger fires — examples:
   - Battery drops below **20 %** of full charge.
   - User leaves a predefined geofence.
   - User explicitly selects "Move Activity".
   - Network quality drops below threshold.
4. **Suspension and Capture (1 mark):** Host A freezes the activity; the Capture Engine snapshots the state.
5. **Serialization and Transport (1 mark):** State is encoded (XML/Protobuf) and sent over the network.
6. **Discovery of Target Host (1 mark):** Service Discovery identifies a suitable Host B.
7. **Resume and Garbage Collection (1 mark):** Host B deserializes, re-binds services, and resumes the activity; Host A deletes its local copy after acknowledgement.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

**(a)** Derive the **total migration time equation** $T_{mig} = K \cdot S_{state} + \dfrac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \beta$ for an ABC system. Define each variable. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An activity of state size **4 MB** must be migrated over a network of bandwidth **100 Mbps**. Given $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s/byte}$, $k_{ser} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s/byte}$, and $R_{IO} = 200\,\text{MB/s}$, $\beta = 0.05\,\text{s}$, compute the total migration time. Is it within the KTU latency target of **500 ms**? **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Derivation of $T_{mig}$ (7 Marks)**

Migration is a 4-stage serial pipeline: Capture → Serialize → Transmit → Resume.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define stage times.

$$T_{cap} = \frac{S_{state}}{R_{IO}}, \quad T_{ser} = k_{ser} \cdot S_{state}, \quad T_{tx} = \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}}, \quad T_{res} = \alpha \cdot S_{state} + \beta$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Sum the four stages.

$$T_{mig} = \frac{S_{state}}{R_{IO}} + k_{ser} \cdot S_{state} + \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \alpha \cdot S_{state} + \beta$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Group state-size terms. Let $K = \dfrac{1}{R_{IO}} + k_{ser} + \alpha$.

$$T_{mig} = K \cdot S_{state} + \frac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \beta$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Define all variables.
- $S_{state}$ — state size in bytes.
- $P_{size}$ — packet size in bits.
- $B_{net}$ — network bandwidth in bps.
- $R_{IO}$ — host I/O throughput in bytes/s.
- $k_{ser}$ — serialization coefficient (s/byte).
- $\alpha$ — resume coefficient (s/byte).
- $\beta$ — resume offset (s).

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert units.
$$S_{state} = 4\,\text{MB} = 4 \times 10^6\,\text{bytes}, \quad P_{size} = 4 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 3.2 \times 10^7\,\text{bits}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute each stage.
- $T_{cap} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^6} = 0.02\,\text{s}$
- $T_{ser} = (2.5 \times 10^{-8})(4 \times 10^6) = 0.10\,\text{s}$
- $T_{tx} = \dfrac{3.2 \times 10^7}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.32\,\text{s}$
- $T_{res} = (1.2 \times 10^{-8})(4 \times 10^6) + 0.05 = 0.048 + 0.05 = 0.098\,\text{s}$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Sum them up.

$$T_{mig} = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.32 + 0.098 = 0.538\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare to KTU target.

Since $T_{mig} = 538\,\text{ms} > 500\,\text{ms}$, the migration **exceeds the KTU latency target by 38 ms**. 

**Recommendation:** Increase bandwidth or compress the state (e.g., gzip) to bring $T_{mig}$ below 500 ms.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit mistakes** (–2 marks): Forgetting to convert **MB → bytes → bits** when computing $T_{tx}$. KTU examiners are ruthless on this.
> 2. **Confusing *host* with *server*** (–1 mark): In ABC, a *host* is a **user device**, not a centralized server. Writing "server" loses a mark.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" of migration** (–1 mark): Always state at least **one trigger** (battery, geofence, manual).
> 4. **Omitting Service Registry role** (–1 mark): ABC migration requires **re-binding of services** on the target host — examiners look for this keyword.
> 5. **Forgetting garbage collection step** (–0.5 mark): Mention that the source host deletes its snapshot after ACK.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **ABC** = Activity-Based Computing; the **activity** is the atomic unit of user work.
- ✅ **Host** = the device running the activity; **Hosting Mechanism** = the middleware that captures, transports, and resumes activities.
- ✅ The **5-layer ABC architecture** is: *User → Device → Middleware → Network → Service*.
- ✅ Migration triggers include: **battery ≤ 20 %**, **geofence exit**, **user command**, **network degradation**.
- ✅ Migration pipeline: **Capture → Serialize → Transmit → Resume** (4 serial stages).
- ✅ Total latency: $T_{mig} = K \cdot S_{state} + \dfrac{P_{size}}{B_{net}} + \beta$.
- ✅ KTU target: $T_{mig} \le \mathbf{500\,ms}$; reliability: $P_{success} \ge \mathbf{0.99}$.
- ✅ Always re-bind services on the **target host** via the **Service Registry**.
- ✅ Garbage collect the source snapshot **only after ACK** from the target.
- ✅ ABC is used in **smart hospitals, Industry 4.0, IoT control rooms, and digital twins** — name at least one application in long answers for an extra 1 mark.
- ✅ Common protocols: **TCP/UDP, SOAP, REST/HTTPS, mDNS, UPnP**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principle for Networked Control Systems: Local Simple, Remote Complex (LSRC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Principle for Networked Control Systems: Local Simple, Remote Complex (LSRC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LSRC (Local Simple, Remote Complex)** is a foundational design principle in Networked Control Systems (NCS) which prescribes that the **computational, intelligence, and decision-making burden** must be pushed away from the field-level sensor/actuator nodes (local) and concentrated at a higher-tier supervisory controller, server, or cloud infrastructure (remote). The local nodes are reduced to elementary functions such as sampling, pre-processing, and actuation, while the remote station absorbs the sophisticated control laws, estimation, optimization, and fault-diagnosis tasks.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this principle is treated as a *design philosophy* rather than a mathematical law, governing **resource allocation**, **network bandwidth economy**, and **fault isolation** in distributed control architectures.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **chain of small branch post offices** connected to a **central head post office**:

- The **local branch office (Local Node)** does *very little* — it just receives a letter, weighs it, puts a basic stamp, and forwards it. It does **not** decide international routes, calculate postage matrices, or maintain customer databases.
- The **head office (Remote Node)** does *all the complex work* — it maintains the routing tables, handles disputes, processes international dispatches, and runs the analytics engine.

If we reverse this (Complex Local, Simple Remote), every tiny branch office would need its own powerful computer, trained staff, and database — a recipe for disaster, cost, and unreliability. The same logic drives LSRC: keep field hardware dumb, cheap, robust, and let the heavy-lifting happen on a powerful, easily-upgradable remote platform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Metrics in LSRC:**
> - Local Processing Power → **low** (e.g., 8-bit / 32-bit microcontrollers)
> - Remote Processing Power → **high** (e.g., multi-core CPUs, GPUs, cloud)
> - Network Bandwidth Demand → **minimized** through on-node pre-filtering
> - Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of local nodes → **high** (simple = reliable)
> - Latency budget tolerated at remote tier → **larger** (predictable, scheduled)

### 1.3 Why LSRC is a Pillar of Modern NCS

The proliferation of **IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things)**, **cyber-physical systems**, and **cloud-based SCADA** has made LSRC more relevant than ever. The principle aligns naturally with **edge-cloud computing paradigms** where edge devices (local) act as thin clients and the cloud (remote) acts as the brain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Decomposition Under LSRC

#### 2.1.1 Functions Allocated to the **LOCAL (Simple)** Tier

- **Signal acquisition** — sampling the physical process variable $y(t)$ at period $h$.
- **First-order digital filtering** — low-pass noise rejection (e.g., exponential smoothing):
$$y_f[k] = \alpha \, y[k] + (1-\alpha)\, y_f[k-1]$$
- **Thresholding & event detection** — generating an alarm only when a variable exceeds bounds.
- **Actuator command execution** — translating a digital set-point $u[k]$ into a physical stimulus.
- **Basic health-ping** — transmitting "I am alive" beacons.
- **Local fail-safe logic** — emergency shutdown if communication with remote node is lost for more than $T_{watchdog}$ seconds.

#### 2.1.2 Functions Allocated to the **REMOTE (Complex)** Tier

- **State estimation** — Kalman filter, Luenberger observer, particle filter.
- **Multivariable control law computation** — MPC, $H_\infty$, fuzzy, neural-network based.
- **Optimization** — solving quadratic programs online (e.g., model predictive control).
- **Fault detection & isolation (FDI)** — parity checks, statistical residuals.
- **Coordination of multiple local nodes** — supervisory loop, consensus algorithms.
- **Data logging, analytics, and long-term storage.**

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind LSRC — Five Engineering Drivers

1. **Cost Minimization** — Cheap 32-bit MCUs at the field instead of powerful processors.
2. **Reliability** — Simple local firmware has fewer bugs and longer MTBF.
3. **Maintainability** — Upgrading the "brain" requires no physical visit to the plant.
4. **Scalability** — Adding new local nodes does not require duplicating expensive control infrastructure.
5. **Network Economy** — Only compressed, essential, or event-triggered packets traverse the network.

### 2.3 The 'How' — Communication Flow Under LSRC

The canonical LSRC data flow is **upward (telemetry)** and **downward (commands)**. The local node transmits only **what is necessary** (compressed or event-driven data), and the remote node transmits only **control set-points**, not raw algorithms.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the table below as a single-glance revision for any LSRC numerical or conceptual problem in the KTU ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|----------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Local sampling period | $h = T_s$ | seconds | Sets the rate at which the local node wakes up |
| 2 | Network-induced delay | $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ | seconds | Sensor-to-controller + controller-to-actuator |
| 3 | Total round-trip latency budget | $\tau_{total} \le \tau_{max}$ | seconds | Upper bound for stability |
| 4 | Event-triggered transmission rate | $f_{ET} = \dfrac{1}{h_{ET}}$ | Hz | Only send when $\vert y[k] - y[k-1] \vert > \delta$ |
| 5 | Bandwidth requirement (periodic) | $BW = \dfrac{N_b \cdot f_s}{R_{codec}}$ | bits/second | Raw NCS bandwidth if no compression |
| 6 | Bandwidth requirement (LSRC, compressed) | $BW_{LSRC} = r_c \cdot BW, \quad 0 < r_c < 1$ | bits/second | Achieved compression ratio |
| 7 | Local pre-filter smoothing | $y_f[k] = \alpha y[k] + (1-\alpha) y_f[k-1]$ | — | Removes high-freq noise before transmission |
| 8 | Effective closed-loop delay | $\tau_{eff} = h/2 + \tau$ | seconds | Used in stability margins |
| 9 | Maximum allowable delay (Liu et al.) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\arctan(\omega \sqrt{1-\xi^2}/\xi)}{\omega \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$ | seconds | For 2nd-order plant with damping $\xi$ |
| 10 | Watchdog time for fail-safe | $T_{wd} = K \cdot h, \quad K \in \mathbb{N}$ | seconds | Time before local node goes autonomous |
| 11 | Information age (AoI) | $\Delta(t) = t - u(t)$ | seconds | Freshness of last received packet at remote |
| 12 | Remote computation time | $t_c = f(\text{algorithm complexity})$ | seconds | Time for MPC, optimization, etc. |

> **Notation guard:** All absolute-value bars in formulas above are typed as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

LSRC underpins **smart-grid SCADA**, **autonomous-vehicle fleet management** (edge sensors → cloud planner), **remote surgical robotics** (local motors → surgeon console), and **water-distribution telemetry** (cheap RTUs → central SCADA).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How LSRC Reduces Bandwidth Requirement

**Problem setup.** Suppose a local node samples a process variable at $f_s = 100$ Hz using a 16-bit ADC, and transmits uncompressed. The naive bandwidth demand is:

$$BW_{raw} = N_b \cdot f_s$$

**Step 1.** Substitute the numerical values.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{raw} &= 16 \text{ bits} \times 100 \text{ samples/s} \\
&= 1600 \text{ bits/s} \\
&= 1.6 \text{ kbps per channel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Introduce the LSRC design choice: the local node applies a **first-order exponential filter** with smoothing factor $\alpha$ and a **dead-band quantizer** with threshold $\delta$. Only changes exceeding $\delta$ are transmitted (event-triggered paradigm).

$$
y_{tx}[k] = \begin{cases} y_f[k], & \text{if } \vert y_f[k] - y_f[k-1] \vert > \delta \\ \text{no transmission}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3.** Let the event-triggered transmission rate be $f_{ET} \ll f_s$. Define the **LSRC compression ratio** $r_c = f_{ET}/f_s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{LSRC} &= r_c \cdot BW_{raw} \\
&= r_c \cdot N_b \cdot f_s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** Numerical example. Assume $\delta$ is tuned so that only 10% of samples are transmitted: $r_c = 0.1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BW_{LSRC} &= 0.1 \times 1.6 \text{ kbps} \\
&= 0.16 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion.** LSRC achieves a **10× bandwidth reduction** for this channel. For an NCS with $N = 50$ channels, the total saving is $50 \times 1.44 = 72$ kbps, which is critical on shared wireless industrial links.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Tolerable Network Delay Under LSRC

**Setup.** Consider a 2nd-order plant with natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\xi$, controlled over an NCS. The maximum delay before instability is given by the classical **Liu et al. (2006) bound**:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}{\xi}\right)}{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}
$$

**Step 1.** Let $\omega_n = 2$ rad/s and $\xi = 0.7$ (a well-damped process).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{1 - \xi^2} &= \sqrt{1 - 0.49} = \sqrt{0.51} \approx 0.7141 \\
\omega_n \sqrt{1 - \xi^2} &= 2 \times 0.7141 = 1.4282 \\
\frac{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}{\xi} &= \frac{1.4282}{0.7} = 2.0403 \\
\arctan(2.0403) &\approx 1.1138 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the bound.

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{1.1138}{1.4282} \approx 0.780 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3.** Apply the LSRC delay decomposition.

$$
\tau_{total} = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} + t_c
$$

- $\tau_{sc}$ = sensor → remote controller
- $\tau_{ca}$ = remote controller → actuator
- $t_c$ = remote computation time

**Step 4.** Allocation under LSRC: because the complex algorithm runs remotely, $t_c$ is **non-negligible** (e.g., MPC solve time). The LSRC designer must budget for it.

$$
t_c \le \tau_{max} - (\tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca})
$$

If $\tau_{sc} = \tau_{ca} = 0.2$ s, then $t_c \le 0.780 - 0.4 = 0.380$ s — the remote solver must finish in 380 ms.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Local vs Remote Allocation

| Concern | Local (Simple) Node | Remote (Complex) Node |
|---------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Sampling | $h = 1/f_s$ small, high-rate | Optional, may run at slower supervisory rate |
| Computation | Linear ops only: add, multiply, filter | Nonlinear optimization, matrix inversion |
| Memory | KB-level flash/RAM | GB to TB |
| Power | Battery / harvested energy acceptable | Mains-powered, UPS-backed |
| Network role | Producer of filtered telemetry | Consumer of telemetry + producer of set-points |
| Update cadence | High (e.g., 100 Hz) | Lower (e.g., 1–10 Hz for supervisory) |
| Failure impact | Loss of one field reading | Loss of entire plant (single point of failure) |
| Security | Physical tamper resistance | Cyber-firewall, intrusion detection |
| Cost per node | < \$50 typically | \$ thousands (server) shared across $N$ nodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

- All node IDs are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `localNodeA`, `remoteNodeB`).
- All labels are double-quoted with raw uppercase/lowercase text only.
- No markdown bold/italic or HTML tables inside labels.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LSRC Networked Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    plantA["Physical Plant Process Variable y of t"]
    localA["Local Node 1 Sensor MCU Simple Sampling and Filter"]
    localB["Local Node 2 Actuator MCU Simple Command Execution"]
    localC["Local Node 3 Sensor MCU Threshold and Event Detect"]
    netA["Network Layer CAN or Ethernet or Wireless"]
    remA["Remote Controller Complex MPC or H Infinity Solver"]
    remB["Remote Estimator Kalman Filter and FDI"]
    remC["Remote Supervisor Coordination and Logging"]
    remD["Remote HMI Dashboard and Analytics"]

    plantA --> localA
    plantA --> localC
    localA --> netA
    localC --> netA
    netA --> remA
    netA --> remB
    remA --> netA
    remB --> netA
    netA --> localB
    remA --> remC
    remB --> remC
    remC --> remD
```

### 4.3 Data-Flow vs Decision-Flow View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOCAL_TIER["LOCAL TIER SIMPLE TIER"]
        s1["Sample y of t at h"]
        s2["Filter y_f of k equals alpha y plus 1 minus alpha y_f of k minus 1"]
        s3["Deadband Decision transmit if delta exceeded"]
        s4["Actuator Apply u of k"]
    end

    subgraph NETWORK["NETWORK"]
        n1["Upward Telemetry Compressed or Event Driven"]
        n2["Downward Setpoint u of k"]
    end

    subgraph REMOTE_TIER["REMOTE TIER COMPLEX TIER"]
        r1["State Estimation Kalman"]
        r2["Control Law MPC or PID Plus Gain Sched"]
        r3["FDI Residual Analysis"]
        r4["Coordinator Multi Node Sync"]
    end

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> n1 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> n2 --> s4
    n1 --> r3
    r1 --> r4
```

### 4.4 Trade-off Triangle in LSRC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cost["Cost per Local Node LOW"]
    bw["Network Bandwidth MINIMIZED"]
    compl["Remote Algorithm Complexity HIGH"]

    cost --- bw
    bw --- compl
    compl --- cost

    optLSRC["LSRC Equilibrium Point"]
    cost -.-> optLSRC
    bw -.-> optLSRC
    compl -.-> optLSRC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Define the LSRC design principle for a Networked Control System. List any four functions that are typically delegated to the remote (complex) node.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
LSRC stands for *Local Simple, Remote Complex*. It is a design philosophy that pushes the computational and intelligence burden away from the field-level sensor/actuator nodes (kept simple) to a higher-tier supervisory or server node (made complex). Local nodes perform only elementary tasks (sampling, basic filtering, actuation), while the remote node handles state estimation, multivariable control laws, optimization, and fault diagnosis. **Four functions at the remote node:** (i) Kalman-filter based state estimation, (ii) Model Predictive Control law computation, (iii) Fault Detection and Isolation using residual analysis, (iv) Multi-node coordination and long-term data logging.

---

**Q2. Why is the LSRC principle especially attractive for wireless and bandwidth-constrained NCS implementations? Justify in three points.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
(i) **Bandwidth economy** — by transmitting only filtered, threshold-triggered or compressed data, the radio channel is used sparingly, which is critical on shared wireless industrial links. (ii) **Energy economy at the field node** — a simple local MCU spends less time computing and transmitting, prolonging battery life. (iii) **Centralized upgrades** — improvements to the control law, estimator, or security patches can be deployed at the remote node without re-visiting the field, reducing maintenance cost and downtime.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Local Simple, Remote Complex (LSRC)** design principle for a Networked Control System. Clearly differentiate the roles of the local and remote tiers. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A wireless NCS samples a process variable at $f_s = 200$ Hz using a 12-bit ADC. Under LSRC, only 8% of the samples are transmitted (event-triggered) and a 2:1 additional compression is applied. Compute (i) the raw bandwidth, (ii) the LSRC bandwidth, and (iii) the percentage saving. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** LSRC is a hierarchical design philosophy that minimizes local-node complexity and centralizes sophisticated control, estimation, and diagnostic functions at a remote station.
- **Block diagram (3 marks):** Plant → [Local Sensor MCU: Sample, Filter, Threshold] → Network → [Remote Controller: Estimator + Control Law + FDI + Coordinator] → Network → [Local Actuator MCU: Execute u(k)].
- **Differentiation (2 marks):** Local = simple, low-power, deterministic, failsafe; Remote = complex, scalable, easily upgradable, shared across N nodes.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Raw bandwidth.

$$
BW_{raw} = N_b \cdot f_s = 12 \times 200 = 2400 \text{ bits/s} = 2.4 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** After event-triggering with $r_{ET} = 0.08$:

$$
BW_{ET} = 0.08 \times 2400 = 192 \text{ bits/s}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** After 2:1 compression ($r_c = 0.5$):

$$
BW_{LSRC} = 0.5 \times 192 = 96 \text{ bits/s} = 0.096 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Percentage saving.

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{BW_{raw} - BW_{LSRC}}{BW_{raw}} \times 100\% = \frac{2400 - 96}{2400} \times 100\% = 96\%
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** Discuss the **five engineering drivers** that motivate the adoption of LSRC in modern industrial NCS, with one-line justification for each. `[7 Marks]`

**(b)** A 2nd-order plant with $\omega_n = 1.5$ rad/s and $\xi = 0.6$ is controlled over a network where $\tau_{sc} = 0.15$ s and $\tau_{ca} = 0.20$ s. Using Liu et al.'s bound, determine the **maximum allowable remote computation time** $t_c^{max}$ that keeps the closed loop stable. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution (a):** Five drivers (1.4 marks each, totaling 7):

1. **Cost minimization** — cheap local MCUs replace expensive field PLCs.
2. **Reliability** — simple firmware = longer MTBF at the field.
3. **Maintainability** — remote upgrades avoid plant shutdowns.
4. **Scalability** — adding nodes does not duplicate expensive control hardware.
5. **Network economy** — filtered/event-driven traffic reduces bandwidth and energy.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute $\sqrt{1 - \xi^2}$.

$$
\sqrt{1 - 0.36} = \sqrt{0.64} = 0.8
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute numerator argument of arctan.

$$
\frac{\omega_n \sqrt{1-\xi^2}}{\xi} = \frac{1.5 \times 0.8}{0.6} = \frac{1.2}{0.6} = 2.0
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** $\arctan(2.0) \approx 1.1071$ rad.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $\tau_{max}$.

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{1.1071}{1.5 \times 0.8} = \frac{1.1071}{1.2} \approx 0.9226 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Solve for $t_c^{max}$.

$$
t_c^{max} = \tau_{max} - (\tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}) = 0.9226 - (0.15 + 0.20) = 0.5726 \text{ s}
$$

**Conclusion:** The remote controller must finish its computation (e.g., MPC solve) within **≈ 573 ms** to preserve closed-loop stability.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on LSRC questions:**
> 1. **Confusing 'Local' with 'Local Controller'** — in LSRC, the local node is the sensor/actuator node, not a local feedback loop. Writing "the local PID runs on the field" is incorrect under pure LSRC.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract the communication delays** when applying Liu et al.'s bound; the bound is on **total** delay $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sc} + t_c + \tau_{ca}$.
> 3. **Mixing units** — bandwidth must be reported in bps or kbps consistently; $f_s$ in Hz.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in 7-mark questions — a missing diagram costs at least 1–2 marks even if the prose is correct.
> 5. **Claiming "LSRC eliminates delay"** — it does not. It *centralizes* delay to a predictable remote site; the delay still exists and must be budgeted.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LSRC = Local Simple, Remote Complex** — a *design philosophy*, not a theorem.
- **Local tier** owns: sampling, pre-filtering, thresholding, actuation, watchdog fail-safe.
- **Remote tier** owns: state estimation, advanced control law (MPC/$H_\infty$), FDI, coordination, logging.
- **Five drivers:** cost, reliability, maintainability, scalability, bandwidth/energy economy.
- **Key formula:** $BW_{LSRC} = r_c \cdot r_{ET} \cdot N_b \cdot f_s$.
- **Stability bound (Liu et al.):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\arctan(\omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}/\xi)}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\xi^2}}$.
- **Delay budget identity:** $\tau_{total} = \tau_{sc} + t_c + \tau_{ca} \le \tau_{max}$.
- **Trade-off triangle:** cost ↓, bandwidth ↓, remote complexity ↑ — these three goals converge at the LSRC equilibrium.
- **Real-world alignment:** LSRC maps to **edge-cloud computing**, **IIoT**, **smart-grid SCADA**, **cloud robotics**.
- **Watchdog parameter:** $T_{wd} = K \cdot h$ ensures the local node enters autonomous fail-safe if the remote link drops.
- **AoI (Age of Information):** a vital freshness metric in LSRC, $\Delta(t) = t - u(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modular Construction and Learning Algorithms of Neuro-fuzzy Networks for LSRC Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modular Construction and Learning Algorithms of Neuro-fuzzy Networks for LSRC Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Neuro-fuzzy Networks

A **Neuro-fuzzy Network** is a hybrid intelligent system that synergistically integrates the **qualitative reasoning capability of fuzzy logic** with the **quantitative learning capability of artificial neural networks (ANN)**. The fusion is realized by translating a fuzzy inference system (FIS) into a connectionist, layered architecture whose connection weights (parameters) are tunable through data-driven learning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT862 / Module 2):**
> A neuro-fuzzy network is a multilayer feedforward structure whose nodes perform **fuzzification**, **antecedent conjunction (T-norm)**, **rule activation**, **consequent evaluation**, and **defuzzification** — with all membership-function and consequent parameters adapted via **gradient-based and/or least-squares-based learning**.

For **LSRC (Least-Squares-based Regression/Classification)** implementation, the network is a *linear-in-parameters* model, meaning the output is a **linear function of the consequent parameters** for fixed premise (membership) parameters. This linearity is the structural enabler that permits **closed-form least-squares identification** of consequent weights in a single batch or recursive pass.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Adjustable Expert Committee"

Imagine a **panel of domain experts** (the fuzzy rules) advising a plant operator. Each expert has two parts to their advice:

- A **trigger condition** (the *premise*, e.g., "if temperature is HIGH and pressure is MODERATE") — this is like the expert's *specialty*.
- A **recommended action** (the *consequent*, e.g., "then valve opening ≈ 60%") — this is the expert's *best guess*.

Initially, every expert is a **novice**: their trigger sensitivities and recommended actions are inaccurate. Neuro-fuzzy learning is the process of:

1. Letting the experts **observe historical process data** (supervised learning).
2. **Tuning their sensitivities** (membership function shape) using gradient descent.
3. **Tuning their recommended actions** (consequent weights) using **least-squares regression/classification (LSRC)**.

The **modular construction** means the experts are organized into **independent, replaceable modules** (e.g., one module per rule, or one module per operating regime). Each module can be trained, validated, and even swapped out without disturbing the rest of the network — a property highly valued in **networked control systems** where subsystems are added/removed dynamically.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Synergy:**
> - Fuzzy logic → interpretability, prior expert knowledge embedding, handling of linguistic uncertainty.
> - Neural networks → adaptive parameter tuning, universal approximation, fault tolerance.
> - LSRC → fast, statistically optimal estimation of linear-in-parameter consequents in **one shot** (batch) or **recursively** (RLS).

### 1.3 Standard Architectural Families Used for LSRC

| Family | Acronym | Key Feature | Use in LSRC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System | **ANFIS** | 5–6 layer, Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK) | Most common for LSRC |
| Fuzzy Adaptive Learning Control Network | **FALCON** | 5 layer, dual networks (condition + action) | Classification extensions |
| Generalized Approximate Reasoning IC | **GARIC** | Reinforcement learning variant | Indirect LSRC |
| Self-Organizing Neural Fuzzy Inference Net | **SONFIN** | Online, growing structure | Online recursive LSRC |
| Neuro-Fuzzy Classification | **NEFCLASS** | Pattern classification | LSRC for class boundaries |

### 1.4 Why LSRC? — The Mathematical Justification

In a **first-order Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK)** neuro-fuzzy system with $R$ rules and $n$ inputs $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$, the output is

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{R}\, w_r(\mathbf{x})\,\bigl(\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r\bigr)}{\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{R}\, w_r(\mathbf{x})}$$

For **fixed** membership parameters (and thus fixed $w_r(\mathbf{x})$), the numerator is a **linear function** of the consequent parameters $\theta = \{\mathbf{a}_r, b_r\}_{r=1}^{R}$. LSRC exploits this linearity to deliver the **optimal unbiased estimator** under additive white Gaussian noise (Wiener–Kolmogorov condition).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modular Construction — Layered Block Decomposition

A neuro-fuzzy network for LSRC is decomposed into **five functional modules**, each with a clearly defined I/O contract.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The five modules are:**
> 1. **M1 – Input / Fuzzification Module:** Maps crisp $x_i$ to membership degrees $\mu_{A_{ij}}(x_i)$.
> 2. **M2 – Rule Antecedent (T-norm) Module:** Computes rule firing strength $w_r(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i}\, \mu_{A_{ir}}(x_i)$.
> 3. **M3 – Consequent (Linear / Classification) Module:** Computes per-rule output $\hat{y}_r = \mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r$ (regression) or $\hat{y}_r = \mathrm{sign}(\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r)$ (classification).
> 4. **M4 – Normalization Module:** Computes normalized firing strength $\bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x}) = w_r(\mathbf{x}) / \sum_{k} w_k(\mathbf{x})$.
> 5. **M5 – Aggregation / Defuzzification Module:** Combines $\hat{y}_r$ using $\bar{w}_r$ to produce $f(\mathbf{x})$.

#### 2.1.1 Mathematical Per-Module Specification

**Module M1 (Fuzzification).** For Gaussian membership functions (the most common in LSRC implementations due to differentiability),

$$\mu_{A_{ij}}(x_i) \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{1}{2}\,\frac{(x_i - c_{ij})^{2}}{\sigma_{ij}^{2}}\right)$$

where $c_{ij}$ and $\sigma_{ij}$ are the **center** and **width** of the $j$-th membership function for input $i$.

**Module M2 (T-norm / Product).** Using the product T-norm,

$$w_r(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n}\, \mu_{A_{ir}}(x_i)$$

**Module M3 (Consequent).** For regression,

$$\hat{y}_r(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\, a_{ir}\,x_i \,+\, b_r \;=\; \boldsymbol{\theta}_r^{T}\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$$

with the augmented input $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = [x_1,\ldots,x_n, 1]^{T}$ and $\boldsymbol{\theta}_r = [a_{1r},\ldots,a_{nr}, b_r]^{T}$.

**Module M4 (Normalization).**

$$\bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{w_r(\mathbf{x})}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{R}\, w_k(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Module M5 (Aggregation).**

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{R}\, \bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x})\,\hat{y}_r(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \sum_{r=1}^{R}\, \bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x})\,\boldsymbol{\theta}_r^{T}\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$$

### 2.2 Why "Modular"? — The Engineering Rationale

- **Plug-and-play reusability:** A trained module for one operating regime can be **stored, transmitted over a network, and re-instantiated** at a remote controller — vital in **networked/cloud control**.
- **Fault isolation:** Corruption of one module's parameters (e.g., due to packet drops) degrades only the associated rule, not the entire network.
- **Incremental learning:** New operating data can train a new module and add it to the ensemble without retraining older modules (true of growing SONFIN-type networks).
- **Parallelism:** Each module is computationally independent, enabling FPGA / GPU / distributed implementation.

### 2.3 Learning Algorithms — The Hybrid Two-Phase Strategy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cornerstone insight for KTU 2024:**
> Neuro-fuzzy networks are **bilinear** in parameters — nonlinear in *premise* (membership) parameters and linear in *consequent* parameters. The optimal strategy is the **Hybrid Learning Rule (HLR)**:
> - **Forward pass (Layer 4 → 5):** Identify consequent parameters by **LSRC** (closed-form).
> - **Backward pass (Layer 1 → 2):** Identify premise parameters by **Gradient Descent (GD) / Backpropagation**.

#### 2.3.1 Least-Squares Identification of Consequents (Batch LSRC)

Given $N$ I/O training pairs $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}, y^{(k)})\}_{k=1}^{N}$, the TSK network output can be written **column-wise** as

$$\mathbf{y} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\boldsymbol{\theta} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}$$

where:

- $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ is the target output vector.
- $\boldsymbol{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}$ stacks all consequent parameters; $M = R(n+1)$.
- $\boldsymbol{\Phi} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times M}$ is the **regression matrix** whose $k$-th row is
$$ \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)} \;=\; \bigl[\,\bar{w}_1(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(k)T},\; \bar{w}_2(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(k)T},\; \ldots,\; \bar{w}_R(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})\,\tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(k)T}\,\bigr] $$
- $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}$ is the residual (assumed zero-mean i.i.d. Gaussian).

The **LSRC estimator** is

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\mathrm{LS}} \;=\; \bigl(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\bigr)^{-1}\,\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}\mathbf{y}$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{\dagger}$ is the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse**. This is the **batch / one-shot** solution.

#### 2.3.2 Recursive Least Squares (RLS) for Online LSRC

For **real-time networked control**, batch LSRC is infeasible (data arrives sequentially). Define the exponentially weighted cost:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}, k) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k}\, \lambda^{k-j}\,\bigl(y^{(j)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)}\boldsymbol{\theta}\bigr)^{2}$$

where $\lambda \in (0,1]$ is the **forgetting factor** (typically $0.95 \le \lambda \le 0.999$).

The RLS updates are:

$$\mathbf{P}(k) \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[\mathbf{P}(k-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(k-1)\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\,\mathbf{P}(k-1)}{\lambda + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\,\mathbf{P}(k-1)\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}}\right]$$

$$\mathbf{L}(k) \;=\; \mathbf{P}(k)\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}$$

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) \;=\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1) + \mathbf{L}(k)\,\bigl(y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1)\bigr)$$

where $\mathbf{P}(k)$ is the **covariance matrix** and $\mathbf{L}(k)$ the **Kalman gain**. Initialisation: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(0) = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{P}(0) = \alpha \mathbf{I}$ with $\alpha \in [10^{3}, 10^{6}]$.

#### 2.3.3 Gradient Descent on Premise Parameters

With the consequent parameters fixed by LSRC, the premise parameters (e.g., $c_{ij}$ and $\sigma_{ij}$) are updated by minimising the squared error

$$E \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(y - f(\mathbf{x}))^{2}$$

using

$$c_{ij}^{\mathrm{new}} \;=\; c_{ij} - \eta_{c}\,\frac{\partial E}{\partial c_{ij}}, \qquad \sigma_{ij}^{\mathrm{new}} \;=\; \sigma_{ij} - \eta_{\sigma}\,\frac{\partial E}{\partial \sigma_{ij}}$$

where $\eta_c, \eta_{\sigma} > 0$ are learning rates. The gradient chain-rule expansion is:

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial c_{ij}} \;=\; -(y - f)\,\frac{\partial f}{\partial w_r}\,\frac{\partial w_r}{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}\,\frac{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}{\partial c_{ij}}$$

For Gaussian membership:

$$\frac{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}{\partial c_{ij}} \;=\; \mu_{A_{ij}}\,\frac{(x_i - c_{ij})}{\sigma_{ij}^{2}}, \qquad \frac{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}{\partial \sigma_{ij}} \;=\; \mu_{A_{ij}}\,\frac{(x_i - c_{ij})^{2}}{\sigma_{ij}^{3}}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables / Units | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gaussian MF | $\mu_{A}(x) = \exp\!\bigl(-\tfrac{1}{2}\tfrac{(x-c)^{2}}{\sigma^{2}}\bigr)$ | $c$: center, $\sigma$: width | Differentiable → enables GD |
| 2 | Product T-norm | $w_r = \prod_{i} \mu_{A_{ir}}(x_i)$ | $w_r \in [0,1]$ | Differentiable, simpler than min |
| 3 | TSK aggregation | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{r=1}^{R} \bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x})\,(\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x}+b_r)$ | $\bar{w}_r$: normalized firing | Linear-in-consequents |
| 4 | Batch LSRC | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times M}$ | Optimal under i.i.d. Gaussian noise |
| 5 | RLS covariance update | $\mathbf{P}(k) = \tfrac{1}{\lambda}\bigl[\mathbf{P}(k\!-\!1) - \tfrac{\mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\phi}\mathbf{P}}{\lambda+\boldsymbol{\phi}\mathbf{P}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{T}}\bigr]$ | $\lambda\in(0,1]$ | $\lambda < 1$ → forgetting |
| 6 | RLS parameter update | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) = \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k\!-\!1) + \mathbf{P}(k)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}(y^{(k)}\!-\!\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k\!-\!1))$ | Kalman gain implicit | Online, $O(M^{2})$ |
| 7 | Premise GD | $c_{ij}^{\mathrm{new}} = c_{ij} - \eta_c\,\frac{(x_i-c_{ij})}{\sigma_{ij}^{2}}\,\mu_{A_{ij}}\,\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_r}$ | $\eta_c > 0$ | Second-order (chain rule) |
| 8 | Condition number (stability) | $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}) < 10^{6}$ | Dimensionless | Above → use ridge regression |
| 9 | Regularized LSRC (ridge) | $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi} + \rho \mathbf{I})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ | $\rho > 0$ | Tikhonov regularization for noisy data |
| 10 | Generalization bound (PAC) | $\mathrm{Gen} \le \sqrt{\tfrac{2\sigma^{2}\ln(2/\delta)}{N}} + \sqrt{\tfrac{M\ln(2N/M)+\ln(2/\delta)}{N}}$ | $\delta$: confidence | Links training-error to test-error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:**
> - **Chemical process control** → pH / distillation column control (Teppola & Minkkinen).
> - **Automotive** → engine idle-speed and air-fuel ratio control.
> - **Robotics** → inverse kinematics learning and compliant control.
> - **Networked/Cloud control** → remote adaptive control where the *training* runs in the cloud and only the *module* is transmitted to the edge.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Closed-Form LSRC for Consequent Parameters

**Statement:** For a TSK neuro-fuzzy network with fixed premise parameters, the consequent parameter vector $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ that minimises the sum of squared errors is unique and given by the normal-equation solution.

**Step 1 — Define the scalar error and aggregate cost.**

The instantaneous squared error for the $k$-th data point is

$$e^{(k)} \;=\; y^{(k)} - f(\mathbf{x}^{(k)}) \;=\; y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\boldsymbol{\theta}$$

The aggregate cost over $N$ points is

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\, \bigl(e^{(k)}\bigr)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\,\bigl(y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\boldsymbol{\theta}\bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the cost in compact matrix form.**

Let $\mathbf{y} = [y^{(1)},\ldots,y^{(N)}]^{T}\in\mathbb{R}^{N}$ and $\boldsymbol{\Phi} = [\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(1)T};\ldots;\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(N)T}]\in\mathbb{R}^{N\times M}$. Then

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic form.**

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\bigl(\mathbf{y}^{T}\mathbf{y} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\theta}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta}\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ and set to zero (stationarity).**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; -\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta} \;\stackrel{!}{=}\; \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the normal equations.**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\,\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

This is the **ordinary least-squares (OLS)** or **LSRC** estimator.

**Step 6 — Verify it is a global minimum (Hessian positive-definite).**

$$\frac{\partial^{2} J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}^{2}} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi} \;\succ\; 0 \quad\text{when } \boldsymbol{\Phi}\text{ has full column rank.}$$

Hence the stationary point is the **unique global minimum**.

**Step 7 — Properties of the estimator (Gauss–Markov).**

Under $\mathbb{E}[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}] = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathrm{Cov}(\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}) = \sigma^{2}\mathbf{I}$:

- $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}] = \boldsymbol{\theta}$ (unbiased).
- $\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}) = \sigma^{2}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}$ (minimum variance among linear unbiased estimators).

### 3.2 Derivation II — RLS Recursion from Windowed LSRC

**Step 1 — Cost at step $k$.**

$$J_k(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k}\, \lambda^{k-j}\,\bigl(y^{(j)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)}\boldsymbol{\theta}\bigr)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Define the weighted normal-equations matrix.**

$$\mathbf{R}(k) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k}\, \lambda^{k-j}\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)}, \qquad \mathbf{r}(k) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k}\, \lambda^{k-j}\,\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)T} y^{(j)}$$

**Step 3 — Recursive update of $\mathbf{R}(k)$.**

$$\mathbf{R}(k) \;=\; \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(k-1) + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}$$

**Step 4 — Identify $\mathbf{P}(k) = \mathbf{R}(k)^{-1}$ and apply the matrix-inversion lemma.**

For $\mathbf{R}(k) = \lambda \mathbf{R}(k-1) + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}$, the Woodbury identity gives

$$\mathbf{P}(k) \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[\mathbf{P}(k-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(k-1)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\mathbf{P}(k-1)}{\lambda + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\mathbf{P}(k-1)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Derive the parameter update.**

From $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) = \mathbf{R}(k)^{-1}\mathbf{r}(k) = \mathbf{P}(k)\mathbf{r}(k)$, expanding $\mathbf{r}(k) = \lambda \mathbf{r}(k-1) + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T} y^{(k)}$ and using $\mathbf{P}(k)\lambda \mathbf{r}(k-1) = \mathbf{P}(k)\lambda \mathbf{R}(k-1)\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1) = (I - \mathbf{P}(k)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)})\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1)$ (by the matrix-inversion lemma), one obtains the canonical RLS update:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) \;=\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1) + \mathbf{P}(k)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\,\bigl(y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1)\bigr)$$

**Step 6 — Computational complexity.**

Each RLS step costs $O(M^{2})$ operations; this is the basis of the well-known trade-off **LSRC $\Leftrightarrow$ LMS**: faster convergence but higher per-step cost.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Gradient of Cost w.r.t. Premise Parameters (Gaussian MF)

**Step 1 — Total error derivative (chain rule).**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial c_{ij}} \;=\; \frac{\partial E}{\partial f}\,\frac{\partial f}{\partial w_r}\,\frac{\partial w_r}{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}\,\frac{\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}}{\partial c_{ij}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute each factor explicitly.**

$\partial E/\partial f = -(y - f)$.

$\partial f/\partial w_r = (\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r - f) / \sum_k w_k$ (this follows from differentiating the weighted average).

$\partial w_r/\partial \mu_{A_{ij}} = \prod_{i'\neq i}\, \mu_{A_{i'r}} = w_r / \mu_{A_{ij}}$ (since $w_r$ is a product over $i$).

$\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}/\partial c_{ij} = \mu_{A_{ij}}\,(x_i - c_{ij})/\sigma_{ij}^{2}$ (differentiating the Gaussian).

**Step 3 — Compose.**

$$\frac{\partial E}{\partial c_{ij}} \;=\; -(y - f)\,\frac{(\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r - f)}{\sum_k w_k}\,w_r\,(x_i - c_{ij})/\sigma_{ij}^{2}$$

This is the gradient signal that drives the backward-pass GD update of $c_{ij}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hybrid-learning termination criterion (KTU 2024):**
> Training ends when either (i) $\|E(t) - E(t-1)\| < \epsilon$ (absolute error tolerance), (ii) a maximum epoch count is reached, or (iii) $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})$ exceeds a threshold and ridge regularisation must be engaged.

### 3.4 Numerical Mini-Example (Illustrative, KTU-style)

Consider a 2-rule, 1-input, 1-output TSK network trained on three points: $(0,0)$, $(1,1)$, $(2,4)$. With membership centres $c_1 = 0, c_2 = 2$, width $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = 1$, and **fixed** premise parameters, compute the LSRC estimate of $(\mathbf{a}_1, b_1, \mathbf{a}_2, b_2)$.

**Step 1 — Firing strengths at each point.**

At $x=0$: $\mu_{A_1} = e^{0} = 1$, $\mu_{A_2} = e^{-2} \approx 0.1353$, so $w_1 = 1, w_2 = 0.1353$, $\sum w = 1.1353$, $\bar{w}_1 \approx 0.8808, \bar{w}_2 \approx 0.1192$.

At $x=1$: $\mu_{A_1} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065, \mu_{A_2} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$, so $w_1 = w_2 = 0.3679$, $\bar{w}_1 = \bar{w}_2 = 0.5$.

At $x=2$: symmetric to $x=0$ with $w_1 = 0.1353, w_2 = 1$, $\bar{w}_1 \approx 0.1192, \bar{w}_2 \approx 0.8808$.

**Step 2 — Build the regression matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$.**

Each row $k$ is $[\bar{w}_1^{(k)} x^{(k)}, \bar{w}_1^{(k)}, \bar{w}_2^{(k)} x^{(k)}, \bar{w}_2^{(k)}]$:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0.8808 & 0 & 0.1192 \\ 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 0.5 \\ 0.2384 & 0.1192 & 1.7616 & 0.8808 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Solve $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.**

Carrying out the multiplication (delegated as a standard KTU calculator exercise):

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.3068 & 0.1792 & 1.0054 & 0.5027 \\ 0.1792 & 1.1256 & 0.5027 & 0.8808 \\ 1.0054 & 0.5027 & 3.2018 & 1.6009 \\ 0.5027 & 0.8808 & 1.6009 & 0.8724 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 1.4544 \\ 1.1196 \\ 7.5408 \\ 4.0080 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving (e.g., via Gaussian elimination) yields approximately

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \;\approx\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.96 \\ 0.00 \\ 1.94 \\ 0.00 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The LSRC has recovered the underlying quadratic $y = x^{2}$ as a *piecewise linear* approximation: rule 1 (active near $x=0$) returns $\approx 0.96 x$ (slope of $x^{2}$ at $x=0$ would be $0$, but the data drives a small positive slope), rule 2 (active near $x=2$) returns $\approx 1.94 x$ (slope of $x^{2}$ at $x=2$ is $4$, averaged with neighbouring region). With more rules and gradient-descent refinement of $c_1, c_2$, the network would converge to a near-perfect fit.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Modular Construction of a TSK Neuro-fuzzy Network (ANFIS-style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[Input x1] --> M1A[M1A Fuzzifier MF 1A]
    X1 --> M1B[M1B Fuzzifier MF 1B]
    X2[Input x2] --> M2A[M2A Fuzzifier MF 2A]
    X2 --> M2B[M2B Fuzzifier MF 2B]
    M1A --> R1Node[Rule R1 T norm product]
    M1B --> R2Node[Rule R2 T norm product]
    M2A --> R1Node
    M2B --> R2Node
    R1Node --> W1[Normalized firing w1 bar]
    R2Node --> W2[Normalized firing w2 bar]
    W1 --> A1[Consequent linear a1T x plus b1]
    W2 --> A2[Consequent linear a2T x plus b2]
    X1 --> A1
    X2 --> A1
    X1 --> A2
    X2 --> A2
    A1 --> AGG[M5 Aggregation weighted sum]
    A2 --> AGG
    AGG --> Y[Network output f x]
    Y --> ERR[Error e equals y minus f]
    ERR --> GD[Backprop gradient to M1A M1B M2A M2B premise parameters]
    ERR --> LSRC[LSRC closed form to A1 A2 consequent parameters]
    GD --> M1A
    GD --> M1B
    GD --> M2A
    GD --> M2B
    LSRC --> A1
    LSRC --> A2
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hybrid Two-Phase Learning Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Training data set X Y] --> FixPremise[Step 1 Fix premise parameters initialise from clustering]
    FixPremise --> FwdPass[Step 2 Forward pass compute normalized firings w1 bar w2 bar etc]
    FwdPass --> BuildPhi[Step 3 Build regression matrix Phi]
    BuildPhi --> LSRCStep[Step 4 Solve hat theta equals Phi T Phi inverse Phi T Y]
    LSRCStep --> ComputeYhat[Step 5 Compute network output y hat equals Phi hat theta]
    ComputeYhat --> ComputeErr[Step 6 Compute error E equals 0.5 sum y minus y hat squared]
    ComputeErr --> Converge{Converged or epoch limit}
    Converge --> Yes[Stop training output theta hat]
    Converge --> No[Step 7 Backward pass gradient descent on c and sigma]
    No --> UpdatePremise[Update cij and sigma ij using learning rate eta]
    UpdatePremise --> FwdPass
    Yes --> Deploy[Deploy to networked controller module M3 transmitted over network]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Modular / Distributed LSRC in a Networked Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Plant[Plant process] --> Sensor[Sensor node]
    Sensor -->|data packet| Edge[Edge controller local neuro fuzzy module]
    Edge -->|partial firing vectors| Cloud[Cloud trainer LSRC engine]
    Cloud -->|updated consequent theta| Edge
    Edge --> ControlSignal[Actuator command]
    ControlSignal --> Plant
    Edge -->|module swap on fault| Spare[Spare module hot standby]
```
