## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution (PECPT862 / Module 2):**
> - **Part A (2 × 3 = 6 marks):** Short definitions / derivations.
> - **Part B (1 × 14 marks, with internal choice between Q1 and Q2):** Detailed analytical / design questions, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a neuro-fuzzy network. Why is it particularly suited for LSRC-based parameter identification?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **neuro-fuzzy network** (3.1) is a hybrid intelligent system that represents a fuzzy inference system as a multilayer neural architecture whose connection weights encode fuzzy-set membership and rule-consequent parameters, allowing both qualitative reasoning and data-driven adaptation.

For LSRC suitability (3.2): when premise parameters are fixed, the network output is **linear in the consequent parameters**, allowing the standard **closed-form least-squares estimator** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ to be applied directly, delivering optimal unbiased estimates in one batch or via RLS recursion.

(3.3) The linearity is the structural foundation that makes the LSRC machinery applicable; without it, only nonlinear optimisation would be possible.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the role of the product T-norm and the normalized firing strength in a TSK neuro-fuzzy system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Product T-norm** (3.1) computes a rule's overall firing strength as the product of all its antecedent membership degrees: $w_r(\mathbf{x}) = \prod_{i}\, \mu_{A_{ir}}(x_i)$. It is preferred over the min T-norm in LSRC-based training because it is **continuously differentiable**, enabling gradient-based learning of premise parameters.

**Normalized firing strength** (3.2) $\bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x}) = w_r(\mathbf{x}) / \sum_{k} w_k$ rescales each rule's contribution so that $\sum_r \bar{w}_r = 1$. This converts the weighted sum into a **convex combination of rule outputs**, ensuring smoothness, interpretability (each $\bar{w}_r$ is the *relative* rule activation), and numerical stability in the regression matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ used for LSRC.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Detailed Questions (14 marks, with internal choice)

#### **Question B1 (Choice 1).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neatly labelled block diagram, describe the **modular construction** of a Takagi–Sugeno–Kang (TSK) neuro-fuzzy network. Explain the role of each module in the overall computation and state the output equation in its final aggregated form.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same TSK network, derive the **closed-form least-squares (LSRC) estimator** for the consequent parameter vector $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. State the **Gauss–Markov properties** of the estimator and discuss why the condition $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}) < 10^{6}$ is important.

---

**Model Solution to B1(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Modular decomposition into five functional blocks (refer diagram in §4.1):

1. **M1 – Fuzzification:** Each input $x_i$ is mapped through $J_i$ Gaussian (or other) membership functions $\mu_{A_{ij}}(x_i) = \exp(-\tfrac{1}{2}\tfrac{(x_i-c_{ij})^{2}}{\sigma_{ij}^{2}})$.
2. **M2 – Rule Antecedent (T-norm):** For rule $r$, the firing strength is $w_r = \prod_{i} \mu_{A_{ir}}(x_i)$.
3. **M3 – Consequent (Linear):** Each rule produces $\hat{y}_r = \mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r$.
4. **M4 – Normalization:** $\bar{w}_r = w_r / \sum_k w_k$.
5. **M5 – Aggregation:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{r} \bar{w}_r \hat{y}_r$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Role of each module:

- M1 and M2 encode **prior expert knowledge** of input fuzziness and rule structure.
- M3 is the **linear-in-parameter** subnetwork — the target of LSRC.
- M4 ensures **partition-of-unity** for stable aggregation.
- M5 produces the **defuzzified crisp output** suitable for control action.

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Final aggregated form:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{R}\, w_r(\mathbf{x})\bigl(\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r\bigr)}{\displaystyle\sum_{r=1}^{R}\, w_r(\mathbf{x})}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Engineering rationale of modularity: plug-and-play deployability over networks, fault isolation, incremental growth, parallel computation.

---

**Model Solution to B1(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Set up the cost. With fixed premise parameters (and thus fixed $\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}$ for each data point $k$),

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\sum_{k=1}^{N}\,(y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,(\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{T}(\mathbf{y} - \boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta})$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — Normal-equation derivation (full chain as in §3.1 Steps 3–5):

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \;=\; -\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y} + \boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}\boldsymbol{\theta} \;=\; \mathbf{0} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\mathrm{LS}} \;=\; (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — Gauss–Markov properties (1 mark each statement, 2 marks total):

- (i) **Unbiased:** $\mathbb{E}[\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\mathrm{LS}}] = \boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- (ii) **Minimum variance** among all linear unbiased estimators of $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.
- (iii) $\mathrm{Cov}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\mathrm{LS}}) = \sigma^{2}(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}$.

**Step 4 (2 marks)** — Condition number discussion. The condition number $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}) = \sigma_{\max}/\sigma_{\min}$ measures the **sensitivity of the LSRC solution to noise and round-off**. If $\kappa > 10^{6}$:

- The matrix is **ill-conditioned**; $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ may have huge variance.
- Remedies: (i) **Tikhonov (ridge) regularisation** $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi} + \rho \mathbf{I})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ with $\rho > 0$; (ii) **QR decomposition** of $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$; (iii) **SVD-based pseudoinverse**; (iv) data normalisation / feature scaling.

---

#### **Question B2 (Choice 2).** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the **hybrid two-phase learning algorithm** for training a TSK neuro-fuzzy network. Clearly identify the **forward pass (LSRC step)** and the **backward pass (gradient-descent step)**, and justify why this hybrid strategy is superior to pure gradient descent.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** update equations from the exponentially weighted cost function. State the role of the **forgetting factor $\lambda$** and discuss the trade-off between **convergence speed** and **steady-state error**.

---

**Model Solution to B2(a):**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Motivation. Premise parameters $\{c_{ij}, \sigma_{ij}\}$ make the network *nonlinear* in $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ overall, but the network is *bilinear*: nonlinear in premises, linear in consequents.

**Step 2 (3 marks)** — Forward pass (LSRC):

- Fix premises (initialised e.g. by k-means / grid partitioning of input space).
- For each training sample $k$, compute $\bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x}^{(k)})$ and stack into the regression matrix $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$.
- Solve the normal equations to obtain $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}_{\mathrm{LS}}$ in one shot.
- The forward pass is **closed-form** — no learning rate, no oscillation.

**Step 3 (3 marks)** — Backward pass (gradient descent):

- With $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ fixed, propagate the error $E = \tfrac{1}{2}(y - f)^{2}$ backward through the network.
- Apply the chain rule (full derivation in §3.3) to obtain $\partial E/\partial c_{ij}$ and $\partial E/\partial \sigma_{ij}$.
- Update: $c_{ij}^{\mathrm{new}} = c_{ij} - \eta_c \cdot \partial E / \partial c_{ij}$, similarly for $\sigma_{ij}$.

**Step 4 — Why hybrid is superior (1 mark):**

- **Pure GD** on the joint $(\text{premise}, \text{consequent})$ parameter space suffers from slow convergence, local minima, and learning-rate sensitivity.
- The hybrid scheme **decouples** the optimisation: the fast, optimal LSRC handles the easy (linear) part, and GD handles the hard (nonlinear) part. This is the **well-known advantage of the ANFIS algorithm** (Jang, 1993).

---

**Model Solution to B2(b):**

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — Exponentially weighted cost:

$$J_k(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k}\, \lambda^{k-j}\,(y^{(j)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)}\boldsymbol{\theta})^{2}, \qquad \lambda \in (0,1]$$

**Step 2 (3 marks)** — Derive normal-equation recursion. Define $\mathbf{R}(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)}$ and $\mathbf{r}(k) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda^{k-j} \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(j)T} y^{(j)}$. Then

$$\mathbf{R}(k) \;=\; \lambda\,\mathbf{R}(k-1) + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}, \qquad \mathbf{r}(k) \;=\; \lambda\,\mathbf{r}(k-1) + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}y^{(k)}$$

Setting $\mathbf{P}(k) = \mathbf{R}(k)^{-1}$ and applying the **matrix-inversion lemma**:

$$\mathbf{P}(k) \;=\; \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[\mathbf{P}(k-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(k-1)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\mathbf{P}(k-1)}{\lambda + \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\mathbf{P}(k-1)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}}\right]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — Derive the parameter update. From $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) = \mathbf{P}(k)\mathbf{r}(k)$ and after algebraic simplification:

$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k) \;=\; \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1) + \underbrace{\mathbf{P}(k)\boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)T}}_{\text{Kalman gain } \mathbf{L}(k)}\,(y^{(k)} - \boldsymbol{\phi}^{(k)}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k-1))$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — Role of the forgetting factor $\lambda$ and the trade-off:

- $\lambda = 1$: infinite memory, all past samples weighted equally; slowest tracking, lowest steady-state error.
- $\lambda < 1$ (e.g. $0.95$–$0.999$): finite *effective window* of $\approx 1/(1-\lambda)$ samples; faster tracking of time-varying dynamics, but higher variance (larger steady-state error) and risk of numerical blow-up if $\lambda$ is too small.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the chain rule in GD pass:** Many students incorrectly update premise parameters with the *error signal alone*, missing the multiplicative factor $\partial \mu_{A_{ij}}/\partial c_{ij} \propto (x_i - c_{ij})/\sigma_{ij}^{2}$. This gives an update that ignores the membership function shape — **loses 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the ridge term in ill-conditioned LSRC:** If the question includes noisy data, you MUST add $\rho \mathbf{I}$ to $\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}$. Pure OLS with $\kappa > 10^{6}$ will be marked down.
> 3. **Confusing RLS covariance update with the parameter update:** RLS has *two* coupled equations (one for $\mathbf{P}(k)$, one for $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}(k)$). Writing only one is **partial credit at best**.
> 4. **Ignoring normalisation:** $\bar{w}_r$ requires explicit division by $\sum w_k$. Omitting this and using raw $w_r$ violates the convex-combination structure.
> 5. **Confusing LSRC with LMS:** LMS uses a fixed step size, RLS uses a data-dependent Kalman gain. Examiners will spot this immediately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist (Module 2 / LSRC topic):**

- **Neuro-fuzzy network = fuzzy inference system in neural-network form**, with parameters trained by data.
- **Modular construction = five functional blocks**: M1 (fuzzification), M2 (T-norm), M3 (consequent linear / classification), M4 (normalization), M5 (aggregation/defuzzification).
- **TSK aggregation equation**: $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{r=1}^{R} \bar{w}_r(\mathbf{x}) (\mathbf{a}_r^{T}\mathbf{x} + b_r)$, with $\bar{w}_r = w_r / \sum_k w_k$.
- **Gaussian MF** is the default because it is everywhere differentiable — required for gradient-based learning.
- **Product T-norm** is differentiable; min T-norm is not — prefer product in trainable networks.
- **LSRC estimator (batch)**: $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = (\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})^{-1}\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\mathbf{y}$.
- **Gauss–Markov theorem** guarantees that OLS is **BLUE** (Best Linear Unbiased Estimator) under i.i.d. zero-mean noise.
- **Condition number $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi}) < 10^{6}$** is the rule-of-thumb stability gate; otherwise apply **ridge regularisation** with $\rho \mathbf{I}$.
- **Hybrid learning** = forward-pass LSRC for consequents + backward-pass GD for premises. The bilinear structure of TSK networks is what makes this decomposition valid.
- **RLS recursion** uses exponentially weighted cost with forgetting factor $\lambda \in (0.95, 0.999)$; trade-off: small $\lambda$ → fast tracking but high variance, $\lambda = 1$ → slow tracking but low variance.
- **RLS cost per step** is $O(M^{2})$; LMS is $O(M)$ — a classic speed-vs-complexity trade-off.
- **Premise-parameter gradient (Gaussian MF)**: $\partial \mu_{A}/\partial c = \mu_{A}(x-c)/\sigma^{2}$, $\partial \mu_{A}/\partial \sigma = \mu_{A}(x-c)^{2}/\sigma^{3}$.
- **Networked-control relevance**: modular construction enables module-level transmission, incremental learning, and graceful degradation under packet loss.
- **Common architectures**: ANFIS, FALCON, GARIC, SONFIN, NEFCLASS — all fit the 5-module pattern.
- **Rule explosion**: the number of parameters grows as $R(n+1)$; use **input selection**, **rule pruning**, or **growing structures** (SONFIN) to keep complexity manageable.
- **Convergence diagnostics**: monitor both training error and $\kappa(\boldsymbol{\Phi}^{T}\boldsymbol{\Phi})$; if the latter rises, switch to ridge or stop training.
- **Examination trap**: always quote the LSRC formula with the **regression matrix** $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ explicitly defined; merely writing $(\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^{T}\mathbf{y}$ without specifying $\boldsymbol{\Phi}$ loses a mark.
- **Engineering applications** (must cite at least one in 14-mark answers): chemical process control, automotive engine control, robotic inverse kinematics, smart-grid load forecasting, networked/cloud control, biomedical signal classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues in Software, Middleware, and Hardware Platforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Issues in Software, Middleware, and Hardware Platforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Issues in Software, Middleware, and Hardware Platforms

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Networked Control System (NCS)**, information between spatially distributed components — sensors, controllers, and actuators — is exchanged over a **shared communication network**. While networking offers flexibility, modularity, and low installation cost, it simultaneously introduces **layered issues** across three foundational stacks:

- **Software Platform Issues** – Operating system behaviour, scheduling policies, real-time guarantees, computational footprint, and code determinism.
- **Middleware Platform Issues** – Abstraction layers, distributed object brokering, Quality of Service (QoS), and inter-process/inter-node communication semantics.
- **Hardware Platform Issues** – Physical transceivers, embedded processors, sensor/actuator non-idealities, clock drift, and bandwidth-bound physical media.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The syllabus treats these issues as *cross-cutting concerns* that fundamentally dictate the **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)**, **data dropout tolerance**, and **stability margins** of any networked controller.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **three-lane toll booth on a national highway**:

- **Hardware = the toll booth itself** (booths, sensors, barriers, cameras). If the camera is slow or the barrier is sluggish, cars pile up — no matter how smart the system is.
- **Middleware = the traffic-management radio network** connecting all booths to a central command. If messages get jammed, lost, or repeated, the central command cannot coordinate well.
- **Software = the central command's brain** — the control algorithm running on servers. Even with perfect hardware and clear comms, a poorly written, non-real-time brain makes wrong, late decisions.

A networked control system is **only as strong as the weakest of these three layers**, and this module dissects each one.

### Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> **Core NCS Metrics (must memorise):**
> - **τ_sc** – Sensor-to-Controller delay (typically **10 ms – 100 ms** over Ethernet)
> - **τ_ca** – Controller-to-Actuator delay
> - **τ = τ_sc + τ_ca** – Total network-induced delay
> - **γ** – Data dropout rate (**0 ≤ γ ≤ 1**, where γ = 1 means total loss)
> - **h** – Sampling period (**seconds**)
> - **B** – Network bandwidth (**bits/second**)
> - **MADB** – Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (**seconds**)

---

## 1.2 Why These Issues Matter in NCS

Traditional control assumes:
- Zero-delay feedback
- Synchronous sampling
- Perfect data delivery

Real networked systems violate **all three assumptions**, and the violations originate at the **software, middleware, and hardware layers**. Hence, analysing these layers is a **prerequisite** before designing any compensation technique (e.g., Smith predictor, event-triggered control, MPC over networks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Taxonomy of NCS Issues

### A. Software Platform Issues

Software is the *logical brain* that executes the control law. In NCS, software runs on **embedded controllers, RTOS-based nodes, or distributed servers**.

#### Key Software-Level Issues:

- **Real-time determinism** – Control loops require *bounded execution time*. A general-purpose OS (Windows, Linux without patches) does **not** guarantee this. Hence **RTOS platforms** (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS, RT-Linux) are preferred.
- **Scheduling jitter** – Even when a task completes within its deadline, *variation* in completion time degrades control performance.
- **Pre-emption and priority inversion** – A low-priority sensor task holding a mutex can block a high-priority controller task → this is the classic **priority inversion problem** solved by *priority inheritance protocols*.
- **Code footprint** – Embedded NCS nodes have constrained Flash/RAM (often **< 512 KB**). Heavyweight middleware like full CORBA stacks cannot fit.
- **Computational delay τ_c** – Time taken by the controller to compute $u(k)$ once data arrives. Adds to total delay: $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_c + \tau_{ca}$.
- **Fault tolerance and recovery** – Software must handle node failures gracefully (watchdog timers, fail-safe states).
- **Versioning and configuration drift** – Heterogeneous nodes may run different firmware versions, creating subtle behavioural mismatches.
- **Security stack overhead** – TLS/DTLS handshakes add **50–200 ms** of session-establishment latency on each reconnect.

#### Scheduling Schemes Used in NCS Software:

| Scheme | Notation | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rate Monotonic (RM) | Static priority by period | Periodic control loops |
| Deadline Monotonic (DM) | Static priority by deadline | Mixed-periodic tasks |
| Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Dynamic priority | Soft real-time NCS |
| Least Laxity First (LLF) | Dynamic by laxity | Multi-loop systems |

### B. Middleware Platform Issues

Middleware is the *software glue* between distributed components. It abstracts hardware and OS heterogeneity so that sensor nodes, controllers, and actuators can interoperate.

#### Core Middleware Issues in NCS:

- **End-to-end latency** introduced by middleware marshalling/unmarshalling, ORB (Object Request Broker) call routing, and stub-skeleton binding.
- **Lack of real-time guarantees** – Traditional middleware (e.g., standard CORBA, Java RMI) is **non-real-time** by design.
- **QoS heterogeneity** – Some middleware provides multiple QoS levels (e.g., real-time CORBA, RT-CORBA), but configuration is non-trivial.
- **Discovery and naming overhead** – Service discovery (e.g., DDS, UPnP) consumes bandwidth.
- **Time synchronisation** – Middleware like **DDS** provides built-in timestamping, whereas CORBA relies on external NTP — itself network-dependent.
- **Interoperability** – Mixing vendors (e.g., OPC UA client talking to a proprietary fieldbus) creates gateway latency.

#### Middleware Taxonomy Used in NCS:

| Middleware Family | Real-Time Support | Typical Latency | NCS Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CORBA / RT-CORBA | Optional (RT-CORBA) | 1–10 ms | Industrial DCS |
| DCOM | Limited | 5–20 ms | Legacy Windows SCADA |
| Java RMI | Poor | 10–50 ms | Research prototypes |
| OPC UA | Yes (Pub/Sub) | 1–5 ms | Industry 4.0 |
| DDS (OMG) | Excellent | < 1 ms | Hard real-time NCS |
| MQTT | No (broker-based) | 10–100 ms | IoT monitoring, not control |

> [!NOTE]
> **DDS and RT-CORBA** are the only mainstream middleware with formal real-time guarantees and are the **exam favourites**.

### C. Hardware Platform Issues

Hardware issues are the *physical-layer realities* that ultimately bound NCS performance.

#### Major Hardware Issues:

- **Sensor noise and quantisation** – Encoders have finite resolution (e.g., 12-bit → $2^{12} = 4096$ counts/rev).
- **Actuator saturation and dead-zone** – Physical actuators cannot deliver arbitrarily large signals instantaneously.
- **Clock drift** – Quartz crystals drift at **±10 to ±100 ppm**, causing sensor/actuator timestamps to diverge; for a **1 GHz** system this translates to microsecond-level misalignment.
- **Network interface card (NIC) buffering** – Buffers add a deterministic but non-zero delay.
- **Signal propagation delay** – For optical/copper media, propagation is $\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s; on a 1 km link this gives ~**5 µs**, usually negligible vs. queuing delay.
- **Electromagnetic interference (EMI)** – Industrial environments introduce bit errors; retransmissions raise effective delay.
- **Bandwidth limitation** – Fieldbus (CAN, Profibus) is typically **< 1 Mbps**; Ethernet is **10/100/1000 Mbps**; Wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G-URLLC) is variable.
- **Power-constrained nodes** – Battery-powered wireless sensor nodes limit sampling frequency.

#### Hardware-to-Delay Mapping:

| Hardware Element | Delay Contribution | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensor sampling + A/D | τ_s | 1–10 µs |
| NIC transmit buffering | τ_tx | 10–100 µs |
| Network propagation | τ_prop | µs–ms |
| Switch/router queuing | τ_q | µs–ms (variable) |
| NIC receive buffering | τ_rx | 10–100 µs |
| Actuator D/A + lag | τ_a | 1–10 ms |

Total: $\tau = \tau_s + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop} + \tau_q + \tau_rx + \tau_a$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Definition | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\tau_{sc}$ | Sensor-to-controller delay | Time from sensor read to controller input | seconds |
| $\tau_{ca}$ | Controller-to-actuator delay | Time from controller output to actuator apply | seconds |
| $\tau$ | Total delay | $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} + \tau_c$ | seconds |
| $\tau_{MADB}$ | Max Allowable Delay Bound | Largest $\tau$ preserving stability | seconds |
| $h$ | Sampling period | Inverse of sampling frequency $f_s$ | seconds |
| $\gamma$ | Data dropout rate | Fraction of lost packets | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $B$ | Bandwidth | $B = \frac{N_{bits}}{T_{frame}}$ | bits/s |
| $f_{clk}$ | Clock frequency | Drives CPU throughput | Hz |
| $\Delta_{drift}$ | Clock drift | $\Delta_{drift} = \rho \cdot t$, $\rho$ in ppm | seconds |
| $T_{exec}$ | Task execution time | $T_{exec} = \frac{C}{f_{clk}}$, $C$ = cycles | seconds |
| $U_{RM}$ | RM utilisation bound | $U_{RM} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | dimensionless |
| $J$ | Scheduling jitter | $J = T_{exec}^{max} - T_{exec}^{min}$ | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** In delay-stability problems, the bound $\tau_{MADB}$ is derived from the system's characteristic equation. Always write the full delay expression $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_c + \tau_{ca}$ — partial expressions lose marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (CAN bus, FlexRay):** Hardware timing constraints drive ECU scheduling on RTOS (AUTOSAR).
- **Process Industries (PROFIBUS, Foundation Fieldbus):** OPC UA over TSN bridges legacy fieldbus hardware with modern middleware.
- **Smart Grid & DERs:** IEC 61850 middleware rides on substation hardware with strict 4 ms latency budgets.
- **Aerial Robotics & UAVs:** Lightweight middleware (DDS-XRCE, ROS 2) over hardware-constrained Wi-Fi/5G links.
- **Medical devices (IEC 80001):** Closed-loop drug delivery uses hardware-redundant NCS with fail-safe middleware.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Network-Induced Delay Bound

In a discrete-time NCS, the control update at step $k$ uses the most recent sensor packet. If a packet is delayed beyond one sampling period $h$, that sample is effectively *lost*. Hence the stability bound must include the worst-case delay.

**Setup:** Consider a sampled-data system with sampling period $h$, network delay $\tau$ bounded by $\tau \leq \tau_{max}$, and controller $u(k) = Kx(k)$.

**Step 1 — Express the delayed state reaching the controller:**

$$ x_c(k) = x(k - d_k) $$

where $d_k$ is the integer delay (in samples) at step $k$, with $1 \leq d_k \leq d_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Bound $d_{max}$ by ceiling of the worst-case delay:**

$$ d_{max} = \left\lceil \frac{\tau_{max}}{h} \right\rceil $$

**Step 3 — Write the closed-loop dynamics with bounded delay:**

$$ x(k+1) = Ax(k) + B K x(k - d_k) $$

**Step 4 — Convert to augmented state for Lyapunov analysis:**

$$ z(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x(k) \\ x(k-1) \\ \vdots \\ x(k - d_{max}) \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 5 — Augmented closed-loop form:**

$$ z(k+1) = \Phi \cdot z(k) $$

$$ \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} A & 0 & \cdots & BK \\ I & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & I & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 6 — Stability condition:** The system is stable iff spectral radius $\rho(\Phi) < 1$.

**Step 7 — Maximum Allowable Delay Bound:**

$$ \tau_{MADB} = d_{max} \cdot h $$

This is the **delay bound** the hardware/software/middleware stack must guarantee.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: RM Utilisation Bound for NCS Tasks

**Step 1 — For $n$ periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic scheduling, Liu & Layland bound:**

$$ U_{RM} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$

**Step 2 — For large $n$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.**

**Step 3 — Apply to NCS:** A 3-task NCS (sensing, control, actuation) needs $U_{RM} \leq 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.779$.

**Step 4 — Verification example:** Let $T_1=10\,ms$, $T_2=20\,ms$, $T_3=50\,ms$, with $C_i$ = 1, 2, 5 ms respectively.

$$ U_{RM} = \frac{1}{10} + \frac{2}{20} + \frac{5}{50} = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.3 $$

Since $0.3 < 0.779$, the task set is schedulable.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Clock Drift Across Distributed Nodes

**Step 1 — Two nodes A and B have clocks with drift $\rho_A$ and $\rho_B$ (in ppm).**

**Step 2 — After time $t$, the offset between them is:**

$$ \Delta_{drift}(t) = (\rho_A - \rho_B) \cdot t $$

**Step 3 — For $\rho_A = +50$ ppm, $\rho_B = -30$ ppm, over $t = 3600$ s (1 hour):**

$$ \Delta_{drift} = (50 - (-30)) \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 = 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600 $$

$$ \Delta_{drift} = 0.288 \text{ seconds} $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** In 1 hour, node clocks drift by **288 ms** — enough to break timestamp-based control sequencing. Hence NCS hardware must implement **IEEE 1588 PTP** or **NTP** synchronisation.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing a Platform Stack

| Application | Recommended Hardware | Recommended Middleware | Recommended Software Stack | Expected $\tau$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slow process control (refineries) | PLC + PROFIBUS | OPC UA | RT-Linux + IEC 61131-3 | 50–200 ms |
| Automotive ECU network | CAN/FlexRay | AUTOSAR middleware | OSEK/VDX RTOS | 1–5 ms |
| Hard real-time motion control | EtherCAT slaves | RT-CORBA / DDS | VxWorks / RT-Linux | < 1 ms |
| IoT-based monitoring (non-control) | Wi-Fi MCU + MQTT broker | MQTT | FreeRTOS + TCP/IP stack | 100–1000 ms |
| TSN-based Industry 4.0 | TSN switches + 802.1Qbv | OPC UA Pub/Sub | Linux PREEMPT-RT | 0.1–10 ms |

## 3.5 Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-node NCS uses CAN bus (bandwidth $B = 250$ kbps). Each data frame is $N = 130$ bits. Sensor sampling period is $h = 20$ ms. Compute (a) frame transmission time, (b) maximum throughput, (c) whether frame delay exceeds sampling period.

**Solution:**

$$ T_{frame} = \frac{N}{B} = \frac{130}{250{,}000} = 5.2 \times 10^{-4}\text{ s} = 0.52\text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{Max frames/sec} = \frac{1}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{0.52 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1923 \text{ frames/s} $$

$$ T_{frame} \ll h \;\; (0.52\text{ ms} \ll 20\text{ ms}) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{transmission delay is negligible vs. sampling period.} $$

Conclusion: At this load, the CAN hardware introduces negligible delay; remaining delay comes from software scheduling and middleware processing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered NCS Platform Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Physical Process\nPlant] --> nodeB[Sensor Node\nHardware Layer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[A-D Conversion\nHardware]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sensor Driver\nSoftware]
    nodeD --> nodeE[RTOS Task Scheduling\nSoftware]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Marshalling Layer\nMiddleware]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Network Interface Card\nHardware]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Shared Network Medium\nHardware]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Controller NIC\nHardware]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Unmarshalling Layer\nMiddleware]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Control Algorithm\nSoftware]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Actuator Driver\nSoftware]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Actuator Hardware\nD-A + Plant]
```

## 4.2 Issue-Severity Heatmap (Where Each Layer Hurts Most)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Software Issues\nScheduling Jitter\nPriority Inversion\nCode Bloat] --> nodeX[Impact on NCS\nLatency + Jitter]
    nodeB[Middleware Issues\nMarshalling Delay\nQoS Mismatch\nNo RT Guarantees] --> nodeX
    nodeC[Hardware Issues\nClock Drift\nEMI / Bit Errors\nBandwidth Limit] --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY[Net Effect\nMADB Violation\nStability Loss]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Sources of Delay in NCS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Sensor\nSampling tau_s] --> nodeB[Encoder\ntau_enc]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Tx Buffer\ntau_tx]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Network\nPropagation + Queuing tau_net]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Rx Buffer\ntau_rx]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Controller\nCompute tau_c]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Tx Buffer\ntau_tx2]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Network\ntau_net2]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Decoder\ntau_dec]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Actuator\ntau_a]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Plant]
```

## 4.4 Middleware Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Need Hard RT\nsub-ms Latency] --> nodeB[Use DDS or RT-CORBA]
    nodeC[Need Industry 4.0\nInteroperability] --> nodeD[Use OPC UA Pub Sub]
    nodeE[Need IoT Cloud\nBest Effort] --> nodeF[Use MQTT]
    nodeG[Legacy Windows\nSCADA] --> nodeH[Use DCOM or OPC DA]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three issues arising in the software platform of a Networked Control System.*

**Model Answer:**
The three major software platform issues are:
1. **Real-time scheduling** – The control algorithm must execute within a bounded time; non-RTOS platforms cause deadline misses.
2. **Priority inversion** – A low-priority task holding a shared resource can block high-priority control tasks, degrading stability.
3. **Computational overhead of security stacks** – TLS/DTLS sessions add latency that can violate the Maximum Allowable Delay Bound.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between CORBA and DDS as middleware for networked control applications.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | CORBA | DDS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Architecture | Client-server via ORB | Publish-subscribe, brokerless |
| Real-time support | Optional via RT-CORBA | Built-in real-time |
| Typical latency | 1–10 ms | < 1 ms |
| Discovery | Naming service | Built-in automatic |
| Best for | Industrial DCS | Hard real-time, distributed NCS |

DDS is preferred for **time-critical NCS**; CORBA for **legacy integration**.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **sources of delay in a typical NCS** and explain how **hardware, software, and middleware layers** each contribute to these delays. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

In a Networked Control System, the closed loop traverses three physical platforms:

**1. Hardware Layer Contributions:**
- Sensor sampling and A/D conversion: $\tau_s$ (~µs–ms)
- NIC transmit/receive buffering: $\tau_{tx}, \tau_{rx}$
- Physical media propagation: $\tau_{prop}$ (~5 µs/km)
- Switch/router queuing: $\tau_q$ (variable, dominant in congested networks)
- Clock drift between sensor and controller: $\Delta_{drift}$
- Actuator lag: $\tau_a$

**2. Middleware Layer Contributions:**
- Marshalling of data into wire format: $\tau_{marsh}$
- ORB/broker routing: $\tau_{broker}$
- Unmarshalling: $\tau_{unmarsh}$
- Service discovery / naming overhead: $\tau_{disc}$

**3. Software Layer Contributions:**
- RTOS scheduling delay (waiting for CPU): $\tau_{sched}$
- Control computation time: $\tau_c$
- Driver execution time: $\tau_{drv}$
- Priority inversion handling: extra blocking delay

**Total delay:**
$$ \tau_{total} = \tau_s + \tau_{tx} + \tau_{prop} + \tau_q + \tau_{rx} + \tau_{marsh} + \tau_{broker} + \tau_{unmarsh} + \tau_{sched} + \tau_c + \tau_{tx2} + \tau_{prop2} + \tau_q2 + \tau_{rx2} + \tau_{unmarsh2} + \tau_{drv} + \tau_a $$

For stability: $\tau_{total} \leq \tau_{MADB}$.

---

**(b)** A 3-task NCS uses Rate Monotonic scheduling. The task periods and execution times are: $T_1=10\,ms, C_1=2\,ms$; $T_2=30\,ms, C_2=4\,ms$; $T_3=60\,ms, C_3=6\,ms$. **(i)** Compute the utilisation. **(ii)** Verify schedulability using the Liu–Layland bound. **(iii)** Suggest a remedy if the bound is violated. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Utilisation calculation:**

$$ U_{RM} = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{30} + \frac{6}{60} $$

$$ U_{RM} = 0.200 + 0.1333 + 0.100 = 0.4333 $$

**(ii) Liu–Layland bound for $n=3$:**

$$ U_{bound} = 3 \left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 $$

Since $0.4333 < 0.7798$, the task set is **schedulable under RM**.

**(iii) If violated, remedies include:**
- Migrate to **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling which guarantees $U \leq 1.0$.
- Reduce $C_i$ by optimising code, using faster processors, or offloading computation to dedicated hardware.
- Split the controller task into smaller sub-tasks with shorter periods.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **issues introduced by middleware in NCS**, with examples of CORBA, OPC UA, and DDS. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Middleware Issues in NCS:**

Middleware abstracts hardware and OS heterogeneity. However, in NCS it introduces **non-trivial delays and real-time challenges**:

**1. Marshalling/Unmarshalling Overhead:** Every parameter must be serialised into a wire format (CDR for CORBA, UA binary for OPC UA, CDR-based for DDS). This adds **0.1–1 ms** per call.

**2. Lack of Real-Time Guarantees:** Standard CORBA and Java RMI are **best-effort**. Without RT-CORBA extensions, deadlines cannot be guaranteed. This violates the MADB.

**3. Broker Bottlenecks:** Client-server middleware (CORBA, OPC DA) has a central broker/ORB that can become a **single point of congestion and failure**.

**4. Discovery and Naming Overhead:** Service discovery broadcasts consume bandwidth, problematic on fieldbus.

**5. Time Synchronisation:** CORBA relies on external NTP; DDS has built-in timestamping — a major advantage for NCS.

**Middleware Examples:**

- **CORBA / RT-CORBA:** Used in industrial DCS. RT-CORBA adds priority propagation. Latency: 1–10 ms.
- **OPC UA:** Industry 4.0 standard. Has Pub/Sub mode for multicast. Excellent security. Latency: 1–5 ms.
- **DDS:** OMG standard, brokerless Pub/Sub with 23 QoS policies. Best for hard real-time NCS. Latency: < 1 ms.

**Conclusion:** Middleware choice is dictated by **latency budget, scalability, and interoperability** requirements.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **hardware-level issues** affecting NCS performance, with focus on **clock drift, sensor quantisation, and bandwidth limitations**. A wireless sensor node uses a 16-bit ADC with input range **0–5 V**. Compute the **quantisation step** and the **minimum detectable voltage change**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hardware Issues:**

**1. Clock Drift:** Quartz crystals drift at 10–100 ppm. Two unsynchronised nodes accumulate offset $\Delta_{drift} = (\rho_A - \rho_B) t$. This breaks timestamp ordering and can cause **out-of-sequence packet reception**, destabilising control.

**2. Sensor Quantisation:** Real sensors output discrete levels, not continuous values. A 16-bit ADC with 0–5 V range gives:

$$ Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5}{2^{16} - 1} = \frac{5}{65535} $$

$$ Q = 7.63 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 76.3 \text{ µV} $$

So the **quantisation step** is **76.3 µV**, and the **minimum detectable voltage change** is also **76.3 µV**.

**3. Bandwidth Limitations:**
- Fieldbus (CAN, PROFIBUS): ~1 Mbps
- Wired Ethernet: 10–1000 Mbps
- Wireless: 1–600 Mbps (variable)

When bandwidth $B$ is fixed, frame transmission time $T_{frame} = N/B$. If $T_{frame} > h$, the network becomes the bottleneck.

**Other issues:** Actuator saturation, EMI-induced bit errors, propagation delay, power constraints.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not write** "middleware delays" without naming specific middleware (CORBA/DDS/OPC UA) — generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. In the 16-bit ADC problem, **always use $2^n - 1$**, not $2^n$, in the denominator. Using $2^n$ = 65536 gives $Q = 76.29$ µV, which is **wrong** and loses 1 mark.
> 3. For Liu–Layland, do not confuse the **necessary condition** ($U \leq 1$) with the **sufficient bound** ($U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$). The question asks for the bound.
> 4. When listing issues, students often **omit clock drift and quantisation**, focusing only on bandwidth — examiner deducts 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NCS issues are layered:** Software (logical), Middleware (abstraction), Hardware (physical).
- **Total network delay** = $\tau_{sc} + \tau_c + \tau_{ca}$, must stay below $\tau_{MADB}$ for stability.
- **Real-time scheduling** uses RM, DM, EDF, LLF; Liu–Layland bound: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- **Priority inversion** is a classic software issue — solved by priority inheritance.
- **Middleware latency ranking** (best to worst for NCS): DDS < RT-CORBA < OPC UA < CORBA < OPC DA < DCOM < Java RMI < MQTT.
- **OPC UA Pub/Sub and DDS** are exam favourites for Industry 4.0 NCS.
- **Clock drift** = $\Delta_{drift} = (\rho_A - \rho_B) \cdot t$; PTP/NTP synchronisation required.
- **Quantisation step** for $n$-bit ADC, range $V_{FS}$: $Q = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$.
- **Frame transmission time** = $N/B$; must be much smaller than sampling period $h$.
- **Hardware bottleneck** dominates when $T_{frame} \geq h$.
- **Middleware bottleneck** dominates when marshalling + broker delay dominates.
- **Software bottleneck** dominates under priority inversion and non-RTOS scheduling.
- **Stability condition for delayed NCS:** Spectral radius of augmented matrix $\Phi < 1$.
- **Standard NCS metrics:** $\tau_{sc}, \tau_{ca}, \tau, \gamma, h, B, \tau_{MADB}, J, \rho_{drift}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-World Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real-World Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a spatially distributed control system wherein sensors, controllers, and actuators exchange information over a **shared communication network**. Real-world applications of NCS refer to the deployment of such feedback loops — where control signals, measurement data, and supervisory commands traverse wired or wireless channels — across industrial, commercial, aerospace, healthcare, and infrastructure domains.

The mathematical plant-controller-actuator chain in an NCS is formally expressed as:

$$
x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k - \tau_k)
$$

$$
u(k) = K x(k - \tau_{sc,k})
$$

where $\tau_k$ and $\tau_{sc,k}$ represent the **actuator-to-controller delay** and the **sensor-to-controller delay** respectively, both of which are typically bounded, random, and induced by the network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Real-world applications are *not* merely case studies. They are the **validation ground** for every theoretical tool covered in Modules 1 and 2 — Lyapunov stability, event-triggered control, fault-tolerant estimation, and security-aware control all derive their engineering relevance from the application constraints described in this module.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Human Nervous System as an NCS:** Imagine your body as a Networked Control System. Your **brain** is the controller, your **spinal cord and nerves** are the shared communication network, your **muscles** are the actuators, and your **senses (eyes, skin, proprioceptors)** are the sensors. When you touch a hot surface, the signal from your fingertip (sensor) travels through nerves (network), reaches the spinal cord/brain (controller), and a reflex command (control signal) returns through the same nerves to your muscles (actuator) to withdraw the hand — all within milliseconds. The "network delay" in this analogy is the time it takes for the nerve impulse to travel, and a "packet drop" is equivalent to a missed signal (e.g., a numb fingertip). Real-world NCS applications face the *same* challenges: delay, jitter, packet loss, and bandwidth limits.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics in NCS Applications

The following metrics are universally used to characterize the quality of any real-world NCS deployment:

- **Network-Induced Delay ($\tau$)** — Typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**; bounded by $[0, \tau_{max}]$.
- **Packet Loss Rate ($\gamma$)** — Expressed as a **percentage (%)**; tolerable up to 30% in most industrial protocols.
- **Jitter ($\sigma_\tau$)** — Standard deviation of delay in **ms**.
- **Bandwidth ($B$)** — Measured in **bits per second (bps)**, **kbps**, or **Mbps**.
- **Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB)** — The critical $\tau_{max}$ below which closed-loop stability is guaranteed.
- **Sampling Period ($h$)** — The discrete-time interval at which the plant is sampled, in **seconds (s)** or **ms**.
- **Mean Square Error (MSE)** — Performance index for tracking accuracy.
- **Control Performance Index (CPI)** — Composite metric for evaluating NCS quality of service.

### 1.4 Why Real-World Applications Matter in NCS Theory

Real-world NCS deployments impose **non-ideal operating conditions** that pure theoretical analysis cannot capture:

1. **Bounded but stochastic delays** — Not constant, not Gaussian, often Markovian or bursty.
2. **Packet disorder and dropout** — Out-of-order arrival and lost packets degrade stability.
3. **Quantization effects** — Finite-bit ADC/DAC introduces quantization noise.
4. **Cyber-physical attacks** — False data injection, replay attacks, and denial-of-service.
5. **Heterogeneous networks** — Wi-Fi, 5G, Ethernet, CAN, and Profibus coexist in modern plants.
6. **Energy constraints** — Wireless sensor nodes have limited battery life (often **3–5 years** for industrial IoT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every application in this module is essentially a **constrained optimization problem**: maximize control performance under bounded network resources, stochastic delays, and security threats.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Real-World NCS Applications

Real-world applications are classified along three principal axes:

1. **By Network Type:**
   - **Wired NCS** — Ethernet/IP, Profinet, CAN, Modbus, Foundation Fieldbus.
   - **Wireless NCS** — WirelessHART, ISA 100.11a, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, 5G URLLC.
   - **Hybrid NCS** — Wired backbone with wireless field devices (most common in Industry 4.0).

2. **By Spatial Scale:**
   - **Local NCS** — Single machine, single controller (e.g., CNC machine tool).
   - **Distributed NCS** — Multiple controllers over a plant-wide network (e.g., refinery).
   - **Wide-Area NCS** — Geographically dispersed (e.g., smart grid, water distribution).

3. **By Application Domain:**
   - Industrial automation, smart grid, transportation, healthcare, aerospace, robotics.

### 2.2 Mathematical Modelling of Application-Level NCS

The discrete-time state-space model of a linear NCS with **network-induced delay and packet loss** is:

$$
x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k - d_k)
$$

where $d_k \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, d_{max}\}$ is the **time-varying delay** at step $k$. When a packet is dropped, the controller typically applies a **zero-order hold (ZOH)** on the last received control input:

$$
u(k) = K x(k - d_k), \quad \text{if packet received}
$$

$$
u(k) = u(k-1), \quad \text{if packet dropped}
$$

The closed-loop dynamics then become:

$$
x(k+1) = A x(k) + B K x(k - d_k)
$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description / Use** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plant dynamics (continuous) | $\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | Linear time-invariant plant | States in SI units |
| NCS discrete dynamics | $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k - \tau_k)$ | Discrete plant with network delay | — |
| Lyapunov stability | $V(x) = x^T P x, \; P \succ 0$ | Quadratic Lyapunov function | — |
| Lyapunov decrement | $\Delta V = V(k+1) - V(k) < 0$ | Stability condition | — |
| LMI condition | $\begin{bmatrix} -P^{-1} & \Phi \\ \Phi^T & -P \end{bmatrix} \prec 0$ | Delay-dependent stability LMI | — |
| MADB condition | $\tau_{max} < \frac{-\ln(\rho(A_{cl}))}{\alpha}$ | Maximum Allowable Delay Bound | seconds (s) |
| Packet loss stability bound | $\gamma < 1 - \rho(A_{cl})$ | Tolerable drop rate | dimensionless (%) |
| Smith Predictor compensation | $\hat{x}(t) = x(t) + \int_{t-\tau}^{t} e^{A(s-t+\tau)} B u(s) \, ds$ | Delay compensation | — |
| Event-triggered condition | $e^T(k) \Phi e(k) \geq \sigma x^T(k) \Phi x(k)$ | Triggering threshold | dimensionless |
| WirelessHART latency | $\tau_{WH} \leq 100$ ms | Industrial wireless budget | ms |
| 5G URLLC latency | $\tau_{5G} \leq 1$ ms | Ultra-reliable low-latency | ms |
| IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee) latency | $\tau_{ZB} \approx 15{-}30$ ms | Low-power wireless | ms |
| CAN bus latency | $\tau_{CAN} \leq 1$ ms @ 1 Mbps | Automotive/industrial | ms |
| Mean Square Error (MSE) | $MSE = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \vert y(k) - r(k) \vert^2$ | Tracking performance | — |
| Network utilization ($\eta$) | $\eta = \frac{N_{transmitted}}{N_{sampling instants}}$ | Bandwidth efficiency | dimensionless |
| Control Performance Index | $CPI = \int_{0}^{T} \left( x^T Q x + u^T R u \right) dt$ | LQR cost | — |
| Networked control cost | $J = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left( x^T(k) Q x(k) + u^T(k) R u(k) \right)$ | Infinite-horizon LQR | — |
| Spectral radius condition | $\rho(A_{cl}) < 1$ | Asymptotic stability | — |
| Data-rate theorem | $R > \log_2 \det(I + B^T P B)$ | Minimum bit-rate for stability | bits/sample |
| Cyber-attack detection | $\vert y(k) - C \hat{x}(k) \vert > \delta_{th}$ | Residual-based attack detection | — |

### 2.4 Why These Formulas Matter in Real Applications

- **Lyapunov-based LMIs** are the *primary tool* for certifying stability in industrial NCS deployments (e.g., certifying a chemical plant controller before commissioning).
- **MADB** determines whether a **Wi-Fi** network ($\tau_{max} \approx 50$ ms) or **5G URLLC** ($\tau_{max} \approx 1$ ms) is required for a given application.
- **Event-triggered conditions** reduce wireless sensor energy consumption by **60–80%**, critical for battery-powered field devices.
- **The data-rate theorem** underpins **bit-rate allocation** in bandwidth-constrained networks like **LoRaWAN** (max 50 kbps).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** In KTU exam problems, the most commonly tested formula derivations are: (1) MADB from spectral radius, (2) Lyapunov decrement for delay-dependent stability, and (3) Event-triggered threshold derivation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of NCS Theory

| **Engineering Field** | **NCS Theory Applied** | **Real-World Outcome** |
|---|---|---|
| Power systems | Wide-area NCS, WAMS | **PMU-based damping** of inter-area oscillations |
| Automotive | CAN/LIN/FlexRay NCS | **Drive-by-wire**, ADAS, autonomous platoons |
| Industrial automation | Fieldbus NCS, OPC UA | **Industry 4.0** smart factories |
| Aerospace | Time-triggered Ethernet, TTE | **Fly-by-wire** in Boeing 787, Airbus A350 |
| Healthcare | Wireless body area networks | **Remote surgery**, closed-loop insulin pumps |
| Smart cities | LPWAN, 5G NCS | **Adaptive traffic control**, water distribution |
| Process control | SCADA/DCS over TCP/IP | **Refineries**, water treatment plants |
| Robotics | Multi-agent NCS, consensus | **Warehouse automation** (Amazon Kiva) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) from Spectral Radius

**Problem Setup:** A discrete-time NCS has closed-loop dynamics $x(k+1) = A_{cl} x(k - d_k)$ with $d_k \in [0, d_{max}]$. Find the maximum $d_{max}$ for asymptotic stability.

**Step 1 — Identify the closed-loop matrix.**
For a state-feedback NCS with $u(k) = K x(k - d_k)$, the closed-loop matrix is:

$$
A_{cl} = A + B K
$$

where $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the system matrix and $B K$ is the controller gain product.

**Step 2 — State the stability condition in terms of spectral radius.**
For a discrete-time system, asymptotic stability requires that the spectral radius of the closed-loop matrix be strictly less than unity:

$$
\rho(A_{cl}) = \max_i \vert \lambda_i(A_{cl}) \vert < 1
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the state into delay-free and delayed components.**
The state at step $k$ can be expressed in terms of the initial state and the delayed state:

$$
x(k) = A_{cl}^k x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} A_{cl}^{k-1-i} B_K x(i - d_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the norm bound.**
Take the spectral norm on both sides and use submultiplicativity:

$$
\|x(k)\| \leq \rho(A_{cl})^k \|x(0)\| + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \rho(A_{cl})^{k-1-i} \|B_K\| \|x(i - d_i)\|
$$

**Step 5 — Bound the delayed state norm.**
Since $d_k \leq d_{max}$, we have $\|x(i - d_i)\| \leq \max_{j \in [i - d_{max}, i]} \|x(j)\|$. Using the Gronwall–Bellman inequality for discrete time:

$$
\|x(k)\| \leq \rho(A_{cl})^k \|x(0)\| \cdot e^{\|B_K\| d_{max} k}
$$

**Step 6 — Impose the convergence requirement.**
For the system to be asymptotically stable, the combined growth factor must decay, requiring:

$$
\rho(A_{cl}) \cdot e^{\|B_K\| d_{max}} < 1
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the maximum allowable delay bound.**

$$
d_{max} < \frac{-\ln(\rho(A_{cl}))}{\|B_K\|}
$$

**Final MADB expression:**

$$
\boxed{\tau_{MADB} = \frac{-\ln(\rho(A + BK))}{\|BK\|}}
$$

This is the **maximum network-induced delay** below which closed-loop asymptotic stability is preserved.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Lyapunov-Based Delay-Dependent Stability Condition

**Problem Setup:** Show that the NCS $x(k+1) = A x(k) + A_d x(k - d_k)$ is asymptotically stable if a positive definite matrix $P \succ 0$ exists satisfying a Linear Matrix Inequality (LMI).

**Step 1 — Define the augmented state vector.**
Let $z(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x(k) \\ x(k - d_k) \end{bmatrix}$. The augmented dynamics become:

$$
z(k+1) = \Phi \, z(k), \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi = \begin{bmatrix} A & A_d \\ I & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the quadratic Lyapunov candidate.**

$$
V(z(k)) = z^T(k) P z(k), \quad P = P^T \succ 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Lyapunov decrement.**

$$
\Delta V(k) = V(k+1) - V(k) = z^T(k+1) P z(k+1) - z^T(k) P z(k)
$$

Substituting the augmented dynamics:

$$
\Delta V(k) = z^T(k) \Phi^T P \Phi z(k) - z^T(k) P z(k) = z^T(k) \left( \Phi^T P \Phi - P \right) z(k)
$$

**Step 4 — Impose the negative definiteness condition.**
For asymptotic stability, $\Delta V(k) < 0$ for all $z(k) \neq 0$, which requires:

$$
\Phi^T P \Phi - P \prec 0
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to LMI using Schur complement.**
Applying the Schur complement with $P^{-1}$, the inequality $\Phi^T P \Phi - P \prec 0$ is equivalent to:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} -P^{-1} & \Phi \\ \Phi^T & -P \end{bmatrix} \prec 0
$$

**Step 6 — Final delay-dependent LMI condition.**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{bmatrix} -P^{-1} & A & A_d \\ A^T & -P & 0 \\ A_d^T & 0 & -P \end{bmatrix} \prec 0
}
$$

This is the **delay-dependent LMI** that, if feasible, certifies asymptotic stability of the NCS for all bounded delays $d_k \in [0, d_{max}]$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Event-Triggered Control Threshold

**Problem Setup:** Derive the event-triggering condition that decides when the sensor should transmit a new measurement to the controller over a bandwidth-limited network.

**Step 1 — Define the measurement error.**
Let $x(k)$ be the current state and $\hat{x}(k)$ be the last transmitted state. The triggering error is:

$$
e(k) = \hat{x}(k) - x(k)
$$

**Step 2 — Define the control input based on the transmitted state.**

$$
u(k) = K \hat{x}(k) = K \left( x(k) + e(k) \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the closed-loop dynamics.**

$$
x(k+1) = (A + BK) x(k) + BK e(k) = A_{cl} x(k) + B_K e(k)
$$

**Step 4 — Define the Lyapunov function and compute its decrement.**

$$
V(x(k)) = x^T(k) P x(k)
$$

$$
\Delta V = x^T(k+1) P x(k+1) - x^T(k) P x(k)
$$

Expanding $x(k+1)$:

$$
\Delta V = (A_{cl} x(k) + B_K e(k))^T P (A_{cl} x(k) + B_K e(k)) - x^T(k) P x(k)
$$

**Step 5 — Bound the cross terms using the triggering condition.**
For stability, we require $\Delta V \leq -\alpha \|x(k)\|^2$ for some $\alpha > 0$. After applying the Cauchy–Schwarz and AM–GM inequalities, sufficient stability is guaranteed if:

$$
e^T(k) \Phi e(k) \leq \sigma^2 \, x^T(k) \Phi x(k)
$$

**Step 6 — State the final event-triggered threshold.**

$$
\boxed{
\| e(k) \|^2 \leq \sigma^2 \| x(k) \|^2, \quad 0 < \sigma < 1
}
$$

The sensor transmits a new measurement **only when** this condition is violated, drastically reducing network traffic. In typical industrial deployments, choosing $\sigma = 0.05$ to $\sigma = 0.2$ reduces transmissions by **70–90%** compared to periodic time-triggered sampling.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Real-World Application Mapping

| **Application** | **Network** | **Typical Delay Bound** | **Critical NCS Theory** | **Industry Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Grid (WAMS) | Fiber + 5G | 50–100 ms | Wide-area damping, PMU-based control | IEEE C37.118 |
| Autonomous Vehicles | CAN, Ethernet, 5G-V2X | 1–10 ms | Multi-agent consensus, platoon control | IEEE 802.11p |
| UAV Swarms | Ad-hoc Wi-Fi, 5G | 5–50 ms | Consensus, formation control | MAVLink |
| Industrial SCADA | Ethernet/IP, Profinet | 1–10 ms | Event-triggered, fault-tolerant | IEC 62443 |
| Process Control | Foundation Fieldbus | 10–50 ms | Smith predictor, MPC | ISA 88/95 |
| Remote Surgery | Dedicated fiber + 5G | 1–5 ms | Haptic feedback NCS, bilateral teleop | IEEE 11073 |
| Smart Buildings | BACnet, ZigBee, LoRaWAN | 50–500 ms | Energy-optimal HVAC control | ASHRAE 135 |
| Railway Signaling | GSM-R, LTE-R | 100–500 ms | Track-circuit NCS, ETCS | ETCS Level 2/3 |
| Underwater ROVs | Acoustic modem | 0.5–5 s | Delay-tolerant NCS, prediction | JANUS standard |
| Warehouse Robots | Wi-Fi 6, 5G | 5–20 ms | Multi-agent path planning | VDA 5050 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
