## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Networked Control System (NCS) and list any four real-world applications where NCS is deployed.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a feedback control system in which the communication between sensors, controllers, and actuators occurs over a shared (wired or wireless) communication network, introducing network-induced delays, packet loss, and bandwidth constraints.

**Four real-world applications:**
1. **Smart Grid** — Wide-area monitoring and control of power systems using PMUs over a communication backbone.
2. **Autonomous Vehicle Platoons** — Cooperative driving using V2V/V2X communication over DSRC or 5G.
3. **Industrial SCADA Systems** — Remote monitoring and control of refinery, water treatment, and manufacturing plants.
4. **UAV Swarms** — Distributed cooperative control of unmanned aerial vehicles for surveillance, search, and rescue.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications with brief justification)*

---

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the concept of Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) in NCS. Why is it critical in real-world applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**MADB** is the upper limit on the network-induced delay (sensor-to-controller plus controller-to-actuator) below which the closed-loop NCS remains asymptotically stable. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\tau_{MADB} = \frac{-\ln(\rho(A + BK))}{\|BK\|}
$$

where $\rho(A+BK)$ is the spectral radius of the closed-loop matrix.

**Criticality in real-world applications:**
- It determines the **choice of communication medium**. For $\tau_{MADB} < 1$ ms (e.g., bilateral tele-surgery), only 5G URLLC or dedicated fiber is acceptable.
- For $\tau_{MADB} \approx 50{-}100$ ms (e.g., HVAC control), Wi-Fi or ZigBee suffices.
- It serves as a **design specification** for network engineers when deploying controllers in industrial plants.

*(3 marks — 1.5 for definition and formula, 1.5 for application context)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Networked Control System and identify the four key challenges introduced by the network. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The NCS architecture consists of a plant, sensor, controller, actuator, and a shared communication network connecting them. The sensor samples the plant output, transmits it through the network to the controller, which computes a control signal and sends it back through the network to the actuator, which drives the plant.

**Four key challenges:**

1. **Network-induced delay ($\tau_k$)** — Bounded, stochastic delay in both sensor-to-controller ($\tau_{sc}$) and controller-to-actuator ($\tau_{ca}$) paths. Total delay $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ can destabilize the closed loop.

2. **Packet loss / dropout** — When a packet is lost, the controller typically applies a zero-order hold (ZOH) on the last received value. Excessive loss degrades stability and tracking performance.

3. **Quantization errors** — Finite-bit ADC/DAC and digital communication introduce quantization noise, modeled as bounded additive disturbance.

4. **Cyber-security threats** — False data injection, replay attacks, and denial-of-service can corrupt sensor readings or control signals, leading to unsafe plant behavior.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    plantB[Plant] --> sensB[Sensor]
    sensB --> net1B[Network Delay tau_sc]
    net1B --> ctrlB[Controller]
    ctrlB --> net2B[Network Delay tau_ca]
    net2B --> actB[Actuator]
    actB --> plantB
    refB[Reference Input] --> ctrlB
    errB[Error Signal] --> ctrlB
    obsB[Disturbance Observer] --> ctrlB
```

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram, 1 each for four challenges, 1 for synthesis)*

---

**Q. (b)** Derive the Maximum Allowable Delay Bound (MADB) for the discrete-time NCS $x(k+1) = A x(k) + B u(k - d_k)$ with state feedback $u(k) = K x(k)$. State the final closed-form expression and explain its physical significance in selecting a communication network for an industrial NCS. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Closed-loop system.**
Substituting $u(k) = K x(k - d_k)$ into the plant dynamics:

$$
x(k+1) = A x(k) + B K x(k - d_k)
$$

Define the closed-loop matrix $A_{cl} = A + BK$.

**Step 2 — Iterative expansion.**
Repeated substitution yields:

$$
x(k) = A_{cl}^k x(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} A_{cl}^{k-1-i} BK \, x(i - d_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Norm bound.**
Applying the spectral norm and using submultiplicativity $\vert \vert A_{cl}^{k-1-i} \vert \vert \leq \rho(A_{cl})^{k-1-i}$:

$$
\vert\vert x(k) \vert\vert \leq \rho(A_{cl})^k \vert\vert x(0) \vert\vert + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \rho(A_{cl})^{k-1-i} \vert\vert BK \vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert x(i - d_i) \vert\vert
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the discrete Gronwall–Bellman inequality.**

$$
\vert\vert x(k) \vert\vert \leq \rho(A_{cl})^k \vert\vert x(0) \vert\vert \cdot e^{\vert\vert BK \vert\vert \, d_{max} \, k}
$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic stability condition.**
For $x(k) \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$:

$$
\rho(A_{cl}) \cdot e^{\vert\vert BK \vert\vert \, d_{max}} < 1
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the MADB.**

$$
\boxed{d_{max} < \frac{-\ln(\rho(A + BK))}{\vert\vert BK \vert\vert}}
$$

**Physical significance:** This bound dictates the **maximum tolerable network delay**. For example, if $\rho(A_{cl}) = 0.9$ and $\vert\vert BK \vert\vert = 0.05$, then $d_{max} \approx 2.1$ s, suitable for HVAC control over LoRaWAN. However, for bilateral tele-surgery with $d_{max} < 5$ ms, the controller gain $K$ and sampling period $h$ must be redesigned to satisfy the bound, typically requiring 5G URLLC or dedicated fiber.

*(7 marks — 1 each for steps 1–4, 1 for step 5, 1 for step 6, 1 for physical significance)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Describe any four real-world applications of Networked Control Systems. For each, identify the network medium, typical delay bound, and the key control challenge. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| **Application** | **Network Medium** | **Delay Bound** | **Key Control Challenge** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Grid (Wide-Area Monitoring)** | Fiber-optic + 4G/5G | 50–100 ms | Inter-area oscillation damping with PMU-based WAMS |
| **Autonomous Vehicle Platoons** | DSRC (802.11p), 5G-V2X | 1–10 ms | String stability with constant spacing policy |
| **Industrial SCADA / DCS** | Ethernet/IP, Profinet, WirelessHART | 1–10 ms | Handling deterministic scheduling in mixed-criticality traffic |
| **UAV Swarms for Surveillance** | Ad-hoc Wi-Fi, 5G, MAVLink | 5–50 ms | Maintaining connectivity in mobile ad-hoc topology |
| **Remote Robotic Surgery** | Dedicated fiber, 5G URLLC | 1–5 ms | Bilateral tele-operation with haptic feedback and delay compensation |
| **Smart Building HVAC** | BACnet, ZigBee, LoRaWAN | 50–500 ms | Energy-optimal control under packet loss and slow dynamics |

**Key control challenges explained briefly:**
- **Smart Grid:** Time-varying delay, cyber-attack resilience, large-scale distributed control.
- **Vehicle Platoons:** String stability (disturbance amplification along the platoon), time-varying vehicle dynamics.
- **SCADA:** Mixed traffic priorities, integration of legacy serial protocols with modern TCP/IP networks.
- **UAV Swarms:** Link intermittency due to mobility, bandwidth-limited multi-hop routing.

*(7 marks — 1.5 each for two well-explained applications, 1 for the remaining two, 0.5 for synthesis)*

---

**Q. (b)** With a suitable diagram, describe the architecture of a Smart Grid Wide-Area NCS. Explain how PMU data is used for real-time monitoring and control of inter-area oscillations. Discuss the role of Wide-Area Damping Controllers (WADCs) and the impact of communication delay on their performance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture of Smart Grid Wide-Area NCS:**

A smart grid NCS consists of geographically dispersed power generators equipped with **Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs)** that sample voltage and current phasors at **30–60 samples/second** (synchronized via GPS). PMU data is aggregated at **Phasor Data Concentrators (PDCs)** and transmitted over a **fiber-optic / 4G / 5G wide-area network** to a **Central Control Center** hosting the **Energy Management System (EMS)** and **SCADA**. The control center computes damping signals and dispatches them to **local substations** controlling **FACTS devices, HVDC links, and transformer tap changers**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    genC[Power Generator] --> pmuC[PMU Sampled at 30 to 60 fps]
    pmuC --> pdcC[Phasor Data Concentrator]
    pdcC --> wanC[Wan Fiber Optic Backbone]
    wanC --> cccC[Central Control Center EMS SCADA]
    cccC --> wanC
    wanC --> subC[Substation Local Controller]
    subC --> factsC[FACTS HVDC and Tap Changer]
    factsC --> genC
    cccC --> wadcC[WADC Robust H infinity Damping]
    wadcC --> cccC
```

**PMU-based real-time monitoring:**
PMUs provide **synchronized, time-stamped phasor measurements** that enable real-time observability of the entire interconnection. The **Wide-Area Monitoring System (WAMS)** detects low-frequency inter-area oscillations (**0.1–0.8 Hz**) using modal analysis (e.g., Prony analysis, Hankel Singular Values) and triggers control actions.

**Role of WADCs:**
Wide-Area Damping Controllers use remote PMU signals (e.g., tie-line power flows, bus frequencies) to synthesize supplementary control inputs to **PSS (Power System Stabilizers)**, **FACTS**, or **HVDC** modulators. The objective is to add damping torque to the inter-area modes, pushing them into the left half-plane.

**Impact of communication delay:**
Wide-area communication introduces **time delays of 50–100 ms** (sometimes up to 500 ms over legacy SCADA links). This delay:
- **Reduces phase margin** of the WADC and can destabilize the system if the controller is designed without delay compensation.
- Requires **delay-dependent robust control** techniques (e.g., $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ synthesis, $\mu$-analysis, or predictor-based compensation) to guarantee robust damping across the worst-case delay.
- In modern **5G-enabled grids**, URLLC reduces delay to **<10 ms**, dramatically expanding the feasible bandwidth of WADCs and improving damping performance by **30–50%**.

*(7 marks — 2 for architecture and diagram, 2 for PMU/WADC explanation, 2 for delay impact, 1 for synthesis)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse NCS with conventional control systems** — In NCS, the network itself is part of the closed loop, not merely a transport layer. Marks are deducted if this is not explicitly stated.
> 2. **MADB derivation is graded strictly step-by-step** — Skipping the Gronwall–Bellman step results in partial credit only. Always show the inequality $\rho(A_{cl}) \cdot e^{\|BK\| d_{max}} < 1$ explicitly.
> 3. **In Part B sub-part (b) on Smart Grid**, students frequently forget to mention the **GPS time-synchronization** aspect of PMUs, which is essential to their operation.
> 4. **Lyapunov function must be positive definite** — A common error is choosing $V = x^T x$ without showing $P \succ 0$.
> 5. **Event-triggered conditions require both triggering error and state threshold** — Writing only one is incomplete.
> 6. **Industrial network names (Profinet, EtherNet/IP, WirelessHART) must be spelled correctly** — Spelling errors are penalized.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NCS Definition:** A feedback control system with sensors, controllers, and actuators communicating over a shared network, characterized by delay ($\tau_k$), packet loss ($\gamma$), and bandwidth ($B$).
- **MADB Formula:** $\tau_{MADB} = \frac{-\ln(\rho(A_{cl}))}{\|BK\|}$ — Determines the maximum tolerable network delay for stability.
- **Lyapunov Stability LMI:** $\Phi^T P \Phi - P \prec 0$ with $\Phi = \begin{bmatrix} A & A_d \\ I & 0 \end{bmatrix}$ — Certifies delay-dependent stability.
- **Event-Triggered Threshold:** $\|e(k)\|^2 \leq \sigma^2 \|x(k)\|^2$ with $0 < \sigma < 1$ — Reduces network traffic by 70–90%.
- **Industrial Network Latency Benchmarks:** CAN ≈ 1 ms @ 1 Mbps; Profinet ≈ 1 ms; WirelessHART ≈ 100 ms; 5G URLLC ≤ 1 ms; LoRaWAN ≈ 1–5 s.
- **Key Applications:** Smart grid (PMU/WADC, 50–100 ms), autonomous vehicles (V2X, 1–10 ms), UAV swarms (ad-hoc, 5–50 ms), industrial SCADA (1–10 ms), tele-surgery (1–5 ms).
- **Lyapunov candidate:** $V(x) = x^T P x$, $P = P^T \succ 0$ — Always state positive definiteness.
- **Cyber-Physical Threats:** False data injection, replay attack, DoS — must be addressed in modern NCS design via residual-based detection ($|y - C\hat{x}| > \delta_{th}$).
- **Industry 4.0 Backbone:** OPC UA + TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) over Ethernet, enabling microsecond-level synchronization for mixed-criticality traffic.
- **Data-Rate Theorem:** $R > \log_2 \det(I + B^T P B)$ — Minimum bit-rate for stabilization, central to bandwidth-limited NCS design.
- **Packet Loss Stability Bound:** $\gamma < 1 - \rho(A_{cl})$ — Tolerable drop rate for asymptotic stability.
- **Schur Complement Trick:** Convert the Lyapunov inequality $\Phi^T P \Phi - P \prec 0$ into an LMI of the form $\begin{bmatrix} -P^{-1} & \Phi \\ \Phi^T & -P \end{bmatrix} \prec 0$.
- **Industrial Standards:** IEC 62443 (security), IEEE C37.118 (PMU), ISA 88/95 (batch control), VDA 5050 (AGV), ETCS (railways).
- **Real-World NCS Trade-off:** Lower delay → higher cost (5G URLLC vs. Wi-Fi); higher robustness → conservative performance; more security → more computation.
- **Exam Tip:** Always pair a *block diagram* with a *mathematical model* in 14-mark answers. KTU examiners allocate ~2 marks for diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mathematical Modeling of NCS as a Switched System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mathematical Modeling of NCS as a Switched System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a spatially distributed control system wherein the sensors, controllers, and actuators are interconnected through a shared (often bandwidth-limited and unreliable) communication network. Because the network induces phenomena such as variable time-delays, packet dropouts, quantization, and scheduling constraints, the closed-loop dynamics of an NCS cannot be captured by a single linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Switched System Formulation (KTU 2024 Module 3 — Core Definition)**
> 
> An NCS can be rigorously modeled as a **discrete-time switched system** of the form
> $$x(k+1) = A_{\sigma(k)}\, x(k) + B_{\sigma(k)}\, u(k)$$
> $$y(k) = C_{\sigma(k)}\, x(k)$$
> where $\sigma(k) : \mathbb{Z}^{+} \rightarrow \mathcal{S} = \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$ is the **switching signal** that selects, at every sampling instant $k$, one of $N$ possible subsystem dynamics $\{A_i, B_i, C_i\}$ corresponding to the prevailing network condition (delay interval, packet-loss state, or active node under a medium-access protocol).

The pair $(x_0, \sigma)$ with $x_0 = x(0)$ and a switching sequence $\sigma = (\sigma(0), \sigma(1), \dots)$ uniquely determines the trajectory of the system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Traffic-Signal Intersection"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> 
> Imagine a four-way intersection controlled by a traffic signal. The cars flowing through obey a **single set of physical laws** (Newtonian motion), but the *control rule* changes abruptly when the light turns green, amber, or red. Each light state corresponds to a different "mode" of operation. From the outside observer, the *combined* car–light system behaves like a **switched system** — one underlying physical process, but a finite number of "rules" that govern its evolution.
> 
> An NCS works exactly the same way. The plant keeps its own physics, but the **network** keeps flipping the rulebook:
> - When the network delay is **small** → subsystem mode 1 (plant + fresh data)
> - When the network delay is **large** → subsystem mode 2 (plant + stale data)
> - When a **packet is dropped** → subsystem mode 3 (controller holds last input)
> 
> Therefore, the closed-loop NCS is *naturally* a switched system with $N$ modes indexed by network conditions.

## 1.3 Why an NCS is Necessarily a Switched System

| Network Phenomenon | Resulting Mode-Switch | Discrete Mode Index |
|---|---|---|
| Delay $\tau \in [0, h)$ | Single-step fresh data | $\sigma = 1$ |
| Delay $\tau \in [h, 2h)$ | Two-step old data | $\sigma = 2$ |
| Delay $\tau \in [(N-1)h, Nh)$ | $N$-step old data | $\sigma = N$ |
| Packet dropout | Zero-input hold | $\sigma = N+1$ |
| Quantization level $\ell$ | Saturated/quantized mode | $\sigma = N+1+\ell$ |

## 1.4 Three Canonical Switching Scenarios in NCS

1. **Delay-triggered switching** — $\sigma$ depends on the interval into which the current round-trip delay falls.
2. **Event-triggered / packet-loss switching** — $\sigma$ toggles between "data-arrived" and "data-dropped" sub-dynamics.
3. **Protocol-based switching** — Under Round-Robin (RR) or Try-Once-Discard (TOD), $\sigma$ selects the active sensor/actuator node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Board Valuations**
> 
> The number of subsystem modes $N$ is computed as $N = \lceil \tau_{\max} / h \rceil + 1$, where $\tau_{\max}$ is the maximum allowable delay bound and $h$ is the sampling period. This single number is the **first marking checkpoint** in any KTU 14-mark derivation.

## 1.5 Learning Outcomes Addressed (CO Mapping)

| KTU 2024 Course Outcome | Coverage in this Topic |
|---|---|
| **CO3** — Analyze NCS under network imperfections | Switched-system abstraction of delay/dropout |
| **CO4** — Apply stability tools to NCS | Lyapunov-based switched-stability analysis |
| **CO5** — Design controllers using switching framework | Dwell-time-based controller synthesis |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of NCS-to-Switched-System Mapping

The transformation from a "physical NCS" to a "switched-system model" follows a deterministic four-step pipeline:

- **Step 1 — Continuous-time plant model.** Start with the nominal plant $\dot{x}_p(t) = A_p x_p(t) + B_p u(t)$ controlled over a network that introduces a *time-varying* delay $\tau(t) \in [0, \tau_{\max}]$.
- **Step 2 — Quantize the delay.** Partition the interval $[0, \tau_{\max}]$ into $N$ disjoint sub-intervals $\mathcal{I}_1, \mathcal{I}_2, \dots, \mathcal{I}_N$ of length $h$ (sampling period). Each sub-interval defines a discrete mode.
- **Step 3 — Discretize the augmented state.** Define an augmented state $x(k) = [x_p^T(k) \;\; x_p^T(k-1) \;\; \dots \;\; x_p^T(k-N+1)]^T$ so that the previous delayed inputs can be retrieved from the state itself. Discretize each continuous subsystem using a zero-order hold.
- **Step 4 — Define the switching signal.** $\sigma(k) = i \iff \tau(k) \in \mathcal{I}_i$. The mapping $\sigma$ is exogenous (driven by the network), and the resulting closed-loop system is the **discrete-time switched system** with $N$ modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for the KTU exam?**
> 
> Almost every 14-mark question in this module begins with a continuous-time plant + a delay interval. The examiner expects the student to *recognize* the multi-mode structure, *augment* the state, and *produce* the $(A_i, B_i)$ matrices. Skipping Step 3 (state augmentation) costs a full 3–4 marks.

## 2.2 Mathematical Architecture of the Discrete-Time Switched NCS

For a plant $\dot{x}_p(t) = A_p x_p(t) + B_p u(t)$ with sampled-data controller $u(k) = K x_p(k - d_k)$ where $d_k \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$ is the integer delay at step $k$:

- **State augmentation:**

$$x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_p(k) \\ x_p(k-1) \\ \vdots \\ x_p(k-N+1) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{nN}$$

- **Subsystem matrix $A_i$ for delay $d = i-1$:**

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & \Gamma K & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ I & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & I & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & \cdots & 0 & I & 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{nN \times nN}$$

- **Where:**

$$\Phi = e^{A_p h}, \quad \Gamma = \int_0^h e^{A_p s}\, ds\, B_p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each subsystem $A_i$ is a **shifted upper-block-triangular** matrix. Only the *first block row* changes with $i$; the lower block rows are identical for all $i$. This structural property is the key to applying common-Lyapunov techniques efficiently.

## 2.3 Switching-Signal Constraints

A switching signal $\sigma$ is called **admissible** if it satisfies one of the following engineering constraints:

| Constraint | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Dwell time (DT)** | $t_{k+1} - t_k \geq \tau_d$ | Mode must remain active for at least $\tau_d$ steps |
| **Average dwell time (ADT)** | $N_\sigma(k_2, k_1) \leq (k_2 - k_1)/\tau_a + N_0$ | Switching is "slow on the average" |
| **Range dwell time** | $\tau_d \leq t_{k+1} - t_k \leq \tau_D$ | Min and max duration both bounded |
| **Markovian** | $\Pr(\sigma_{k+1} = j \mid \sigma_k = i) = \pi_{ij}$ | Stochastic switching governed by TPM $\Pi$ |

where $N_\sigma(k_2, k_1)$ is the number of discontinuities (switches) of $\sigma$ on the interval $(k_1, k_2]$ and $N_0 \geq 0$ is the chatter bound.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switched NCS state equation | $x(k+1) = A_{\sigma(k)} x(k) + B_{\sigma(k)} u(k)$ | $\sigma(k) \in \mathcal{S}$ |
| 2 | Number of delay-induced modes | $N = \lceil \tau_{\max}/h \rceil + 1$ | $\tau_{\max}$, $h$ |
| 3 | Continuous-to-discrete plant | $\Phi = e^{A_p h}, \quad \Gamma = \int_0^h e^{A_p s} B_p \, ds$ | ZOH discretization |
| 4 | Augmented subsystem | $A_i$ has $\Gamma K$ in block $(1, i+1)$ and $I$ in $(j+1, j)$ | $i = 0, \dots, N-1$ |
| 5 | Common Lyapunov (CLF) condition | $A_i^T P A_i - P \prec 0, \quad P \succ 0, \quad \forall i$ | Quadratic stability |
| 6 | Multiple Lyapunov condition | $A_i^T P_i A_i - P_i \prec 0, \quad \forall i$ | Mode-wise stability |
| 7 | Dwell time (sufficient) | $\tau_d > \ln \mu / \alpha$ | $\alpha = -\ln(\max_i \vert \lambda_{\max}(A_i^T A_i) \vert)$ |
| 8 | Average dwell time (sufficient) | $\tau_a > \tau_a^{*} = \ln \mu / a$ | $a = \min_i (-\ln \lambda_{\max}(A_i^T P_i A_i / P_i))$ |
| 9 | ADT decay condition | $V_{\sigma(k)}(x_k) \leq \mu^a V_{\sigma(k-1)}(x_{k-1})$ | $a \in (0,1)$, $\mu \geq 1$ |
| 10 | LMI for ADT stability | $\begin{bmatrix} -P_i & \sqrt{\mu}\,A_i^T P_j \\ \sqrt{\mu}\,P_j A_i & -P_j \end{bmatrix} \prec 0$ | Cross-mode bound |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial process automation** — Fieldbus (PROFIBUS, EtherCAT) networks naturally produce mode-switched dynamics; switched-system analysis yields *guaranteed* stability margins even under worst-case delay.
- **Vehicle platooning and autonomous driving** — V2V communication suffers intermittent dropouts; the longitudinal controller is designed as a switched system with "connected" and "isolated" modes.
- **Smart-grid secondary control** — Distributed generators communicate over a shared wireless bus; the secondary controller is a switched system with $N$ modes indexed by link quality.
- **Aerospace flight control** — Fly-by-wire buses (e.g., MIL-STD-1553) have deterministic time-triggered switching; the *exact* switched-system model is used for clearance certification.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Continuous NCS to Switched Discrete Model

**Given:** Linear plant $\dot{x}_p(t) = A_p x_p(t) + B_p u(t)$ controlled over a network with sampling period $h$ and bounded time-varying delay $\tau(t) \in [0, (N-1)h]$.

**Assumption:** The network implements a *time-stamp* mechanism so that at instant $k$, the controller computes $u(k) = K x_p(k - d_k)$ where $d_k = \lfloor \tau(k)/h \rfloor \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$.

### Step 1 — Discretize the open-loop plant

Using a zero-order hold on the input, the exact discrete-time plant sampled at instants $t_k = kh$ is

$$x_p(k+1) = \Phi \, x_p(k) + \Gamma \, u(k)$$

where

$$\Phi = e^{A_p h}, \quad \Gamma = \int_{0}^{h} e^{A_p s} B_p \, ds$$

**Conversion logic:** The exact solution of $\dot{x}_p = A_p x_p + B_p u$ with $u$ constant on $[kh, (k+1)h)$ is $x_p((k+1)h) = e^{A_p h} x_p(kh) + \int_{kh}^{(k+1)h} e^{A_p ((k+1)h - s)} B_p u(s)\, ds$. Substituting $u(s) = u(k)$ and changing variable $\eta = (k+1)h - s$ gives the stated $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$.

### Step 2 — Inject the network-induced delay

If $d_k = i - 1$, then $u(k) = K x_p(k - (i-1))$. Hence

$$x_p(k+1) = \Phi x_p(k) + \Gamma K x_p(k - (i-1))$$

**Conversion logic:** The plant "sees" the control input that was computed $i-1$ samples ago, because of the network delay.

### Step 3 — Augment the state vector

To express the delayed plant as a Markovian state equation, define

$$x(k) = \begin{bmatrix} x_p(k) \\ x_p(k-1) \\ x_p(k-2) \\ \vdots \\ x_p(k-N+1) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Conversion logic:** We need $N$ memory slots to remember the plant state from $N-1$ samples back. The augmented state has dimension $nN$ where $n = \dim(x_p)$.

### Step 4 — Write the switched-system equation

Substituting the delay model and augmenting:

$$x(k+1) = A_{\sigma(k)} x(k)$$

with

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & 0 & \cdots & 0 & \Gamma K & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ I & 0 & \cdots & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & I & \cdots & 0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & I & 0 & 0 & \cdots & 0 \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{nN \times nN}$$

The non-zero off-diagonal block $\Gamma K$ appears in the $(1, i+1)$-th block position because the delay equals $i-1$ samples.

### Step 5 — Define the switching law

$$\sigma(k) = i \iff d_k = i - 1 \iff \tau(k) \in [(i-1)h, \, ih)$$

The switching signal is **piecewise constant** and right-continuous by convention.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Common Lyapunov Function (CLF) Stability Test

**Claim:** If there exists a positive-definite matrix $P \succ 0$ such that

$$A_i^T P A_i - P \prec 0, \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{S}$$

then the switched system $x(k+1) = A_{\sigma(k)} x(k)$ is **globally uniformly exponentially stable (GUES)** under *arbitrary* switching.

**Proof:**

- **Step 1 — Define a candidate Lyapunov function.**

$$V(x(k)) = x(k)^T P x(k), \quad P \succ 0$$

Because $P \succ 0$, we have $\lambda_{\min}(P) \Vert x \Vert^2 \leq V(x) \leq \lambda_{\max}(P) \Vert x \Vert^2$.

- **Step 2 — Compute the one-step decrement.**

$$\Delta V = V(x(k+1)) - V(x(k)) = x(k)^T (A_{\sigma(k)}^T P A_{\sigma(k)} - P) x(k)$$

- **Step 3 — Apply the assumed inequality.**

Let $Q_i = P - A_i^T P A_i \succ 0$ for all $i \in \mathcal{S}$. Pick $\alpha = \min_i \lambda_{\min}(Q_i / P) > 0$ so that $Q_i \geq \alpha P$. Then

$$\Delta V \leq -\alpha V(x(k))$$

- **Step 4 — Recursive bound.**

Iterating the inequality:

$$V(x(k)) \leq (1 - \alpha)^k V(x(0)) \leq e^{-\alpha k} V(x(0))$$

- **Step 5 — Conclusion.**

Since $V(x(k)) \geq \lambda_{\min}(P) \Vert x(k) \Vert^2$,

$$\Vert x(k) \Vert^2 \leq \frac{\lambda_{\max}(P)}{\lambda_{\min}(P)} e^{-\alpha k} \Vert x(0) \Vert^2$$

Therefore, $x(k) \to 0$ exponentially fast for *any* switching signal $\sigma$. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the CLF test restrictive?**
> 
> The CLF condition requires a *single* $P$ to work for *all* $N$ modes simultaneously. For NCS with large $N$ (long delay bounds), this is often infeasible. In such cases, one must weaken the assumption to **multiple Lyapunov functions** or to **dwell-time-bounded switching**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Dwell Time (ADT) Sufficient Condition

**Setup:** Each mode $i$ has its own Lyapunov function $V_i(x) = x^T P_i x$ with $P_i \succ 0$, and there exist scalars $\mu \geq 1$, $0 < a < 1$ such that

$$V_i(x) \leq \mu V_j(x), \quad \forall i, j \in \mathcal{S}$$

$$V_i(A_i x) \leq (1 - a) V_i(x), \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{S}$$

**Claim:** If the switching signal $\sigma$ satisfies the ADT constraint

$$\tau_a > \tau_a^{*} = -\frac{\ln \mu}{\ln(1 - a)} = \frac{\ln \mu}{a'}$$

with $a' = -\ln(1 - a) > 0$, then the switched system is GUES.

**Proof Sketch:**

- **Step 1 — Trajectory between two switches.** Suppose mode $i_k$ is active from step $k_k$ to $k_{k+1} - 1$. Let $\tau_k = k_{k+1} - k_k$. Then

$$V_{i_k}(x(k_{k+1})) \leq (1 - a)^{\tau_k} V_{i_k}(x(k_k))$$

- **Step 2 — Cross-mode Lyapunov growth at the switch.** At step $k_{k+1}$ the mode changes, so

$$V_{i_{k+1}}(x(k_{k+1})) \leq \mu V_{i_k}(x(k_{k+1})) \leq \mu (1 - a)^{\tau_k} V_{i_k}(x(k_k))$$

- **Step 3 — Chain the inequalities.** Telescoping from $k_0 = 0$ to $k_N = k$:

$$V_{\sigma(k)}(x(k)) \leq \mu^{N_\sigma(0,k)} (1 - a)^k V_{\sigma(0)}(x(0))$$

- **Step 4 — Apply the ADT bound.** By definition, $N_\sigma(0, k) \leq k/\tau_a + N_0$. Substituting:

$$V_{\sigma(k)}(x(k)) \leq \mu^{k/\tau_a + N_0} (1 - a)^k V_{\sigma(0)}(x(0)) = \mu^{N_0} \left[ \mu^{1/\tau_a} (1 - a) \right]^k V_{\sigma(0)}(x(0))$$

- **Step 5 — Exponent negativity.** The bracket is $< 1$ iff $\mu^{1/\tau_a} (1 - a) < 1$ iff $\tau_a > \ln \mu / (-\ln(1 - a))$. This is precisely the ADT condition. Hence $V$ decays exponentially and the system is GUES. ∎

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — LMI for Joint ADT + Controller Synthesis

A KTU-favorite problem: jointly design the controller gain $K$ and the ADT bound $\tau_a$ such that the closed-loop switched NCS is stable.

**Decision variables:** $P_i \succ 0$, $Q_i = P_i^{-1} \succ 0$, $Y$ (controller proxy such that $K = Y Q^{-1}$), scalar $\mu \geq 1$, $a \in (0,1)$.

**LMI (per mode $i$):**

$$\begin{bmatrix} -Q_i & \sqrt{\mu}\,(\Phi Q_i + \Gamma Y_i)^T \\ \sqrt{\mu}\,(\Phi Q_i + \Gamma Y_i) & -Q_j \end{bmatrix} \prec 0, \quad \forall (i, j) \in \mathcal{S} \times \mathcal{S}$$

**Interpretation:** The LMI enforces the cross-mode Lyapunov growth $V_j(x_{k+1}) \leq \mu V_i(x_k)$ when switching from $i$ to $j$.

**Recover $K$:** $K_i = Y_i Q_i^{-1}$ for each mode $i$, giving a *mode-dependent* controller $u(k) = K_{\sigma(k)} x(k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**
> 
> The LMI above is *bilinear* in $P_i$ and $P_j$ if written in the original (uninverted) form. The change of variable $Q = P^{-1}$ is **mandatory** to convert it into a standard LMI tractable by SeDuMi/MOSEK. Forgetting this step costs 3 marks.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Plant:** $\dot{x}_p = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & -0.5 \end{bmatrix} x_p + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} u$, with $h = 0.1$ s, $\tau_{\max} = 0.2$ s, $K = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$.

- **Step 1 — Compute $\Phi$ and $\Gamma$.**

$$\Phi = e^{A_p h} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.09516 \\ 0 & 0.95123 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \Gamma = \int_0^{0.1} e^{A_p s} B_p\, ds = \begin{bmatrix} 0.00484 \\ 0.09516 \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Step 2 — Number of modes.** $N = \lceil 0.2 / 0.1 \rceil + 1 = 3$. Modes correspond to $d = 0, 1, 2$.

- **Step 3 — Augmented state.** $x(k) \in \mathbb{R}^{6}$ with $x(k) = [x_p^T(k) \;\; x_p^T(k-1) \;\; x_p^T(k-2)]^T$.

- **Step 4 — Sub-matrices $A_1$ (delay 0), $A_2$ (delay 1), $A_3$ (delay 2):**

$$A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & \Gamma K & 0 \\ I & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I & 0 \end{bmatrix}_{6 \times 6}, \quad A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & 0 & \Gamma K \\ I & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A_3 = \begin{bmatrix} \Phi & 0 & 0 \\ I & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & I & 0 \end{bmatrix} + \text{path through } A_2, A_3$$

- **Step 5 — Test CLF feasibility.** Solving the joint LMI yields $P = \text{diag}(P_1, P_2)$ with smallest eigenvalue $0.42$, confirming GUES under arbitrary switching.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Switched-System NCS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Plant["Plant (Continuous Time)"]
    Sensor["Sensor Node"]
    NetworkA["Network Channel (Delay tau k)"]
    Quantizer["Quantizer / Packet Loss Detector"]
    ModeSel["Mode Selection Logic (sigma k)"]
    CtrlBank["Controller Bank (K1, K2, ..., KN)"]
    Mux["Mux Switch"]
    ZOH["Zero Order Hold"]
    Out["Actuator Output"]

    Plant --> Sensor
    Sensor --> NetworkA
    NetworkA --> Quantizer
    Quantizer --> ModeSel
    ModeSel -->|selects K sigma k| CtrlBank
    CtrlBank --> Mux
    Mux --> ZOH
    ZOH --> Out
    Out --> Plant

    ModeSel -.->|sigma k in S| Mux

    subgraph "Discrete Time Augmented State x k in R nN"
        State1["x p k"]
        State2["x p k minus 1"]
        State3["x p k minus N plus 1"]
        State1 --> State2 --> State3
    end

    Mux --> State1
    State3 -->|feedback to mode bank| CtrlBank
```

## 4.2 Mode-Switching State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Mode1: Initialization with tau in 0 h
    Mode1 --> Mode2: tau k crosses h
    Mode1 --> Mode3: tau k crosses 2h
    Mode2 --> Mode1: tau k drops below h
    Mode2 --> Mode3: tau k crosses 2h
    Mode3 --> Mode2: tau k drops below 2h
    Mode3 --> Mode1: tau k drops below h
    Mode1 --> Dropout: Packet loss detected
    Mode2 --> Dropout: Packet loss detected
    Mode3 --> Dropout: Packet loss detected
    Dropout --> Mode1: New packet received
    Dropout --> Mode2: New packet received
    Dropout --> Mode3: New packet received
```

## 4.3 Lyapunov Decay Across Switching Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: V sigma 0 x 0 = V0"]
    ModeI["Active mode i: V decreases as 1 minus a raised to tau k"]
    Switch["At switch k plus 1: V increases by factor mu"]
    ModeJ["Active mode j: V decreases again"]
    Decay["Total V sigma k x k bounded by mu N sigma times 1 minus a raised to k times V0"]
    Stop["GTES iff tau a greater than ln mu divided by a prime"]

    Start --> ModeI
    ModeI --> Switch
    Switch --> ModeJ
    ModeJ --> Decay
    Decay --> Stop
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NCS["NCS Physical Layer"]
    D["Variable Network Delay tau k in 0 tau max"]
    P["Packet Dropout Indicator delta k in 0 1"]
    Q["Quantization Level q k in 1 to L"]
    S["Scheduling Protocol Round Robin or TOD"]

    NCS --> D
    NCS --> P
    NCS --> Q
    NCS --> S

    D -->|"partition into N intervals"| M1["Subsystem A i for delay in I i"]
    P -->|"binary state"| M2["Subsystem A drop when delta k is 0"]
    Q -->|"L discrete levels"| M3["L sub systems with quantized input"]
    S -->|"M nodes scheduled"| M4["M sub systems with one active node"]

    M1 --> Switched["Unified Switched System x k plus 1 is A sigma k x k"]
    M2 --> Switched
    M3 --> Switched
    M4 --> Switched

    Switched --> Analyze["Stability via CLF or ADT"]
    Analyze --> Design["Mode Dependent Controller Design"]
```
