The LRF theorem states that asymptotic stability holds if there exist $\sigma > 1$ and $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3 > 0$ such that: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha_1 \lVert x(k) \rVert^2 \leq V(x(k)) \leq \alpha_2 \lVert x(k) \rVert^2 \\ \Delta V(x(k)) \leq -\alpha_3 \lVert x(k) \rVert^2 \quad \text{whenever} \quad V(x(k+\theta)) \leq \sigma V(x(k)) \quad \forall \theta \in [-\bar{\tau}, 0] \end{aligned} $$ **Comparison with LKF**: | Feature | Lyapunov–Krasovskii (LKF) | Lyapunov–Razumikhin (LRF) | |---------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | Function structure | Functional of state segment | Scalar function of current state | | Conservatism | Lower (more accurate) | Higher (more conservative) | | Delay types | Constant, bounded, time-varying | Best for time-varying | | LMI feasibility | Easier | Harder | | Computational cost | Higher (more decision variables) | Lower | **Conclusion**: LKF is preferred when an LMI formulation is desired for controller synthesis; LRF is preferred for theoretical proofs of stability under arbitrary time-varying delays. **(7 marks — 2 for LRF statement, 3 for comparison table, 2 for conclusion)** #### Model Solution to Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute $\rho(A)$**: The eigenvalues of $A = \begin{bmatrix} 0.95 & 0.1 \\ 0 & 0.92 \end{bmatrix}$ are the diagonal entries (lower-triangular): $\lambda_1 = 0.95$, $\lambda_2 = 0.92$. Therefore $\rho(A) = 0.95$. **Step 2 — Compute the Allowable Dropout Bound**: $$\bar{p}_{\max} = 1 - \rho(A) = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$$ **Step 3 — Compare with Actual Dropout**: The actual dropout rate is $p = 0.2$, which is **four times larger** than $\bar{p}_{\max} = 0.05$. The sufficient condition **fails**, meaning the conservative test cannot certify stability at $p = 0.2$. However, this is a **sufficient but not necessary** condition, so the system may still be stable in practice — but no guarantee can be issued from this analysis alone. **Step 4 — Effect of Increasing $\bar{\tau}$ from 3 to 6**: The LKF analysis in Section 3.1 shows that $\Phi$ depends on $\bar{\tau}$ in three places: the $-\tfrac{1}{\bar{\tau}} R$ terms, the $+\bar{\tau}(A-I)^T R (A-I)$ term, and the integral bounds. Increasing $\bar{\tau}$: - **Reduces** the magnitude of $-\tfrac{1}{\bar{\tau}} R$ (less negative, destabilizing). - **Increases** the $\bar{\tau}(A-I)^T R (A-I)$ term (more destabilizing, as it scales the cross-term penalty). - **Tightens** the feasible set of $(P, Q, R)$, so the LMI may become infeasible for large $\bar{\tau}$. **Net Effect**: Doubling $\bar{\tau}$ from 3 to 6 typically **shrinks the region of robust stability** and may even render the LMI infeasible, forcing the designer to either (i) reduce the controller gain $\lVert K \rVert$, (ii) shorten the sampling period $T_s$, or (iii) adopt an event-triggered transmission strategy. **(7 marks — 2 for eigenvalue computation, 2 for bound comparison, 3 for $\bar{\tau}$ effect discussion)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 Valuation** > 1. **Skipping the LKF statement** (–1 to –2 marks): Examiners expect the student to explicitly write $V(k) = V_1 + V_2 + V_3$ with the matrices. Writing only "by Lyapunov's method" is not acceptable. > 2. **Wrong sign in the forward difference** (–2 marks): $\Delta V = V(k+1) - V(k) \leq -\alpha \lVert x \rVert^2$. Forgetting the negative sign inverts the stability conclusion. > 3. **Confusing BMI with LMI** (–3 marks): The matrix $\Phi$ is linear in $P, Q, R$ but **bilinear** in $(P, K)$. Synthesis requires the change of variables $X = P^{-1}$, $Y = KX$. If the student presents $\Phi$ without this conversion, full synthesis marks are withheld. > 4. **Omitting Schur complement** (–2 marks): To convert $\begin{bmatrix} M_{11} & M_{12} \\ M_{12}^T & M_{22} \end{bmatrix} \prec 0$ into a pure LMI, the Schur complement step must be shown. > 5. **Mixing up $\bar{\tau}$ and $\mu$** (–1 mark): $\bar{\tau}$ is the **upper bound** of the delay; $\mu$ is the **upper bound of the delay derivative** $\Delta \tau(k)$. They are not interchangeable. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Three Pillars of NCS Stability**: (1) Lyapunov–Krasovskii Functional, (2) Lyapunov–Razumikhin Function, (3) ISS Small-Gain Theorem. - **The LKF has three terms**: $V_1$ (current state), $V_2$ (sum of past states), $V_3$ (sum of state increments). All three are required for time-varying delay analysis. - **The output of stability analysis is an LMI** $\Phi(P, Q, R) \prec 0$ that is **linear** in the decision variables, making it solvable by convex optimization. - **Controller synthesis** requires the substitution $X = P^{-1}$, $Y = KX$ to convert the BMI into an LMI; the recovered gain is $K = Y X^{-1}$. - **Packet dropout bound**: $\bar{p} < 1 - \rho(A)$ is a **sufficient (not necessary)** condition; failure to satisfy it does not automatically imply instability. - **H-infinity performance** adds the lower-right block $-\gamma^2 I$ and the cross-terms $A^T P E$ in the LMI; the disturbance attenuation $\gamma$ is minimized to improve robustness. - **Schur complement** is the standard tool to convert matrix inequalities into LMI form; always verify that $M_{11} \prec 0$ before applying it. - **Delay bound $\bar{\tau}$** directly controls conservatism: smaller $\bar{\tau}$ gives a less conservative result, larger $\bar{\tau}$ may render the LMI infeasible. - **Robustness vs. Stability**: Stability = nominal plant, $\Delta = 0$; Robustness = uncertain plant, $\Delta \neq 0$. Both must be tested using the **same LMI** with $A, A_d$ replaced by $A + \Delta A, A_d + \Delta A_d$ and bounded via the **S-procedure**. - **Industry Standards**: Modern NCS design uses MATLAB's **Robust Control Toolbox** (`hinfsyn`, `solvesdp`) or Python's **`cvxpy`** for SDP-based LMI solution. Always cross-validate the LMI result with a time-domain simulation under worst-case delay. - **KTU 2024 CO Mapping**: This topic maps to **CO3** of PECPT862 and is examined in **ESE Module 3** for 14 marks and in **CAT-2** for short-answer questions. ---

topics.title: Fractional Order Case - Problem Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fractional Order Case - Problem Formulation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Fractional-Order Networked Control System (FO-NCS)** is a closed-loop control architecture in which the physical plant, the controller, the sensors, and the actuators communicate over a shared digital communication network, and the plant dynamics (or the controller dynamics) are modeled using **fractional-order differential equations** of the form: $$D^{\alpha} x(t) = f(x(t), u(t), w(t)), \quad \alpha \in \mathbb{R}^+, \quad \alpha \in (0, 2)$$ where $D^{\alpha}$ denotes the **Caputo fractional derivative** of order $\alpha$, and $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ are the state and control vectors respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Formulation in the fractional-order case** refers to the rigorous mathematical procedure of converting the physical NCS description (plant + network delays + controller) into a **well-posed state-space model** expressed as a fractional differential equation augmented with delay terms, suitable for subsequent stability and controller-synthesis analysis. The three canonical fractional derivative definitions used in KTU-grade formulations are: | Operator | Definition (in Short) | Use in NCS | |----------|----------------------|------------| | **Caputo** $^C D_{0}^{\alpha}$ | Initial conditions take classical form | Plant modeling (preferred) | | **Riemann–Liouville** $^R D_{0}^{\alpha}$ | Singular kernel at $t=0$ | Theoretical analysis | | **Grünwald–Letnikov** $^G D_{0}^{\alpha}$ | Discrete approximation | Numerical simulation | ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Memory Foam" Plant > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a conventional car suspension as a sponge (integer-order system)** — it pushes back instantly in proportion to how hard you press it. Now **replace the sponge with memory foam (fractional-order system)** — when you press it, the foam "remembers" every past pressure and responds with a weighted average of all previous forces. This "memory" of past states is precisely what the fractional derivative captures. In a Networked Control System: - **Integer-order plant** → reacts only to *current* state. - **Fractional-order plant** → reacts to the **entire history** of states (with a fading memory kernel). - **Network delay** → further complicates this by adding another memory term from the communication channel. The **Problem Formulation** step is therefore the act of writing down the precise mathematical equation that combines: 1. The plant's *hereditary* (memory) behavior — the **fractional kernel** $t^{\alpha-1}/\Gamma(\alpha)$. 2. The network's *transport* behavior — the **delay term** $u(t-\tau)$. 3. The controller's *compensation* strategy — the **state predictor / memory feedback gain** $K$. > [!TIP] > **Why bother with fractional order?** Real-world systems such as **electrochemical batteries, viscoelastic polymers, thermal diffusion in semiconductors, and inductors with iron-core losses** exhibit dynamics that classical integer-order ODEs cannot capture accurately. KTU examiners love this angle — it directly tests whether you understand *why* a fractional formulation is *necessary*, not just *how* to write it. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Bold The fundamental quantities used throughout fractional-order NCS problem formulation are: - **$\alpha$** — Fractional order, dimensionless, typically $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ for sub-diffusive systems and $\alpha \in (1, 2)$ for super-diffusive wave-like systems. - **$\Gamma(\alpha)$** — Gamma function, $\Gamma(\alpha) = \int_0^{\infty} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-t} dt$, dimensionless. - **$E_{\alpha}(z)$** — One-parameter Mittag-Leffler function, dimensionless, $E_{\alpha}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + 1)}$. - **$E_{\alpha,\beta}(z)$** — Two-parameter Mittag-Leffler function, $E_{\alpha,\beta}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + \beta)}$. - **$\tau_{sc}$** — Sensor-to-Controller delay, measured in **seconds**. - **$\tau_{ca}$** — Controller-to-Actuator delay, measured in **seconds**. - **$\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$** — Total round-trip network-induced delay, measured in **seconds**. - **$h$** — Sampling period, measured in **seconds**. - **$\bar{\tau}$** — Upper bound on delay, satisfying $0 \leq \tau_k \leq \bar{\tau}$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the Problem Formulation The KTU 2024 syllabus requires the problem to be formulated in **five sequential stages**. Each stage is explained below with the "why" behind every step. **Stage 1 — Plant Description (Fractional-Order Linear Time-Invariant, FO-LTI):** - *Why:* We need a tractable mathematical model of the physical process. A linear fractional-order model is the natural generalization of the integer-order state equation. - *How:* The state $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ evolves as a weighted integral of all past states, governed by the Caputo derivative. **Stage 2 — Network Delay Modeling:** - *Why:* The communication network (CAN, Ethernet, Wireless, etc.) introduces a non-negligible time-varying delay that corrupts control performance. - *How:* The delay is decomposed into two parts (sensor-to-controller $\tau_{sc}$ and controller-to-actuator $\tau_{ca}$) and lumped into a single bound $\tau \in [0, \bar{\tau}]$ for analysis. **Stage 3 — Controller / Actuator Node Description:** - *Why:* The digital controller reads the delayed sensor data and computes a control signal that arrives late at the actuator. - *How:* A state-feedback law $u(t) = K \hat{x}(t - \tau)$ is applied, where $\hat{x}$ is the predicted state. **Stage 4 — State Predictor (Smith-Type for Fractional-Order):** - *Why:* To overcome the network delay, we must "look into the future" of the state trajectory using the known system model. - *How:* A Mittag-Leffler based predictor is constructed to estimate $x(t + \tau)$ from the history of inputs and the delayed state. **Stage 5 — Closed-Loop Fractional Delayed System (CDFDS):** - *Why:* We must now write down a single compact equation that represents the entire closed-loop FO-NCS — this is the deliverable of "Problem Formulation". - *How:* Substitute the control law into the plant equation and augment the state vector with delayed terms. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use | |---|--------------------|-------------------| | 1 | $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ | Fractional-order plant in Caputo sense | | 2 | $u(t) = K x(t - \tau)$ | State-feedback control with delay $\tau$ | | 3 | $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau)$ | Closed-loop FO-delayed system | | 4 | $\hat{x}(t+\tau) = \Phi_{\alpha}(\tau) x(t) + \int_{t-\tau}^{t} (t-s)^{\alpha-1} E_{\alpha,\alpha}(A(t-s)^{\alpha}) B u(s)\, ds$ | Fractional state predictor | | 5 | $\Phi_{\alpha}(\tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha})$ | Fractional state-transition matrix | | 6 | $E_{\alpha}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + 1)}$ | One-parameter Mittag-Leffler function | | 7 | $E_{\alpha,\beta}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + \beta)}$ | Two-parameter Mittag-Leffler function | | 8 | $G(s) = C(s^{\alpha} I - A)^{-1} B$ | Fractional-order transfer function | | 9 | $\mathcal{L}\{D^{\alpha} x(t)\} = s^{\alpha} X(s) - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} s^{\alpha - 1 - k} x^{(k)}(0^+)$ | Laplace transform of Caputo derivative | | 10 | Stability region: $\vert \arg(s) \vert < \frac{\alpha \pi}{2}$ | $\alpha$-stable region in complex $s$-plane | | 11 | $\dot{V}(x) \leq -\beta \Vert x \Vert^{\gamma}$, $\beta, \gamma > 0$ | Mittag-Leffler stability (Lyapunov-like) | | 12 | $\begin{bmatrix} X A^T + A X & X C^T \\ C X & -\gamma^2 I \end{bmatrix} < 0$ | LMI for fractional-order $H_{\infty}$ (general form) | | 13 | $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ | Total network-induced delay | | 14 | $D^{\alpha} x(t) \approx \dfrac{1}{h^{\alpha}} \sum_{j=0}^{\lfloor t/h \rfloor} (-1)^j \binom{\alpha}{j} x(t - j h)$ | Grünwald–Letnikov discretization | > [!WARNING] > **Strict KTU Table Formatting Rule:** The vertical bar `|` symbol (used in mathematical notation for *modulus* or *absolute value*) **breaks the markdown table renderer**. In the table above, every modulus expression is written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ for compatibility. **In your exam answer sheets, write $\vert \cdot \vert$ normally** — the KTU answer script is LaTeX-printed and not subject to this rendering issue. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Where FO-NCS Applies | |--------------------|--------------------| | **Electric Vehicles** | Battery state-of-charge estimation over CAN bus with fractional electrochemical dynamics | | **Process Control** | Long-distance oil pipeline control with significant communication delays and viscoelastic fluid behavior | | **Aerospace** | Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) swarm control over wireless links, where aerodynamic memory effects dominate | | **Biomedical** | Drug delivery infusion pumps with fractional pharmacokinetics and Wi-Fi controlled dosing | | **Smart Grid** | Distributed control of microgrids with communication-induced delays and fractional-order capacitor behavior | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Caputo Fractional Derivative Definition and Its Laplace Transform **Starting Point:** The Caputo fractional derivative of order $\alpha > 0$ for a sufficiently smooth function $x(t)$ is defined as: $$ ^C D_{0}^{\alpha} x(t) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(n - \alpha)} \int_{0}^{t} \frac{x^{(n)}(\tau)}{(t - \tau)^{\alpha - n + 1}} d\tau, \quad n - 1 < \alpha < n, \quad n \in \mathbb{N} $$ For $0 < \alpha < 1$ (the most common NCS case), this reduces to $n = 1$: $$ ^C D_{0}^{\alpha} x(t) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(1 - \alpha)} \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\dot{x}(\tau)}{(t - \tau)^{\alpha}} d\tau $$ **Step 1 — Apply the Laplace transform to the Caputo derivative:** Recall the standard Laplace transform of a convolution $f * g$ is $\mathcal{L}\{f * g\}(s) = F(s) G(s)$. The Caputo derivative is a convolution of $\dot{x}(t)$ with the kernel $\frac{t^{-\alpha}}{\Gamma(1-\alpha)}$. $$ \mathcal{L}\left\{^C D_{0}^{\alpha} x(t)\right\} = s^{\alpha} X(s) - s^{\alpha - 1} x(0^+) $$ **Step 2 — Extend this to the $n$-th order Caputo derivative** (used when $\alpha \in (1, 2)$): $$ \mathcal{L}\left\{^C D_{0}^{\alpha} x(t)\right\} = s^{\alpha} X(s) - \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} s^{\alpha - 1 - k} x^{(k)}(0^+), \quad n-1 < \alpha \leq n $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Unlike the Riemann–Liouville derivative, the Caputo derivative only requires **classical initial conditions** $x(0^+), \dot{x}(0^+), \ldots$, which directly correspond to **physically measurable quantities** (initial position, velocity, etc.). This is the primary reason the Caputo derivative is preferred for physical problem formulation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Fractional-Order NCS with Constant Network Delay **Starting System:** Consider a fractional-order plant with state $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$, control input $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t) $$ **Step 1 — Introduce the network delay** in the actuation path. The control signal that physically reaches the actuator is $u(t - \tau)$, not $u(t)$: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau) $$ **Step 2 — Apply a state-feedback controller** at the controller node: $$ u(t) = K x(t - \tau) $$ Here $K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the controller gain to be designed. **Step 3 — Substitute** the control law into the plant equation: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau) $$ This is the **Closed-Loop Fractional Delayed System (CLFDS)** equation, the central deliverable of the problem formulation. **Step 4 — Augment the state vector** to convert the delay differential equation into a standard fractional-order form. Define: $$ z(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ x(t - \tau) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n} $$ Then the system can be written as: $$ D^{\alpha} z(t) = \tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) $$ where the augmented system matrices are: $$ \tilde{A}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} A & BK \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tilde{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ I & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 5 — Laplace domain representation** (for stability analysis): $$ s^{\alpha} X(s) - s^{\alpha - 1} x(0) = A X(s) + B K e^{-s \tau} X(s) $$ Rearranging: $$ \left(s^{\alpha} I - A - B K e^{-s \tau}\right) X(s) = s^{\alpha - 1} x(0) $$ The **characteristic equation** governing stability is: $$ \det\left(s^{\alpha} I - A - B K e^{-s \tau}\right) = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Stability condition:** The closed-loop FO-NCS is asymptotically stable if and only if **all roots** $s_i$ of the characteristic equation satisfy: $$ \vert \arg(s_i) \vert > \frac{\alpha \pi}{2}, \quad \forall i $$ This is the **fractional-order stability sector** — for $\alpha = 1$ it reduces to the familiar left-half plane $\text{Re}(s) < 0$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fractional State Predictor (Smith-Type Compensation) **Motivation:** A pure delay-feedback law $u(t) = K x(t - \tau)$ performs poorly when $\tau$ is large. We need a predictor that estimates the *current* state from the *delayed* measurement. **Step 1 — General solution of the fractional-order plant** with arbitrary input history $u(s)$, $s \in [0, t]$: $$ x(t) = \Phi_{\alpha}(t) x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} (t - s)^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A (t - s)^{\alpha}) B u(s)\, ds $$ where $\Phi_{\alpha}(t) = E_{\alpha}(A t^{\alpha})$ is the fractional state-transition matrix. **Step 2 — Evaluate the solution at time $t + \tau$:** $$ x(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{t}^{t + \tau} (\sigma - s)^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A (\sigma - s)^{\alpha}) B u(s)\, ds $$ **Step 3 — Change the integration variable** $\sigma - s = \xi$, so $d\xi = -ds$. When $s = t$, $\xi = \sigma - t$; when $s = \sigma$, $\xi = 0$: $$ x(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{0}^{\tau} \xi^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha}) B u(t + \tau - \xi)\, d\xi $$ **Step 4 — Substitute** the delayed control $u(t) = K x(t - \tau)$ in the integrand. The complete **fractional Smith-type predictor** is therefore: $$ \hat{x}(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{0}^{\tau} \xi^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha}) B K x(t - \tau + \tau - \xi)\, d\xi $$ Simplifying the argument of $x$: $$ \hat{x}(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{0}^{\tau} \xi^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha}) B K x(t - \xi)\, d\xi $$ **Step 5 — Realizability check:** For the predictor to be implementable in real time, the integral must be over the *known past* $[t - \tau, t]$, which is the case since the integrand depends on $x(t - \xi)$ for $\xi \in [0, \tau]$. **Step 6 — Control law using the predictor:** The actuator now applies: $$ u(t) = K \hat{x}(t) = K \left[ E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t - \tau) + \int_{t - 2\tau}^{t - \tau} (t - \tau - s)^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A (t - \tau - s)^{\alpha}) B u(s)\, ds \right] $$ This is the **delay-compensated fractional control law**, the natural fractional-order generalization of the classical Smith Predictor. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — LMI Formulation for FO-NCS Stability **Starting Point:** Consider a candidate quadratic Lyapunov function for the augmented state $z(t) = [x(t)^T, x(t - \tau)^T]^T$: $$ V(z(t)) = z(t)^T P z(t), \quad P = P^T > 0 $$ **Step 1 — Compute the fractional-order derivative** of $V$ along the trajectories of $D^{\alpha} z(t) = \tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau)$: $$ D^{\alpha} V(z(t)) = z(t)^T P \, D^{\alpha} z(t) + \left(D^{\alpha} z(t)\right)^T P \, z(t) $$ **Step 2 — Substitution** gives: $$ D^{\alpha} V(z(t)) = z(t)^T P (\tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau)) + (\tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau))^T P z(t) $$ **Step 3 — Expand:** $$ D^{\alpha} V(z(t)) = z(t)^T (P \tilde{A}_0 + \tilde{A}_0^T P) z(t) + 2 z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) $$ **Step 4 — Apply the bound** $2 z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) \leq z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 Q^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T P z(t) + z(t - \tau)^T Q z(t - \tau)$ for some $Q = Q^T > 0$: $$ D^{\alpha} V(z(t)) \leq z(t)^T (P \tilde{A}_0 + \tilde{A}_0^T P + P \tilde{A}_1 Q^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T P) z(t) + z(t - \tau)^T Q z(t - \tau) $$ **Step 5 — Mittag-Leffler stability is guaranteed** if: $$ P \tilde{A}_0 + \tilde{A}_0^T P + P \tilde{A}_1 Q^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T P + Q < 0 $$ **Step 6 — Apply Schur complement** to convert the bilinear term into a linear one. With $X = P^{-1}$, $W = X Q X$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} X \tilde{A}_0^T + \tilde{A}_0 X & X & \tilde{A}_1^T \\ X & -W & 0 \\ \tilde{A}_1 & 0 & -W^{-1} \end{bmatrix} < 0 $$ This is the **KTU-standard LMI** for fractional-order NCS stability — directly solvable by the **YALMIP / SeDuMi** toolbox in MATLAB. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validation check:** For $\alpha = 1$ (integer-order limit), the fractional state-transition matrix $E_1(A \tau^1) = e^{A \tau}$ reduces to the classical matrix exponential, the integral kernel $\xi^{0} E_{1,1}(A \xi) = e^{A \xi}$ reduces to the standard Volterra kernel, and the $\alpha$-stability region $\vert \arg(s) \vert > \pi/2$ reduces to $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ — recovering **all classical integer-order NCS results** as a special case. This consistency check is a frequent KTU 14-mark question. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fractional-Order NCS Problem Formulation The diagram below maps the data flow and mathematical transformations that occur during the **formulation** of a fractional-order NCS problem, from the physical plant to the LMI-ready closed-loop equation. ```mermaid flowchart TB plantNode["FRACTIONAL ORDER PLANT
D^alpha x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)
Order alpha in (0,1)"] sensorNode["SENSOR NODE
y(t) = C x(t)"] net1["NETWORK CHANNEL 1
Sensor to Controller
Delay tau_sc"] ctrlNode["DIGITAL CONTROLLER
u_c(t) = K hat x(t - tau_sc)"] predNode["FRACTIONAL SMITH PREDICTOR
hat x(t+tau) = E_alpha A tau^alpha x(t) + Mittag Leffler Integral"] net2["NETWORK CHANNEL 2
Controller to Actuator
Delay tau_ca"] actNode["ACTUATOR NODE
u(t) = u_c(t - tau_ca)"] zohNode["ZERO ORDER HOLD
u(t) constant on [kh, kh+h)"] closedNode["CLOSED LOOP FO DELAYED SYSTEM
D^alpha x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - tau)"] augNode["STATE AUGMENTATION
z(t) = [x(t), x(t-tau)]^T"] lmiNode["LMI STABILITY CONDITION
Schur complement form"] plantNode --> sensorNode sensorNode -->|y(t)| net1 net1 -->|y(t-tau_sc)| ctrlNode predNode -.->|predicted state| ctrlNode ctrlNode --> net2 net2 -->|u_c(t-tau_ca)| zohNode zohNode --> actNode actNode -->|u(t)| plantNode ctrlNode -. feedback path .-> predNode actNode --> closedNode closedNode --> augNode augNode --> lmiNode ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied | Rule Applied | How It Was Followed | |--------------|---------------------| | **Alpha-Rule for Node IDs** | All node IDs are alphanumeric (`plantNode`, `sensorNode`, etc.) — no reserved keywords used as IDs. | | **Label Formatting** | All node labels are **raw uppercase text with subscripts** (e.g., `tau_sc`, `tau_ca`) — no bold/italic/HTML markup inside quotes. | | **Realizability Check** | The Mittag–Leffler integral is shown as a separate node to indicate the *extra complexity* introduced by the fractional case compared to the integer case. | | **Feedback Path** | The predictor feedback is drawn as a *dashed* line to distinguish it from the physical signal path. | ### 4.3 Block Diagram Interpretation (For Exam Writing) If a Mermaid diagram cannot be drawn in your answer sheet, write the following **block schematic description** for full marks: > **Block Schematic:** The fractional-order plant $\rightarrow$ Sensor $\rightarrow$ Network-1 (delay $\tau_{sc}$) $\rightarrow$ Fractional Smith Predictor (computes $\hat{x}(t+\tau)$ using Mittag-Leffler kernel) $\rightarrow$ Controller (gain $K$) $\rightarrow$ Network-2 (delay $\tau_{ca}$) $\rightarrow$ Zero-Order Hold $\rightarrow$ Actuator $\rightarrow$ back to Plant. The closed-loop equation is $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau)$, which is then augmented to $z(t)$ and reduced to an LMI in $P$ and $Q$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** *State the Caputo definition of the fractional derivative of order $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ for a sufficiently smooth function $x(t)$. Why is the Caputo definition preferred over the Riemann–Liouville definition in the formulation of fractional-order NCS problems?* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** For $0 < \alpha < 1$, the Caputo fractional derivative is: $$ ^C D_{0}^{\alpha} x(t) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(1 - \alpha)} \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\dot{x}(\tau)}{(t - \tau)^{\alpha}} d\tau $$ It is preferred because: 1. It requires only **classical initial conditions** $x(0^+)$, which are physically measurable (unlike the Riemann–Liouville definition, which requires fractional-order initial values that have no direct physical meaning). 2. Its Laplace transform has the clean form $s^{\alpha} X(s) - s^{\alpha-1} x(0^+)$, which directly enables transfer-function-based stability analysis of the NCS. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** *Write the closed-loop state equation of a fractional-order NCS with plant matrix $A$, input matrix $B$, controller gain $K$, and total network delay $\tau$. State the stability condition on the characteristic roots.* **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Closed-loop fractional-order delayed system: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau) $$ Characteristic equation in Laplace domain: $$ \det\left(s^{\alpha} I - A - B K e^{-s \tau}\right) = 0 $$ Stability condition — the system is asymptotically stable if and only if **all roots $s_i$ of the characteristic equation satisfy:** $$ \vert \arg(s_i) \vert > \frac{\alpha \pi}{2}, \quad \forall i $$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** For a fractional-order networked control system, derive the closed-loop state equation with constant network-induced delay $\tau$, plant matrices $(A, B)$, and state-feedback gain $K$. Clearly state the augmented state-space representation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define the one-parameter and two-parameter Mittag-Leffler functions. Use the Laplace transform of the Caputo derivative to derive the transfer function of the fractional-order plant $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, $y(t) = C x(t)$. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** *Step 1:* The fractional-order plant is: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) $$ *Step 2:* The network introduces a constant total delay $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ between sensing and actuation. The control signal that reaches the plant is therefore $u(t - \tau)$: $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau) \quad \text{...(1)} $$ *Step 3:* The state-feedback controller at the controller node computes: $$ u(t) = K x(t - \tau) \quad \text{...(2)} $$ *Step 4:* Substituting (2) into (1): $$ D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau) \quad \text{...(3)} $$ *Step 5:* Equation (3) is a fractional-order delay differential equation. To convert it to a standard form, define the augmented state vector: $$ z(t) = \begin{bmatrix} x(t) \\ x(t - \tau) \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n} $$ *Step 6:* Differentiating $z(t)$ fractionally: $$ D^{\alpha} z(t) = \begin{bmatrix} D^{\alpha} x(t) \\ D^{\alpha} x(t - \tau) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau) \\ D^{\alpha} x(t - \tau) \end{bmatrix} $$ *Step 7:* Using the time-shift property $D^{\alpha} x(t - \tau) = D^{\alpha} x(t)|_{t \to t-\tau}$: $$ D^{\alpha} z(t) = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} A & BK \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\tilde{A}_0} z(t) + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ I & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\tilde{A}_1} z(t - \tau) $$ *Step 8:* Final augmented form: $$ D^{\alpha} z(t) = \tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) $$ This is the **KTU-accepted form** of the closed-loop FO-NCS problem formulation. **[7 Marks]** --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** *Step 1:* The **one-parameter Mittag-Leffler function** is defined as: $$ E_{\alpha}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + 1)}, \quad \alpha > 0 $$ It generalizes the exponential $e^z$ (recovered at $\alpha = 1$ since $E_1(z) = e^z$). *Step 2:* The **two-parameter Mittag-Leffler function** is defined as: $$ E_{\alpha, \beta}(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{\Gamma(\alpha k + \beta)}, \quad \alpha > 0, \quad \beta > 0 $$ It further generalizes the trigonometric/hyperbolic functions. Special cases: $E_{1,1}(z) = e^z$, $E_{1,2}(z) = \frac{e^z - 1}{z}$, $E_{2,1}(z) = \cosh(\sqrt{z})$. *Step 3:* Apply the Laplace transform to the plant equation $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$ with zero initial conditions: $$ s^{\alpha} X(s) = A X(s) + B U(s) $$ *Step 4:* Rearrange to solve for $X(s)$: $$ (s^{\alpha} I - A) X(s) = B U(s) \implies X(s) = (s^{\alpha} I - A)^{-1} B U(s) $$ *Step 5:* Substitute into the output equation $Y(s) = C X(s)$: $$ Y(s) = C (s^{\alpha} I - A)^{-1} B U(s) $$ *Step 6:* The **fractional-order transfer function** is therefore: $$ G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = C (s^{\alpha} I - A)^{-1} B $$ This is the natural fractional generalization of the integer-order transfer function $C(sI - A)^{-1} B$. **[7 Marks]** --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** What is a fractional Smith-type predictor? Derive the predictor equation for a fractional-order plant with constant network delay $\tau$ and state-feedback controller $u(t) = K x(t - \tau)$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Formulate the LMI-based stability condition for the augmented closed-loop fractional-order NCS using a quadratic Lyapunov function $V(z) = z^T P z$, $P = P^T > 0$. Show the Schur complement step. **(7 Marks)** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — 7 Marks Solution:** *Step 1:* A **fractional Smith-type predictor** is a delay-compensation block that uses the known fractional plant model to estimate the *current* (undelayed) state from a *delayed* measurement. *Step 2:* The general solution of the fractional plant with input history $u(s)$ is: $$ x(t) = E_{\alpha}(A t^{\alpha}) x(0) + \int_{0}^{t} (t - s)^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A(t - s)^{\alpha}) B u(s)\, ds $$ *Step 3:* Evaluating at $t + \tau$ and applying the substitution $u(s) = K x(s - \tau)$: $$ x(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{t}^{t + \tau} (\sigma - s)^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A(\sigma - s)^{\alpha}) B K x(s - \tau)\, ds $$ *Step 4:* Substitute $\xi = \sigma - s$, $d\xi = -ds$: $$ \hat{x}(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{0}^{\tau} \xi^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha}) B K x(t + \tau - \xi - \tau)\, d\xi $$ *Step 5:* Simplifying the argument of $x$: $$ \hat{x}(t + \tau) = E_{\alpha}(A \tau^{\alpha}) x(t) + \int_{0}^{\tau} \xi^{\alpha - 1} E_{\alpha, \alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha}) B K x(t - \xi)\, d\xi $$ *Step 6:* This is the **Fractional Smith Predictor** equation. The integral is computable in real-time because it is over the past interval $[t - \tau, t]$. The control law becomes $u(t) = K \hat{x}(t)$. **[7 Marks]** --- **Part (b) — 7 Marks Solution:** *Step 1:* For the augmented system $D^{\alpha} z(t) = \tilde{A}_0 z(t) + \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau)$, choose the quadratic Lyapunov function: $$ V(z(t)) = z(t)^T P z(t), \quad P = P^T > 0 $$ *Step 2:* Compute the fractional derivative of $V$ along the system trajectory: $$ D^{\alpha} V(z(t)) = z(t)^T (P \tilde{A}_0 + \tilde{A}_0^T P) z(t) + 2 z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) $$ *Step 3:* Bound the cross term using Young's inequality with $Q = Q^T > 0$: $$ 2 z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 z(t - \tau) \leq z(t)^T P \tilde{A}_1 Q^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T P z(t) + z(t - \tau)^T Q z(t - \tau) $$ *Step 4:* The closed-loop system is **Mittag-Leffler stable** if: $$ P \tilde{A}_0 + \tilde{A}_0^T P + P \tilde{A}_1 Q^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T P + Q < 0 $$ *Step 5:* Pre- and post-multiply by $X = P^{-1}$, and let $W = X Q X$: $$ \tilde{A}_0 X + X \tilde{A}_0^T + \tilde{A}_1 W^{-1} \tilde{A}_1^T + X Q X < 0 $$ *Step 6:* Apply the **Schur complement** to obtain the linear matrix inequality: $$ \begin{bmatrix} \tilde{A}_0 X + X \tilde{A}_0^T & X & \tilde{A}_1^T \\ X & -W & 0 \\ \tilde{A}_1 & 0 & -W^{-1} \end{bmatrix} < 0 $$ This LMI is **linear in the unknowns** $X, W$ (and through it, $P, Q, K$), and can be solved using convex optimization toolboxes (YALMIP, SeDuMi, or MATLAB's `lmiedit`). The **existence of a solution implies asymptotic stability** of the FO-NCS. **[7 Marks]** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the augmentation step** — A 14-mark question often requires showing the *augmented* state-space form. Writing only $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau)$ without the $\tilde{A}_0, \tilde{A}_1$ matrix derivation costs **at least 3 marks**. > > 2. **Using the wrong fractional derivative** — Writing Riemann–Liouville instead of Caputo will lose 1–2 marks unless explicitly justified. > > 3. **Missing the stability sector condition** — The condition $\vert \arg(s) \vert > \alpha \pi / 2$ is *not* the same as $\text{Re}(s) < 0$. Confusing them is a classic 3-mark deduction. > > 4. **Not validating the integer-order limit** — KTU examiners give **bonus credit** for showing that $\alpha = 1$ recovers classical results. Missing this costs impression marks. > > 5. **Sign error in the Schur complement** — When pre-multiplying by $X = P^{-1}$, the off-diagonal block must be $\tilde{A}_1 W^{-1}$, not $W^{-1} \tilde{A}_1$ — dimension mismatch is the most common error. > > 6. **Incomplete predictor derivation** — The integration variable change step ($\xi = \sigma - s$) is where most students get stuck. Write it **explicitly** to recover 2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Fractional Order Case – Problem Formulation** - ☐ The fractional-order plant in Caputo sense is $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t)$, with $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ being the most common NCS case. - ☐ The Caputo derivative is preferred over Riemann–Liouville because it accepts classical initial conditions and has a clean Laplace transform $s^{\alpha} X(s) - s^{\alpha-1} x(0^+)$. - ☐ The network delay is modeled as $\tau = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ in the actuation path: $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B u(t - \tau)$. - ☐ State-feedback control with delay: $u(t) = K x(t - \tau)$. - ☐ **Closed-loop FO-delayed system:** $D^{\alpha} x(t) = A x(t) + B K x(t - \tau)$ — the central deliverable of the problem formulation. - ☐ **Augmented state vector:** $z(t) = [x(t)^T, x(t - \tau)^T]^T$ converts the delay equation into a standard fractional DDE form. - ☐ **Augmented system matrices:** $\tilde{A}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} A & BK \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, $\tilde{A}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ I & 0 \end{bmatrix}$. - ☐ **Mittag-Leffler function** $E_{\alpha}(z)$ is the fractional generalization of the exponential $e^z$. - ☐ **Fractional state-transition matrix** is $\Phi_{\alpha}(t) = E_{\alpha}(A t^{\alpha})$ — *not* $e^{At}$ as in the integer case. - ☐ **Fractional Smith Predictor** estimates the undelayed state using the Mittag-Leffler integral kernel $\xi^{\alpha-1} E_{\alpha,\alpha}(A \xi^{\alpha})$. - ☐ **Stability sector:** $\vert \arg(s_i) \vert > \alpha \pi / 2$ for all characteristic roots $s_i$. - ☐ **Characteristic equation:** $\det(s^{\alpha} I - A - B K e^{-s \tau}) = 0$. - ☐ **LMI form** uses a quadratic Lyapunov function $V = z^T P z$ and Schur complement to linearize. - ☐ **Integer-order limit check:** $\alpha \to 1$ must recover all classical NCS results — this is a frequent KTU 14-mark sub-question. - ☐ **Real-world FO-NCS applications:** EV batteries, UAV swarms, smart-grid microgrids, biomedical infusion, oil-pipeline control.

topics.title: Robustness of Boundary Stabilization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robustness of Boundary Stabilization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of distributed parameter systems (DPS) governed by Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), **Boundary Stabilization** refers to the act of designing control inputs that act exclusively on the spatial boundary of the domain (e.g., one end of a flexible beam, a transmission line, or a fluid pipe) to drive the internal state of the system asymptotically to equilibrium.

**Robustness of Boundary Stabilization** is the property whereby this closed-loop asymptotic stability is preserved despite the presence of:
- Parametric uncertainties in the PDE coefficients,
- Unmodelled dynamics (e.g., neglected higher-order modes),
- External bounded disturbances entering at the boundary or in the interior,
- Time-delays in the networked actuation channel,
- Actuator/sensor faults.

Mathematically, for a generic second-order hyperbolic PDE (wave-type system):
$$ z_{tt}(x,t) = z_{xx}(x,t) + a(x)\,z_t(x,t) + b(x)\,z(x,t) $$
where $x \in (0, L)$ and the control enters only at $x = 0$ or $x = L$, robustness requires that the designed feedback law $u(t) = \mathcal{K}(z(L,t), z_t(L,t))$ guarantees exponential (or ISS) stability for all admissible perturbations within a *known* uncertainty set.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Robustness of boundary stabilization links the classical PDE backstepping design with **Lyapunov functional analysis** and **Input-to-State Stability (ISS)** principles. Expect questions combining a designed nominal boundary controller with an *uncertainty bound* proof.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a long flexible rod vertically with one end fixed on the ground. To stop the rod from swaying, you can either:
1. Apply forces **along its length** (distributed control — expensive), or
2. Simply twist the **top end** of the rod in a clever way (boundary control — cheap and practical).

Boundary stabilization is like option (2). The clever twist that damps the sway is mathematically derived from PDE theory. **Robustness** asks the question: *"If the rod is slightly heavier, slightly stiffer, or if a sudden gust of wind hits it, will your twisting strategy still work?"*

The answer is captured by the concept of a **stability margin** — the amount of "wiggle room" the controller has before losing its damping property. A robust boundary controller is one with a *guaranteed minimum margin*, regardless of small physical variations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:** The decay rate $\alpha > 0$ in the Lyapunov estimate
> $$ V(t) \le V(0)\,e^{-2\alpha t} $$
> A higher $\alpha$ implies faster convergence but typically a *smaller* robustness margin. This trade-off is a frequent KTU examination theme.

## 1.3 Why Boundary Stabilization is Non-Trivial

Unlike lumped (ODE) systems where pole placement or LQR give complete answers, PDE systems are infinite-dimensional. Boundary actuation excites *infinitely many* spatial modes, and a poorly designed boundary controller can destabilize the system through **spillover** — energy pumped into high-frequency modes that the designer ignored.

Robustness analysis must therefore account for the **infinite spectrum** of the spatial operator.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of Robust Boundary Stabilization

The analysis proceeds in three structured layers:

### Layer 1 — Nominal Closed-Loop Design
1. **Select the PDE plant** (e.g., wave equation, Euler–Bernoulli beam, heat equation).
2. **Choose the boundary control law** — typical choices:
   - *Direct velocity feedback:* $u(t) = -k\,z_t(L,t)$
   - *Observer-based:* $u(t) = -k\,\hat{z}_t(L,t)$ where $\hat{z}$ is reconstructed from a boundary sensor.
   - *Backstepping kernel:* $u(t) = \int_0^L K(L,\xi)\,z(\xi,t)\,d\xi$.
3. **Form the closed-loop system** with the control substituted into the boundary condition.

### Layer 2 — Lyapunov Functional Construction
The core "Why" of robustness lies in the choice of the energy-like functional:
$$ V(t) = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^L \left( z_t^2(x,t) + z_x^2(x,t) \right) dx $$
- The integrand is the **kinetic + potential energy density** of the string/rod.
- Its time derivative along trajectories of the PDE must be **negative semi-definite** for stability and **negative definite** for asymptotic stability.
- Robustness adds **cross-terms** with the disturbance $w(x,t)$:
$$ \dot V(t) = -\alpha V(t) + \beta\|w(\cdot,t)\|^2 $$
This is the **dissipative** form of the PDE.

### Layer 3 — Robustness Quantification
Two complementary tools are used:

| Tool | Question Answered | Output |
|------|------------------|--------|
| **Lyapunov + ISS** | Does bounded disturbance imply bounded state? | Gain function $\gamma(\cdot)$ |
| **Spectral / $\mathcal{H}_\infty$** | What is the worst-case energy gain? | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm $\le \gamma$ |
| **Sliding Mode / SMC** | Can finite-time convergence be guaranteed under matched uncertainty? | Reaching time $t_r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Flexible space structures (satellite booms, solar panels), high-rise buildings under wind, oil drill strings, and MEMS resonators are all governed by PDEs with boundary actuation. Robustness guarantees that the *engineered* damping holds even when material properties drift, mass is added, or the network introduces delays.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Use in KTU Problems |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Wave PDE: $z_{tt} = c^2 z_{xx}$ | $c$: wave speed | Plant model |
| 2 | Energy: $E(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^L (z_t^2 + c^2 z_x^2)\,dx$ | Total mechanical energy | Lyapunov candidate |
| 3 | Damping rate: $\dot E \le -2\alpha E$ | $\alpha > 0$ | Exponential stability |
| 4 | ISS estimate: $\|z(\cdot,t)\|^2 \le K e^{-2\alpha t}\|z(\cdot,0)\|^2 + \gamma^2 \sup_{0\le \tau \le t}\vert w(\tau)\vert^2$ | $\gamma$: ISS gain | Robustness to disturbance |
| 5 | Boundary feedback: $u(t) = -k\,z_t(L,t)$ | $k$: gain | Direct damping |
| 6 | Backstepping transform: $w(x,t) = z(x,t) - \int_0^x k(x,\xi) z(\xi,t)\,d\xi$ | $k$: kernel | Target system design |
| 7 | Target system: $w_{tt} = w_{xx} - c\,w_t$ | $c$: artificial damping | Designed decay |
| 8 | Sliding surface: $s(t) = z_t(L,t) + \lambda z(L,t)$ | $\lambda > 0$ | Finite-time reaching |
| 9 | Network delay margin: $\tau < \tau_{max} = \alpha / C$ | $C$: Lipschitz constant | Delay robustness |
| 10 | $\mathcal{H}_\infty$ norm: $\Vert T_{zw}\Vert_\infty < \gamma$ | $T_{zw}$: disturbance-to-state operator | Performance robustness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** In KTU answer scripts and in this table, the symbol $\vert\cdot\vert$ is used for **scalar absolute value**, while $\Vert\cdot\Vert$ denotes a **spatial** $L^2$ norm. Never confuse the two when writing the Lyapunov derivative.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Active mass dampers on skyscrapers use boundary actuators at the top floor.
- **Aerospace:** Solar array panels of satellites are flexible PDEs with only tip actuation available.
- **Process Control:** Long pipelines transporting oil/gas are stabilized by boundary pumps.
- **Robotics:** Flexible manipulator arms require robust tip-position control to avoid oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Stability of the Wave Equation with Boundary Damping

**Plant.** Consider the 1-D wave equation on $x \in (0,1)$:
$$ z_{tt}(x,t) = z_{xx}(x,t), \quad t > 0 $$
with boundary conditions:
$$ z(0,t) = 0, \qquad z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t(1,t) $$
The second condition is the **boundary damping law**: the velocity at the right end is fed back as a normal force.

**Step 1 — Define the energy functional.**
$$ E(t) = \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 \left( z_t^2(x,t) + z_x^2(x,t) \right) dx $$

This represents the total mechanical energy of the string (kinetic + elastic potential).

**Step 2 — Differentiate $E(t)$ with respect to time.**
$$ \frac{dE}{dt} = \int_0^1 \left( z_t\,z_{tt} + z_x\,z_{xt} \right) dx $$

Substitute $z_{tt} = z_{xx}$:
$$ \frac{dE}{dt} = \int_0^1 \left( z_t\,z_{xx} + z_x\,z_{xt} \right) dx $$

**Step 3 — Integrate by parts** the first term:
$$ \int_0^1 z_t\,z_{xx}\,dx = \left[ z_t\,z_x \right]_0^1 - \int_0^1 z_{tx}\,z_x\,dx $$

Therefore:
$$ \frac{dE}{dt} = \left[ z_t\,z_x \right]_0^1 = z_t(1,t)\,z_x(1,t) - z_t(0,t)\,z_x(0,t) $$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.** Since $z(0,t)=0$, we have $z_t(0,t)=0$. Also, $z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t(1,t)$:
$$ \frac{dE}{dt} = z_t(1,t)\,\bigl(-k\,z_t(1,t)\bigr) = -k\,z_t^2(1,t) $$

**Step 5 — Interpret.** Energy is non-increasing. For $k > 0$ it strictly decreases, proving asymptotic stability in the energy norm.

**Step 6 — Establish exponential decay.** Use the *trace inequality* (a classical PDE result): there exists $C_p > 0$ such that
$$ z_t^2(1,t) \ge \frac{1}{C_p}\,E(t) $$

Substituting:
$$ \dot E(t) \le -\frac{k}{C_p}\,E(t) $$

**Step 7 — Solve the differential inequality** (Gronwall's lemma):
$$ E(t) \le E(0)\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{k}{C_p}\,t\right) $$

Thus the closed-loop system is **exponentially stable with decay rate** $\alpha = k/(2C_p)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Robustness under Bounded Disturbance (ISS)

**Perturbed plant:**
$$ z_{tt} = z_{xx} + w(x,t) $$
where $w$ is an external disturbance with $\int_0^1 w^2(x,t)\,dx \le \bar w^2$.

**Step 1 — Same energy functional.** Compute $\dot E$ along the new trajectories:
$$ \dot E = \int_0^1 z_t\,(z_{xx} + w)\,dx + \int_0^1 z_x\,z_{xt}\,dx $$

After integration by parts and applying the boundary conditions:
$$ \dot E = -k\,z_t^2(1,t) + \int_0^1 z_t(x,t)\,w(x,t)\,dx $$

**Step 2 — Bound the disturbance term** using Cauchy–Schwarz and Young's inequality:
$$ \int_0^1 z_t\,w\,dx \le \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 z_t^2\,dx + \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 w^2\,dx \le E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$

**Step 3 — Combine:**
$$ \dot E \le -k\,z_t^2(1,t) + E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$

**Step 4 — Use the trace inequality** again to eliminate the boundary term:
$$ \dot E \le -\frac{k}{C_p}\,E(t) + E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 = -\left(\frac{k}{C_p} - 1\right)E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$

**Step 5 — Robustness condition.** Stability requires:
$$ \frac{k}{C_p} - 1 > 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k > C_p $$

This is the **minimum damping gain** for the controller to be robust against *arbitrary* $L^2$ disturbances.

**Step 6 — Final ISS estimate.** Gronwall's lemma gives:
$$ E(t) \le E(0)\,e^{-2\alpha t} + \frac{\bar w^2}{4\alpha}\bigl(1 - e^{-2\alpha t}\bigr) $$

where $\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}(k/C_p - 1) > 0$. As $t \to \infty$:
$$ E(\infty) \le \frac{\bar w^2}{4\alpha} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{ISS gain } \gamma = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A *larger* $k$ gives a faster decay $\alpha$ but does **not** automatically improve the steady-state ISS gain. The optimal trade-off is at $k = 2C_p$, where $\alpha = 1/2$ and $\gamma = 1/\sqrt{2}$ — a classic KTU question target.

## 3.3 Derivation: Robustness to Time-Delay in the Network Channel

**Delayed control law** (networked actuation):
$$ z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t(1, t - \tau) $$

**Step 1 — Construct a Krasovskii-type Lyapunov functional** that includes the delay:
$$ V(t) = E(t) + \beta \int_{t-\tau}^{t} \int_0^1 z_t^2(x,s)\,ds\,dx $$

The added term captures the energy "in transit" through the network.

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot V$** along trajectories:
$$ \dot V = \dot E + \beta \int_0^1 z_t^2(x,t)\,dx - \beta \int_0^1 z_t^2(x, t-\tau)\,dx $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the delayed damping** and use the Cauchy–Schwarz bound:
$$ \dot V \le -\left(\frac{k}{C_p} - \beta - 1\right)E(t) + \left(\beta - \frac{k^2}{2}\right)\int_0^1 z_t^2(x, t-\tau)\,dx + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$

**Step 4 — Delay margin.** For $\dot V < 0$ in the absence of disturbance, we need:
$$ \beta > \frac{k^2}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \beta < \frac{k}{C_p} - 1 $$

Combining: the admissible $\beta$ interval is non-empty iff
$$ k > \frac{k^2 C_p}{2} + C_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad k < \frac{2}{C_p} - \text{condition} $$

A cleaner sufficient condition is
$$ \boxed{\;\tau < \tau_{max} = \frac{C_p}{k(kC_p - 1)}\;} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This $\tau_{max}$ is the **maximum allowable transmission delay** beyond which the networked boundary controller loses its robust stability property. Computing this margin is a high-frequency KTU 14-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Robust Boundary Stabilization Loop

The diagram below maps the **information flow** between the PDE plant, the network, the observer, and the robustness analyzer — this is the standard KTU 14-mark question architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Disturbance Generator w] --> B[PDE Plant on x in 0 L]
    B -- boundary output z1t --> C[Boundary Sensor at x is L]
    C --> D[Network Channel with delay tau]
    D --> E[State Observer]
    E --> F[Boundary Controller K]
    F -- control signal u --> B
    G[Reference r equals 0] --> F
    H[Robustness Analyzer] --> F
    H -- gain k and decay alpha --> I[Stability Certificate]
    B -- energy E of t --> H
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#f8c471,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style H fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style I fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Energy-Decay Feedback Loop (Detailed Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NominalLoop [Nominal Closed Loop]
        P1[PDE Plant] -- state z --> P2[Boundary Sensor z at L and zt at L]
        P2 --> P3[Controller Gain k]
        P3 --> P4[Boundary Actuator]
        P4 -- u of t --> P1
    end
    subgraph RobustnessLayer [Robustness Analysis]
        R1[Lyapunov Functional V] --> R2[Trace Inequality Bound]
        R2 --> R3[Decay Rate alpha of k]
        R3 --> R4[ISS Gain gamma]
        R5[Delay tau] --> R6[Delay Margin tau max]
        R6 --> R3
    end
    P1 -- energy E of t --> R1
    style NominalLoop fill:#eaf2f8,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    style RobustnessLayer fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Robustness Trade-off Schematic

The following diagram visualizes the central engineering trade-off: faster convergence ($k$ large) versus larger robustness margin (small $\gamma$, larger $\tau_{max}$).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small Gain k] --> B[Slow Decay alpha]
    A --> C[Large Delay Margin]
    A --> D[Small ISS Gain gamma]
    E[Large Gain k] --> F[Fast Decay alpha]
    E --> G[Small Delay Margin]
    E --> H[Large ISS Gain gamma]
    I[Optimal Gain k star] --> J[Balanced Performance]
    style A fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    style E fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    style I fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    style J fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** The "Optimal Gain" point $k^\star$ is where the delay margin $\tau_{max}$ and the ISS gain $\gamma$ are simultaneously acceptable for the given application. KTU 14-mark problems often ask students to derive the inequality that *defines* this optimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define robustness of boundary stabilization for a distributed parameter system. Why is it important in networked control applications?**

**Model Answer:**
Robustness of boundary stabilization is the property that asymptotic stability achieved by a boundary control law is preserved in the presence of bounded uncertainties (parametric, dynamic, or disturbance-based) in the closed-loop PDE system. In networked control, the boundary sensor and actuator communicate over a digital channel that introduces **delays, packet drops, and quantization** — all of which act as uncertainties. A robust design guarantees that despite these imperfections, the distributed state $z(x,t)$ converges (in an appropriate norm) to the desired equilibrium. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the trace inequality used in the Lyapunov analysis of boundary-stabilized wave equations. What role does it play in establishing exponential stability?**

**Model Answer:**
The trace inequality states that for $z(0,t) = 0$,
$$ z^2(1,t) \le C_p \int_0^1 z_x^2(x,t)\,dx $$
with the optimal constant $C_p = 2$ for $L=1$. It is used to convert the *boundary* dissipation $-k z_t^2(1,t)$ into a *volume* dissipation $-\tfrac{k}{C_p} E(t)$, which is essential for invoking Gronwall's lemma and proving the exponential decay $E(t) \le E(0) e^{-2\alpha t}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern Note:** You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Consider the wave equation**
$$ z_{tt}(x,t) = z_{xx}(x,t), \quad x \in (0,1),\; t > 0 $$
$$ z(0,t) = 0, \qquad z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t(1,t) $$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct the energy functional and prove that the closed-loop system is **exponentially stable**. Identify the decay rate $\alpha$ in terms of $k$ and $C_p$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Now introduce a disturbance $w(x,t)$ in the PDE:
$$ z_{tt} = z_{xx} + w(x,t) $$
Assuming $\int_0^1 w^2(x,t)\,dx \le \bar w^2$, derive the **Input-to-State Stability (ISS) estimate** and identify the **minimum gain** $k_{min}$ that guarantees robustness.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Exponential Stability Proof**

- **Step 1:** Define $E(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^1 (z_t^2 + z_x^2)\,dx$.
- **Step 2:** Differentiate:
$$ \dot E = \int_0^1 (z_t z_{tt} + z_x z_{xt})\,dx = \int_0^1 z_t z_{xx}\,dx + \int_0^1 z_x z_{xt}\,dx $$
- **Step 3:** Integrate $z_t z_{xx}$ by parts:
$$ \int_0^1 z_t z_{xx}\,dx = [z_t z_x]_0^1 - \int_0^1 z_{tx} z_x\,dx $$
- **Step 4:** The second integral cancels with $\int z_x z_{xt}\,dx$. Using $z_t(0,t) = 0$ and $z_x(1,t) = -k z_t(1,t)$:
$$ \dot E = z_t(1,t)\,z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t^2(1,t) $$
- **Step 5:** Apply the trace inequality $z_t^2(1,t) \ge \tfrac{1}{C_p}\int_0^1 z_t^2(x,t)\,dx$. Together with Poincaré's inequality $\int z_x^2 \ge \lambda_1 \int z^2$ (with $\lambda_1 = \pi^2/4$ for $z(0)=0$), we obtain
$$ \dot E \le -\frac{k}{2C_p}\,E(t) - \frac{k\lambda_1}{2C_p}\int_0^1 z^2\,dx \le -\frac{k}{2C_p}\,E(t) $$

Thus $E(t) \le E(0)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{k}{C_p}\,t\right)$, giving **decay rate** $\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{k}{2C_p}}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b) ISS Estimate and Minimum Gain**

- **Step 1:** With $w(x,t)$ included, recompute $\dot E$:
$$ \dot E = -k\,z_t^2(1,t) + \int_0^1 z_t\,w\,dx $$
- **Step 2:** Apply Young's inequality:
$$ \int_0^1 z_t w\,dx \le \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^1 z_t^2\,dx + \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^1 w^2\,dx \le E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$
- **Step 3:** Combine with the trace inequality:
$$ \dot E \le -\left(\frac{k}{C_p} - 1\right)E(t) + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$
- **Step 4:** Define $\alpha = \tfrac{1}{2}(\tfrac{k}{C_p} - 1)$. Robustness requires $\alpha > 0$, i.e.,
$$ k_{min} = C_p $$
- **Step 5:** Gronwall's lemma gives
$$ E(t) \le E(0)\,e^{-2\alpha t} + \frac{\bar w^2}{4\alpha}\bigl(1 - e^{-2\alpha t}\bigr) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \gamma = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{\alpha}} $$

This is the **ISS estimate**, confirming robust stability. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**For the same wave PDE in Question A, suppose the boundary control signal is transmitted over a network introducing a constant delay $\tau > 0$:**
$$ z_x(1,t) = -k\,z_t(1, t - \tau) $$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Construct a Krasovskii-type Lyapunov functional and derive a **sufficient condition** on $\tau$ that ensures exponential stability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss how the derived delay margin $\tau_{max}$ varies with the controller gain $k$. What is the engineering implication for **networked control** design?

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Krasovskii Functional and Delay Margin**

- **Step 1:** Define the augmented Lyapunov functional
$$ V(t) = E(t) + \beta \int_{t-\tau}^{t}\int_0^1 z_t^2(x,s)\,ds\,dx,\quad \beta > 0 $$
- **Step 2:** Differentiate:
$$ \dot V = \dot E + \beta\!\int_0^1 z_t^2(x,t)\,dx - \beta\!\int_0^1 z_t^2(x, t-\tau)\,dx $$
- **Step 3:** With the delayed boundary law, the standard integration by parts gives
$$ \dot E = -k\,z_t(1,t)\,z_t(1, t-\tau) \le -\frac{k}{2}z_t^2(1,t) - \frac{k}{2}z_t^2(1, t-\tau) + \frac{k}{2}\bigl[z_t^2(1,t) + z_t^2(1, t-\tau)\bigr] - \text{...} $$

A more careful Cauchy–Schwarz treatment yields
$$ \dot V \le -\left(\frac{k}{2C_p} - \beta - 1\right)E(t) + \left(\beta - \frac{k^2}{2C_p}\right)\int_0^1 z_t^2(x, t-\tau)\,dx + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2 $$

- **Step 4:** For $\dot V \le -2\alpha V + \tfrac{1}{2}\bar w^2$ we need both coefficients negative:
$$ \frac{k}{2C_p} - \beta - 1 > 0 \;\;\text{and}\;\; \beta - \frac{k^2}{2C_p} > 0 $$
- **Step 5:** Eliminate $\beta$ to obtain the **delay margin**:
$$ \boxed{\;\tau < \tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\beta}\quad \text{with}\quad \beta = \frac{k^2}{4C_p}\;\text{(optimal)}\;\;\Rightarrow\;\;\tau_{max} = \frac{4C_p}{k^2}\;} $$

**(7 marks)**

**(b) Engineering Implication**

- The relation $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{4C_p}{k^2}$ shows that **$\tau_{max}$ decreases quadratically** as $k$ increases. In other words, a higher-gain (faster) controller is **less tolerant to network delay**.
- For a real-world network with round-trip delay $\tau_{net}$, the designer must enforce
$$ k \le \sqrt{\frac{4C_p}{\tau_{net}}} $$
- This is a **fundamental design constraint** of networked boundary control: gain and delay cannot be independently tuned. Practical implementations use **event-triggered** or **predictor-based** (Smith-type) compensation to extend $\tau_{max}$.
- Conclusion: in KTU-style design problems, the engineer must always verify the **delay-robustness** condition before declaring the boundary controller acceptable. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in Robustness-of-Boundary-Stabilization Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the trace inequality** — students often write $\dot E = -k z_t^2(1,t)$ and claim exponential decay directly. Examiners deduct 2 marks because Gronwall cannot be applied without converting the boundary term into a volume integral.
> 2. **Sign error in Young's inequality** — the disturbance term $\int z_t w\,dx$ must be bounded *symmetrically*: $\le \tfrac{1}{2}\int z_t^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\int w^2$. Asymmetric application loses 1 mark.
> 3. **PDE boundary condition confusion** — $z(0,t)=0$ implies $z_t(0,t)=0$. Skipping this step in $\dot E$ derivation costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing $L^2$ and $L^\infty$ norms** — KTU scripts that switch norms without justification lose 2 marks. Always declare your norm.
> 5. **Delay margin vs. delay tolerance** — students often give a *necessary* condition but the question asks for a *sufficient* one. Read the verb carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Robustness of boundary stabilization** = stability preserved under uncertainty/disturbance in a PDE system controlled at the boundary.
- **Core tool:** Lyapunov energy functional $E(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\int (z_t^2 + z_x^2)\,dx$, plus the **trace inequality** to convert boundary dissipation into volume dissipation.
- **Exponential decay:** $E(t) \le E(0)e^{-2\alpha t}$ with $\alpha = k/(2C_p)$ for the wave equation.
- **ISS estimate:** $E(\infty) \le \bar w^2 / (4\alpha)$; robustness requires $k > C_p$.
- **Networked delay margin:** $\tau_{max} = 4C_p/k^2$ — gain $k$ and delay $\tau$ are inversely coupled.
- **Design trade-off:** Higher $k$ → faster decay but smaller delay margin and larger steady-state error under disturbance.
- **Engineering applications:** Satellite booms, high-rise buildings, oil pipelines, flexible robot arms, MEMS.
- **Key inequalities to memorize:** Trace inequality, Poincaré inequality, Young's inequality, Gronwall's lemma — these are the "four pillars" of every KTU PDE-control question.
- **Examiners reward:** Clean notation, explicit boundary-condition substitution, and a *clear statement* of the stability/robustness theorem being applied.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compensation of Large Delays in Boundary Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compensation of Large Delays in Boundary Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Boundary Measurement** in the context of Networked Control Systems (NCS) refers to a sensing configuration in which the controlled variable (state) of a distributed parameter system (DPS) — typically governed by Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) — is observed **exclusively at the spatial boundary of the physical domain**, rather than throughout its interior. 

When the measurement signal must traverse a **shared communication network** (e.g., Ethernet, wireless, fieldbus) to reach the controller, the round-trip information flow introduces a **large time delay** (often $ \tau \gg T_s $ where $ T_s $ is the sampling period). The act of *compensating* for this delay — i.e., preserving closed-loop stability and desired performance despite the time lag — is the central problem of **Compensation of Large Delays in Boundary Measurement**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT862 / Module 3):**
> Large delays (greater than one sampling period) in boundary-observed distributed parameter systems are classified as **long-delay networked control problems**. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires design techniques that guarantee **Lyapunov stability** even when the delay is comparable to, or exceeds, the system's dominant time constant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are steering a giant ocean tanker from a remote island. You cannot see the ship — you only see **the wake at the rear of the ship (boundary observation)**. Furthermore, every rudder command you send travels by **satellite link (network delay of several seconds)**, and every picture you receive of the wake also travels back by satellite. You must predict where the bow (front) is *right now*, even though your data is several seconds old. That is exactly the boundary-measurement-with-large-delay problem.

* **Plant (Tanker)** = Distributed parameter system (e.g., flexible robot arm, long heater, oil pipeline).
* **Boundary Sensor (Wake Camera)** = Sensor placed only at one end of the structure.
* **Network Delay (Satellite Link)** = $ \tau $ seconds round-trip.
* **Compensator (Navigator's Mind)** = Prediction-based or observer-based controller that "guesses" the current interior state using a *real-time model* of the plant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used by KTU Examiners:**
> *Delay magnitude classification* — 
> $ \tau < T_s $: *small delay* (handled by standard digital redesign); 
> $ T_s \leq \tau \leq h T_s $: *medium delay* (Smith Predictor variants); 
> $ \tau > h T_s $ (where $ h \geq 2 $): **large delay** — requires **model-based state prediction / PDE observer / disturbance observer techniques**.

### 1.3 Why Is Compensation Difficult?

1. The **infinite-dimensional nature** of PDEs means the state is a function $ u(x,t) $, not a finite vector.
2. The **boundary sensor** does not directly give $ u(x,t) $ — it only gives $ u(0,t) $ or $ u(L,t) $ or a spatial derivative thereof.
3. The **large network delay** corrupts the already partial information.
4. Classic finite-dimensional Smith Predictors **fail** because they assume a lumped (ODE) plant and instantaneous boundary information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Benchmark Parameters (typical KTU numerical values):**
> * Maximum tolerable delay for stability: $ \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \,\lambda_{\max}} $, where $ \lambda_{\max} $ is the largest eigenvalue of the spatial operator.
> * Wave propagation speed: $ c $ (m/s).
> * Actuator-saturation limit: $ u_{\max} $ (N or V).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying PDE Plant Model

Consider a canonical **one-dimensional wave equation** on the spatial domain $ x \in [0, L] $, which is the standard KTU benchmark for boundary-controlled flexible structures:

$$
\dfrac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial t^2} \;=\; c^2 \, \dfrac{\partial^2 w(x,t)}{\partial x^2} \;+\; f(x,t)
$$

where:
* $ w(x,t) $ = transverse displacement (m),
* $ c = \sqrt{T/\rho} $ = wave speed, with $ T $ = tension (N) and $ \rho $ = linear density (kg/m),
* $ f(x,t) $ = distributed disturbance.

**Boundary conditions (controlled end at $ x = L $):**
$$
w(0,t) = 0 \quad \text{(clamped end — measured)}, \quad w_x(L,t) = \dfrac{U(t)}{T} \quad \text{(actuated end)}
$$

**Boundary measurement (only the left end is observed through the network):**
$$
y(t) = w(0, t - \tau) \;+\; n(t)
$$

where $ \tau $ is the **network-induced delay** and $ n(t) $ is measurement noise.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of the Compensation Strategy

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the Loss of Information:**
With only $ w(0, t - \tau) $ available, the controller cannot directly reconstruct $ w(x, t) $. The system is **under-observed**.

**Step 2 — Augment with a Real-Time Model (Digital Twin):**
Run a finite-difference or spectral discretisation of the PDE on the controller's processor. This is the **"Model Plant"** or **Estimator Plant**.

**Step 3 — Use the Delayed Measurement to Drive the Estimator:**
A **Luenberger-type PDE observer** uses the *time-stamped* measurement $ w(0, t - \tau) $ to correct the predicted state. The error $ e(x,t) = \hat{w}(x,t) - w(x,t) $ must decay to zero.

**Step 4 — Design the Boundary Control Law on the Estimated State:**
Apply a **backstepping boundary controller** to the *estimated* state $ \hat{w}(L, t) $. The backstepping transformation converts the original PDE into a *target* stable PDE.

**Step 5 — Compensate for the Delay Inside the Observer:**
Add a **delay-compensating term** — typically a finite integral over the history of past controls and measurements — that explicitly contains $ \tau $ in its kernel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $ \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial t^2} = c^2 \dfrac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} $ | 1-D wave PDE | Plant model |
| 2 | $ \hat{w}_t(x,t) = c^2 \hat{w}_{xx}(x,t) + L \bigl( y(t) - \hat{w}(0,t-\tau) \bigr) $ | PDE observer dynamics | State estimation |
| 3 | $ U(t) = -k \hat{w}(L,t) - \int_0^{L} K(L,\xi)\, \hat{w}(\xi,t)\, d\xi $ | Backstepping boundary control | Stabilisation |
| 4 | $ V(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^L \!\! \bigl[ w_t^2 + c^2 w_x^2 \bigr]\, dx $ | Lyapunov functional | Stability proof |
| 5 | $ \dot{V} \leq -\mu V + \gamma \tau^2 \vert U \vert^2 $ | $ \mu $-exponential stability bound | Delay tolerance |
| 6 | $ \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \lambda_{\max}} $ | Max tolerable delay | Design rule |
| 7 | $ G_p(s) = \dfrac{e^{-\tau s}}{T_p s + 1} $ | Lumped analogue (Smith baseline) | Comparison only |
| 8 | $ \hat{w}(x,t) = \mathcal{P} \bigl[ U(\cdot),\, y(\cdot) \bigr] $ | Predictor map $ \mathcal{P} $ | Compact notation |
| 9 | $ \Delta(s) = 1 - e^{-\tau s} $ | Delay-block transfer function | Robustness margin |
| 10 | $ \dot{e} = A e + B\bigl( U(t-\tau) - U(t) \bigr) $ | Delay error dynamics | Error convergence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Compensation of large delays in boundary measurement is critical in:
> * **Flexible-link robotic manipulators** (Boeing/Dassault assembly lines) where the tip sensor is wired through a wireless bus.
> * **Long oil and gas pipelines** where flow is measured only at the pumping station.
* **Vibration control of high-rise buildings** with accelerometers at the roof communicating wirelessly to base isolators.
* **Telerobotic surgery systems** (Da Vinci research platforms) where round-trip latency may exceed 50 ms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Delay-Compensating Boundary Observer

We will derive the **PDE observer with explicit delay compensation** for the wave equation with boundary measurement.

**Step A — Write the Plant PDE in State Form**

Define the state vector $ \mathbf{z}(x,t) = \bigl[\, w(x,t), \; w_t(x,t) \,\bigr]^T $. The wave equation can be written as:

$$
\mathbf{z}_t(x,t) = \mathcal{A}\, \mathbf{z}(x,t) \;+\; \mathbf{B}\, U(t)
$$

where the spatial operator $ \mathcal{A} $ is given by:

$$
\mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ c^2 \, \partial_{xx} & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}\!_{\text{interior}} \;\; \text{(control enters only at boundary $ x=L $)}
$$

**Step B — Add the Network Delay to the Measurement Equation**

$$
y(t) = \mathbf{C}\, \mathbf{z}(0, t-\tau) \;+\; n(t), \quad \mathbf{C} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

This says we measure *only the displacement at $ x=0 $*, and that information arrives $ \tau $ seconds late.

**Step C — Propose the Observer Structure**

$$
\hat{\mathbf{z}}_t(x,t) = \mathcal{A}\, \hat{\mathbf{z}}(x,t) \;+\; \mathbf{L}(x)\, \bigl[\, y(t) - \mathbf{C}\, \hat{\mathbf{z}}(0,t-\tau) \,\bigr]
$$

where $ \mathbf{L}(x) $ is the **observer gain kernel** to be designed.

**Step D — Define the Estimation Error**

$$
\mathbf{e}(x,t) = \hat{\mathbf{z}}(x,t) - \mathbf{z}(x,t)
$$

Subtracting the plant dynamics from the observer dynamics:

$$
\mathbf{e}_t(x,t) = \mathcal{A}\, \mathbf{e}(x,t) \;-\; \mathbf{L}(x)\, \mathbf{C}\, \mathbf{e}(0, t-\tau) \;-\; \mathbf{L}(x)\, n(t)
$$

**Step E — Choose the Observer Gain via Backstepping Transformation**

Introduce the invertible transformation $ \mathbf{e}(x,t) = \mathbf{p}(x,t) - \int_0^x \mathbf{P}(x,\xi)\, \mathbf{p}(\xi,t)\, d\xi $, where $ \mathbf{P}(x,\xi) $ is a $ 2 \times 2 $ kernel matrix to be found.

Target system (designed to be exponentially stable):

$$
\mathbf{p}_t(x,t) = \mathcal{A}_0\, \mathbf{p}(x,t) \quad \text{with} \quad \mathcal{A}_0 = \mathcal{A} - \mu I
$$

The kernel $ \mathbf{P} $ must satisfy the **observer kernel PDE**:

$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial x}(x,\xi) \;+\; \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial \xi}(x,\xi)\, \mathcal{A}_0 \;=\; \mathcal{A}\, \mathbf{P}(x,\xi) \;\; \text{on } 0 \leq \xi \leq x \leq L
$$

with the boundary condition at $ \xi = x $ (transversal):

$$
\mathbf{P}(x, x) = \mathcal{A} - \mathcal{A}_0 = \mu I
$$

and the **delay-coupling condition** at $ \xi = 0 $:

$$
\mathbf{P}(x, 0) = \mathbf{L}(x) \, \mathbf{C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $ -\mathbf{L}(x)\, \mathbf{C}\, \mathbf{e}(0, t-\tau) $ appears in the error system because of the **delayed** boundary measurement. Solving the above coupled kernel equations yields the **delay-compensating gain** $ \mathbf{L}(x) $ as a *function of the delay $ \tau $*.

**Step F — Stability Conclusion**

Defining the Lyapunov functional:

$$
V(t) = \dfrac{1}{2} \int_0^L \Bigl[ \, e_2^{\,2}(x,t) \;+\; c^2 e_1^{\,2}_x(x,t) \,\Bigr]\, dx
$$

One can show that the time derivative satisfies:

$$
\dot{V}(t) \;\leq\; -c_1 \, V(t) \;+\; c_2 \, \tau^2 \, \sup_{s \in [t-\tau, t]} \vert e_1(0, s) \vert^2
$$

Provided $ \tau < \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \mu} $, the functional $ V(t) $ decays **exponentially**, and thus $ \mathbf{e}(x,t) \to 0 $ as $ t \to \infty $.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A flexible aluminium beam of length $ L = 1 $ m, wave speed $ c = 2 $ m/s, is controlled at $ x = L $ with a force actuator. The boundary sensor at $ x = 0 $ reports displacement through a network with delay $ \tau = 0.2 $ s. Design the observer gain $ L(x) $ using the backstepping kernel, and determine the maximum tolerable delay.

**Step 1 — Compute $ \lambda_{\max} $ for the clamped–free beam:** $ \lambda_{\max} = \dfrac{(2n+1)\pi}{2L} \approx \dfrac{\pi}{2} $ for the first mode.

**Step 2 — Maximum tolerable delay:**

$$
\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \, \lambda_{\max}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \cdot \pi / 2} = 1 \text{ second}
$$

**Step 3 — Check the design:** Since $ \tau = 0.2 $ s $ < \tau_{\max} = 1 $ s, the design is **feasible**; the closed-loop error will decay.

**Step 4 — Compute the observer gain kernel (first-mode approximation):**

$$
L(x) = \mu \cdot \cos\!\bigl( \lambda_{\max} \, x \bigr) \cdot e^{-\alpha \tau}, \quad \alpha = \dfrac{c \,\pi}{2 L}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
L(x) = \mu \cdot \cos\!\bigl( 1.5708\, x \bigr) \cdot e^{-0.6283 \times 0.2} \approx \mu \cdot \cos(1.5708\, x) \cdot 0.882
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $ e^{-\alpha \tau} $ in the gain explicitly shows the **delay-compensating attenuation** — the longer the delay, the *weaker* the correction term, which prevents over-amplification of stale measurements.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference r_t] --> B[Controller K_hat]
    B --> C[Network Actuator Channel]
    C --> D[Boundary Actuator at x is L]
    D --> E[Distributed Plant PDE on 0 to L]
    E --> F[Boundary Sensor at x is 0]
    F --> G[Network Measurement Channel delay tau]
    G --> H[Delay Compensating PDE Observer]
    H --> I[Estimated State w_hat x t]
    I --> B
    H --> J[PDE Model Plant Internal Copy]
    J --> H
    E --> K[Disturbance f x t]
    K --> E
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PlantWorld["Physical Plant Domain 0 to L"]
        PW1[Clamped End x is 0]
        PW2[Flexible Structure governed by Wave PDE]
        PW3[Actuated End x is L with Force U t]
        PW1 --- PW2 --- PW3
    end
    subgraph SensorNet["Sensor + Network Block"]
        SN1[Boundary Displacement Sensor y t]
        SN2[Network Encoder with Time Stamp]
        SN3[Communication Delay tau seconds]
        SN1 --> SN2 --> SN3
    end
    subgraph ControllerProc["Controller Processor"]
        CP1[Time Stamp Buffer]
        CP2[PDE Observer with Kernel L x]
        CP3[Backstepping Transformation]
        CP4[Boundary Control Law U t]
        CP1 --> CP2 --> CP3 --> CP4
    end
    subgraph ActNet["Actuator Network Block"]
        AN1[Communication Delay tau seconds]
        AN2[Decoder and D A Converter]
        AN1 --> AN2
    end
    PW1 --> SN1
    SN3 --> CP1
    CP4 --> AN1
    AN2 --> PW3
    PW2 --> CP2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** 
> The *real-time model plant* (block CP2 ↔ CP3) acts as a **Digital Twin** of the physical PDE. It receives *delayed* boundary measurements and produces an *undelayed* estimate of the full state $ \hat{w}(x,t) $. The control $ U(t) $ is then computed from this fresh estimate, so the network delay only enters through the *observer correction term*, not the *command signal*.

### 4.3 Information-Flow Timeline (Compensation Event Sequence)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Plant as Physical Plant
    participant Sensor as Boundary Sensor
    participant Net as Network
    participant Obs as PDE Observer
    participant Ctrl as Boundary Controller
    participant Act as Actuator
    Plant->>Sensor: w(0, t-τ) measured
    Sensor->>Net: packet sent with time stamp
    Net->>Obs: arrives at t
    Obs->>Obs: time-stamp check, correct using kernel L(x)
    Obs->>Ctrl: fresh estimate ŵ(L, t)
    Ctrl->>Act: command U(t) computed
    Act->>Plant: force applied at x = L
    Note over Plant,Obs: Delay τ is internal to observer — controller sees no delay
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define *boundary measurement* in the context of a distributed parameter networked control system. Why does it pose a more difficult estimation problem than interior (full-domain) measurement?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Boundary measurement* refers to sensing the state of a PDE-governed plant only at the spatial boundary (e.g., $ y(t) = w(0, t) $ or $ y(t) = w_x(L, t) $), rather than at every interior point.
