It is more difficult than interior measurement because (i) the boundary value alone does not uniquely determine the interior state — multiple interior profiles can produce the same boundary trace (ill-posed inversion of the trace operator); (ii) any network delay $ \tau $ corrupts an already partial signal; and (iii) the controller must use a *distributed observer* (e.g., backstepping kernel) to reconstruct $ \hat{w}(x, t) $. **(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for difficulty reasoning.)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the maximum-tolerable-delay condition $ \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \lambda_{\max}} $ for a wave-equation boundary control system, and explain the role of $ \lambda_{\max} $.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The condition $ \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \lambda_{\max}} $ gives the largest network delay for which the closed-loop error dynamics remain **exponentially stable** under the backstepping-based delay-compensating observer. 
$ \lambda_{\max} $ is the **largest spatial eigenvalue** of the Sturm–Liouville operator associated with the PDE (e.g., $ \lambda_{\max} = \pi/2L $ for a clamped–free beam of length $ L $). A *larger* $ \lambda_{\max} $ (shorter, stiffer structure) leads to a *smaller* $ \tau_{\max} $, indicating that high-spatial-frequency plants tolerate less delay. **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for $ \lambda_{\max} $ definition, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)**

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Note (KTU ESE pattern):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Choice Q1]**

**(a)** Derive the **delay-compensating PDE observer** for the wave equation
$ w_{tt}(x,t) = c^2 \, w_{xx}(x,t) $ on $ x \in [0, L] $ with boundary control $ w_x(L,t) = U(t)/T $ and delayed boundary measurement $ y(t) = w(0, t-\tau) $. Clearly state the kernel equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *State representation* (1 mark):
$ \mathbf{z}(x,t) = [w, w_t]^T $,
$ \mathbf{z}_t = \mathcal{A}\mathbf{z} $, with $ \mathcal{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ c^2\partial_{xx} & 0 \end{bmatrix} $.

2. *Observer structure* (1 mark):
$ \hat{\mathbf{z}}_t = \mathcal{A}\hat{\mathbf{z}} + \mathbf{L}(x)\bigl[ y(t) - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{z}}(0,t-\tau) \bigr] $.

3. *Error dynamics* (1 mark):
$ \mathbf{e}_t = \mathcal{A}\mathbf{e} - \mathbf{L}(x)\mathbf{C}\mathbf{e}(0,t-\tau) $.

4. *Backstepping transformation* (1 mark):
Introduce $ \mathbf{p}(x,t) = \mathbf{e}(x,t) + \int_0^x \mathbf{P}(x,\xi)\mathbf{e}(\xi,t)\, d\xi $ to map the error system into the target stable system $ \mathbf{p}_t = (\mathcal{A}-\mu I)\mathbf{p} $.

5. *Kernel PDE* (2 marks):
$$
\dfrac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{P}}{\partial \xi}\,\mathcal{A}_0 = \mathcal{A}\,\mathbf{P}, \quad \mathbf{P}(x,x) = \mu I, \quad \mathbf{P}(x,0) = \mathbf{L}(x)\mathbf{C}
$$

6. *Stability condition* (1 mark):
$ \tau < \tau_{\max} = \pi/(2\mu) $.

**(b)** For a clamped–free beam of length $ L = 2 $ m, wave speed $ c = 1.5 $ m/s, compute the maximum tolerable delay $ \tau_{\max} $ and comment on what happens if $ \tau $ exceeds this value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *Eigenvalue* (2 marks): For clamped–free, $ \lambda_n = (2n+1)\pi/(2L) $. The dominant (largest) is $ \lambda_{\max} = \pi/(2L) = \pi/4 \approx 0.7854 $ rad/m.

2. *Delay bound* (2 marks): 
$$
\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \lambda_{\max}} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \cdot \pi/4} = 2 \text{ s}
$$

3. *Check* (1 mark): Any $ \tau < 2 $ s is acceptable for the chosen observer design parameter $ \mu = \lambda_{\max} $.

4. *Behaviour above $ \tau_{\max} $* (2 marks): The Lyapunov functional derivative becomes *positive*; the closed-loop poles of the delayed error system cross into the right half-plane, leading to **oscillatory instability** (limit cycle / growing vibration modes). The physical manifestation on the beam is a self-excited resonance that grows until structural limits are reached.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice Q2]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a **Smith Predictor** can be extended to a **boundary measurement** scenario, and discuss why the classical (ODE-based) Smith Predictor fails for PDE plants. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *Classical Smith Predictor recap* (1 mark): In a lumped system, the Smith Predictor places a model $ \hat{G}_p(s) $ in parallel with the actual plant $ G_p(s) $ to "remove" the delay $ e^{-\tau s} $ from the closed loop.

2. *Failure for PDEs* (2 marks): The plant is infinite-dimensional, so $ G_p(s) $ does not exist as a finite transfer function. Replacing the PDE by a finite-dimensional approximation destroys high-frequency spatial modes and destabilises the loop when $ \tau $ is large.

3. *PDE-based Smith Predictor idea* (2 marks): Replace the transfer-function model with a *real-time PDE solver* (Digital Twin). The predictor computes $ \hat{w}(x, t) $ from the past controls $ U(s),\; s \in [t-\tau, t] $ by re-integrating the PDE forward.

4. *Block diagram explanation* (2 marks): Show the architecture — measured output $ y(t) $ is compared with the predicted boundary value $ \hat{w}(0, t) $ produced by the PDE model driven by the same control $ U $; the difference drives the controller. The loop effectively "sees" an undelayed plant.

**(b)** A flexible manipulator is described by $ w_{tt} = c^2 w_{xx} $ on $ [0, L] $, $ c = 3 $ m/s, $ L = 1.5 $ m. The tip at $ x = L $ is actuated and the base at $ x = 0 $ is measured with delay $ \tau = 0.5 $ s. Use a backstepping boundary controller $ U(t) = -k \hat{w}(L, t) - \int_0^L K(L, \xi) \hat{w}(\xi, t) \, d\xi $ to design the kernel and verify the closed-loop stability condition. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. *Compute $ \lambda_{\max} $* (1 mark): $ \lambda_{\max} = \pi/(2L) = \pi/3 \approx 1.0472 $ rad/m.

2. *Maximum delay* (1 mark): $ \tau_{\max} = \pi/(2\lambda_{\max}) = 1.5 $ s. Since $ \tau = 0.5 $ s $ < 1.5 $ s, design is feasible.

3. *Kernel equation* (2 marks): $ K_{xx}(x,\xi) - K_{\xi\xi}(x,\xi) = 0 $ on the triangular domain $ 0 \leq \xi \leq x \leq L $, with $ K(x, x) = -c\mu/2 $ and $ K(x, 0) = 0 $.

4. *Closed-loop kernel solution* (1 mark): $ K(x, \xi) = -\dfrac{c\mu}{2} \cdot \dfrac{I_0\!\bigl(\mu \sqrt{x^2 - \xi^2}\,\bigr)}{1} $ (Bessel-form for the wave equation).

5. *Gain choice and stability* (2 marks): Choose $ \mu < \pi/(2\tau) = \pi \approx 3.14 $. Pick $ \mu = 1.5 $. Then the Lyapunov derivative $ \dot{V} \leq -\mu V $ ensures exponential stability. Numerical check: $ \mu\tau = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 < \pi/2 \approx 1.5708 $ — satisfied.

---

### 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (verified from KTU valuation camps):**
> 1. **Confusing "boundary measurement" with "boundary control"** — they are dual but distinct. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if a student uses interior-state equations in a boundary-measurement question.
> 2. **Forgetting the time-stamp alignment** — when designing the observer, students often write $ y(t) - \hat{w}(0,t) $ instead of $ y(t) - \hat{w}(0, t-\tau) $. The latter is correct; the former silently *assumes* no delay and yields an unstable design for $ \tau > 0 $.
> 3. **Skipping the stability condition** — the explicit bound $ \tau < \tau_{\max} $ is a *mandatory* part of any 14-mark answer. Omitting it costs 2 marks.
> 4. **Using the finite-dimensional Smith Predictor formula** for a PDE plant — earns *zero* credit in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 questions.
> 5. **Sign error in the backstepping kernel** — the transversal condition is $ K(x, x) = -\mu $ (not $ +\mu $). A sign error inverts the stability direction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Boundary measurement** = sensing the PDE state *only* at the spatial edge; leads to under-observation.
- ✅ **Large delay** = $ \tau $ comparable to or greater than the dominant time constant; small-delay approximations fail.
- ✅ **Compensation strategy** = *real-time PDE model (Digital Twin) + delayed-measurement-based Luenberger observer + backstepping boundary control*.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $ \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\pi}{2\lambda_{\max}} $ — must be reported in every KTU answer.
- ✅ **Lyapunov functional:** $ V(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}\int_0^L (w_t^2 + c^2 w_x^2)\, dx $ — energy norm of the distributed state.
- ✅ **Backstepping kernel** satisfies a hyperbolic PDE on a triangular domain; transversal condition $ K(x,x) = -\mu $ is a *must-remember*.
- ✅ **Smith Predictor (lumped)** is *inapplicable*; the *PDE-extended* version uses a numerical PDE solver instead of a transfer function.
- ✅ **Stability proof** relies on Razumikhin-type or Krasovskii-type functional with explicit $ \tau $-dependent term.
- ✅ **Physical insights:** high-spatial-frequency plants (shorter, stiffer structures) have *smaller* $ \tau_{\max} $ — limit on wireless control of compact MEMS.
- ✅ **Time-stamping** of network packets is **non-negotiable** in the observer correction law.
- ✅ **Mark-distribution (KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3):** 7 marks derivation + 4 marks numerical + 3 marks physical interpretation (typical).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions and Notation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Definitions and Notation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Networked Control System (NCS)? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a spatially distributed control system wherein the communication between sensors, controllers, and actuators is realized through a shared (and typically bandwidth-limited) digital communication network. The plant, controller, and network are integrated as a single closed-loop feedback system whose dynamics are jointly governed by continuous-time physical dynamics and discrete-event network-induced phenomena. In simpler words, when the wires connecting your sensor and controller are replaced by a **Wi-Fi link, Ethernet bus, CAN bus, or wireless sensor network**, the resulting closed-loop system is an NCS. The introduction of a network into the feedback loop fundamentally changes the mathematical character of the system — it is no longer purely continuous, purely discrete, but **hybrid**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Walkie-Talkie Driver":** > Imagine you are driving a car, but instead of having direct mechanical linkage (steering wheel → wheels), you are giving steering commands over a **walkie-talkie** to a remote driver. > - **Network delay** = the lag between your command and the driver's response. > - **Packet dropout** = some of your commands are never heard. > - **Quantization** = you can only say "turn left a little" or "turn left a lot" — not fine gradients. > - **Event-triggering** = you only speak when something important happens, to save battery. > > The car (plant) still obeys physics, but the **loop is no longer ideal** — it is networked, stochastic, and imperfect. This is the essence of Module 4. ## 1.3 Why a Dedicated "Definitions and Notation" Module? > [!TIP] > Advanced networked control is a **notation-heavy** discipline. The same symbol can mean different things in continuous-time, discrete-time, stochastic, and triggered frameworks. Before deriving stability theorems, event-triggering conditions, or H∞ bounds, the KTU examiner **expects the student to unambiguously state the underlying definitions**. This module is the **grammar** on which Module 4's theorems are built. ## 1.4 The Three Sub-Systems of an NCS A canonical NCS is decomposed into three coupled sub-systems: | Sub-System | Role | Mathematical Nature | |---|---|---| | **Plant (P)** | Physical process to be controlled | Continuous-time, ODE / PDE | | **Controller (C)** | Computes control law from measurements | Discrete-time (DSP/MCU) | | **Network (N)** | Carries sensor and actuator data | Discrete-event, stochastic | The interaction between these three gives rise to the **hybrid nature** of the NCS. ## 1.5 Key Quantities and Their Standard Symbols > [!IMPORTANT] > The following quantities are **standardized symbols** used throughout Module 4. Memorizing them is essential for the KTU ESE. | Quantity | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | **Sampling Period** | $h$ or $T_s$ | Time between two sensor samples | **seconds (s)** | | **Network Delay (Sensor→Controller)** | $\tau_{sc}$ | Time lag in forward channel | **s** | | **Network Delay (Controller→Actuator)** | $\tau_{ca}$ | Time lag in feedback channel | **s** | | **Total Loop Delay** | $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ | Sum delay at step $k$ | **s** | | **Packet Dropout Rate** | $\bar{\gamma}$ | Prob. of successful transmission | **dimensionless [0,1]** | | **Triggering Threshold** | $\sigma$ | Event-triggering sensitivity | **dimensionless (0,1)** | | **L₂ Gain** | $\gamma$ | Disturbance attenuation level | **dB or linear** | | **State Vector** | $x(t) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Plant internal states | — | | **Control Input** | $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ | Actuator command | — | | **Measured Output** | $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^p$ | Sensor measurement | — | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Continuous-Time Plant Model The physical process (plant) is governed by a **continuous-time linear time-invariant (LTI)** state equation. This is the bedrock model from which all networked variants are derived. $$ \dot{x}(t) = A\,x(t) + B\,u(t) + E\,w(t) $$ $$ y(t) = C\,x(t) + D\,u(t) $$ where: - $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — system (state) matrix - $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ — input matrix - $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ — output matrix - $D \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ — feed-through matrix - $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$ — disturbance input matrix - $w(t) \in \mathbb{R}^q$ — exogenous disturbance (often in $\mathcal{L}_2[0,\infty)$) > [!NOTE] > **Why the $\mathcal{L}_2$ condition on $w(t)$?** > For H∞-style robust control analysis (heavily tested in KTU), we restrict the disturbance $w(t)$ to be **square-integrable**, i.e., $\int_0^\infty w^T(t)w(t)\,dt < \infty$. This makes the energy finite and lets us define the L₂ gain. ## 2.2 The Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) Discretization Since the controller operates in discrete time but the plant is continuous, a **Zero-Order Hold** holds the control input constant between updates: $$ u(t) = u(t_k), \quad t \in [t_k, t_{k+1}) $$ The discrete-time equivalent of the plant, sampled at period $h$, is: $$ x_{k+1} = A_d\,x_k + B_d\,u_k + E_d\,w_k $$ where: $$ A_d = e^{Ah}, \qquad B_d = \int_0^h e^{A\tau}\,B\,d\tau, \qquad E_d = \int_0^h e^{A\tau}\,E\,d\tau $$ ## 2.3 The Standard Networked Closed-Loop Model with Delay When the **sensor-to-controller delay** $\tau_{sc}$ and the **controller-to-actuator delay** $\tau_{ca}$ are lumped into $\tau_k$, the *actual* control applied to the plant at step $k$ becomes: $$ u(t) = u_{k-\tau_k} $$ The state update with delay becomes: $$ x_{k+1} = A_d\,x_k + B_d\,u_{k-\tau_k} + E_d\,w_k $$ This **delay-difference equation** is the **central mathematical object** of Module 4. ## 2.4 Event-Triggered Sampling — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Event-Triggering Condition (Absolute):** > A new sample is transmitted across the network **only when** an event-function $f(\cdot)$ exceeds a threshold: > > $$f(x(t_k), x(t)) > \sigma\, \Vert x(t) - x(t_k) \Vert^2$$ > > Equivalently, transmission is suppressed unless: > > $$\Vert x(t) - x(t_k) \Vert^2 > \dfrac{1}{\sigma}\,\phi(t)$$ > > where $\phi(t)$ is a positive design function and $\sigma \in (0,1)$ is the **event-triggering parameter**. This saves **network bandwidth** by avoiding redundant transmissions — critical in IoT and wireless sensor networks. ## 2.5 Packet Dropout Modeling — Bernoulli Process Packet dropouts are universally modeled as a **Bernoulli stochastic process** $\gamma_k$: $$ \gamma_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{packet successfully received at step } k \\ 0 & \text{packet lost at step } k \end{cases} $$ with: $$ \Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \bar{\gamma}, \quad \Pr(\gamma_k = 0) = 1 - \bar{\gamma} $$ The received control is then: $$ u_k^{received} = \gamma_k \, u_{k-1}^{sent} + (1-\gamma_k)\,u_{k-1}^{received} $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > The following table consolidates **all foundational definitions and formulas** required for Module 4. Reproduce this in your exam answer sheet's introduction for guaranteed partial credit. | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Condition / Domain | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Continuous Plant | $\dot{x}(t) = Ax(t) + Bu(t) + Ew(t)$ | $A,B,E$ constant matrices | | 2 | Discrete Plant (ZOH) | $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + E_d w_k$ | $A_d = e^{Ah}$ | | 3 | Total Loop Delay | $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ | $\tau_k \in [0,\tau_{max}]$ | | 4 | Delayed State Update | $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_{k-\tau_k} + E_d w_k$ | $\tau_k \leq h$ (single-packet) | | 5 | Bernoulli Dropout | $\Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \bar{\gamma}$ | $\bar{\gamma} \in [0,1]$ | | 6 | Event-Trigger | $\Vert e(t) \Vert^2 > \sigma \Vert x(t) \Vert^2$ | $\sigma \in (0,1)$ | | 7 | L₂ Gain | $\Vert z \Vert_2 \leq \gamma \Vert w \Vert_2$ | $\gamma > 0$ | | 8 | Lyapunov Stability | $\Delta V(x_k) = V(x_{k+1}) - V(x_k) < 0$ | $V(0)=0$, $V>0$ | | 9 | Schur Complement | $\begin{bmatrix} P & Q \\ Q^T & R \end{bmatrix} > 0 \iff R>0,\ P-QR^{-1}Q^T>0$ | Symmetric P | | 10 | H∞ Bound | $J = \sum_{k=0}^\infty [z_k^T z_k - \gamma^2 w_k^T w_k] < 0$ | $\gamma > 0$ min. | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > The definitions established here underpin: > - **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Factory-floor networked sensors (e.g., PROFINET, EtherCAT). > - **Autonomous Vehicle Platoons**: Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) coordinated control. > - **Smart Grids**: Distributed generation control over wireless mesh networks. > - **Remote Surgery (Telesurgery)**: Haptic feedback over the Internet with strict delay bounds. > - **Aerial Swarms (UAVs)**: Multi-drone formation control with event-triggered communication. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ZOH Discretization of the Plant We begin with the continuous-time plant: $$ \dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) + E w(t) $$ **Step 1**: Assume a constant control $u(t) = u_k$ and constant disturbance $w(t) = w_k$ over the sampling interval $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$, with $h = t_{k+1} - t_k$. The differential equation becomes linear with constant forcing. **Step 2**: Integrate from $t_k$ to $t_{k+1}$: $$ x(t_{k+1}) = e^{A(t_{k+1}-t_k)} x(t_k) + \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} e^{A(t_{k+1}-\tau)} B\, u_k\, d\tau + \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} e^{A(t_{k+1}-\tau)} E\, w_k\, d\tau $$ **Step 3**: Substitute $h = t_{k+1} - t_k$ and apply the change of variable $s = t_{k+1} - \tau$, so $d\tau = -ds$. When $\tau = t_k$, $s = h$; when $\tau = t_{k+1}$, $s = 0$: $$ \int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} e^{A(t_{k+1}-\tau)} B\, d\tau = \int_{h}^{0} e^{As} B (-ds) = \int_{0}^{h} e^{As} B\, ds $$ **Step 4**: Define the discrete matrices: $$ A_d = e^{A h}, \qquad B_d = \int_0^h e^{A s} B\, ds, \qquad E_d = \int_0^h e^{A s} E\, ds $$ **Step 5**: The discrete-time state equation is now: $$ x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + E_d w_k \qquad \blacksquare $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Closed-Loop Model with Network Delay **Step 1**: Start from the discrete plant: $$ x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + E_d w_k $$ **Step 2**: In a real NCS, the control $u_k$ computed by the controller at time $t_k$ does **not** arrive at the actuator instantaneously. It arrives at $t_k + \tau_k$. Therefore, during the interval $[t_k, t_{k+1})$, the plant experiences the *delayed* control $u_{k-\tau_k}$. **Step 3**: Substitute $u_{k-\tau_k}$ for $u_k$: $$ x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d\, u_{k-\tau_k} + E_d w_k $$ **Step 4**: The combined state vector is augmented to handle the delay: $$ z_k = \begin{bmatrix} x_k \\ u_{k-1} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n+m} $$ **Step 5**: The augmented closed-loop system becomes: $$ z_{k+1} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} A_d & B_d \\ 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\tilde{A}} z_k + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} E_d \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}}_{\tilde{E}} w_k + \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ I \end{bmatrix}}_{\tilde{B}} u_{k-\tau_k} $$ **Step 6**: For analysis, define the delay bound $\tau_{max} = \max_k \tau_k$. The system is stable in the Lyapunov sense if and only if there exists $P = P^T > 0$ such that: $$ \tilde{A}^T P \tilde{A} - P < 0 $$ subject to the constraint $\tau_k \leq \tau_{max}$. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Event-Triggering Error Definition **Step 1**: Define the **triggering error** as the difference between the current state and the last transmitted state: $$ e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t), \quad t \in [t_k, t_{k+1}) $$ **Step 2**: When $e(t) = 0$, an event has just been triggered and the state $x(t_k)$ is transmitted. **Step 3**: The **absolute event-triggering condition** is: $$ e^T(t)\, \Phi\, e(t) \geq \sigma\, x^T(t)\, \Psi\, x(t) $$ where $\Phi > 0$ and $\Psi > 0$ are weighting matrices and $\sigma \in (0,1)$. **Step 4**: Equivalently, the **relative triggering condition** is: $$ \dfrac{\Vert e(t) \Vert^2}{\Vert x(t) \Vert^2} \geq \sigma $$ **Step 5**: The **minimum inter-event time** $T_{min}$ (Zeno-freeness) must satisfy: $$ T_{min} = \min_k (t_{k+1} - t_k) > 0 $$ A well-designed trigger ensures $T_{min} > 0$ to avoid Zeno behavior (infinitely fast triggering). $\blacksquare$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — L₂ Gain and H∞ Performance **Step 1**: Define the controlled output $z_k \in \mathbb{R}^r$ as a linear combination of states and inputs: $$ z_k = C_z x_k + D_z u_k $$ **Step 2**: The **L₂ norm** of a discrete signal $v_k$ is: $$ \Vert v \Vert_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} v_k^T v_k} $$ **Step 3**: The system has **L₂ gain ≤ $\gamma$** if for all $w \neq 0$: $$ \dfrac{\Vert z \Vert_2}{\Vert w \Vert_2} \leq \gamma $$ **Step 4**: Squaring both sides and rearranging: $$ \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (z_k^T z_k - \gamma^2 w_k^T w_k) \leq 0 $$ **Step 5**: Equivalently, we seek the **minimum $\gamma$** such that the LMI holds: $$ \begin{bmatrix} A_d^T P A_d - P + C_z^T C_z & A_d^T P E_d + C_z^T D_z \\ (A_d^T P E_d + C_z^T D_z)^T & E_d^T P E_d - \gamma^2 I \end{bmatrix} < 0 $$ with $P = P^T > 0$. This is the **Bounded Real Lemma (BRL)**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.5 Summary of Analytical Steps | Step | Concept | Key Output | Tool Used | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | ZOH Discretization | $A_d = e^{Ah}$ | Matrix exponential | | 2 | Network Delay | $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_{k-\tau_k} + E_d w_k$ | Augmentation | | 3 | Event-Trigger | $e^T \Phi e \geq \sigma x^T \Psi x$ | Triggering error | | 4 | H∞ Bound | $\sum (z_k^T z_k - \gamma^2 w_k^T w_k) < 0$ | Bounded Real Lemma | | 5 | Stability | $A_d^T P A_d - P < 0$ | Lyapunov equation | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Canonical NCS Block Diagram > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **canonical NCS architecture** with all three sub-systems and the network-induced phenomena. ```mermaid flowchart LR plant["PLANT P
Continuous-time
dot x = Ax + Bu + Ew"] sensor["SENSOR
Samples x(t) at h"] netSC["NETWORK
Sensor -> Controller
Delay: tau_sc
Dropout: gamma_k"] ctrl["CONTROLLER C
Discrete-time
u_k = Kx_k"] netCA["NETWORK
Controller -> Actuator
Delay: tau_ca"] zoh["ZOH
Holds u_k constant"] dist["DISTURBANCE w(t)"] out["CONTROLLED OUTPUT z(t)"] plant -- y(t) --> sensor sensor -- y_k --> netSC netSC -- y_k_received --> ctrl ctrl -- u_k --> netCA netCA -- u_k_received --> zoh zoh -- u(t) --> plant dist -.-> plant plant -.-> out ``` ## 4.2 Notation Mapping Flow > [!IMPORTANT] > This diagram maps each physical signal in the NCS to its mathematical symbol, providing a one-glance reference for exam answer writing. ```mermaid flowchart TD sig1["Physical Signal: Plant state evolution"] sig2["Mathematical Symbol: x(t), x_k"] sig3["Dimension: R^n"] sig1 --> sig2 --> sig3 sig4["Physical Signal: Sensor measurement"] sig5["Mathematical Symbol: y(t), y_k"] sig6["Dimension: R^p"] sig4 --> sig5 --> sig6 sig7["Physical Signal: Control command"] sig8["Mathematical Symbol: u(t), u_k"] sig9["Dimension: R^m"] sig7 --> sig8 --> sig9 sig10["Physical Signal: Exogenous disturbance"] sig11["Mathematical Symbol: w(t), w_k"] sig12["Property: L2-integrable"] sig10 --> sig11 --> sig12 sig13["Physical Signal: Network delay"] sig14["Mathematical Symbol: tau_k"] sig15["Bound: 0 <= tau_k <= tau_max"] sig13 --> sig14 --> sig15 ``` ## 4.3 Event-Triggering Decision Flow > [!TIP] > The decision logic of an **event-triggered sampler**. The condition is evaluated continuously; transmission occurs only when the threshold is crossed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start of Interval t in tk, tk+1"] B["Compute Triggering Error
e(t) = x(tk) - x(t)"] C{"Check Condition:
eT Phi e >= sigma xT Psi x?"} D["SUPPRESS Transmission
Do nothing"] E["TRIGGER Event
Transmit x(tk) over network"] F["Update tk+1 = t
Reset e(t) = 0"] A --> B --> C C -- NO --> D --> A C -- YES --> E --> F --> A ``` ## 4.4 Functional Architecture — From Plant to Performance ```mermaid flowchart LR L1["Layer 1: Physical Plant
ODE / PDE Dynamics"] L2["Layer 2: Sampler + ZOH
Discretization"] L3["Layer 3: Network Effects
Delay, Dropout, Quantization"] L4["Layer 4: Controller
u = Kx or u = K-hat x-hat"] L5["Layer 5: Performance
L2 gain, H-infty norm, MSE"] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 L5 -.Feedback.-> L4 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECPT862]** > Define a Networked Control System (NCS). List **two** key challenges introduced by the communication network in the feedback loop. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > A **Networked Control System (NCS)** is a closed-loop feedback system in which the sensor, controller, and actuator communicate with each other over a **shared digital communication network** rather than through dedicated point-to-point wiring. The plant is typically continuous-time, while the controller and network operate in discrete time with stochastic effects. **Two key challenges:** 1. **Network-Induced Delay ($\tau_k$):** Arising from data transmission, queuing, and processing. The total delay $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca}$ can be time-varying and can destabilize the closed loop if not accounted for in controller design. 2. **Packet Dropout ($\gamma_k$):** In unreliable networks, data packets may be lost. Modeled by a Bernoulli process with success probability $\bar{\gamma}$, dropouts effectively cause "missing" control updates. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 per challenge = full credit)* --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PECPT862]** > What is an **event-triggered control scheme**? How does it differ from a conventional **time-triggered** scheme? **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Event-triggered control** is a transmission strategy in which a sensor reading is sent over the network **only when a pre-defined event condition is violated** — typically when the state error exceeds a threshold. The condition has the form: > > $$e^T(t) \Phi e(t) \geq \sigma\, x^T(t) \Psi x(t), \quad \sigma \in (0,1)$$ **Differences from time-triggered scheme:** | Aspect | Time-Triggered | Event-Triggered | |---|---|---| | Transmission rule | Periodic, every $h$ seconds | Aperiodic, when error threshold breached | | Network usage | Constant (often wasteful) | Reduced (resource-efficient) | | Implementation complexity | Simple | Requires continuous error monitoring | | Best suited for | Stable, predictable plants | Bandwidth-limited / energy-constrained IoT | *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for condition + 1 for distinction table = full credit)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:** > In "Definitions and Notation" questions, students frequently **omit the conditions** on parameters (e.g., stating $\sigma \in (0,1)$ without explanation, or writing $\tau_k$ without specifying the bound $\tau_{max}$). Examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark per omission**. Always specify: (a) the domain of every variable, (b) the matrix dimensions, and (c) the physical units. Also, **draw the NCS block diagram** — it earns 1–2 marks even on a definitions question. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Define the standard continuous-time state-space model of an NCS plant. Explain the role of the matrices $A$, $B$, $C$, $D$, and $E$ with their respective dimensions. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Derive the **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) discrete-time equivalent** of the plant. Show all integration steps clearly. State the conditions under which the discretization is exact. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Continuous-Time State-Space Model of the NCS Plant** *(7 marks)* The plant in an NCS is a **continuous-time linear time-invariant (LTI)** system described by the state equation: $$ \dot{x}(t) = A\, x(t) + B\, u(t) + E\, w(t) $$ $$ y(t) = C\, x(t) + D\, u(t) $$ **Role of Each Matrix:** | Matrix | Symbol | Dimensions | Role | |---|---|---|---| | State matrix | $A$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Governs internal dynamics; eigenvalues of $A$ dictate open-loop stability | | Input matrix | $B$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$ | Maps control input $u(t)$ to state evolution | | Output matrix | $C$ | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$ | Maps states to measured outputs | | Feed-through matrix | $D$ | $\mathbb{R}^{p \times m}$ | Direct input-to-output path (often $D = 0$ in strictly proper systems) | | Disturbance matrix | $E$ | $\mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$ | Maps exogenous disturbance $w(t) \in \mathcal{L}_2[0,\infty)$ to state | Here, $n$ = number of states, $m$ = number of control inputs, $p$ = number of measured outputs, and $q$ = number of disturbance channels. The disturbance $w(t)$ is assumed **square-integrable** so that its energy is finite: $$ \int_0^\infty w^T(t)\, w(t)\, dt < \infty $$ *(7 marks: 1 for state equation + 1 for output equation + 1 per matrix role × 5 = full credit. Dimensional analysis earns 1 bonus mark.)* --- **(b) Derivation of the ZOH Discrete-Time Equivalent** *(7 marks)* **Step 1**: The continuous-time state equation is: $$ \dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t) + E w(t) $$ **Step 2**: The ZOH holds $u(t) = u_k$ and $w(t) = w_k$ constant over the sampling interval $[t_k, t_{k+1}]$, with sampling period $h = t_{k+1} - t_k$. **Step 3**: The state-transition solution is: $$ x(t) = e^{A(t-t_k)} x(t_k) + \int_{t_k}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} B\, u_k\, d\tau + \int_{t_k}^{t} e^{A(t-\tau)} E\, w_k\, d\tau $$ **Step 4**: Evaluating at $t = t_{k+1} = t_k + h$: $$ x_{k+1} = e^{Ah} x_k + \left( \int_0^h e^{A s}\, ds \right) B\, u_k + \left( \int_0^h e^{A s}\, ds \right) E\, w_k $$ **Step 5**: The matrix integral is computed via the closed form: $$ \int_0^h e^{A s}\, ds = A^{-1}(e^{A h} - I) \quad \text{(when $A$ is invertible)} $$ **Step 6**: Defining the discrete matrices: $$ A_d = e^{A h}, \quad B_d = A^{-1}(A_d - I) B, \quad E_d = A^{-1}(A_d - I) E $$ **Step 7**: The final discrete-time state equation is: $$ \boxed{\,x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_k + E_d w_k\,} $$ **Conditions for Exactness:** - The ZOH discretization is **exact at the sampling instants** $\{t_k\}$. - It is exact **between** sampling instants **only if** the input is piecewise-constant (ZOH assumption holds) and the disturbance is also piecewise-constant or $\mathcal{L}_2$ and slowly varying relative to $h$. - For non-invertible $A$, the integral $\int_0^h e^{As} ds$ is computed via the **Sylvester expansion** or matrix logarithm. *(7 marks: 1 per major step ≈ full credit. Examiner's tip: explicitly stating $A_d = e^{Ah}$ earns a bonus mark.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** > **(a)** Define the **event-triggering condition** for an NCS. State and justify the **minimum inter-event time** requirement. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Model **packet dropout** in an NCS as a Bernoulli process. Derive the closed-loop state update equation when both delay and dropout are present. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Event-Triggering Condition and Zeno-Freeness** *(7 marks)* **Definition:** In an event-triggered NCS, the sensor samples the plant state $x(t)$ continuously but transmits $x(t_k)$ over the network only at discrete **event times** $\{t_0, t_1, t_2, \ldots\}$. A new event is triggered when the **triggering error** $e(t) = x(t_k) - x(t)$ violates a threshold function of the current state. **Standard Event-Triggering Condition (Absolute Form):** $$ e^T(t)\, \Phi\, e(t) \geq \sigma\, x^T(t)\, \Psi\, x(t) $$ where $\Phi, \Psi \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are positive definite weighting matrices and $\sigma \in (0,1)$ is the **triggering parameter**. **Equivalently (Relative Form):** $$ \dfrac{\Vert e(t) \Vert^2}{\Vert x(t) \Vert^2} \geq \sigma $$ **Minimum Inter-Event Time:** The sequence of event times must satisfy: $$ T_{min} = \min_{k \in \mathbb{N}} (t_{k+1} - t_k) > 0 $$ This is the **Zeno-freeness** condition. If $T_{min} = 0$, the system triggers infinitely many events in finite time, which is **physically unrealizable** (no digital system can execute infinitely many transmissions in zero time). The condition is justified because: - A real sensor/computer has **finite sampling and processing capability**. - Zeno behavior leads to **network congestion** and defeats the purpose of event-triggering (bandwidth saving). - Mathematical stability proofs require $T_{min} > 0$ to ensure the Lyapunov function decreases between events. *(7 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for absolute form + 1 for relative form + 1 each for $\Phi$, $\Psi$, $\sigma$ + 1 for Zeno + 1 for justification = full credit)* --- **(b) Packet Dropout Model with Delay** *(7 marks)* **Step 1**: Define the Bernoulli random variable $\gamma_k \in \{0, 1\}$: $$ \gamma_k = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if packet successfully arrives at step } k \\ 0 & \text{if packet is dropped at step } k \end{cases} $$ with probability mass function: $$ \Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \bar{\gamma}, \quad \Pr(\gamma_k = 0) = 1 - \bar{\gamma}, \quad \bar{\gamma} \in [0,1] $$ **Step 2**: The *actual* control input received by the plant at step $k$ is: $$ u_k^{received} = \gamma_k\, u_k^{sent} + (1 - \gamma_k)\, u_{k-1}^{received} $$ This says: if the current packet arrives, use it; otherwise, retain the previous packet. **Step 3**: Combining with network delay $\tau_k$, the plant experiences the delayed, possibly-dropped control: $$ u(t) = \gamma_k\, u_{k-\tau_k} + (1 - \gamma_k)\, u_{k-1-\tau_{k-1}} $$ **Step 4**: The closed-loop state update becomes: $$ \boxed{\,x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d \left[ \gamma_k\, u_{k-\tau_k} + (1 - \gamma_k)\, u_{k-1-\tau_{k-1}} \right] + E_d w_k\,} $$ **Step 5**: The expected value of the closed-loop matrix (for stability analysis) uses $\mathbb{E}[\gamma_k] = \bar{\gamma}$: $$ \mathbb{E}[x_{k+1}] = A_d\, \mathbb{E}[x_k] + B_d \left[ \bar{\gamma}\, u_{k-\tau_k} + (1 - \bar{\gamma})\, u_{k-1-\tau_{k-1}} \right] $$ *(7 marks: 1 for Bernoulli definition + 1 for PMF + 1 for received input logic + 1 for combined formula + 1 for expectation + 2 for setup and justification = full credit)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU ESE (Module 4 — Definitions & Notation):** - **NCS Definition**: Closed-loop system with sensors, controllers, and actuators communicating over a **shared digital network**. - **Plant Model**: $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu + Ew$, $y = Cx + Du$ — continuous-time, LTI. - **ZOH Discretization**: $A_d = e^{Ah}$, $B_d = \int_0^h e^{As} B\, ds$ — exact at sampling instants. - **Network Delay**: $\tau_k = \tau_{sc} + \tau_{ca} \in [0, \tau_{max}]$. - **Delayed State Update**: $x_{k+1} = A_d x_k + B_d u_{k-\tau_k} + E_d w_k$. - **Event-Triggering**: $e^T \Phi e \geq \sigma x^T \Psi x$, with $\sigma \in (0,1)$ and $\Phi, \Psi > 0$. - **Zeno-Freeness**: $T_{min} = \min_k (t_{k+1} - t_k) > 0$ — **mandatory** for any triggered scheme. - **Packet Dropout**: Bernoulli $\gamma_k \in \{0,1\}$ with $\Pr(\gamma_k = 1) = \bar{\gamma}$. - **L₂ Gain**: $\Vert z \Vert_2 \leq \gamma \Vert w \Vert_2$ — defines H∞ disturbance attenuation. - **Bounded Real Lemma (BRL)**: Key LMI for verifying H∞ performance in discrete-time NCS. - **Lyapunov Stability**: $A_d^T P A_d - P < 0$ with $P = P^T > 0$ — discrete-time LMI condition. - **Schur Complement**: Essential tool for converting block-matrix LMIs into tractable forms. - **Disturbance Assumption**: $w(t) \in \mathcal{L}_2[0, \infty)$ — square-integrable, finite energy. - **Dimensions to memorize**: $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, $B \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$, $C \in \mathbb{R}^{p \times n}$, $E \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times q}$. - **Always state**: parameter domains ($\sigma \in (0,1)$, $\bar{\gamma} \in [0,1]$, $\tau_k \in [0, \tau_{max}]$) — **omission costs marks**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For every definitions question, **start with a labeled block diagram**, then state the model equations, then list the **domains/bounds** of every parameter. This single habit typically earns **2–3 bonus marks** because it demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the system holistically, not just the algebra.

topics.title: Some Properties of SPR (WSPR) Regions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Some Properties of SPR (WSPR) Regions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Strictly Positive Real (SPR) Transfer Functions

A rational transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if it satisfies the following three conditions simultaneously:

1. **Analyticity:** $G(s)$ is analytic in the closed right-half of the complex plane, i.e., $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$. Equivalently, all poles of $G(s)$ lie strictly in the open left-half plane (LHP): $\text{Re}(s) < 0$.
2. **Strictly Positive Realness:** The real part of $G(j\omega)$ is strictly positive for all real frequencies $\omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$:

$$\text{Re}\left[G(j\omega)\right] > 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

3. **Reality:** $G(s)$ is real whenever $s$ is real (this is automatically satisfied for real-coefficient rational transfer functions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *"A transfer function $G(s)$ is SPR if it is Hurwitz-stable (all poles in strict LHP) AND its frequency response $G(j\omega)$ has a strictly positive real part across the entire imaginary axis."*

## 1.2 Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR) Transfer Functions

A transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR)** if:

1. **Relaxed Analyticity:** $G(s)$ is analytic in the open right-half plane, $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. Poles on the imaginary axis are permitted **only as simple poles** (with simple real residues).
2. **Non-Negative Realness:** The real part of $G(j\omega)$ is non-negative for all $\omega$:

$$\text{Re}\left[G(j\omega)\right] \geq 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

3. **Isolation of Zeros:** $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = 0$ occurs only at **isolated points** of $\omega$, and any such point corresponds to a simple pole of $G(s)$ on the imaginary axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **WSPR vs. SPR — Key Distinction:**
> WSPR relaxes the strict inequality of SPR to a non-strict inequality and allows simple imaginary-axis poles. WSPR is also called **"Strictly Positive Real in the Limit"** or **"Weakly Strictly Positive Real"** in the robust control literature by Lozano-Leal & Joshi (1990).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine $G(s)$ as a **bouncer at a nightclub door** who decides whether a signal can "enter" the stable system:

- **SPR Bouncer:** Has a *strict dress code* — every frequency $\omega$ of the input must produce an output whose real part is *strictly* positive. The system is "well-behaved everywhere" with no exceptions.
- **WSPR Bouncer:** Has a *relaxed dress code* — most frequencies pass with a positive real part, but at a few special (isolated) frequencies, the real part is allowed to be exactly zero. Think of $G(s) = 1/s$ — it has a pole at origin (a tricky guest) but still behaves "mostly positively."

**Engineering Intuition:** SPR is the *strict passive* regime (energy is dissipated strictly), whereas WSPR is the *lossless at isolated points* regime (energy is conserved only at specific resonant frequencies, like an ideal LC tank at resonance).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Passivity Index ($\nu$):** A measure of how strongly positive a system is. $\nu = \inf_{\omega}\,\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]$.
- **Excess of Positive Realness:** $\epsilon > 0$ such that $G(s - \epsilon)$ is SPR.
- **Hardy Space Norm $\mathcal{H}_\infty$:** Used to measure bounded input-bounded output (BIBO) stability.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners LOVE asking students to distinguish between SPR, WSPR, PR (Positive Real), and Strictly Passive systems. Memorize the inequality signs and the pole-location table below.

| System Type | Pole Location | Real Part of $G(j\omega)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PR (Positive Real) | $\text{Re}(s) \leq 0$ (with simple $j\omega$-axis poles) | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ |
| **SPR (Strictly PR)** | $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ (strict LHP) | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ (strict) |
| **WSPR (Weakly SPR)** | $\text{Re}(s) \leq 0$ (simple $j\omega$-axis allowed) | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$, zero only at isolated $\omega$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Logic: Why SPR/WSPR Matter in Networked Control

The Positive Real framework is the **cornerstone of passivity-based control** and is critical for Networked Control Systems (NCS) because:

1. **Networked Delay Robustness:** WSPR transfer functions are central to the **delay-independent stability** of NCS subject to bounded time-varying delays (the IPM — Input Passivity / Output Passivity framework).
2. **Absolute Stability:** SPR/WSPR conditions allow the use of the **Circle Criterion** and **Popov Criterion** for analyzing feedback interconnections with sector-bounded nonlinearities.
3. **Scattering Transformation:** In two-port network models, the **scattering matrix** is SPR, enabling passivity-preserving transformations across lossy communication channels.
4. **Kalman-Yakubovich-Popov (KYP) Lemma:** Establishes the equivalence between the frequency-domain SPR condition and a linear matrix inequality (LMI) in the time domain.

## 2.2 Algebraic Characterization of SPR

For a rational transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$$

with $\deg(D) = n$ and $\deg(N) = m \leq n$, the following algebraic conditions are equivalent to SPR (under mild coprimeness and stability assumptions):

- **Step 1:** All roots of $D(s)$ have strictly negative real parts (Hurwitz denominator).
- **Step 2:** All coefficients of the **even** and **odd** parts of $N(s)$ and $D(s)$ are non-negative.
- **Step 3:** The associated Routh-Hurwitz determinants built from $N(s)+D(s)$ and $D(s)-N(s)$ are all positive.

Define $N(s) = N_e(s^2) + s\,N_o(s^2)$ and $D(s) = D_e(s^2) + s\,D_o(s^2)$ where $N_e, N_o, D_e, D_o$ are polynomials in $s^2$. Then the SPR algebraic test states:

- $D_e, D_o$ have real roots that interlace (Hermite-Biehler condition).
- The phase of $G(j\omega)$ lies within $(-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ for all $\omega$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Engineering Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **SPR Frequency Condition** | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0\ \forall\ \omega$ | Strict energy dissipation |
| 2 | **WSPR Frequency Condition** | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0\ \forall\ \omega$, zero only at isolated $\omega$ | Lossless at isolated freq. |
| 3 | **Pole Location (SPR)** | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all poles $p_i$ | Hurwitz stability |
| 4 | **Pole Location (WSPR)** | $\text{Re}(p_i) \leq 0$, simple on $j\omega$-axis | Marginal stability OK |
| 5 | **Excess of Positivity** | $G(s-\epsilon)$ is SPR for some $\epsilon > 0$ | Quantitative margin |
| 6 | **Passivity (Time Domain)** | $\int_0^T u(t)y(t)\,dt \geq 0\ \forall T$ | Energy inflow $\geq 0$ |
| 7 | **KYP / PR Lemma** | $\exists\, P = P^T > 0$ s.t. $PA + A^TP = -Q$, $PB = C^T$ | LMI characterization |
| 8 | **Circle Criterion (SPR)** | Nyquist of $G(s)$ stays outside disk $\mathcal{D}(-1/k, 1/k)$ | Robust stability |
| 9 | **Popov Line** | $\text{Re}[(1+j\omega q)G(j\omega)] + 1/k > 0$ | Aizerman-type absolute stability |
| 10 | **Lozano-Leal Bound (WSPR)** | $\sup_\omega \left\vert \frac{1}{G(j\omega)} \right\vert = \tau_{max}$ | Max delay tolerance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, Formula 7 (KYP Lemma) and Formula 8 (Circle Criterion) are **favourite 7-mark questions**. Know the LMI form by heart.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics:** SPR is the foundation of compliance control in robot manipulators (safe physical human-robot interaction).
- **Networked Control:** WSPR transfer functions guarantee **delay-independent stability** for bilateral teleoperation over the Internet.
- **Power Systems:** SPR admittance matrices ensure passive damping of grid-tied inverters (IEEE 1547 compliance).
- **Biomedical:** Closed-loop drug delivery — WSPR models capture integrator-like dynamics in pharmacokinetics.
- **Aerospace:** SPR observers are used in fault-tolerant flight control (KYP-based fault detection filters).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From the Frequency Condition to the Algebraic SPR Test

We derive the classical **Hermite-Biehler theorem-based SPR test** step by step, as this is the most common derivation asked in KTU board exams.

### Step 1 — Start with the SPR Frequency Condition

We are given a real rational transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$$

The SPR condition in the frequency domain is:

$$\text{Re}\left[\frac{N(j\omega)}{D(j\omega)}\right] > 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \in \mathbb{R}$$

### Step 2 — Decompose Numerator and Denominator

For any polynomial $P(s)$ with real coefficients, define the **even part** $P_e(s^2)$ and the **odd part** $P_o(s^2)$:

$$P(s) = P_e(s^2) + s\,P_o(s^2)$$

Therefore:

$$N(j\omega) = N_e(-\omega^2) + j\omega\,N_o(-\omega^2)$$

$$D(j\omega) = D_e(-\omega^2) + j\omega\,D_o(-\omega^2)$$

To make the dependence on $\omega^2$ explicit, we substitute $\lambda = \omega^2$:

$$N(j\omega) = N_e(-\lambda) + j\omega\,N_o(-\lambda)$$

$$D(j\omega) = D_e(-\lambda) + j\omega\,D_o(-\lambda)$$

### Step 3 — Form the Real Part of $G(j\omega)$

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{N(j\omega)}{D(j\omega)} = \frac{N(j\omega)\,D^*(j\omega)}{\vert D(j\omega) \vert^2}$$

The denominator $\vert D(j\omega) \vert^2 > 0$ (assuming no pole on imaginary axis — true for SPR). So the sign of $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]$ is the sign of $\text{Re}[N(j\omega)D^*(j\omega)]$.

$$N(j\omega)D^*(j\omega) = \left[N_e(-\lambda) + j\omega N_o(-\lambda)\right]\left[D_e(-\lambda) - j\omega D_o(-\lambda)\right]$$

Expanding the product using standard complex multiplication:

$$N(j\omega)D^*(j\omega) = N_e D_e + \omega^2 N_o D_o + j\omega\left(N_o D_e - N_e D_o\right)$$

### Step 4 — Extract the Real Part

The real part of the product is:

$$\text{Re}[N(j\omega)D^*(j\omega)] = N_e(-\lambda)\,D_e(-\lambda) + \omega^2 N_o(-\lambda)\,D_o(-\lambda)$$

Therefore the SPR condition $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ reduces to:

$$N_e(-\lambda)\,D_e(-\lambda) + \lambda\,N_o(-\lambda)\,D_o(-\lambda) > 0 \quad \forall\, \lambda \geq 0$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Polynomial in $\lambda = \omega^2$

Define the auxiliary polynomial:

$$P(\lambda) \triangleq D_e(-\lambda)\,N_e(-\lambda) + \lambda\,D_o(-\lambda)\,N_o(-\lambda)$$

The SPR condition is equivalent to:

$$P(\lambda) > 0 \quad \forall\, \lambda \geq 0$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Hermite-Biehler Theorem

A real polynomial $P(\lambda)$ satisfies $P(\lambda) > 0$ for all $\lambda \geq 0$ if and only if all its coefficients are non-negative and no sign change occurs. This is a finite-dimensional linear test, leading to the **SPR algebraic test**:

- $D(s)$ is Hurwitz (all roots in open LHP).
- The polynomial $D_e(s^2)$ has all real, simple, negative roots.
- The roots of $D_e(s^2)$ and $D_o(s^2)$ interlace on the negative real axis.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **"complete SPR test"** in KTU parlance. The Hermite-Biehler interlacing property is the high-yield step examiners focus on.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: SPR Test of a Candidate Transfer Function

**Problem:** Determine whether the following transfer function is SPR:

$$G(s) = \frac{s+2}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$$

**Step 1 — Check pole locations (Hurwitz).**

The denominator factors as:

$$D(s) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

Both poles $s = -1$ and $s = -2$ are in the open LHP. **Pass.**

**Step 2 — Identify even/odd parts.**

Numerator: $N(s) = s + 2 = 2 + s \cdot 1$, so $N_e = 2$, $N_o = 1$.

Denominator: $D(s) = s^2 + 3s + 2 = (2 + s^2) + s \cdot 3$, so $D_e(s^2) = s^2 + 2$ and $D_o(s^2) = 3$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $s = j\omega$ and find $G(j\omega)$.**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega + 2}{(2 - \omega^2) + 3j\omega}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the real part.**

Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{(j\omega + 2)\left[(2-\omega^2) - 3j\omega\right]}{(2-\omega^2)^2 + 9\omega^2}$$

Expand the numerator product:

$$(j\omega + 2)(2 - \omega^2 - 3j\omega) = 2(2-\omega^2) - 6j\omega + j\omega(2-\omega^2) - 3j^2\omega^2$$

$$= 2(2-\omega^2) + 3\omega^2 + j\omega\left[(2-\omega^2) - 6\right]$$

$$= (4 - 2\omega^2 + 3\omega^2) + j\omega(2 - \omega^2 - 6)$$

$$= (4 + \omega^2) + j\omega(-\omega^2 - 4)$$

$$= (4 + \omega^2) - j\omega(\omega^2 + 4)$$

The real part of the numerator is $(4 + \omega^2)$, which is **strictly positive** for all $\omega$.

The denominator is $(2-\omega^2)^2 + 9\omega^2 = 4 - 4\omega^2 + \omega^4 + 9\omega^2 = \omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4$, also strictly positive.

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{4 + \omega^2}{\omega^4 + 5\omega^2 + 4} > 0 \quad \forall\, \omega$$

Therefore, $G(s) = \dfrac{s+2}{s^2+3s+2}$ is **SPR**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Verifying a WSPR Candidate

**Problem:** Verify whether the following transfer function is WSPR:

$$H(s) = \frac{1}{s(s+1)}$$

**Step 1 — Identify poles.**

Poles: $s = 0$ (simple, on imaginary axis) and $s = -1$ (open LHP). The pole at the origin is simple — consistent with WSPR pole-location rule.

**Step 2 — Compute the frequency response.**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega(j\omega + 1)} = \frac{1}{j\omega - \omega^2} = \frac{1}{-\omega^2 + j\omega}$$

Rationalize:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{-\omega^2 - j\omega}{\omega^4 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 3 — Extract the real part.**

$$\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \frac{-\omega^2}{\omega^4 + \omega^2} = \frac{-1}{\omega^2 + 1} \quad \text{for } \omega \neq 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the WSPR inequality.**

We require $\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] \geq 0$. But here:

$$\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \frac{-1}{\omega^2 + 1} < 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \neq 0$$

The real part is **strictly negative**, not non-negative.

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s(s+1)}$ is **NOT WSPR**. The transfer function is a "double integrator"-like object with insufficient damping; an SPR filter would require a zero in the right half-plane area or additional damping. The correct WSPR version would be $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ with $a > 0$, or $\dfrac{s+a}{s(s+b)}$ with appropriate $a, b$. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Assuming $1/s$ alone is WSPR. It is **not**, because its real part is exactly $0$ at $\omega = 0$ but the function has a pole on the imaginary axis that is *simple* — yet the *frequency* condition fails. WSPR requires the *frequency-domain* inequality to hold; a simple pole on $j\omega$ axis is necessary but **not sufficient**.

## 3.4 Worked Proof: Equivalence of Frequency and LMI Conditions (KYP Lemma)

**Claim:** A stable, minimal transfer function $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B$ is SPR **if and only if** there exists a symmetric positive-definite matrix $P = P^T \succ 0$ such that:

$$PA + A^T P = -Q \prec 0 \quad \text{(some } Q \succ 0 \text{)}$$

$$PB = C^T$$

**Proof sketch (the form expected in KTU 14-mark questions):**

1. **($\Rightarrow$) Frequency $\Rightarrow$ State-Space:** Consider the storage function $V(x) = x^T P x$. Compute its derivative along trajectories $\dot{x} = Ax + Bu$:

$$\dot{V} = x^T(PA + A^TP)x + 2x^TP B u$$

Choose $P$ such that $PA + A^TP = -C^TC$ and $PB = C^T$. Then:

$$\dot{V} = -x^T C^T C x + 2x^T C^T u = -\vert y \vert^2 + 2y^T u$$

Since $V(x) \geq 0$ and $V(0) = 0$, integrating from $0$ to $T$:

$$\int_0^T u(t) y(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2}V(x(T)) + \frac{1}{2}\int_0^T y^2(t)\, dt \geq 0$$

This is the **passivity inequality**, equivalent to $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$.

2. **($\Leftarrow$) State-Space $\Rightarrow$ Frequency:** Conversely, suppose the LMI conditions hold. Substitute $s = j\omega$ into the resolvent identity:

$$G(j\omega) + G^*(j\omega) = C(j\omega I - A)^{-1}B + B^T(-j\omega I - A^T)^{-1}C^T$$

Using the matrix identity $(j\omega I - A)^{-*} P + P (j\omega I - A)^{-1} = \int_0^\infty e^{A^T t} Q e^{At} dt \succ 0$, and applying the LMI relations, one obtains $G(j\omega) + G^*(j\omega) \succ 0$, i.e., $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$. $\blacksquare$

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark KTU questions, present the KYP lemma in the **LMI form** — it is the most examiner-friendly format. Always include the explicit matrix inequalities, not just a verbal statement.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: SPR / WSPR Verification Pipeline

This block diagram represents the **decision workflow** a student or designer should follow to verify whether a given transfer function is SPR, WSPR, PR, or none.

```mermaid

    INP["INPUT: G of s"]:::inputBox --> STAB["STEP 1: Check Pole Locations"]:::stepBox
    STAB --> QSTAB{"Are all poles in strict LHP?"}:::decisionBox
    QSTAB -- YES --> SPRPATH["Proceed to SPR Branch"]:::pathBox
    QSTAB -- NO --> SIMPCHK{"Are imaginary axis poles simple only?"}:::decisionBox
    SIMPCHK -- YES --> WSPRPATH["Proceed to WSPR Branch"]:::pathBox
    SIMPCHK -- NO --> NOTPR["NOT POSITIVE REAL"]:::rejectBox

    SPRPATH --> FREQSPR["STEP 2: Evaluate Re of G j omega"]:::stepBox
    FREQSPR --> QSPR{"Is Re G j omega strictly greater than 0 for all omega?"}:::decisionBox
    QSPR -- YES --> SPROOF["OUTPUT: G is SPR"]:::acceptBox
    QSPR -- NO --> FALLSPR["G is PR but not SPR"]:::warnBox
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define a Strictly Positive Real (SPR) transfer function. State the two main conditions it must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A rational transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if:

1. **Stability:** $G(s)$ is analytic in $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$, i.e., all its poles lie strictly in the open left-half complex plane (Hurwitz condition).
2. **Positive Realness:** The real part of its frequency response is strictly positive for all real frequencies $\omega$:

$$\text{Re}\left[G(j\omega)\right] > 0 \quad \forall\ \omega \in (-\infty,\ \infty)$$

The third condition, reality of $G(s)$ for real $s$, is implicit for real-coefficient systems. *(1 mark for stability condition, 1.5 marks for positive realness inequality, 0.5 marks for completeness.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between SPR and WSPR transfer functions. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SPR | WSPR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pole Location | Strict LHP only | LHP + simple poles on $j\omega$-axis |
| Real Part Inequality | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ (strict) | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ (non-strict) |
| Zero Crossing of Real Part | Never | Only at isolated $\omega$ |

- **SPR Example:** $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$ — all poles in LHP, real part $\dfrac{2}{4+\omega^2} > 0$. *(1.5 marks for table, 0.75 marks for SPR example, 0.75 marks for WSPR example.)*
- **WSPR Example:** $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ with $a > 0$ when $a \to 0^+$, or $\dfrac{s+2}{s^2+s+1}$ for relaxed conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`**

**(a)** *State the Kalman-Yakubovich-Popov (KYP) Lemma for SPR systems. With the help of necessary state-space equations, derive the relationship between the frequency-domain SPR condition and the corresponding LMI conditions. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *For the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2+4s+3}$, verify whether it is SPR using the frequency-domain condition. Show all calculations explicitly. **(7 marks)***

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Statement of KYP Lemma (2 marks):**

Let $(A, B, C, D)$ be a minimal state-space realization of a stable transfer function $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$. Then $G(s)$ is **SPR** if and only if there exists a real symmetric matrix $P = P^T \succ 0$ such that:

$$PA + A^T P = -C^T C - Q \prec 0$$

$$PB - C^T = 0 \quad \text{(or } PB = C^T \text{)}$$

where $Q = Q^T \succ 0$ is some positive-definite matrix.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider the linear system:

$$\dot{x}(t) = A x(t) + B u(t), \quad y(t) = C x(t)$$

with input $u(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$ and output $y(t) \in \mathbb{R}^m$. Define the storage function:

$$V(x) = x^T P x, \quad P = P^T \succ 0$$

Compute the time-derivative of $V$ along the system trajectory:

$$\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x} = x^T(A^T P + PA)x + 2x^T P B u$$

Substituting the LMI conditions $PA + A^TP = -C^TC - Q$ and $PB = C^T$:

$$\dot{V} = -x^T C^T C x - x^T Q x + 2 x^T C^T u = -\vert y \vert^2 - x^T Q x + 2 y^T u$$

Rearranging:

$$2 y^T u = \dot{V} + \vert y \vert^2 + x^T Q x \geq \dot{V} + \vert y \vert^2$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $T$ (assuming $x(0) = 0$):

$$2 \int_0^T y^T(t) u(t)\, dt = V(x(T)) + \int_0^T \vert y(t) \vert^2 dt \geq \int_0^T \vert y(t) \vert^2 dt \geq 0$$

This is the **input-output passivity inequality**, which in the frequency domain is equivalent to:

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0 \quad \forall\ \omega$$

Adding the strict LMI $-Q \prec 0$ strengthens it to the strict inequality $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$, confirming the **SPR** characterization.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2+4s+3}$

**Step 1 — Identify poles (1 mark):**

$$s^2 + 4s + 3 = (s+1)(s+3)$$

Poles: $s = -1, s = -3$, both in strict LHP. **Hurwitz condition satisfied.**

**Step 2 — Compute $G(j\omega)$ (2 marks):**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega + 3}{(j\omega)^2 + 4j\omega + 3} = \frac{j\omega + 3}{-\omega^2 + 4j\omega + 3} = \frac{j\omega + 3}{(3-\omega^2) + 4j\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize to find real part (3 marks):**

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{(j\omega + 3)\left[(3-\omega^2) - 4j\omega\right]}{(3-\omega^2)^2 + 16\omega^2}$$

Expanding the numerator:

$$(j\omega)(3-\omega^2) - 4j^2\omega^2 + 3(3-\omega^2) - 12j\omega$$

$$= j\omega(3 - \omega^2 - 12) + 4\omega^2 + 9 - 3\omega^2$$

$$= j\omega(-\omega^2 - 9) + (\omega^2 + 9)$$

$$= (\omega^2 + 9) - j\omega(\omega^2 + 9)$$

$$= (\omega^2 + 9)(1 - j\omega)$$

The denominator is $(3-\omega^2)^2 + 16\omega^2 = 9 - 6\omega^2 + \omega^4 + 16\omega^2 = \omega^4 + 10\omega^2 + 9$.

**Step 4 — Extract the real part (1 mark):**

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{\omega^2 + 9}{\omega^4 + 10\omega^2 + 9}$$

Since both numerator and denominator are sums of positive terms (squares plus constants), the real part is **strictly positive** for all $\omega$:

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0 \quad \forall\ \omega \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Conclusion:** $G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2+4s+3}$ is **SPR**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often forget to **check pole locations first** before doing the frequency calculation. If the poles are in RHP, the function cannot be SPR — no amount of real-part analysis will save it.
> 2. A common sign error: when expanding $(j\omega)(3-\omega^2 - 12)$, students incorrectly simplify the real part. Double-check the algebraic expansion step-by-step.
> 3. Many students write $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ instead of $> 0$ for SPR — this costs 1 mark. SPR requires the **strict** inequality.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of WSPR (Weakly Strictly Positive Real) transfer functions. Discuss the conditions a transfer function must satisfy to qualify as WSPR, with appropriate mathematical expressions. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Consider the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ where $a > 0$. Analyze the dependence of the WSPR property on the parameter $a$. For what values of $a$ is $H(s)$ WSPR? Is it ever SPR? Justify with the frequency response. **(7 marks)***

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Definition and Conditions (4 marks):**

A rational transfer function $H(s)$ is called **Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR)** if it satisfies the following conditions:

1. **Analyticity in the open right-half plane:** $H(s)$ has no poles in $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. Poles on the imaginary axis $s = j\omega$ are allowed **only as simple poles**.
2. **Non-negative real part of frequency response:** The real part of $H(j\omega)$ is non-negative for all real frequencies:

$$\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] \geq 0 \quad \forall\ \omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

3. **Isolation of zero crossings:** Any frequency $\omega_0$ at which $\text{Re}[H(j\omega_0)] = 0$ must correspond to an isolated point in $\omega$, and $H(s)$ must have a simple pole on the imaginary axis at $s = j\omega_0$.
4. **Reality:** $H(s)$ is real for real $s$ (automatic for real-coefficient systems).

**Distinction from SPR (3 marks):**

| Feature | SPR | WSPR |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pole Location | Strict LHP only | LHP + simple $j\omega$-axis poles |
| Real Part Sign | Strictly positive ($\text{Re} > 0$) | Non-negative ($\text{Re} \geq 0$, zero at isolated $\omega$) |
| Energy Interpretation | Strictly dissipative | Dissipative except at isolated resonance |

The WSPR class is important in **networked control systems with time delays**, where integrators (simple $j\omega = 0$ poles) and marginally stable modes naturally arise. The **Lozano-Leal and Joshi delay-margin bound** for WSPR-based NCS stability is given by:

$$\tau_{max} = \sup_\omega\, \left[\frac{\text{Re}[H(j\omega)]}{\omega \vert H(j\omega) \vert}\right]$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Given:** $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$, $a > 0$.

**Step 1 — Pole analysis (1 mark):**

The single pole is at $s = -a$. Since $a > 0$, the pole lies in the strict LHP. No poles on the imaginary axis. So pole-location conditions are satisfied for both SPR and WSPR.

**Step 2 — Frequency response (2 marks):**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega + a} = \frac{a - j\omega}{a^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 3 — Real part (1 mark):**

$$\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \frac{a}{a^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 4 — Analysis as a function of $a$ (3 marks):**

- **For any $a > 0$:** $\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \dfrac{a}{a^2 + \omega^2} > 0$ for all $\omega$ (since the denominator is strictly positive and the numerator $a > 0$).

- **As $\omega \to \infty$:** $\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] \to 0^+$. The real part approaches zero but never reaches it — it remains strictly positive everywhere.

- **Infimum of the real part:** $\inf_\omega\, \text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \lim_{\omega \to \infty} \dfrac{a}{a^2 + \omega^2} = 0$ (infimum is approached but not attained).

**Step 5 — Conclusion on WSPR and SPR status:**

- **SPR Status:** Since the real part is strictly positive for all finite $\omega$, $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+a}$ with $a > 0$ is **SPR** (in fact, the limit is zero, but it never equals zero at any finite frequency).

- **WSPR Status:** As $a \to 0^+$, the function degenerates to $\dfrac{1}{s}$, which has a pole at the origin (simple, on $j\omega$-axis). In this limiting case:

$$\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = \frac{0}{0 + \omega^2} = 0 \quad \forall\ \omega \neq 0$$

The real part is identically zero for $\omega \neq 0$ — a single isolated "degenerate" WSPR case. For $a > 0$, $H(s)$ remains **SPR**; it becomes **WSPR** only in the limit $a \to 0^+$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Conflating the **infimum** with the **minimum**. Just because $\inf \text{Re}[H(j\omega)] = 0$ does NOT mean the function is not SPR. SPR requires $\text{Re}[H(j\omega)] > 0$ for each $\omega$, not uniformly bounded away from zero. The distinction is subtle but crucial for KTU marks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. Students often write "SPR and WSPR are the same" — this is **false** and costs 2 marks immediately. They are distinct classes with strict containment: $\text{SPR} \subset \text{WSPR} \subset \text{PR}$.
> 2. In part (b), students forget to take the **limit** as $a \to 0$ and miss the elegant WSPR-degeneracy observation. Marks are awarded for the limiting analysis.
> 3. The condition "simple pole on $j\omega$-axis" must be stated precisely — double poles on the imaginary axis disqualify WSPR status.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPR Definition:** Hurwitz + $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0\ \forall\ \omega$.
- **WSPR Definition:** Hurwitz + simple $j\omega$-axis poles allowed + $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ with isolated zeros.
- **Set inclusion:** $\text{SPR} \subsetneq \text{WSPR} \subsetneq \text{PR}$.
- **KYP Lemma:** The bridge between time-domain LMI and frequency-domain SPR.
- **Circle Criterion:** Nyquist of $G(s)$ avoids a forbidden disk in the complex plane; this is the **SPR-based absolute stability test** for sector-bounded nonlinearities.
- **Popov Criterion:** A linearized version of the circle criterion that uses a frequency-dependent line in the Popov plane.
- **Networked Control Link:** WSPR plants in feedback with passive controllers guarantee **delay-independent stability** for bounded time delays.
- **Hermite-Biehler Theorem:** The algebraic SPR test based on the interlacing of even/odd polynomial roots.
- **Two Algebraic Conditions to ALWAYS Check First:** (i) Pole location, (ii) Real-coefficient property.
- **Energy Interpretation:** SPR $\Rightarrow$ strictly dissipative; WSPR $\Rightarrow$ dissipative except at isolated resonances.
- **Excess of Positive Realness $\epsilon$:** $G(s-\epsilon)$ being SPR for some $\epsilon > 0$ provides a quantitative margin of robustness.
- **Tip for Numericals:** Always factor the denominator first; identify the real part via conjugate multiplication; check the sign of the resulting rational function in $\omega^2$.
- **Tip for Theory:** When asked "state conditions," always list (1) analyticity, (2) real-part inequality, and (3) reality. Examiners expect all three.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy (Module 4):**
> Memorize the **3-condition triplet** for SPR and WSPR. Then memorize the **2-condition pair** for the algebraic test (Hurwitz + Hermite-Biehler). Finally, know the **KYP LMI form** by heart. This triplet covers 90% of marks in KTU ESE questions on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization of SPR (WSPR) Regions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Characterization of SPR (WSPR) Regions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Strict Positive Real (SPR) Transfer Functions > [!IMPORTANT] > **SPR (Strictly Positive Real) Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > A rational, proper transfer function $G(s)$ with real coefficients is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if the following three conditions are simultaneously satisfied: > 1. $G(s)$ is analytic in $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$ (no poles in the closed right half-plane, RHP). > 2. $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ for all $\omega \in \mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\}$. > 3. The limit condition: $\lim_{\omega \to \infty} \omega^2 \, \text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ if the degree of the denominator exceeds the degree of the numerator by more than one (i.e., the **relative degree > 2**). Equivalently, a proper rational $G(s)$ is **SPR** if and only if for every **complex number** $s = \sigma + j\omega$ in the open right-half plane ($\text{Re}(s) > 0$), the real part of $G(s)$ is **strictly positive**: $$\text{Re}[G(s)] > 0 \quad \forall \, s : \text{Re}(s) > 0$$ ## 1.2 Formal Definition of Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR) > [!IMPORTANT] > **WSPR (Weakly Strictly Positive Real) Definition:** > A proper, rational transfer function $G(s)$ is **Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR)** if there exists some finite real constant $\epsilon > 0$ such that the shifted function $G(s - \epsilon)$ is Strictly Positive Real (SPR). > $$\exists \; \epsilon > 0 \quad \text{such that} \quad G(s-\epsilon) \text{ is SPR}$$ The WSPR class is a **broader (relaxed) superset** of SPR. It permits $G(s)$ to have **simple poles on the imaginary axis** (e.g., integrators or pure oscillators), provided these poles are "shifted slightly" into the left half-plane to recover strict positivity. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Damper" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Spring-Damper Energy Bank":** > Think of an SPR system as a **mechanical spring with a built-in ideal damper**. > - **SPR** = A perfectly damped spring: It *always absorbs* kinetic energy from any motion and *always outputs* a force whose sign is aligned with the velocity (dissipative, energy-storing). > - **WSPR** = A nearly-ideal spring: It dissipates energy, but might have a very light friction (a tiny "slip"). If you push it slightly to the left of a pole on the imaginary axis, it behaves like an ideal SPR. > - **Non-SPR** = A spring with a motor attached: it can *generate* energy and push back harder than pushed, breaking the passivity contract. In **networked control systems**, the channel and plant together must behave like this "energy damper" to guarantee stability across delays, packet drops, and quantization. ## 1.4 Why SPR/WSPR Matter in Networked Control | Property | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **Passivity** | SPR ⇔ Passive system (input-output energy contract) | | **Lyapunov Stability** | SPR property of error dynamics → exponential convergence | | **MRAC** | Model Reference Adaptive Control is implementable only if a certain loop is SPR | | **Cascade Stability** | Feedback of two passive blocks remains passive | | **Network Robustness** | WSPR condition survives bounded network delays and packet losses | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Equivalent Characterizations of SPR The SPR property can be verified using **three mathematically equivalent frameworks** (this is a KTU-favorite exam point): ### (A) Frequency-Domain Characterization $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \quad \forall \omega \in [-\infty, \infty]$$ > [!NOTE] > The Nyquist plot of $G(j\omega)$ must lie **strictly in the open right-half of the complex plane**, never touching the imaginary axis. ### (B) State-Space (KYP Lemma) Characterization For a minimal realization $(A, B, C, D)$ of $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ with $A$ Hurwitz: $$G(s) \text{ is SPR} \iff \exists \; P = P^T > 0, \quad L, \quad W \text{ such that:}$$ $$\begin{aligned} PA + A^T P &= -L^T L \\ PB &= C^T - L^T W \\ W^T W &= D + D^T \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) Lemma** — a foundational tool in absolute stability analysis and a high-weightage KTU topic. ### (C) Transfer-Function Factorization Characterization A proper $G(s)$ is SPR if and only if it can be written as: $$G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$$ where $N(s)$ and $D(s)$ are **strictly Hurwitz polynomials** (all roots in the open LHP), and the relative degree of $N$ over $D$ is **at most 1** (i.e., $\deg D - \deg N \leq 1$). ## 2.2 Conditions Distinguishing SPR from WSPR | Property | SPR | WSPR | |---|---|---| | Poles in Re(s) ≥ 0 | **None** allowed | **Simple poles on $j\omega$-axis** allowed | | Real part on $j\omega$-axis | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ strictly | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ with isolated zeros | | Strict passivity | Yes | Yes (with extra $\epsilon$ shift) | | KYP Lemma | $P > 0$ exists | $P \geq 0$ exists | | Energy storage | Bounded & strictly dissipative | Bounded & dissipative | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Formula Sheet for SPR / WSPR Characterization** | # | Formula / Condition | Domain | Physical / Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{Re}[G(s)] > 0, \; \forall \text{Re}(s) \geq 0$ | Frequency | Strict positive realness | | 2 | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \; \forall \omega \in \mathbb{R}$ | Frequency | Nyquist plot in open RHP | | 3 | $PA + A^T P = -Q, \; Q > 0$ | State-space | Lyapunov stability of $A$ | | 4 | $PB = C^T - L^T W$ | State-space | Cross-coupling SPR condition | | 5 | $W^T W = D + D^T$ | State-space | Feedthrough SPR condition | | 6 | $\deg D(s) - \deg N(s) \leq 1$ | Polynomial | Excess pole-zero limit | | 7 | All roots of $N(s), D(s) \in \text{LHP}$ | Polynomial | Strict Hurwitz numerator & denominator | | 8 | $G(s-\epsilon)$ is SPR for some $\epsilon > 0$ | Frequency | Definition of WSPR | | 9 | $\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt \geq 0$ | Time-domain | Passivity (input $u$, output $y$) | | 10 | $\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt \geq \epsilon \int_0^\infty u^2(t) \, dt$ | Time-domain | **Strict** Passivity (implies SPR for LTI) | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Networked Control 1. **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC):** The error-dynamics transfer function between the adaptive law and tracking error must be **SPR** for the adaptive gains to converge. If the plant has relative degree > 1, an **augmented error** with an SPR filter is constructed. 2. **Time-Delay Robustness:** WSPR systems tolerate bounded network-induced delays (up to a phase margin of ~30°–60° in practice). 3. **Passive Network Protocols:** A passivity-based scheduler (e.g., **TAP — Tool for Analysis of Passivity**) preserves stability of WSPR plants under packet dropouts. 4. **Cyber-Physical Security:** Detecting "false data injection" attacks requires checking if the plant's SPR property is preserved post-attack. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Passivity to SPR for LTI SISO Systems **Statement:** For a causal, stable, rational LTI SISO system with impulse response $g(t)$ and transfer function $G(s)$, the system is **passive** (i.e., $\int_0^T u(t)y(t)\, dt \geq 0$ for all $u, T$) **if and only if** $G(s)$ is **Positive Real (PR)**, i.e., $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0, \forall \omega$. For **Strictly Passive** systems, the inequality is strict, which translates to **SPR**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation (Parseval's Identity Path) Consider the inner product: $$\langle u, y \rangle_T = \int_0^T u(t) y(t) \, dt$$ Applying **Parseval's theorem** to convert time-domain convolution to frequency-domain product: $$\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} U(-j\omega) \, Y(j\omega) \, d\omega$$ Since $Y(j\omega) = G(j\omega) U(j\omega)$ and $U(-j\omega) = \overline{U(j\omega)}$ for real $u(t)$: $$\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert U(j\omega) \vert^2 \, G(j\omega) \, d\omega$$ Splitting $G(j\omega) = \text{Re}[G(j\omega)] + j \, \text{Im}[G(j\omega)]$ and noting that $\vert U(j\omega) \vert^2$ is real and even: $$\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert U(j\omega) \vert^2 \, \text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \, d\omega$$ For the integral to be **non-negative for all inputs** $u(t)$, the integrand must be non-negative: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0, \quad \forall \omega \in \mathbb{R}$$ For **strict positivity of the integral (strict passivity)**, we need: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \quad \forall \omega \in \mathbb{R}$$ Combined with **analyticity in the closed RHP** (which follows from causality + stability for rational functions), this is the **SPR condition**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relative Degree Constraint for SPR **Claim:** A proper rational SPR transfer function $G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ must have a relative degree of **at most 1**, i.e., $\deg D - \deg N \leq 1$. ### Proof by Asymptotic Behaviour As $s \to \infty$ along the positive real axis (so $s = \sigma \to +\infty$): $$G(\sigma) \approx \frac{b_{n-m} \sigma^{n-m}}{1} = K \sigma^{n-m}$$ where $n = \deg D$, $m = \deg N$, and $K > 0$ (assuming positive leading coefficients). Then: $$\text{Re}[G(\sigma)] = K \sigma^{n-m}$$ For $\sigma \to +\infty$: - If $n - m = 0$ (relative degree 0): $\text{Re}[G(\sigma)] = K > 0$ ✓ (SPR-allowed) - If $n - m = 1$ (relative degree 1): $\text{Re}[G(\sigma)] = K\sigma \to +\infty$ ✓ (SPR-allowed) - If $n - m = 2$: $\text{Re}[G(\sigma)] = K\sigma^2 \to +\infty$, **but** at $s = j\omega$: $$G(j\omega) \approx \frac{K}{(j\omega)^{n-m}} = \frac{K}{(j\omega)^2} = -\frac{K}{\omega^2}$$ This gives $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \to -K < 0$ as $\omega \to \infty$, **violating** the SPR condition! $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Rule (KTU Exam Tip):** If a transfer function has the form $G(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + as + b}$ (second-order denominator, zero numerator), it is **never SPR** — it always has a phase approaching $-180°$, so the real part on the $j\omega$ axis becomes negative. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — KYP Lemma → SPR Algebraic Test **Statement:** For a minimal realization with **Hurwitz $A$** and **$D + D^T > 0$**, the transfer function $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ is SPR **if and only if** there exist matrices $P = P^T > 0$ and $L$ such that: $$PA + A^T P = -L^T L \quad \text{and} \quad PB = C^T - L^T (D + D^T)^{1/2}$$ ### Derivation Outline Start from the **Lyapunov equation candidate**: $$PA + A^T P = -Q, \quad Q > 0$$ Define the auxiliary quantity (the **scattering variable**): $$z(t) = \sqrt{D + D^T} \, u(t) - L^T x(t)$$ Compute the time-derivative of the storage function $V = x^T P x$ along trajectories: $$\dot V = x^T (PA + A^T P) x + 2 x^T P B u = -x^T Q x + 2 x^T P B u$$ Using $PB = C^T - L^T W$ with $W = \sqrt{D + D^T}$: $$\dot V = -x^T Q x + 2 x^T C^T u - 2 x^T L^T W u$$ Add and subtract $u^T (D + D^T) u = u^T W^T W u$ to complete the square: $$\begin{aligned} \dot V + u^T y + y^T u &= -x^T Q x + 2x^T C^T u + 2 u^T D u - 2 x^T L^T W u - u^T W^T W u \\ &= -x^T Q x + 2 x^T C^T u - u^T W^T W u - 2 x^T L^T W u \\ &= -x^T Q x - \left( W u - L x \right)^T \left( W u - L x \right) \\ &= -x^T Q x - \Vert z \Vert^2 \end{aligned}$$ Since $Q > 0$ and $\Vert z \Vert^2 \geq 0$, we have $\dot V + 2 u^T y \leq 0$, which gives the supply rate $u^T y \geq -\frac{1}{2}\dot V$.
