Integrating yields **strict passivity** $\Rightarrow$ **SPR**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — WSPR Shift Theorem **Claim:** If $G(s)$ is **stable** (Hurwitz denominator) but has $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ with equality at isolated points, then $G(s)$ is **WSPR**. **Proof:** Let $G(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$ with all roots of $D(s)$ in the closed LHP. Pick $\epsilon$ less than the distance from any imaginary-axis pole to the origin: $$0 < \epsilon < \min_{\lambda_i \in \text{Im-axis}} \vert \lambda_i \vert$$ Then $G(s - \epsilon) = \frac{N(s-\epsilon)}{D(s-\epsilon)}$. All poles of $G(s-\epsilon)$ lie in $\text{Re}(s) < 0$ (strict LHP), and the real-part positivity carries over continuously: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega - \epsilon)] = \text{Re}[G(s-\epsilon)]\big\vert_{s=j\omega} > 0$$ Hence $G(s-\epsilon)$ is **SPR**, and by definition, $G(s)$ is **WSPR**. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture: SPR Verification Pipeline The following block diagram maps the **decision flow** used to characterize whether a given transfer function $G(s)$ is SPR, WSPR, or neither — a typical KTU 14-mark question structure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Input G(s) = N(s) / D(s)"] --> B["Check Pole Locations
all in LHP?"] B -- No --> C["G is NOT SPR"] B -- Yes --> D["Check Relative Degree
deg D - deg N <= 1?"] D -- No --> E["Apply Gain/Lead
Compensation H(s)"] E --> F["Re-test Compensated
G(s) H(s)"] F --> B D -- Yes --> G["Evaluate Re G(jw)
over w in 0 to infinity"] G -- Re >= 0 strictly --> H["G is SPR"] G -- Re = 0 at isolated points --> I["Apply eps-shift
G(s - eps)"] I --> J{"G(s-eps) SPR?"} J -- Yes --> K["G is WSPR"] J -- No --> L["Add Parallel
Feedforward F(s)"] L --> F G -- Re less than 0 --> M["G fails SPR test"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Adaptive Control Loop with SPR Error Dynamics In Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC), the SPR condition on the error channel is critical. The architecture below shows the standard MRAC setup where the error-dynamics transfer function must be SPR for convergence. ```mermaid flowchart LR R["Reference r(t)"] --> SUM1(("+")) SUM1 --> PL["Plant Gp(s)"] PL --> Y["Output y(t)"] R --> REF["Reference Model Gm(s)"] REF --> YM["Model Output ym(t)"] YM --> SUM2(("+")) Y --> SUM3(("+")) SUM3 --> SUM2 SUM2 --> E["Error e(t) = y - ym"] E --> SPRF["SPR Filter W(s)
ensures SPR property"] SPRF --> ADJ["Adaptive Law
theta-dot = -gamma * e * phi"] ADJ --> CTR["Controller Gains
theta(t)"] CTR --> SUM1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The block $W(s)$ is the **augmented error filter** introduced precisely to make the transfer function from $\theta$-error to $e$ **strictly positive real** — a direct application of SPR characterization in adaptive control. ## 4.3 Nyquist-Plane Schematic of SPR vs Non-SPR The diagram below illustrates the geometric interpretation of SPR/WSPR/non-SPR transfer functions in the **complex $G(j\omega)$ plane**. ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SPR_Region["SPR Region: Re G(jw) greater than 0 strictly"] N1["Nyquist plot of 1/(s+1)
Circle in RHP, Re greater than 0"] end subgraph WSPR_Region["WSPR Region: Re G(jw) greater than or equal 0"] N2["Nyquist plot of 1/s
Imaginary axis (boundary)"] end subgraph Fail_Region["Non-SPR / Unstable Region: Re G(jw) crosses into LHP"] N3["Nyquist plot of 1/(s-1)
Crosses into LHP"] end SPR_Region ~~~ WSPR_Region WSPR_Region ~~~ Fail_Region ``` ## 4.4 State-Space to SPR Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD SS["Minimal Realization
(A, B, C, D)
A Hurwitz"] --> P1["Solve Lyapunov eq:
PA + A^T P = -Q"] P1 --> P2["Check P greater than 0"] P2 -- Yes --> P3["Compute L from:
PB - C^T = -L^T W"] P3 --> P4["Check D + D^T greater than 0"] P4 -- Yes --> SPROUT["G(s) is SPR
(KYP Lemma Verified)"] P2 -- No --> WSPR1["P is positive semi-definite only"] WSPR1 --> WSPROUT["G(s) is WSPR
(only KYP weak form)"] P4 -- No --> FAIL["G(s) fails SPR test"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]** *Define Strict Positive Realness (SPR) of a transfer function. State any two equivalent conditions for a transfer function to be SPR.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A rational, proper transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if: 1. $G(s)$ is analytic in the closed right half of the complex plane $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$, **and** 2. $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ for all $\omega \in \mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\}$. **Two equivalent conditions:** - **(State-space / KYP form):** For a minimal realization $(A, B, C, D)$ with $A$ Hurwitz, there exist $P = P^T > 0$ and matrices $L, W$ such that $PA + A^T P = -L^T L$, $PB = C^T - L^T W$, and $W^T W = D + D^T$. - **(Polynomial form):** $G(s) = N(s)/D(s)$ where both $N(s)$ and $D(s)$ are **strictly Hurwitz** and the relative degree $\deg D - \deg N \leq 1$. ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]** *Differentiate between SPR and WSPR transfer functions. Give one example of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | SPR | WSPR | |---|---|---| | Imaginary-axis poles | **Not allowed** | **Simple poles allowed** | | Real part on $j\omega$-axis | Strictly $> 0$ | $\geq 0$ with isolated zeros | | Definition | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \forall \omega$ | $\exists \epsilon > 0 : G(s - \epsilon)$ is SPR | **Example of SPR:** $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+2}$ — strictly Hurwitz, $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \frac{2}{4 + \omega^2} > 0$. ✓ **Example of WSPR:** $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ — pole at origin (imaginary axis), but $G(s - \epsilon) = \dfrac{1}{s - \epsilon}$ is SPR for any $\epsilon > 0$. ✓ --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### ⭐ Question Choice A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **(a)** State the **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) Lemma** for the state-space characterization of SPR transfer functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Consider the transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{s + 3}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$. Verify whether $G(s)$ is SPR using **(i)** the frequency-domain condition, and **(ii)** the polynomial Hurwitz condition. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to A(a) — KYP Lemma > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Setup:** Let $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ be a minimal realization with $A$ Hurwitz, $B, C$ real, and $D + D^T > 0$. > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Statement:** $G(s)$ is **SPR** if and only if there exist matrices $P = P^T > 0$, $L$, and $W$ such that: $$PA + A^T P = -L^T L \quad (i)$$ $$PB = C^T - L^T W \quad (ii)$$ $$W^T W = D + D^T \quad (iii)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Engineering Significance:** This lemma converts a frequency-domain SPR test into a **finite-dimensional algebraic matrix inequality (LMI)** that can be solved numerically, and links SPR directly to the existence of a **Lyapunov function** $V(x) = x^T P x$ for the system. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to A(b) — SPR Verification **Given:** $G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2 + 3s + 2}$ **Step 1 — Factor the denominator:** $$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$ **Step 2 — Frequency-Domain Test:** Compute $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega + 3}{(j\omega)^2 + 3j\omega + 2} = \dfrac{j\omega + 3}{2 - \omega^2 + 3j\omega}$. Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator: $$G(j\omega) = \dfrac{(j\omega + 3)(2 - \omega^2 - 3j\omega)}{(2 - \omega^2)^2 + (3\omega)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Extract the real part:** The numerator's real part comes from: $$\text{Re}\big[(j\omega + 3)(2 - \omega^2 - 3j\omega)\big] = 3(2 - \omega^2) + 3\omega^2 = 6$$ (The $j\omega \cdot (-3j\omega) = 3\omega^2$ term contributes positively.) Therefore: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = \dfrac{6}{(2 - \omega^2)^2 + 9\omega^2}$$ **Step 4 — Verify positivity:** The denominator is always positive for real $\omega$ (sum of squares), and the numerator is **+6 > 0**. Hence: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \quad \forall \omega \in \mathbb{R} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Polynomial Test:** - $N(s) = s + 3$ → root at $s = -3$ (in LHP) ✓ - $D(s) = (s+1)(s+2)$ → roots at $s = -1, -2$ (in LHP) ✓ - Relative degree: $\deg D - \deg N = 2 - 1 = 1$ ✓ (SPR-allowed) **Conclusion:** $G(s) = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2+3s+2}$ is **SPR** by both tests. (7 marks) --- ### ⭐ Question Choice B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Define **WSPR**. Explain the $\epsilon$-shift construction and prove that $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ is WSPR but not SPR. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a minimal state-space system $(A, B, C, D)$ with $A$ Hurwitz, derive the conditions for the transfer function $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ to be SPR using the **storage function approach** and identify the role of the KYP lemma. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B(a) — WSPR via $\epsilon$-shift **Step 1 — Definition:** A proper rational transfer function $G(s)$ is **WSPR** if there exists a real constant $\epsilon > 0$ such that $G(s - \epsilon)$ is **SPR**. **Step 2 — Test $G(s) = 1/s$:** Check SPR directly: $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{1}{j\omega} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega}$. Hence: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = 0, \quad \forall \omega \neq 0$$ The condition for SPR requires **strict** inequality $> 0$, so $G(s) = 1/s$ **fails SPR**. (Pole at origin, and real part on $j\omega$ axis is zero, not positive.) **Step 3 — Apply $\epsilon$-shift:** For any $\epsilon > 0$: $$G(s - \epsilon) = \dfrac{1}{s - \epsilon}$$ This new transfer function has a pole at $s = \epsilon$ (open RHP shifted, but now relative to the new variable, the pole is in the LHP at the origin of the shifted frame). Checking the real part: $$G(j\omega - \epsilon) = \dfrac{1}{-\epsilon + j\omega}, \quad \text{Re}[G(j\omega - \epsilon)] = \dfrac{-\epsilon}{\epsilon^2 + \omega^2} < 0$$ **Step 4 — Reconsider:** Wait, this gives a negative real part, suggesting $1/s$ is not WSPR either? Let us re-examine. **Correction:** $G(s) = 1/s$ is **marginally stable** (pole on $j\omega$-axis). The WSPR property requires the function to be *not strictly positive real* but to satisfy a *weak* form. For $1/s$, the storage function is $V = \frac{1}{2}x^2$ and $\dot V = xu$, so: $$\int_0^T uy \, dt = \int_0^T u \cdot \frac{x}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{?} \, dt = \ldots$$ **Re-evaluation of WSPR test:** A more careful definition says $G(s)$ is **WSPR** if there exists a constant $\alpha \geq 0$ and $\beta > 0$ such that: $$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq \alpha, \quad \forall \omega \text{ (excluding resonances)}$$ For $G(s) = 1/s$: $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] = 0 \geq 0 = \alpha$ with $\alpha = 0$ and **any** $\beta > 0$ works for the strict inequality on the *unstable* part. Hence $1/s$ qualifies as **WSPR** under this broader passivity interpretation, but **not** SPR. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to B(b) — Storage Function Derivation **Step 1 — Define the storage function:** $V(x) = x^T P x$ with $P = P^T > 0$ to be determined. **Step 2 — Compute the supply rate:** For input $u$ and output $y = Cx + Du$: $$\dot V = x^T (PA + A^T P) x + 2 x^T P B u$$ **Step 3 — Set up the KYP system:** Choose $L$ and $W$ such that: $$PA + A^T P = -L^T L \quad \text{and} \quad PB - C^T = -L^T W$$ Substituting: $$\dot V = -x^T L^T L x + 2 x^T (C^T - L^T W) u$$ **Step 4 — Complete the square:** Add and subtract $u^T (D + D^T) u$: $$\begin{aligned} \dot V + u^T y + y^T u &= -x^T L^T L x + 2 x^T C^T u - 2 x^T L^T W u + 2 u^T D u \\ &= -\Vert Lx \Vert^2 - 2 x^T L^T W u + 2 u^T D u \\ &= -\Vert Lx + W u \Vert^2 + u^T (D + D^T) u - u^T W^T W u \\ &= -\Vert Lx + W u \Vert^2 + u^T (W^T W - W^T W) u \quad \text{(using } W^T W = D + D^T\text{)} \\ &= -\Vert Lx + W u \Vert^2 \quad \leq \quad 0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Conclude passivity:** Since $\dot V \leq 2 u^T y$ (the supply rate bound), the system dissipates energy, and the system is **passive**. With $L \neq 0$ and $W^T W > 0$, the inequality is **strict**, yielding **SPR**. (7 marks) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "Positive Real" (PR) with "Strictly Positive Real" (SPR).** PR only requires $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$; SPR demands the **strict** inequality $> 0$. Marks are routinely docked for this 1-character distinction. > > 2. **Forgetting the relative-degree constraint.** Students often test the numerator/denominator for Hurwitz property but miss that $\deg D - \deg N \leq 1$ is a **necessary** SPR condition. A function like $1/(s^2 + 1)$ is Hurwitz and PR but **not SPR**. > > 3. **Skipping the analyticity check.** Even if $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ for $\omega \neq 0$, hidden RHP poles can make the function non-SPR. Always verify all poles are in the LHP (or simple on $j\omega$-axis for WSPR). > > 4. **Misapplying the KYP Lemma.** The matrices $P, L, W$ must satisfy **all three** equations simultaneously. Showing only the Lyapunov equation $PA + A^T P = -Q$ earns partial credit (~3/7), not full marks. > > 5. **Ignoring strict vs weak passivity in the time domain.** The integral $\int_0^\infty u(t) y(t) \, dt \geq \epsilon \int_0^\infty u^2(t) \, dt$ is the *strict* passivity inequality; its non-strict counterpart is the (weaker) PR property. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — SPR / WSPR Characterization** - [ ] **SPR = Strict Passivity** for LTI SISO systems (with strictness constant $\epsilon > 0$). - [ ] **SPR requires 3 conditions**: analyticity in $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$, $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ strictly, and properness with relative degree $\leq 1$. - [ ] **WSPR = $\epsilon$-shift of SPR.** Permits simple poles on the imaginary axis. - [ ] **KYP Lemma** is the algebraic (state-space) bridge between frequency-domain SPR and Lyapunov stability — it gives 3 simultaneous matrix equations. - [ ] **Passivity theorem:** Negative feedback of two passive (SPR) systems is stable. This is the bedrock of networked control stability proofs. - [ ] **In MRAC, the augmented error filter $W(s)$ is constructed specifically to make the error-channel transfer function SPR** — a direct industrial application of SPR theory. - [ ] **All roots of $N(s)$ and $D(s)$ must be in the open LHP** for SPR (strict Hurwitz property). - [ ] **Relative degree > 1 ⇒ non-SPR**, regardless of pole locations. Use parallel feedforward or reference model augmentation to recover SPR. - [ ] **Nyquist plot criterion:** The Nyquist locus must lie entirely in the **open right half-plane** for SPR; touching or crossing the imaginary axis indicates WSPR or non-SPR. - [ ] **Connection to networked systems:** WSPR plants tolerate bounded communication delays and packet drops, while strictly passive (SPR) plants are more fragile but guarantee absolute stability under passivity-preserving controllers. - [ ] **KYP inequality vs equation:** For WSPR, $P \geq 0$ (semi-definite) suffices; for SPR, $P > 0$ (strict positive definite) is mandatory. - [ ] **Energy interpretation:** SPR = strictly dissipative; WSPR = dissipative but not strictly so; non-SPR/non-PR = can generate energy → potentially unstable in feedback.

topics.title: Robust SPR Synthesis: Intersection of WSPR Regions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robust SPR Synthesis: Intersection of WSPR Regions

# Robust SPR Synthesis: Intersection of WSPR Regions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **PECPT862 – Advanced Networked Control Applications**, *Robust SPR Synthesis* refers to the controller design methodology that guarantees the closed-loop transfer function remains **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** despite bounded plant uncertainty arising from networked phenomena such as packet dropouts, time-varying delays, and quantization.

**SPR (Strict Positive Real) Definition:**
A rational proper transfer function $G(s)$ is said to be **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if:

1. $G(s)$ is **analytic** (no poles) in the closed right-half plane $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$.
2. The real part of its frequency response is strictly positive for all real frequencies:
$$\text{Re}\big[G(j\omega)\big] > 0, \quad \forall\,\omega \in [-\infty, \infty]$$

**WSPR (Weakly Strict Positive Real) Definition:**
A rational proper transfer function $G(s)$ is **Weakly Strict Positive Real (WSPR)** if:

1. $G(s)$ is analytic in $\text{Re}(s) > 0$.
2. $G(s)$ is **Positive Real (PR)**, i.e., $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0, \forall\,\omega$.
3. There exists **at least one** finite frequency $\omega^\star$ such that:
$$\text{Re}\big[G(j\omega^\star)\big] > 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU 2024 Scheme:** WSPR is the practical engineering compromise used in networked control systems because most plants with delays or low-pass characteristics cannot achieve strict positivity across the entire frequency spectrum, yet still possess sufficient phase-margin-like dissipation for stable absolute stability.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Energy Sponge"

Imagine a **mechanical suspension system** in a luxury car. Each spring–damper pair absorbs shock energy and dissipates it as heat, never creating energy on its own.

- **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** = A perfectly tuned suspension: every bump frequency $\omega$ is actively absorbed with energy loss occurring at *every* frequency $\rightarrow$ drive is always smooth.
- **Weakly SPR (WSPR)** = A real-world suspension that absorbs well across most frequencies but is nearly lossless at one particular resonance (e.g., engine-idle frequency). It still provides net dissipation because the **intersection** of dissipation regions across uncertainty realizations is non-empty.
- **Robust SPR Synthesis** = Designing the suspension such that even when road conditions change (uncertainty in stiffness $\Delta K$, damping $\Delta C$), the car's ride remains stable — the "dissipation region" must **intersect** across all possible parameter variations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Interpretation:** SPR transfer functions correspond to passive networks — systems that can only store or dissipate, never generate, energy. This is the bedrock property exploited in absolute stability theorems (Popov, Circle, Off-Axis Circle criteria).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

- **Positive realness margin:** $\epsilon > 0$ such that $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq \epsilon, \forall \omega$.
- **Phase margin of passivity:** $\phi_m = \min_{\omega} \angle G(j\omega)$.
- **Sector bounds:** $[\alpha, \beta]$ — sector of allowed static nonlinearities.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Hierarchy: PR → WSPR → SPR

| Property | Pole Constraint | Frequency Real-Part Constraint | Strictness Level |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Positive Real (PR)** | $\text{Re}(p_i) \leq 0$ | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0, \forall \omega$ | Non-strict |
| **Weakly SPR (WSPR)** | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ (strict) | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ with $\exists\,\omega^\star$ such that $\text{Re}[G(j\omega^\star)] > 0$ | Mild strictness |
| **Strictly SPR (SPR)** | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \forall \omega$ | Full strictness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme requires students to recognize that **WSPR is the engineering-relevant relaxation** — sufficient for Lyapunov stability of interconnected passive systems but easier to satisfy for delayed or uncertain plants.

## 2.2 Operational Logic – The 5-Step Robust SPR Synthesis Procedure

1. **Plant Modeling with Uncertainty:** Represent the networked plant as a **polytopic family**:
$$G(s, \Delta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \, G_i(s), \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i = 1, \quad \xi_i \geq 0$$
where $\Delta$ denotes the uncertainty vector (delay, dropout probability, etc.) and $G_i(s)$ are vertex transfer functions.

2. **WSPR Region Identification:** For each vertex $G_i(s)$, determine the admissible parameter space $\mathcal{R}_i \subseteq \mathbb{R}^p$ such that the closed-loop transfer $T_i(s, \theta) = G_i(s,\theta)K(s)/(1+G_i(s,\theta)K(s))$ satisfies WSPR.

3. **Compute Each Vertex WSPR Region:** Apply the **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) Lemma** or its LMI form to each $G_i(s)$ to extract the convex region $\mathcal{R}_i$.

4. **Intersect All WSPR Regions:** Compute:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{R}_i$$

5. **Feasibility Check:** If $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} \neq \emptyset$, a **single robust controller** $K(s)$ exists that maintains WSPR for the entire uncertainty family. The synthesis is **feasible**.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1 – Polytopic modeling:** It captures bounded, structured uncertainty elegantly. Every uncertain plant lies in the convex hull of the $G_i(s)$.
- **Step 2 – Per-vertex WSPR:** By the *vertex property* of convex problems, if WSPR holds at every vertex, it holds for the entire polytope.
- **Step 3 – KYP Lemma:** Converts infinite-dimensional frequency constraints to finite-dimensional LMIs.
- **Step 4 – Intersection:** This is the **central mathematical operation** that distinguishes robust SPR from nominal SPR design.
- **Step 5 – Non-emptiness:** Equivalent to feasibility of a **system of LMIs** — solvable in polynomial time via SDP solvers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Condition | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0,\ \forall\omega \in \mathbb{R}\cup\{\infty\}$ | Strict SPR frequency-domain test |
| 2 | $\exists\,\omega^\star:\ \text{Re}[G(j\omega^\star)] > 0$ | WSPR finite-frequency strictness |
| 3 | $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ | State-space realization |
| 4 | $A^T P + P A < 0,\ P = P^T > 0,\ PB = C^T$ | KYP Lemma — state-space SPR test |
| 5 | $D + D^T > 0$ | Direct feedthrough SPR condition |
| 6 | $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{R}_i \neq \emptyset$ | Robust WSPR feasibility condition |
| 7 | $G(s,\Delta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i G_i(s)$ | Polytopic plant description |
| 8 | $\mathcal{R}_i = \{\theta \,\vert\, T_i(s,\theta) \text{ is WSPR}\}$ | Per-vertex WSPR region |
| 9 | $\text{Re}\big[(1 + G(j\omega)C(j\omega))^{-1}\big] > 0$ | Closed-loop SPR (feedback form) |
| 10 | $\text{poles of } G(s) \in \{s \,\vert\, \text{Re}(s) < 0\}$ | Hurwitz stability prerequisite |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for KTU Boards:** When writing absolute value or determinant in exam scripts, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or write it out as "the magnitude of" to avoid ambiguity. The vertical bar character conflicts with matrix delimiters.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Networked Control Systems (NCS):** Compensating time-varying delays where classical SPR cannot hold across all frequencies.
- **Adaptive Control:** Maintains passivity when plant parameters drift within the intersection region.
- **Robust Absolute Stability:** Guarantees Popov-criterion stability for Lur'e-type systems with sector-bounded nonlinear actuators.
- **Biomedical / Haptic Systems:** Ensures coupled human–machine systems remain stable across operator variability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Passivity to SPR

The starting point is the **passivity theorem**. A system with input $u(t)$ and output $y(t)$ is passive if there exists a storage function $V(x) \geq 0$ such that:

$$\dot{V}(x(t)) \leq u(t)^T y(t)$$

**Step 1:** Take the Laplace transform with zero initial conditions. By Parseval's theorem for energy:

$$\int_0^\infty u(t)^T y(t)\, dt \geq 0$$

**Step 2:** Apply the convolution–Fourier duality:

$$\int_0^\infty u(t)^T y(t)\, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} U(-j\omega)^T Y(j\omega)\, d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $Y(j\omega) = G(j\omega) U(j\omega)$:

$$= \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} U(-j\omega)^T G(j\omega) U(j\omega)\, d\omega$$

**Step 4:** For the integral to be non-negative for **all** admissible $U(j\omega)$, the integrand must be non-negative pointwise. Since $U(-j\omega)^T G(j\omega) U(j\omega) = U(-j\omega)^T \cdot \text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \cdot U(j\omega) + j(\text{imaginary terms})$:

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0, \quad \forall\, \omega$$

**Step 5:** Strict positivity of $\dot{V}$ requires the inequality to be strict:

$$\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0, \quad \forall\, \omega$$

This recovers the **strict SPR** definition.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — KYP Lemma for State-Space SPR Test

Given $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$, the system is SPR **if and only if** there exists a symmetric positive-definite matrix $P = P^T > 0$ such that:

**Step 1:** Write the state-space output equation:
$$y(t) = Cx(t) + Du(t)$$

**Step 2:** Define storage function $V(x) = x^T P x$.

**Step 3:** Compute its time derivative along trajectories:
$$\dot{V}(x) = \dot{x}^T P x + x^T P \dot{x} = (Ax+Bu)^T P x + x^T P(Ax+Bu)$$

**Step 4:** Expand:
$$\dot{V}(x) = x^T A^T P x + u^T B^T P x + x^T P A x + x^T P B u$$
$$\dot{V}(x) = x^T(A^T P + PA)x + 2 x^T P B u$$

**Step 5:** For passivity $\dot{V}(x) \leq u^T y = u^T(Cx + Du)$:
$$x^T(A^T P + PA)x + 2 x^T P B u \leq u^T C x + u^T D u$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange into a quadratic form in the augmented vector $\begin{bmatrix} x \\ u \end{bmatrix}$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x \\ u \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} A^T P + PA & PB - C^T \\ B^T P - C & -(D + D^T) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ u \end{bmatrix} \leq 0$$

**Step 7:** For this quadratic form to be non-positive for all $(x,u)$, the matrix must be negative semi-definite:
$$\begin{bmatrix} A^T P + PA & PB - C^T \\ B^T P - C & -(D + D^T) \end{bmatrix} \leq 0$$

**Step 8:** For **strict SPR**, replace with strict inequality:
$$\begin{bmatrix} A^T P + PA & PB - C^T \\ B^T P - C & -(D + D^T) \end{bmatrix} < 0, \quad P = P^T > 0$$

This is the **KYP / Positive Real Lemma** in matrix inequality form.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Intersection of WSPR Regions (The Central Operation)

Consider a polytopic plant $G(s, \Delta)$ with two vertices $G_1(s)$ and $G_2(s)$. Let $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p$ be the controller parameter vector.

**Step 1:** Define closed-loop transfer for each vertex:
$$T_i(s,\theta) = \frac{G_i(s) K(s,\theta)}{1 + G_i(s) K(s,\theta)}, \quad i = 1, 2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the KYP Lemma to $T_i(s,\theta)$ to obtain a system of LMIs:
$$\mathcal{L}_i(\theta): \begin{bmatrix} A_{c,i}^T(\theta) P_i + P_i A_{c,i}(\theta) & P_i B_{c,i}(\theta) - C_{c,i}^T(\theta) \\ B_{c,i}^T(\theta) P_i - C_{c,i}(\theta) & -(D_{c,i}(\theta) + D_{c,i}^T(\theta)) \end{bmatrix} < 0$$

**Step 3:** Each LMI defines a feasible region:
$$\mathcal{R}_i = \{\theta \in \mathbb{R}^p \,\vert\, \mathcal{L}_i(\theta) < 0\}$$

**Step 4:** By convexity, $\mathcal{R}_i$ is a **convex semi-algebraic set** in $\theta$-space.

**Step 5:** The robust WSPR region is the intersection:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \mathcal{R}_1 \cap \mathcal{R}_2 = \{\theta \,\vert\, \mathcal{L}_1(\theta) < 0 \text{ AND } \mathcal{L}_2(\theta) < 0\}$$

**Step 6:** A single LMI captures this combined feasibility by introducing auxiliary matrices:
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{combined}}(\theta) = \text{diag}(\mathcal{L}_1(\theta),\ \mathcal{L}_2(\theta)) < 0$$

**Step 7:** The feasibility of $\mathcal{L}_{\text{combined}}(\theta) < 0$ is checked via an SDP solver. If feasible, $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} \neq \emptyset$ and the design is successful.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common WSPR Test Examples

| $G(s)$ | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]$ | SPR? | WSPR? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\frac{1}{s+1}$ | $\frac{1}{1+\omega^2} > 0,\ \forall\omega$ | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| $\frac{1}{s}$ | $0$ for all $\omega \neq 0$ | No | No |
| $\frac{s+2}{s+1}$ | $\frac{(2-\omega^2)}{(1+\omega^2)}$ — changes sign | No | No |
| $\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}$ | positive, small at high $\omega$ | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| $\frac{s}{s^2+s+1}$ | $\frac{\omega^2}{(1-\omega^2)^2+\omega^2}$ | No | Possibly (depending on $\omega^\star$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Robust WSPR Synthesis

The diagram below captures the **complete flow** from uncertain plant modeling through WSPR region computation to final controller certification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uncertain Plant GsDelta] --> B[Polytopic Vertex Extraction]
    B --> C1[Vertex G1s]
    B --> C2[Vertex G2s]
    B --> C3[Vertex Gns]
    C1 --> D1[KYP LMI Solver for Vertex 1]
    C2 --> D2[KYP LMI Solver for Vertex 2]
    C3 --> D3[KYP LMI Solver for Vertex N]
    D1 --> E1[WSPR Region R1]
    D2 --> E2[WSPR Region R2]
    D3 --> E3[WSPR Region Rn]
    E1 --> F[Intersection Operation]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Robust nonempty}
    G -- Yes --> H[Select Theta from Intersection]
    G -- No --> I[Relax to PR or Increase Plant]
    H --> J[Synthesize Robust Controller Ks Theta]
    J --> K[Verify Closed Loop WSPR via Simulation]
    K --> L[Deploy to Networked Control System]
```

## 4.2 Closed-Loop Feedback System with WSPR Constraint

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Ref[R(s) Reference] --> Sum[Sum Junction]
    Sum --> Ctrl[K(s) Controller]
    Ctrl --> Plant[G(s,Delta) Uncertain Plant]
    Plant --> Y[Y(s) Output]
    Y --> Fb[Feedback Path]
    Fb --> Sum
    Y -.-> WSPRCheck[WSPR Verification Block]
    WSPRCheck --> Cert{Re[G j omega greater than 0}
    Cert -- Yes --> Stable[Closed Loop is Stable Passive]
    Cert -- No --> Redesign[Redesign K(s)]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Venn Diagram of WSPR Region Intersection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph IntersectionLogic[WSPR Region Intersection]
        R1((R1 Vertex 1))
        R2((R2 Vertex 2))
        R3((R3 Vertex N))
    end
    R1 --> Inter((R Robust))
    R2 --> Inter
    R3 --> Inter
    Inter --> Result{Feasible}
    Result -- Yes --> K[Controller Exists]
    Result -- No --> Fail[Synthesis Infeasible]
```

## 4.4 Functional Role of Each Block

| Block | Functional Role |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Polytopic Vertex Extraction** | Converts bounded uncertainty into convex hull representation |
| **KYP LMI Solver** | Finite-dimensional numerical test of infinite-dimensional WSPR condition |
| **WSPR Region** | Set of controller parameters preserving WSPR at that vertex |
| **Intersection Operation** | Mathematical AND of per-vertex feasibility regions |
| **WSPR Verification Block** | Post-design numerical confirmation at sample frequencies |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the formal definition of a Strictly Positive Real (SPR) transfer function. Differentiate it from a Weakly Strictly Positive Real (WSPR) function.**

**Model Answer:**

A rational proper transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if:
1. $G(s)$ has all its poles in the open left-half plane, i.e., $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$ for all poles $p_i$.
2. The real part of the frequency response is strictly positive for all frequencies:
$$\text{Re}\big[G(j\omega)\big] > 0, \quad \forall\,\omega \in [-\infty, \infty]$$

A transfer function is **Weakly SPR (WSPR)** if it is Positive Real (i.e., $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$) **AND** there exists at least one finite frequency $\omega^\star$ where the strict inequality holds: $\text{Re}[G(j\omega^\star)] > 0$.

The key difference: SPR requires strict positivity across the **entire frequency spectrum**, whereas WSPR permits the real part to touch zero at some frequencies as long as it is strictly positive at least once. WSPR is the more relaxed and practically achievable condition for delayed/uncertain networked plants.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is WSPR preferred over SPR in robust synthesis for networked control systems? Justify with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

WSPR is preferred over SPR in networked control systems because:
1. **Time delays and packet dropouts** introduce phase lag that makes it nearly impossible to maintain $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ across all $\omega$.
2. WSPR only requires strict positivity at **at least one** frequency, which is achievable for most practical plants.
3. The intersection of WSPR regions across polytopic uncertainty vertices is **non-empty far more often** than the intersection of strict SPR regions.
4. WSPR still guarantees the **Lyapunov stability** of the closed-loop interconnected system via the KYP lemma.

**Example:** Consider a plant with communication delay $\tau$, modeled as $G(s) = \frac{e^{-\tau s}}{s+1}$. As $\omega \to \infty$, $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \to 0$, so strict SPR is violated. However, at $\omega = 0$, $\text{Re}[G(j0)] = 1 > 0$, satisfying WSPR. Hence WSPR is the appropriate framework for such systems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** For the uncertain plant family:
$$G(s, \Delta) = \frac{K}{s^2 + (2+\delta)s + (1+\delta)}, \quad \delta \in [-0.5, 0.5]$$
identify the polytopic vertices and write the WSPR feasibility condition in LMI form using the KYP lemma. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With $K = 1$, derive the WSPR condition explicitly at $\omega^\star = 0$ and at $\omega^\star = 1$, and determine the intersection region of feasible $\delta$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Identify the two vertices by setting $\delta = -0.5$ and $\delta = +0.5$:
$$G_1(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 1.5s + 0.5}, \quad G_2(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 2.5s + 1.5}$$

**Step 2:** Each vertex can be expressed in state-space form $G_i(s) = C(sI - A_i)^{-1} B_i + D_i$:

For $G_1(s)$: $A_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -0.5 & -1.5 \end{bmatrix}, B_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, C_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, D_1 = 0$

For $G_2(s)$: $A_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -1.5 & -2.5 \end{bmatrix}, B_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, C_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, D_2 = 0$

**Step 3:** Apply KYP Lemma. The WSPR LMI feasibility for vertex $i$ requires $P_i = P_i^T > 0$ such that:

$$\begin{bmatrix} A_i^T P_i + P_i A_i & P_i B_i - C_i^T \\ B_i^T P_i - C_i & -(D_i + D_i^T) \end{bmatrix} < 0$$

**Step 4:** The robust WSPR condition is the combined feasibility of both LMIs simultaneously, i.e., the intersection region:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \mathcal{R}_1 \cap \mathcal{R}_2 \neq \emptyset$$

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Evaluate $G(j\omega, \delta) = \frac{1}{(j\omega)^2 + (2+\delta)(j\omega) + (1+\delta)}$ at $\omega = 0$:

$$G(j0, \delta) = \frac{1}{1+\delta}$$

$$\text{Re}[G(j0, \delta)] = \frac{1}{1+\delta}$$

For $\delta \in [-0.5, 0.5]$: $\text{Re}[G(j0,\delta)] \in [\frac{2}{3}, 2] > 0$. So WSPR strict-positivity condition is satisfied at $\omega = 0$ for all $\delta$.

**Step 2:** Evaluate at $\omega = 1$:
$$G(j1, \delta) = \frac{1}{-1 + j(2+\delta) + (1+\delta)} = \frac{1}{\delta + j(2+\delta)}$$

$$\text{Re}[G(j1, \delta)] = \frac{\delta}{\delta^2 + (2+\delta)^2}$$

**Step 3:** For the numerator to be positive: $\delta > 0$, i.e., $\delta \in (0, 0.5]$.

**Step 4:** Intersection of feasible $\delta$ regions: WSPR is satisfied at $\omega = 0$ for all $\delta \in [-0.5, 0.5]$ and at $\omega = 1$ for $\delta \in (0, 0.5]$. The intersection is:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \{\delta \,\vert\, \delta \in (0, 0.5]\}$$

This region is non-empty, confirming **feasibility** of robust WSPR synthesis.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the conceptual difference between nominal SPR synthesis and robust SPR synthesis. Why is the **intersection of WSPR regions** the central mathematical operation in the robust case? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the 2-vertex polytopic family with WSPR feasibility LMIs:
$$\mathcal{L}_1(\theta) = \theta^2 - 3\theta + 2 \leq 0$$
$$\mathcal{L}_2(\theta) = -\theta^2 + 4\theta - 3 \leq 0$$

Find the intersection of feasible $\theta$ regions and determine if a robust controller exists. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Nominal SPR synthesis:** Designs a controller $K(s)$ assuming the plant $G(s)$ is known exactly. The closed-loop $T(s) = GK/(1+GK)$ must satisfy $\text{Re}[T(j\omega)] > 0$ for all $\omega$. Only one KYP-LMI needs to be feasible.

**Step 2 — Robust SPR synthesis:** The plant $G(s, \Delta)$ is uncertain. The controller must maintain SPR/WSPR for **every admissible uncertainty realization**. Multiple LMIs (one per vertex) must hold simultaneously.

**Step 3 — Why intersection is central:** Each vertex $G_i(s)$ imposes its own feasibility constraint on the controller parameter $\theta$, defining a region $\mathcal{R}_i$. Robustness demands $\theta$ be simultaneously feasible for **all** vertices. Mathematically:

$$\theta \in \mathcal{R}_1 \text{ AND } \theta \in \mathcal{R}_2 \text{ AND } \ldots \text{ AND } \theta \in \mathcal{R}_N$$

$$\Leftrightarrow \theta \in \mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{R}_i$$

This intersection is **non-empty if and only if** the combined LMI system is feasible — the definitive criterion for robust WSPR synthesis.

**Step 4:** Practical engineering reason — at high frequencies, different vertices may impose conflicting phase requirements on $K(s)$. The intersection finds the controller gains that satisfy the **worst-case** vertex without sacrificing performance at others.

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Solve $\mathcal{L}_1(\theta) \leq 0$:
$$\theta^2 - 3\theta + 2 \leq 0 \implies (\theta - 1)(\theta - 2) \leq 0 \implies \theta \in [1, 2]$$

**Step 2:** Solve $\mathcal{L}_2(\theta) \leq 0$:
$$-\theta^2 + 4\theta - 3 \leq 0 \implies \theta^2 - 4\theta + 3 \geq 0 \implies (\theta - 1)(\theta - 3) \geq 0$$

This gives $\theta \in (-\infty, 1] \cup [3, \infty)$.

**Step 3:** Compute the intersection:
$$\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = [1, 2] \cap \big((-\infty, 1] \cup [3, \infty)\big)$$

$$= [1, 2] \cap (-\infty, 1] \ \cup\ [1, 2] \cap [3, \infty)$$

$$= \{1\} \ \cup\ \emptyset = \{1\}$$

**Step 4:** The intersection is the singleton set $\{\theta = 1\}$. Since $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} \neq \emptyset$, a **robust controller exists** with the unique parameter choice $\theta^\star = 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting analyticity condition:** Students often write only the frequency condition $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ but forget the pole-location prerequisite. *Loss: up to 1 mark.*
> 2. **Sign error in KYP LMI:** The off-diagonal block is $PB - C^T$, not $C^T - PB$. Sign mistakes propagate and yield infeasible LMIs. *Loss: up to 2 marks.*
> 3. **Conflating PR and SPR:** Saying "Positive Real" when the question asks for "Strictly Positive Real" is a definitional error. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Ignoring feedthrough term:** Forgetting $D + D^T > 0$ for proper SPR systems with non-zero $D$ is a frequent omission.
> 5. **Empty intersection not addressed:** When $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \emptyset$, students must explicitly state *why* — typically due to over-restrictive WSPR or excessive uncertainty.
> 6. **WSPR vs SPR misuse:** Applying SPR conditions (full strict positivity) when WSPR is required (existence of one $\omega^\star$) leads to infeasibility in scenarios where a WSPR controller exists.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPR** = strict positivity at **all** frequencies + Hurwitz poles.
- **WSPR** = PR property + strict positivity at **at least one** frequency $\omega^\star$.
- **WSPR is the engineering-relaxed version** of SPR, suited to delayed/uncertain networked plants.
- The **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) Lemma** is the master tool that converts infinite-dimensional frequency SPR tests into finite-dimensional LMIs.
- **Robust SPR synthesis** requires WSPR (or SPR) to hold for **every vertex** of the polytopic uncertainty description.
- The **intersection of WSPR regions** is the central mathematical operation: $\mathcal{R}_{\text{robust}} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{R}_i$.
- **Feasibility** of the intersection (non-emptiness) is equivalent to the existence of a robust controller.
- The combined LMI $\text{diag}(\mathcal{L}_1, \mathcal{L}_2, \ldots, \mathcal{L}_N) < 0$ is computationally tractable via Semi-Definite Programming (SDP).
- **Passivity interpretation:** WSPR guarantees the closed-loop dissipates more energy than it generates across the operating envelope.
- **Real-world applications:** Networked control with packet dropouts, adaptive systems with parameter drift, absolute stability of Lur'e-type systems, haptic and biomedical coupled systems.
- **Examiner hot-spots:** (i) Sign of KYP off-diagonal block, (ii) Pole analyticity requirement, (iii) Distinction between PR/WSPR/SPR, (iv) Handling of non-empty vs empty intersections, (v) Polytopic vertex enumeration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications to Robust SPR Synthesis for Low-order Systems - The Third-order SPR Synthesis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications to Robust SPR Synthesis for Low-order Systems - The Third-order SPR Synthesis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Strictly Positive Real (SPR)?

In the **frequency domain**, a rational proper transfer function $G(s)$ is called **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if it satisfies two simultaneous conditions:

$$
\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} \;>\; 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \in [-\infty, \infty]
$$

and $G(s)$ is **analytic in the closed right-half plane** $\text{Re}(s) \ge 0$ (i.e., no poles in $\text{Re}(s) \ge 0$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Positive Real (PR)** allows $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} \ge 0$, while **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** demands a *strict* inequality. The strict version is what guarantees passivity with a *strict* energy dissipation margin, and is the cornerstone of absolute stability theory (Popov, Circle, Off-Axis Circle criteria).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Energy-Losing Spring"

Imagine a mass-spring-damper mechanical oscillator. The spring stores energy, the damper *dissipates* it. A **Positive Real** system is like a perfectly elastic spring-damper — it can only absorb or pass energy, never generate it. A **Strictly Positive Real** system is the *more disciplined cousin* — it not only refuses to create energy, it **guarantees a minimum rate of dissipation**. In the frequency domain, this is exactly the requirement that the *real part of the input-output energy ratio* is always positive.

> [!NOTE]
> In **networked control systems**, SPR property of the loop transfer function guarantees robustness against a *very large* class of nonlinearities and time-varying delays satisfying a sector bound (passivity theorem, Zames-Falb, etc.). That is why synthesizing a compensator to enforce SPR is a *central design objective*.

## 1.3 What is the "Third-Order SPR Synthesis"?

It is the specific *low-order specialization* of the robust SPR synthesis problem. We restrict our attention to systems of the form:

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{b_{2}s^{2} + b_{1}s + b_{0}}{s^{3} + a_{2}s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0}}
$$

For such a third-order system, the *infinite-dimensional* SPR constraints reduce to a **finite, explicit, and checkable set of algebraic inequalities on the coefficients** $(a_0, a_1, a_2, b_0, b_1, b_2)$. The synthesis then amounts to: *given uncertain plant parameters, find a controller that places the closed-loop transfer function into this admissible set*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reason third-order is special: for *n = 1* the result is trivial, for *n = 2* it is a small LMIs/inequality set, but for **n = 3 the structure is the largest order where closed-form analytic conditions exist** (Routh-Hurwitz + Hermite bilinear forms remain writable by hand). For *n ≥ 4*, one is forced to numerical LMI/BMI solvers.

## 1.4 Robust SPR Synthesis — Why?

In robust control, the *plant* is uncertain. A design that yields an SPR closed-loop is *automatically* robust against:
- Additive/multiplicative passive uncertainties.
- Sector-bounded nonlinearities (Popov criterion).
- A class of time-delays.

For **low-order systems** (third-order in particular), explicit coefficient inequalities are computationally trivial, making them ideal for **real-time adaptive reconfiguration** in networked and embedded control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Equivalent Definitions of SPR

### (A) Frequency-Domain Definition
A proper rational $G(s)$ is SPR iff:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(i)}\quad G(s) \text{ is analytic in } \text{Re}(s) \ge 0 \\
&\text{(ii)}\quad \text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} > 0 \quad \forall\, \omega \in \mathbb{R}
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

### (B) State-Space Definition (KYP Form)
Let $G(s) = C(sI - A)^{-1}B + D$ be a minimal realization, with $A \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ Hurwitz. Then $G(s)$ is SPR **iff** there exist matrices $P = P^{T} > 0$ and $L$ such that:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
PA + A^{T}P &= -(L^{T}L + \nu\, P B B^{T} P), \quad \nu > 0 \\
PB &= C^{T} - L^{T}W \\
W^{T}W &= D + D^{T} > 0
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Kalman-Yakubovich-Popov (KYP) Lemma** in its *strict* (SPR) form. The parameter $\nu > 0$ injects the *strict* margin into the energy inequality. When $\nu = 0$, we recover the Positive Real (PR) version.

## 2.2 The Third-Order Canonical Plant

We study the strictly proper case (controller synthesis usually places the constant term in the *numerator* via the compensator $D$):

$$
G(s) \;=\; \frac{b_{2}s^{2} + b_{1}s + b_{0}}{s^{3} + a_{2}s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0}}
$$

with $(a_0, a_1, a_2) > 0$ (otherwise it cannot even be Hurwitz).

## 2.3 Hurwitz Conditions on the Denominator (Routh-Hurwitz, n = 3)

For $D(s) = s^{3} + a_{2}s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0}$ to have all roots in $\text{Re}(s) < 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;
a_{2} > 0, \quad a_{1} > 0, \quad a_{0} > 0, \quad a_{2}a_{1} > a_{0}\;}
$$

## 2.4 The Hermite Form of $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}$

Setting $\omega^{2} = x \ge 0$ and using the symmetric product $G(j\omega) + G(-j\omega) = 2\,\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}$:

$$
\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} \;=\; \frac{h(x)}{d(x)}, \quad x = \omega^{2} \ge 0
$$

For a third-order $G(s)$, the numerator polynomial $h(x)$ is **quadratic in $x$**:

$$
h(x) \;=\; \alpha_{0} + \alpha_{1}\,x + \alpha_{2}\,x^{2}
$$

with coefficients $\alpha_i$ being **bilinear** in $(a_j, b_k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sign of $h(x)$ for *all* $x \ge 0$ — combined with $d(x) > 0$ for all $x \ge 0$ — is exactly the SPR condition. Since $d(x) > 0$ is automatic (Hurwitz denominator), **SPR reduces to $h(x) > 0$ for all $x \ge 0$**.

## 2.5 The Bilinear Coefficient Formulas

The classical KYP-derived bilinear forms for the third-order $G(s) = \frac{b_{2}s^{2} + b_{1}s + b_{0}}{s^{3} + a_{2}s^{2} + a_{1}s + a_{0}}$ give the Hermite coefficients as:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{0} &= a_{0}b_{0} \\
\alpha_{1} &= a_{0}b_{2} + a_{2}b_{0} - a_{1}b_{1} \\
\alpha_{2} &= b_{0} - a_{2}b_{2}
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

These are obtained by multiplying out $N(s)D(-s) + N(-s)D(s)$ and grouping in $\omega^{2k}$.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning / Definition** | **Key Condition** |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}$ | Real part of frequency response | $>0\;\forall\omega$ |
| SPR | Strictly Positive Real | Both (i) and (ii) of §2.1 hold |
| PR | Positive Real | $\ge 0$ version of (ii) |
| Hurwitz | All poles in $\text{Re}(s)<0$ | Routh–Hurwitz inequalities |
| $h(x)$ | Hermite numerator | $h(x)>0$ on $x\ge 0$ |
| $\alpha_0$ | $a_0 b_0$ | $> 0$ trivially if $a_0,b_0>0$ |
| $\alpha_1$ | $a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1$ | $> 0$ needed |
| $\alpha_2$ | $b_0 - a_2 b_2$ | $> 0$ needed |
| KYP | Kalman-Yakubovich-Popov | State-space energy test |
| $P = P^T > 0$ | Lyapunov matrix | Solves KYP |
| $\nu > 0$ | Strictness margin | Sets SPR apart from PR |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

| **Application Domain** | **Why SPR Matters** |
|---|---|
| Adaptive Control (MRAC) | SPR error dynamics $\Rightarrow$ global stability (Narendra, Annaswamy) |
| Networked Control (NCS) | Robustness to packet dropouts, time-varying delay (passivity-based NCS) |
| Nonlinear Absolute Stability | Popov / Circle / Off-Axis Circle criterion |
| Passive Bilateral Teleoperation | SPR admittance guarantees coupled stability |
| Robust Servo Control | Strict passivity $\Rightarrow$ tolerance to actuator lag |
| Process Control | Smith Predictor with SPR inner-loop $\Rightarrow$ delay-robust |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU examiner mindset, the *third-order* case is preferred in exam questions because all the SPR conditions reduce to **three (3) explicit bilinear inequalities** — easy to mark, no LMI solver required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hermite Numerator $h(x)$ for n = 3

We start from:

$$
G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{b_{2}(j\omega)^{2} + b_{1}(j\omega) + b_{0}}{(j\omega)^{3} + a_{2}(j\omega)^{2} + a_{1}(j\omega) + a_{0}}
$$

Using $(j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$ and $(j\omega)^3 = -j\omega^3$:

$$
G(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{-b_{2}\omega^{2} + j b_{1}\omega + b_{0}}{-j\omega^{3} - a_{2}\omega^{2} + j a_{1}\omega + a_{0}}
$$

The real part is recovered as:

$$
\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\bigl[G(j\omega) + G(-j\omega)\bigr] \;=\; \frac{N(j\omega)\overline{D(j\omega)} + \overline{N(j\omega)}D(j\omega)}{2\,\vert D(j\omega)\vert^{2}}
$$

The denominator $\vert D(j\omega)\vert^{2} = d(\omega^2)$ is *automatically positive* because $D(s)$ is Hurwitz. Hence **SPR $\Leftrightarrow$ numerator of $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}$ is positive for all $\omega$**.

**Step 1.** Form the symmetric product $S(\omega) = N(j\omega)\,D(-j\omega) + N(-j\omega)\,D(j\omega)$.

**Step 2.** Write $D(j\omega) = (a_0 - a_2\omega^2) + j\omega(a_1 - \omega^2)$, hence $D(-j\omega) = (a_0 - a_2\omega^2) - j\omega(a_1 - \omega^2)$.

**Step 3.** Similarly, $N(j\omega) = (b_0 - b_2\omega^2) + jb_1\omega$ and $N(-j\omega) = (b_0 - b_2\omega^2) - jb_1\omega$.

**Step 4.** Multiply out the symmetric product. Because both $N$ and $D$ have the structure (real part even in $\omega$) $+ j\,$(real part odd in $\omega$), the cross terms cancel and we are left with:

$$
S(\omega) \;=\; 2\,(b_0 - b_2\omega^2)(a_0 - a_2\omega^2) + 2\,b_1\omega^{2}\,(a_1 - \omega^2)
$$

**Step 5.** Group by powers of $\omega$. Substituting $x = \omega^2$:

$$
\boxed{\;
S(\omega) \;=\; 2\bigl[\,\alpha_{0} + \alpha_{1}\,x + \alpha_{2}\,x^{2}\,\bigr] \quad \text{where}\; x = \omega^{2}\;}
$$

with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{0} &= b_{0}\,a_{0} \\
\alpha_{1} &= b_{0}\,a_{2} + b_{2}\,a_{0} - b_{1}\,a_{1} \\
\alpha_{2} &= b_{0} - b_{2}\,a_{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6.** Since $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} = S(\omega)/[2\,d(x)]$ and $d(x) > 0$ for all $x \ge 0$ (Hurwitz denominator), the SPR condition reduces to:

$$
\boxed{\;
h(x) \;=\; \alpha_{0} + \alpha_{1}\,x + \alpha_{2}\,x^{2} \;>\; 0 \quad \forall\, x \ge 0\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **central analytical result** of the third-order SPR synthesis: a *one-variable quadratic in $x \ge 0$* must be positive. Quadratics are the *lowest-degree* polynomial for which *finite* conditions (not just asymptotic) are non-trivial.

## 3.2 Reduction to Finite Algebraic Inequalities

A quadratic $h(x) = \alpha_2 x^2 + \alpha_1 x + \alpha_0$ satisfies $h(x) > 0$ for all $x \ge 0$ **iff** the following three conditions hold:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
&\text{(C1)} \quad \alpha_{0} \;>\; 0 \\
&\text{(C2)} \quad \alpha_{1} \;>\; 0 \\
&\text{(C3)} \quad \alpha_{2} \;\ge\; 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \bigl(\alpha_{2} < 0\ \text{and}\ \alpha_{1}^{2} > 4\,\alpha_{0}\alpha_{2}\bigr)
\end{aligned}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The third condition captures two cases:
> 1. If the leading coefficient is non-negative ($\alpha_2 \ge 0$), the parabola opens upward — positivity on the *ray* $x \ge 0$ is automatic from $\alpha_0, \alpha_1 > 0$.
> 2. If $\alpha_2 < 0$ (parabola opens downward), then we need the discriminant condition: the parabola's real roots are both at $x > 0$, so the curve is still positive on $x \ge 0$. This forces the vertex of the parabola to be at $x = -\alpha_1/(2\alpha_2) > 0$ and $h(x)$ at that vertex to be $> 0$, which simplifies to $\alpha_1^2 - 4\alpha_0\alpha_2 > 0$.

## 3.3 Expansion: Robust SPR Synthesis Step-by-Step

### Setup
Plant uncertainty: $a_i = a_i^{0} + \delta a_i$, $b_i = b_i^{0} + \delta b_i$, with $|\delta a_i| \le \bar{a}_i$, $|\delta b_i| \le \bar{b}_i$.

### Step-by-step Procedure
1. **Form the $\alpha$ functions** as bilinear expressions in $(a_i, b_k)$.
2. **Bound each $\alpha$** using the *interval arithmetic* principle: if $\alpha = u_1 u_2 + u_3 u_4 - u_5 u_6$, the worst case is the sum of all products at the extreme signs.
3. **Enforce (C1), (C2), (C3)** at the *worst-case vertex* of the uncertainty hyper-rectangle.
4. **Iterate over controller parameters** (e.g., PID gains $K_p, K_i, K_d$ or lead/lag compensator zero-pole) until the *vertex test* passes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the algorithm of *vertex testing* (or *interval analysis*) for robust SPR. It is exact when the $\alpha$'s are *monotonic* in each uncertainty, which they are for the third-order case.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Verification of a Given Third-Order Plant

**Problem:** Test whether $G(s) = \dfrac{2s^{2} + 5s + 1}{s^{3} + 6s^{2} + 11s + 6}$ is SPR.

**Step 1 — Hurwitz check.**
$a_2 = 6 > 0$, $a_1 = 11 > 0$, $a_0 = 6 > 0$, and $a_2 a_1 = 66 > a_0 = 6$. ✓

**Step 2 — Compute $\alpha$'s.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{0} &= a_0 b_0 = 6 \times 1 = 6 \\
\alpha_{1} &= a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1 = (6)(2) + (6)(1) - (11)(5) = 12 + 6 - 55 = -37 \\
\alpha_{2} &= b_0 - a_2 b_2 = 1 - 6 \times 2 = 1 - 12 = -11
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check (C1), (C2).**
$\alpha_0 = 6 > 0$ ✓, but $\alpha_1 = -37 < 0$ ✗.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** The system is **not SPR** because $\alpha_1 < 0$. The Hermite form $h(x) = -11 x^2 - 37 x + 6$ is negative for all large $x$ and in fact becomes negative around $x \approx 0.15$.

## 3.5 Analytic Synthesis: Finding $K$ for Output-Feedback SPR

**Problem:** Given a stable plant $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$, design a static gain $K$ so that $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{K}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6 + K}$ is SPR.

**Step 1.** The closed-loop denominator $D(s) = s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + (6+K)$ is Hurwitz for all $K > 0$ (since $a_0 = 6+K > 0$ and the other Hurwitz conditions are unchanged).

**Step 2.** The numerator is $N(s) = K$, hence $b_0 = K, b_1 = 0, b_2 = 0$.

**Step 3.** Compute $\alpha$'s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{0} &= a_0 \cdot K = (6+K) K \\
\alpha_{1} &= a_0 \cdot 0 + a_2 \cdot K - a_1 \cdot 0 = 6K \\
\alpha_{2} &= K - a_2 \cdot 0 = K
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Check (C1), (C2), (C3).** All $\alpha$'s are positive for $K > 0$, and $\alpha_2 = K > 0$ means (C3) is automatic. Therefore the closed-loop is **SPR for any $K > 0$**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *deep result* in adaptive control: for a strictly positive real plant with a *constant* feedback gain, the closed-loop is automatically SPR. The synthesis reduces to picking any positive $K$.

## 3.6 Summary: The Three Steps of the Algorithm

| **Step** | **Action** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Form the Hermite $h(x)$ from $(a_i, b_k)$ | Bilinear $\alpha_i$'s |
| 2 | Verify $h(x) > 0$ on $x \ge 0$ via (C1)–(C3) | 3 algebraic inequalities |
| 3 | (Robust) Test at all uncertainty vertices | Feasibility certificate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Overall SPR Synthesis Workflow

The diagram below shows the **block-level functional architecture** of the third-order robust SPR synthesis pipeline as used in the **networked control** setting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uncertain Third-Order Plant Gp s] --> B[Plant Realization A B C D]
    B --> C[Compute Hermite Coefficients alpha0 alpha1 alpha2]
    C --> D[Apply SPR Conditions C1 C2 C3]
    D --> E{All Three Conditions Satisfied?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Plant is SPR — Passivity Certificate Issued]
    E -- No --> G[Robust Compensator Design Kp Ki Kd]
    G --> H[Closed-Loop Transfer Function Tcl s]
    H --> C
    F --> I[Networked Control Layer: Tolerates Delay and Dropout]
    I --> J[Stable Operation Across NCS Channel]
```

## 4.2 State-Space / Frequency-Domain Equivalence

The diagram below maps the **two equivalent SPR viewpoints** (state-space KYP vs. frequency-domain Hermite), which is the conceptual glue of the entire topic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Transfer Function G of s] --> B[Realization A B C D]
    B --> C[KYP Lemma: Find P greater than 0]
    C --> D{PA plus AT P = negative definite?}
    D -- Yes --> E[SPR Certified via State Space]
    A --> F[Form Hermite h of x = omega squared]
    F --> G[Check h of x greater than 0 for x greater than or equal to 0]
    G --> H[SPR Certified via Frequency Domain]
    E <--> H[Both Are Equivalent by KYP]
```

## 4.3 Robust SPR Synthesis with Uncertainty Bounds

The diagram below shows how the **uncertain plant parameters** are propagated through the SPR synthesis to produce a **robust gain set**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nominal Plant: a0, a1, a2, b0, b1, b2] --> B[Define Uncertainty Box: delta a_i less than bar a_i]
    B --> C[Enumerate Vertices of the Box]
    C --> D[For Each Vertex Compute alpha_i]
    D --> E[Check C1 C2 C3]
    E -- Pass at All Vertices --> F[Robustly SPR — Acceptable]
    E -- Fail at Some Vertex --> G[Tune Compensator Parameters]
    G --> H[Re-Test at All Vertices]
    H --> E
    F --> I[Deploy in Networked Control Loop]
```

## 4.4 NCS Layer — Why SPR Buys Robustness

The diagram below illustrates the **networked control layer** and the role of SPR in handling delay/dropout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller K] --> B[Network Channel: Delay tau and Dropout p]
    B --> C[Plant Gp s]
    C --> D[Sensor Feedback]
    D --> E[Estimator and Delay Compensator]
    E --> A
    C --> F[SPR Certification: Re G of j omega greater than 0]
    F --> G[Passivity Margin Bounds Tolerable tau and p]
    G --> H[Guaranteed Stability over NCS]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**State the frequency-domain definition of a Strictly Positive Real (SPR) transfer function. Why is strictness important in robust control?**

**Model Answer:**

A proper rational transfer function $G(s)$ is SPR if:
1. $G(s)$ is analytic in $\text{Re}(s) \ge 0$ (no RHP poles).
2. $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} > 0$ for all $\omega \in [-\infty, \infty]$.

Strictness injects an energy *dissipation margin* quantified by the parameter $\nu > 0$ in the KYP inequality $PA + A^{T}P = -(C^{T}C + \nu P B B^{T} P)$. This margin is what allows the closed-loop to remain stable under a *non-zero* sector-bounded nonlinearity, time-delay, or packet dropout in a Networked Control System (NCS).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Writing "$\ge 0$" instead of "$> 0$" loses **1 mark** immediately. The strict inequality is the *defining* feature of SPR vs. PR.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**For a third-order transfer function $G(s) = \frac{b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0}{s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}$, write the three bilinear expressions that form the Hermite coefficients $\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\alpha_0 = a_0 b_0, \quad \alpha_1 = a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1, \quad \alpha_2 = b_0 - a_2 b_2
$$

The SPR condition is equivalent to the Hermite polynomial $h(x) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 x + \alpha_2 x^2$ being strictly positive for all $x = \omega^2 \ge 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often miss the minus sign in $\alpha_1 = a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1$. The cross term from the symmetric product is *negative*. One mark is deducted for sign errors.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the three bilinear expressions for the Hermite coefficients $\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$ of a third-order strictly positive real transfer function

$$
G(s) = \frac{b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0}{s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0}
$$

starting from the symmetric product $N(j\omega) D(-j\omega) + N(-j\omega) D(j\omega)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the result from (a), state and prove the three algebraic conditions (C1, C2, C3) for $h(x) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 x + \alpha_2 x^2$ to be strictly positive for all $x \ge 0$. Then verify SPR-ness of the plant

$$
G(s) = \frac{s^2 + 3s + 2}{s^3 + 5s^2 + 8s + 4}
$$

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**Step 1.** Write $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{N(j\omega)}{D(j\omega)}$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
N(j\omega) &= (b_0 - b_2 \omega^2) + j b_1 \omega \\
D(j\omega) &= (a_0 - a_2 \omega^2) + j \omega (a_1 - \omega^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** The real part formula:

$$
\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} = \frac{N(j\omega)\,\overline{D(j\omega)} + \overline{N(j\omega)}\,D(j\omega)}{2 \vert D(j\omega) \vert^2}
$$

**Step 3.** Form the symmetric product. Letting $R_N = b_0 - b_2\omega^2$, $I_N = b_1\omega$, $R_D = a_0 - a_2\omega^2$, $I_D = \omega(a_1 - \omega^2)$:

$$
(R_N + jI_N)(R_D - jI_D) + (R_N - jI_N)(R_D + jI_D) = 2(R_N R_D + I_N I_D)
$$

**Step 4.** Substitute and use $x = \omega^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(R_N R_D + I_N I_D) &= 2(b_0 - b_2 x)(a_0 - a_2 x) + 2 b_1 x (a_1 - x) \\
&= 2\bigl[a_0 b_0 + (a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1) x + (b_0 - a_2 b_2) x^2\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Identify the Hermite coefficients:

$$
\boxed{\;\alpha_0 = a_0 b_0, \quad \alpha_1 = a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1, \quad \alpha_2 = b_0 - a_2 b_2\;}
$$

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Statement of the three conditions:**

$$
\text{(C1) } \alpha_0 > 0, \quad \text{(C2) } \alpha_1 > 0, \quad \text{(C3) } \alpha_2 \ge 0 \text{ OR } \alpha_1^2 > 4\alpha_0 \alpha_2
$$

**Proof sketch.**

- **(C1)**: At $x = 0$, $h(0) = \alpha_0 > 0$ is required.
- **(C2)**: As $x \to \infty$ with $\alpha_2 \ge 0$, we need $h$ to *grow* — that is, the leading coefficient must be non-negative **and** the linear coefficient positive. If $\alpha_1 < 0$, then near $x = 0$ the curve descends. To compensate we need $\alpha_2 > 0$ so that the parabola eventually turns up before re-crossing zero. The intersection of $h(x) = 0$ has discriminant $\Delta = \alpha_1^2 - 4\alpha_0\alpha_2$. If $\alpha_1^2 > 4\alpha_0\alpha_2$, then $h$ has two *positive* real roots, but $h(0) = \alpha_0 > 0$ and $h(\infty) = +\infty$ imply $h > 0$ on the whole $x \ge 0$ half-line.

**Numerical Verification for** $G(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + 3s + 2}{s^3 + 5s^2 + 8s + 4}$:

With $a_2 = 5, a_1 = 8, a_0 = 4, b_2 = 1, b_1 = 3, b_0 = 2$:

**Hurwitz check:** $5 \cdot 8 = 40 > 4$ ✓

**Hermite coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_0 &= 4 \times 2 = 8 \\
\alpha_1 &= 4 \times 1 + 5 \times 2 - 8 \times 3 = 4 + 10 - 24 = -10 \\
\alpha_2 &= 2 - 5 \times 1 = -3
\end{aligned}
$$

(C1) $\alpha_0 = 8 > 0$ ✓
(C2) $\alpha_1 = -10 < 0$ ✗

**Conclusion:** The plant is **not SPR** because (C2) fails.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often "stop" after (C1) and declare SPR. **Both** (C1) and (C2) must be checked. Also: forgetting the *Hurwitz* check on the denominator is another common 1-mark loss. The *full marks* require all three checks.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of the KYP Lemma, explain the state-space conditions for a transfer function $G(s) = C(sI-A)^{-1}B + D$ to be SPR. Identify the role of the parameter $\nu > 0$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A third-order plant is given by $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$. Design a static gain $K$ such that the closed-loop transfer function $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{K}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + (6+K)}$ is SPR. Justify your answer using the Hermite coefficient conditions.

#### **Model Solution to (a)**

**The KYP Lemma for SPR (state-space form).**

A minimal realization $(A, B, C, D)$ of a proper transfer function $G(s)$ is SPR **if and only if** there exist matrices $P = P^{T} > 0$ and a matrix $L$ such that:

$$
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
PA + A^{T}P &= -(L^{T}L + \nu\, P B B^{T} P) \\
PB &= C^{T} - L^{T}W \\
W^{T}W &= D + D^{T} \quad > 0
\end{aligned}\;} \quad \nu > 0
$$

**Role of $\nu$:** It is the *strictness margin*. In the energy inequality

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\bigl(x^{T}Px\bigr) = -x^{T}L^{T}Lx - \nu\, (B^{T}Px)^{2} + 2 u^{T} y \le 2 u^{T} y
$$

the term $-\nu(B^{T}Px)^2$ is the *extra* dissipation that makes $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\} > 0$ *strictly*. Setting $\nu = 0$ recovers the Positive Real (PR) case.

The KYP lemma thus *bridges* the algebraic SPR conditions (Hermite $\alpha$'s) and the dynamic energy inequality, providing the same certificate in two equivalent languages.

#### **Model Solution to (b)**

**Plant:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + 6}$.

**Closed-loop with static gain $K$** (in the unity feedback configuration, $G_{cl}(s) = K G_p(s)/(1 + K G_p(s))$, but for the simpler proportional-loading interpretation $G_{cl} = K G_p$ — the *robust* version is the one we use in MRAC):

$$
G_{cl}(s) = \frac{K}{s^3 + 6s^2 + 11s + (6+K)}
$$

Identify: $a_2 = 6$, $a_1 = 11$, $a_0 = 6+K$, $b_2 = 0$, $b_1 = 0$, $b_0 = K$.

**Hurwitz check:** $a_2 = 6 > 0$, $a_1 = 11 > 0$, $a_0 = 6+K > 0$ (for $K > -6$), and $a_2 a_1 = 66 > 6+K$ for $K < 60$. The plant is Hurwitz for $-6 < K < 60$. Since we choose $K > 0$, this is satisfied.

**Hermite coefficients:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_0 &= a_0 b_0 = (6+K)K \\
\alpha_1 &= a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1 = 0 + 6K - 0 = 6K \\
\alpha_2 &= b_0 - a_2 b_2 = K - 0 = K
\end{aligned}
$$

**SPR conditions:**

- (C1): $\alpha_0 = (6+K)K > 0$ for $K > 0$ ✓
- (C2): $\alpha_1 = 6K > 0$ for $K > 0$ ✓
- (C3): $\alpha_2 = K > 0$ — parabola opens upward — automatic ✓

**Conclusion:** $G_{cl}(s)$ is **SPR for any $K > 0$**.

**Justification:** The closed-loop denominator is Hurwitz, the Hermite quadratic $h(x) = K x^2 + 6K x + (6+K)K$ has all-positive coefficients, and its discriminant is $36K^2 - 4K^2(6+K) = 4K^2(9 - 6 - K) = 4K^2(3 - K)$. For small positive $K$, the discriminant is positive, but positivity on $x \ge 0$ is still guaranteed by $\alpha_0, \alpha_1 > 0$ and $\alpha_2 > 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common error is to *compute* the closed-loop as $\frac{KG_p}{1+KG_p}$ (which is second-order) instead of recognizing the problem as the *multiplicative-gain* structure used in MRAC. Read the denominator carefully — if it is the *plant denominator* plus $K$ in the constant term, this is the **modified plant** $G_{cl}(s) = K G_p(s)$, the standard MRAC augmentation. Mis-interpreting the closed-loop loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Mark Deductions in SPR Synthesis Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing SPR with PR:** Writing $\ge 0$ instead of $> 0$ costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Missing the Hurwitz check:** The denominator must be checked *separately* before the Hermite conditions. Many students skip it.
> 3. **Sign error in $\alpha_1$:** The cross term is *negative* — a frequent algebraic slip.
> 4. **Forgetting the discriminant sub-case of (C3):** When $\alpha_2 < 0$, the discriminant condition $\alpha_1^2 > 4\alpha_0 \alpha_2$ is mandatory.
> 5. **Confusing the closed-loop formula:** Proportional gain on the *plant* vs. unity-feedback closed-loop — different structures, different answers.
> 6. **Ignoring $\nu$ in the KYP statement:** The KYP strictness parameter is the *defining* difference between SPR and PR. Always mention it.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPR = Strict positivity of $\text{Re}\{G(j\omega)\}$ + analyticity in closed RHP.** This is the central definition — write it *first* in every answer.
- **KYP Lemma (strict form):** There exists $P = P^T > 0$ such that $PA + A^T P = -(L^T L + \nu P B B^T P)$ with $\nu > 0$.
- **Third-order canonical form:** $G(s) = (b_2 s^2 + b_1 s + b_0)/(s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0)$.
- **Routh-Hurwitz (n=3):** $a_2, a_1, a_0 > 0$ and $a_2 a_1 > a_0$ — must be checked *first*.
- **Hermite coefficients:** $\alpha_0 = a_0 b_0$, $\alpha_1 = a_0 b_2 + a_2 b_0 - a_1 b_1$, $\alpha_2 = b_0 - a_2 b_2$.
- **Three SPR algebraic conditions:**
  - (C1) $\alpha_0 > 0$,
  - (C2) $\alpha_1 > 0$,
  - (C3) $\alpha_2 \ge 0$ OR $\alpha_1^2 > 4\alpha_0 \alpha_2$.
- **Robust SPR synthesis:** Use *vertex testing* on the uncertainty box. The third-order case is the *largest* where this remains hand-computable.
- **MRAC connection:** For a SPR plant with constant gain $K > 0$, the closed loop remains SPR. This is the foundation of Narendra's MRAC scheme.
- **NCS connection:** SPR certification of the loop transfer function guarantees robustness to time-varying delay and packet dropout up to a known margin.
- **Mnemonic:** **"Hurwitz first, Hermite second, KYP third"** — the natural order of any SPR analysis.
- **Equation to memorize by heart:** $h(x) = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1 x + \alpha_2 x^2 > 0,\; \forall x \ge 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Fourth-order SPR Synthesis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT862 - ADVANCED NETWORKED CONTROL APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Fourth-order SPR Synthesis

# The Fourth-order SPR Synthesis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 4):** This topic directly extends Module 4 concepts on *Hyperstability and Passivity* into the realm of **strictly positive real (SPR)** transfer functions of order four. It is the mathematical bridge between Popov's hyperstability theorem and the practical synthesis of adaptive / networked control laws.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Strictly Positive Real – SPR).**
> A square, real-rational, proper transfer function $\mathbf{G(s)}$ is said to be **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)** if and only if:
> 1. $\mathbf{G(s)}$ is **analytic in $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$** (i.e., all poles lie strictly in the open left-half plane, $\text{Re}(s) < 0$).
> 2. $\text{Re}\,\bigl[\mathbf{G}(j\omega)\bigr] > 0$ for every real frequency $\omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$.
> 3. Any pole of $\mathbf{G(s)}$ on the imaginary axis (which by condition 1 cannot occur) is simple with a positive-definite residue.

A transfer function satisfying only $\text{Re}[\mathbf{G}(j\omega)] \geq 0$ is called **Weakly Positive Real (WPR)** or simply **Positive Real (PR)**.

A **fourth-order SPR synthesis** is therefore the systematic construction of a transfer function of the form

$$
\mathbf{G}_{4}(s) \;=\; \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} \;=\; \frac{b_{3}s^{3}+b_{2}s^{2}+b_{1}s+b_{0}}{s^{4}+a_{3}s^{3}+a_{2}s^{2}+a_{1}s+a_{0}}
$$

such that all three SPR conditions above are simultaneously satisfied.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition – "The Energy-Consuming Spring Analogy":**
> Think of a mechanical spring-damper. An **SPR transfer function** is mathematically equivalent to a passive, dissipative physical system: if you push energy *into* it (apply a bounded input), the system *always absorbs* some energy — it never *creates* or *amplifies* energy on its own.
>
> A **first-order** SPR system is like a simple dashpot. A **second-order** SPR system behaves like a mass-spring-damper. When we climb up to a **fourth-order** SPR system, we are modelling a more sophisticated passive network — say, a doubly-spring-coupled mechanical structure — that still *must* dissipate energy for every input frequency.
>
> In a **networked/adaptive control loop**, the SPR property is our *mathematical guarantee* that even if the controller makes mistakes (adaptation errors, network delays, packet drops), the closed-loop system will not blow up — it will remain stable. The "fourth order" specifically arises because we typically augment the tracking error with **two state-variable filters** to give the engineer enough design freedom to enforce SPR.

### 1.3 Why "Fourth-order" Specifically?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the order become four?**
> In the standard **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC)** with augmented error (originally Monopoli's formulation, 1974), the tracking error $e = y_{m}-y_{p}$ alone is driven by a transfer function that is generally **NOT** SPR. The classical fix is to **augment** $e$ with two auxiliary filtered signals, raising the order of the linear feedforward block from 2 to **exactly 4**. This is the origin of the "fourth-order" tag.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Structural Steps of Fourth-order SPR Synthesis

The synthesis of a 4th-order SPR transfer function proceeds in **three structured phases**:

**Phase 1 – State-Variable Filter (SVF) Design**

Choose a **strictly Hurwitz, monic** filter polynomial

$$
\mathbf{L}(s) \;=\; s^{2}+l_{1}s+l_{0}, \qquad l_{0}>0,\; l_{1}>0.
$$

This filter is the designer's "free parameter" — it shapes the augmented error dynamics.

**Phase 2 – Augmented Error Construction**

Define three auxiliary signals obtained by passing the tracking error $e(t)$ and the control input $u(t)$ through $\mathbf{L}(s)$:

$$
e_{f}(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\Bigl\{\tfrac{E(s)}{L(s)}\Bigr\}, \qquad
u_{f}(t) \;=\; \mathcal{L}^{-1}\!\Bigl\{\tfrac{U(s)}{L(s)}\Bigr\}.
$$

Form the **augmented error vector**

$$
\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(t) \;=\; \Bigl[\,e(t),\;\; e_{f}(t),\;\; u_{f}(t)\,\Bigr]^{\top}.
$$

**Phase 3 – Assemble the 4th-order SPR Block**

The composite transfer function from control input to tracking error becomes

$$
\mathbf{G}_{4}(s) \;=\; \frac{N_{c}(s)}{D_{p}(s)\,L(s)},
$$

where $D_{p}(s)$ is the plant denominator (typically 2nd order in MRAC setups) and $L(s)$ is the 2nd-order SVF, producing an overall **4th-order strictly proper** transfer function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The product of a **Hurwitz plant denominator** $D_{p}(s)$ and a **Hurwitz filter** $L(s)$ remains Hurwitz. The numerator $N_{c}(s)$ is then synthesised so that $\text{Re}[\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega)] > 0$ for all $\omega$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The table below is **high-yield** — these are the exact formulas KTU examiners test in Module 4.

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Units / Domain |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | SPR Frequency Condition | $\text{Re}\bigl[\mathbf{G}(j\omega)\bigr] > 0, \;\; \forall\,\omega \in \mathbb{R}$ | Real axis |
| 2 | SPR Pole Condition | $\text{Re}(p_{i}) < 0, \;\; \forall\,i$ | LHP (Left Half Plane) |
| 3 | 4th-order Denominator | $D_{4}(s)=s^{4}+a_{3}s^{3}+a_{2}s^{2}+a_{1}s+a_{0}$ | Polynomial |
| 4 | Routh-Hurwitz for 4th order | $a_{3}>0,\; a_{2}>0,\; a_{1}>0,\; a_{0}>0,\; a_{3}a_{2}>a_{1},\; a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}>a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}$ | Stability bounds |
| 5 | Positive Real Lemma (state-space, D=0) | $\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{P}=-\mathbf{Q}<0,\;\;\mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}=\mathbf{C}^{\top}$ | $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}^{\top}>0$ |
| 6 | Augmented Error Dynamics | $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(s)=\bigl[\,1,\;1/L(s),\;1/L(s)\,\bigr]^{\top}\,E(s)$ | Vector |
| 7 | Popov Integral Inequality | $\int_{0}^{t_{1}} \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}^{\top}\mathbf{W}\,\boldsymbol{\varphi}\,d\tau \;\geq\; -\gamma_{0}^{2}$ | Popov's criterion |
| 8 | Hyperstability Sufficient Condition | Linear block SPR **and** nonlinear block satisfy Popov's inequality | Combined |
| 9 | Adaptation Law (MIT-rule variant) | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = -\mathbf{\Gamma}\,\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\,\mathbf{r}$ | Gradient descent |
| 10 | Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) | $\mathbf{G}(s)$ is SPR $\iff$ $\exists\,\mathbf{P}>0$ satisfying KYP | LMI form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The 4th-order SPR synthesis is the workhorse for:
> - **MRAC** with uncertain plant parameters
> - **Networked control** where delays are absorbed into the SPR filter
> - **Teleoperation** (master–slave systems) to guarantee passivity despite variable time-delays
> - **Fault-tolerant adaptive control** in aerospace & process industries

### 2.3 Connection to Popov's Hyperstability

The 4th-order SPR block forms the **linear feedforward path** of Popov's standard feedback configuration:

```
     ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
 u ──▶│  Linear SPR  │─────▶│  Nonlinear / │──▶ −u
     │   Block G₄   │       │ Adaptive     │
     └──────┬───────┘       └──────┬───────┘
            │  e (output)          │
            └──────────────────────┘
```

The hyperstability theorem then guarantees **bounded-input bounded-output (BIBO) stability** in the sense of Lyapunov, provided the nonlinear/adaptive block satisfies Popov's integral inequality. This is the heart of **Model Reference Adaptive Control (MRAC) with augmented error**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Routh-Hurwitz Conditions for the 4th-order Denominator

> **Goal:** Show that $D_{4}(s)=s^{4}+a_{3}s^{3}+a_{2}s^{2}+a_{1}s+a_{0}$ is **Hurwitz** (all roots in the LHP) — a necessary condition for SPR.

**Step 1 – Construct the Routh Array.**

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^{4} & 1 & a_{2} & a_{0} \\
s^{3} & a_{3} & a_{1} & 0 \\
s^{2} & \dfrac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}} & a_{0} & 0 \\
s^{1} & \dfrac{\bigl(\tfrac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}}\bigr)a_{1}-a_{3}a_{0}}{\tfrac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}}} & 0 & 0 \\
s^{0} & a_{0} & 0 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Step 2 – Enforce the First-Column Positivity.**

For all roots to lie in the open LHP, every element of the first column must be **strictly positive**:

$$
a_{3} > 0 \quad (1)
$$

$$
\frac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_{3}a_{2} > a_{1} \quad (2)
$$

$$
a_{1} - \frac{a_{3}^{2}\,a_{0}}{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}} > 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_{1}a_{2}a_{3} > a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2} \quad (3)
$$

$$
a_{0} > 0 \quad (4)
$$

**Step 3 – Final Hurwitz Conditions for 4th Order.**

$$
\boxed{\;a_{0}>0,\;\; a_{1}>0,\;\; a_{2}>0,\;\; a_{3}>0,\;\; a_{3}a_{2}>a_{1},\;\; a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}>a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **standard Routh-Hurwitz necessary and sufficient conditions** for a 4th-order polynomial. They are the bedrock of every SPR synthesis — examiners will test these.

### 3.2 Derivation of the SPR Frequency Condition $\text{Re}[\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega)] > 0$

> **Goal:** Derive algebraic inequalities on the coefficients that guarantee $\text{Re}[\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega)] > 0$ for all $\omega$.

Let

$$
\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{b_{3}(j\omega)^{3}+b_{2}(j\omega)^{2}+b_{1}(j\omega)+b_{0}}{(j\omega)^{4}+a_{3}(j\omega)^{3}+a_{2}(j\omega)^{2}+a_{1}(j\omega)+a_{0}}.
$$

**Step 1 – Separate Real and Imaginary Parts.**

Using $j^{2}=-1,\; j^{3}=-j,\; j^{4}=1$:

$$
\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{\bigl(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0}\bigr)+j\bigl(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3}\bigr)}{\bigl(\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0}\bigr)+j\bigl(a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3}\bigr)}.
$$

**Step 2 – Multiply Numerator and Denominator by the Complex Conjugate of the Denominator.**
