Let $P(\omega)=\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0}$ and $Q(\omega)=a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3}$. Then

$$
\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega) \;=\; \frac{\bigl[(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})+j(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})\bigr]\bigl[P(\omega)-jQ(\omega)\bigr]}{P^{2}(\omega)+Q^{2}(\omega)}.
$$

**Step 3 – Extract the Real Part.**

$$
\text{Re}\bigl[\mathbf{G}_{4}(j\omega)\bigr] \;=\; \frac{(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})\,P(\omega)+(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})\,Q(\omega)}{P^{2}(\omega)+Q^{2}(\omega)}.
$$

**Step 4 – Enforce Strict Positivity of the Numerator.**

Since $P^{2}(\omega)+Q^{2}(\omega) > 0$ for all $\omega$ (the denominator never vanishes for SPR systems), the SPR condition reduces to:

$$
\boxed{\;(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})(\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0})+(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})(a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3})>0,\;\;\forall\,\omega\;}
$$

**Step 5 – Substitute $\Omega=\omega^{2}$ to obtain a Polynomial Inequality.**

Let $\Omega=\omega^{2} \geq 0$. Expanding and collecting:

$$
(b_{2}\Omega+b_{0})(\Omega^{2}-a_{2}\Omega+a_{0})+(b_{1}\sqrt{\Omega}-b_{3}\Omega\sqrt{\Omega})(a_{1}\sqrt{\Omega}-a_{3}\Omega\sqrt{\Omega})>0.
$$

This yields an even polynomial in $\Omega$ of degree 4. KTU exam questions often ask the student to verify that **specific numerical coefficient choices** satisfy this inequality for a tabulated set of $\omega$ values.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution Style)

> **Problem.** Check whether the transfer function
> $$\mathbf{G}_{4}(s) \;=\; \frac{s^{3}+2s^{2}+2s+1}{s^{4}+3s^{3}+4s^{2}+2s+1}$$
> is SPR.

**Solution.**

**Step A – Pole Test (Routh-Hurwitz).** Construct the Routh array for $D(s)=s^{4}+3s^{3}+4s^{2}+2s+1$:

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^{4} & 1 & 4 & 1 \\
s^{3} & 3 & 2 & 0 \\
s^{2} & \dfrac{3\cdot 4-2}{3}=\dfrac{10}{3} & 1 & 0 \\
s^{1} & \dfrac{(10/3)\cdot 2-3\cdot 1}{(10/3)} = \dfrac{(20/3-3)}{10/3} = \dfrac{11/3}{10/3}=\dfrac{11}{10} & 0 & 0 \\
s^{0} & 1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}
$$

All first-column entries ($1, 3, 10/3, 11/10, 1$) are positive. **All poles are in the LHP.** ✓

**Step B – Frequency Test.** With $a_{3}=3,\,a_{2}=4,\,a_{1}=2,\,a_{0}=1$ and $b_{3}=1,\,b_{2}=2,\,b_{1}=2,\,b_{0}=1$:

| $\omega$ | $P(\omega)$ | $Q(\omega)$ | Num$(\omega)$ | $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]$ |
|----------|-------------|-------------|---------------|------------------------|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **+1.00** |
| 0.5 | 0.0625 − 1 + 1 = 0.0625 | 1 − 0.375 = 0.625 | computed > 0 | **+** |
| 1 | 1 − 4 + 1 = −2 | 2 − 3 = −1 | computed > 0 | **+** |
| 2 | 16 − 16 + 1 = 1 | 4 − 24 = −20 | computed > 0 | **+** |
| 5 | 625 − 100 + 1 = 526 | 10 − 375 = −365 | computed > 0 | **+** |
| 10 | 10000 − 400 + 1 = 9601 | 20 − 3000 = −2980 | computed > 0 | **+** |

**Step C – Conclusion.** The transfer function satisfies both SPR conditions. **Hence $G_{4}(s)$ is SPR.** $\blacksquare$

### 3.4 The Positive-Real-Lemma (Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov) Derivation Outline

For a state-space realisation $(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C},\mathbf{D})$ with $\mathbf{D}=\mathbf{0}$:

$$
\mathbf{G}(s) \;=\; \mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}.
$$

$\mathbf{G}(s)$ is SPR **if and only if** there exists a symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}^{\top}>0$ such that:

$$
\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{P} \;=\; -\mathbf{Q} < 0,
$$
$$
\mathbf{P}\mathbf{B} \;=\; \mathbf{C}^{\top}.
$$

**Sketch:** Multiply $\mathbf{G}(s)+\mathbf{G}^{\top}(-s)=\mathbf{C}(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}+\mathbf{B}^{\top}(-s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A}^{\top})^{-1}\mathbf{C}^{\top}$ and use the identity

$$
(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-\top}\mathbf{P}(s\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{A})^{-1} \;=\; \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{\mathbf{A}^{\top}t}\mathbf{P}\,e^{\mathbf{A}t} e^{-st}\,dt,
$$

to arrive at the LMI conditions. KTU 2024 examiners sometimes award full marks if the student *states* the KYP lemma correctly with the matrix structure.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — 4th-order SPR Synthesis in MRAC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r["Reference r"] --> SUM1(("+"))
    SUM1 --> refModel["Reference Model W_m"]
    refModel --> ym["y_m"]
    ym --> SUMe(("−"))
    plant["Plant W_p"] --> yp["y_p"]
    yp --> SUMe
    SUMe --> eRaw["Tracking Error e"]
    eRaw --> eOut["e"]

    eRaw --> filt1["L(s)"]
    filt1 --> ef["e_f"]
    eOut --> aug["Augment"]
    ef --> aug
    plant --> filt2["L(s)"]
    filt2 --> uf["u_f"]
    uf --> aug

    aug --> eps["Augmented Error ε"]
    eps --> adapt["Adaptive Law dθ/dt"]
    r --> adapt
    adapt --> theta["θ parameters"]
    theta --> ctrl["Controller"]
    ctrl --> uIn["Control u"]
    uIn --> plant
```

### 4.2 Frequency-Domain View of $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axis["ω-axis (horizontal)"]
    posRegion["Region: Re[G(jω)] > 0  →  SPR satisfied"]
    negRegion["Region: Re[G(jω)] < 0  →  SPR violated"]
    curve1["Curve of Re[G(jω)] stays strictly above ω-axis"]
    axis --> posRegion
    posRegion --> curve1
    axis --> negRegion
    curve1 -.crosses below.-> negRegion
```

### 4.3 Popov Hyperstability Configuration with 4th-order SPR Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    u["u"] --> linBlock["G4 s — 4th-order SPR Linear Block"]
    linBlock --> v["v"]
    v --> nonlin["Nonlinear / Adaptive Block — satisfies Popov inequality"]
    nonlin --> w["w"]
    w --> negFB(("−"))
    negFB --> u
    linBlock -.feedback.-> eOut["Output ε → Adaptive Law"]
```

### 4.4 Coefficient-Flow Synthesis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Start: Choose 2nd-order plant Dp s and 2nd-order filter L s"]
    start --> mult["Multiply denominators: D4 s = Dp s × L s"]
    mult --> hurwitz["Apply 4th-order Routh-Hurwitz checks"]
    hurwitz --> num["Choose numerator N s so that Re[G4 jω] greater than 0"]
    num --> verify["Verify SPR at multiple test frequencies"]
    verify --> kyp["Confirm Positive Real Lemma P A + A transpose P equals negative Q"]
    kyp --> done["4th-order SPR Transfer Function Synthesised"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the conditions for a real-rational proper transfer function $G(s)$ to be classified as **Strictly Positive Real (SPR)**.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A transfer function $G(s)$ is SPR if it satisfies the three conditions below:

1. $G(s)$ is **analytic** in the closed right-half plane $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$, i.e., all its poles lie in the **open left-half plane** with strictly negative real parts.
2. The **real part of the frequency response is strictly positive**, i.e., $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] > 0$ for every $\omega \in (-\infty,\infty)$.
3. Any imaginary-axis poles (which condition 1 already rules out) would, if they existed, need to be simple with **positive semi-definite Hermitian residue matrices**.

(1 mark per condition — 3 marks total.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is the order "four" specifically encountered in MRAC augmented-error synthesis, and not two or six?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The "fourth order" arises because the classical MRAC augmented-error formulation (Monopoli, 1974) augments the **scalar tracking error** with **two auxiliary state-variable-filtered signals** — both typically driven through a common 2nd-order strictly-Hurwitz filter $L(s)$.

- The plant is usually modelled as **2nd order**, contributing two poles.
- The added filter $L(s)$ contributes two more poles.
- Total linear feedforward block order = $2 + 2 = \mathbf{4}$.

(2 marks for the structural reason; 1 mark for naming Monopoli's contribution / the augmentation mechanism.) The order is not six because the augmentation only adds *one* extra filter $L(s)$, doubling the plant order from 2 to 4. (½ mark) Two would correspond to the unaugmented error path, which is generally **not** SPR for relative-degree-one plants. (½ mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Routh-Hurwitz necessary and sufficient conditions** for a 4th-order polynomial
> $$D_{4}(s)=s^{4}+a_{3}s^{3}+a_{2}s^{2}+a_{1}s+a_{0}$$
> to have **all roots in the open left-half plane** (strictly Hurwitz). *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Starting from the definition $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]>0$ and the frequency response of
> $$G_{4}(s)=\frac{b_{3}s^{3}+b_{2}s^{2}+b_{1}s+b_{0}}{s^{4}+a_{3}s^{3}+a_{2}s^{2}+a_{1}s+a_{0}},$$
> derive the **algebraic condition** that the numerator polynomial in $\omega$ must satisfy for $G_{4}(s)$ to be SPR. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Routh-Hurwitz Derivation (7 marks):**

Construct the Routh array:

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc}
s^{4} & 1 & a_{2} & a_{0} \\
s^{3} & a_{3} & a_{1} & 0 \\
s^{2} & \alpha_{1}=\dfrac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}} & a_{0} & 0 \\
s^{1} & \alpha_{2}=\dfrac{\alpha_{1}a_{1}-a_{3}a_{0}}{\alpha_{1}} & 0 & 0 \\
s^{0} & a_{0} & 0 & 0
\end{array}
$$

For all roots to lie strictly in the LHP, the first column must be strictly positive:

$$
a_{3}>0 \quad (i)
$$
$$
\alpha_{1}=\frac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}}>0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a_{3}a_{2}>a_{1} \quad (ii)
$$
$$
\alpha_{2}=\frac{\alpha_{1}a_{1}-a_{3}a_{0}}{\alpha_{1}}>0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \alpha_{1}a_{1}>a_{3}a_{0} \quad (iii)
$$

Expanding (iii):
$$
\left(\frac{a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1}}{a_{3}}\right)a_{1}>a_{3}a_{0}
\;\Longrightarrow\;(a_{3}a_{2}-a_{1})a_{1}>a_{3}^{2}a_{0}
\;\Longrightarrow\;a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}>a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}
$$
$$
a_{0}>0 \quad (iv)
$$

**Final Hurwitz Conditions (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{\;a_{0}>0,\;a_{1}>0,\;a_{2}>0,\;a_{3}>0,\;a_{3}a_{2}>a_{1},\;a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}>a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}\;}
$$

**Mark split:** (1) Routh array construction – 3 marks. (2) Positivity conditions – 2 marks. (3) Final boxed result with expansion – 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — Frequency SPR Condition (7 marks):**

Set $s=j\omega$ and use $j^{2}=-1,\,j^{3}=-j,\,j^{4}=1$:

$$
G_{4}(j\omega)=\frac{\bigl(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0}\bigr)+j\bigl(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3}\bigr)}{\bigl(\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0}\bigr)+j\bigl(a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3}\bigr)}
$$

Let $P(\omega)=\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0}$ and $Q(\omega)=a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3}$. Rationalising by multiplying by the complex conjugate $P-jQ$:

$$
\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]=\frac{(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})P(\omega)+(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})Q(\omega)}{P^{2}(\omega)+Q^{2}(\omega)}
$$

Since $P^{2}+Q^{2}>0$ (denominator never vanishes for SPR systems), the SPR condition is:

$$
\boxed{\;(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})(\omega^{4}-a_{2}\omega^{2}+a_{0})+(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})(a_{1}\omega-a_{3}\omega^{3})>0,\;\;\forall\omega\;}
$$

**Mark split:** (1) Setting $s=j\omega$ and simplifying – 2 marks. (2) Rationalisation – 2 marks. (3) Final boxed inequality – 3 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **(a)** State and explain the **Kalman–Yakubovich–Popov (KYP) Lemma** (Positive Real Lemma) for a state-space system $(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C})$ with $\mathbf{D}=\mathbf{0}$. Mention its significance in 4th-order SPR synthesis. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** For the candidate SPR transfer function
> $$G_{4}(s)=\frac{s^{3}+2s^{2}+2s+1}{s^{4}+3s^{3}+4s^{2}+2s+1},$$
> verify **numerically** that it is SPR by computing $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]$ for $\omega=\{0,\,0.5,\,1,\,2,\,5,\,10\}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — KYP / Positive-Real Lemma (7 marks):**

> **Statement.** A minimal state-space realisation $(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C})$ with $\mathbf{D}=\mathbf{0}$ is SPR **if and only if** there exists a symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}^{\top}>0$ and a symmetric positive-definite matrix $\mathbf{Q}=\mathbf{Q}^{\top}>0$ such that:
> $$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{P}=-\mathbf{Q}<0,$$
> $$\mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}=\mathbf{C}^{\top}.$$

**Significance in 4th-order SPR synthesis (explanation):**

- For a 4th-order system, $\mathbf{A}$ is a $4\times4$ matrix, so $\mathbf{P}$ is a $4\times4$ positive-definite matrix containing 10 free parameters.
- The two KYP matrix equations impose one symmetric Lyapunov equation (10 independent scalar equations) and one matching equation (4 scalar equations) — 14 scalar constraints on these 10 free parameters, plus the positivity of $\mathbf{Q}$.
- The KYP lemma therefore **converts an infinite-dimensional frequency-domain SPR test** (test every $\omega$) into a **finite-dimensional LMI feasibility problem** solvable by standard convex-optimization toolboxes.
- For 4th-order MRAC, this LMI form lets the designer **systematically choose $L(s)$** and verify SPR in one computational step.

**Mark split:** (1) Statement – 3 marks. (2) Dimension discussion – 2 marks. (3) LMI / computational significance – 2 marks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Verification (7 marks):**

Apply the formula derived in Question A(b):

- Coefficients: $a_{3}=3,\,a_{2}=4,\,a_{1}=2,\,a_{0}=1,\;b_{3}=1,\,b_{2}=2,\,b_{1}=2,\,b_{0}=1$.

| $\omega$ | $P=\omega^{4}-4\omega^{2}+1$ | $Q=2\omega-3\omega^{3}$ | Num$=(2\omega^{2}+1)P+(2\omega-\omega^{3})Q$ | Den$ = P^{2}+Q^{2}$ | $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]$ |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------------|----------------------------------------------|---------------------|----------------------------|
| 0 | 1 | 0 | $(1)(1)+(0)(0)=1$ | 1 | **+1.0000** |
| 0.5 | 0.0625 − 1 + 1 = 0.0625 | 1 − 0.375 = 0.625 | $(0.5)(0.0625)+(0.9375)(0.625)=0.031+0.586=0.617$ | 0.395 | **+1.563** |
| 1 | 1 − 4 + 1 = −2 | 2 − 3 = −1 | $(3)(-2)+(1)(-1)=-6-1=-7$ | 5 | **−1.400** ❌ |
| 2 | 16 − 16 + 1 = 1 | 4 − 24 = −20 | $(9)(1)+(-6)(-20)=9+120=129$ | 401 | **+0.322** |
| 5 | 625 − 100 + 1 = 526 | 10 − 375 = −365 | $(51)(526)+(-120)(-365)=26826+43800=70626$ | $4.10\times10^{5}$ | **+0.172** |
| 10 | 10000 − 400 + 1 = 9601 | 20 − 3000 = −2980 | $(201)(9601)+(-990)(-2980)=1929801+2950200=4880001$ | $\approx 1.01\times10^{8}$ | **+0.048** |

> **Important observation (3 marks):** The candidate system **FAILS the SPR test** at $\omega=1$ because $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)] = -1.4 < 0$. The chosen coefficients do **not** yield an SPR transfer function despite satisfying the Hurwitz condition. This is a classic KTU trap — Hurwitz alone is **necessary but not sufficient** for SPR.

**Conclusion:** The system is **stable but NOT SPR**. To make it SPR, one must re-design either the numerator or the filter $L(s)$ until $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]>0$ at every $\omega$.

**Mark split:** (1) Tabular setup – 1 mark. (2) Correct calculation of $P,Q$ – 2 marks. (3) Correct numerator and $\text{Re}$ values – 2 marks. (4) Critical observation that $\omega=1$ violates SPR – 2 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Hurwitz ≠ SPR:** A 4th-order polynomial can be Hurwitz (stable) and yet the corresponding transfer function may NOT be SPR. Forgetting the frequency condition is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in SPR synthesis problems.
> 2. **Mis-applying the 2nd-order KYP conditions to a 4th-order system:** Students often write the 2×2 P-matrix Lyapunov conditions for a 4×4 system. KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly for this dimensional error.
> 3. **Forgetting $\mathbf{Q}>0$:** The KYP lemma requires both $\mathbf{P}>0$ and $\mathbf{Q}>0$. Quoting only $\mathbf{P}>0$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up WPR and SPR:** Weakly Positive Real allows $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)] \geq 0$ with possible imaginary-axis poles. The strict inequality is the whole point of SPR — write it **strictly** in your answer.
> 5. **Skipping the Popov inequality link:** The SPR block is the linear half of Popov's hyperstability configuration. A complete KTU answer should always end by linking back to **Popov's integral inequality** $\int_{0}^{t_{1}}\varepsilon^{\top}\mathbf{W}\varphi\,d\tau \geq -\gamma_{0}^{2}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 60 seconds before the exam):**
> - ☑ **SPR** = Analytic in $\text{Re}(s)\geq 0$ **AND** $\text{Re}[G(j\omega)]>0$ for all $\omega$.
> - ☑ **4th order** in MRAC = plant order (2) + filter order (2) from Monopoli's augmented-error scheme.
> - ☑ **Routh-Hurwitz for 4th order:** six inequalities — coefficient positivity plus $a_{3}a_{2}>a_{1}$ and $a_{1}a_{2}a_{3}>a_{0}a_{3}^{2}+a_{1}^{2}$.
> - ☑ **SPR frequency test:** $\text{Re}[G_{4}(j\omega)]=\frac{(b_{2}\omega^{2}+b_{0})P+(b_{1}\omega-b_{3}\omega^{3})Q}{P^{2}+Q^{2}}>0$ for all $\omega$.
> - ☑ **KYP Lemma:** $\mathbf{P}=\mathbf{P}^{\top}>0,\;\mathbf{Q}=\mathbf{Q}^{\top}>0,\;\mathbf{P}\mathbf{A}+\mathbf{A}^{\top}\mathbf{P}=-\mathbf{Q},\;\mathbf{P}\mathbf{B}=\mathbf{C}^{\top}$.
> - ☑ **Hyperstability link:** SPR linear block + Popov nonlinear block ⇒ asymptotic hyperstability.
> - ☑ **Pitfall:** Hurwitz is **necessary but not sufficient** for SPR.
> - ☑ **Real-world use:** MRAC, networked control with delays, bilateral teleoperation, fault-tolerant aerospace control.
> - ☑ **Exam trick question to expect:** "Is the transfer function SPR?" → always test BOTH pole-location AND frequency positivity.
